topics.title: Generative Modeling - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generative Modeling - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Generative Modeling** is a class of machine learning approaches that aim to learn the underlying data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ of a training dataset in order to generate **new, previously unseen samples** that are statistically similar to the original data. Unlike discriminative models that learn the decision boundary $P(y \mid x)$ for classification, generative models explicitly model *how the data is generated*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (High-Yield KTU)**
> A **Generative Model** learns the joint probability distribution $P(X, Y)$ (or simply $P(X)$ for unsupervised cases) over a dataset, enabling it to produce new samples by sampling from the learned distribution. The fundamental objective is:
> $$\text{Goal: } \theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{\theta}(x_i)$$
> where $\theta$ represents the parameters of the model and $p_{\theta}(x)$ is the estimated probability of generating a data point $x$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **generative model** like a **counterfeit artist** who studies thousands of genuine ₹500 currency notes. The artist doesn't memorize each specific note — instead, they learn the *rules* behind the design: the security thread position, the Mahatma Gandhi portrait style, the colour gradient, the typography of the denomination, and the watermark.

After studying enough genuine notes, the artist can **create a brand-new note** that has never existed before but looks *plausibly real* to a layperson. That is exactly what a generative model does:

| Role | Real-World Analogy | Generative AI Equivalent |
|------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Genuine notes | Real training dataset | Real data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ |
| Artist's brain | Learning algorithm | Neural network with weights $\theta$ |
| Counterfeit rule book | Implicit pattern memory | Learned distribution $p_{\theta}(x)$ |
| Newly printed fake note | Generated output | Synthetic sample $\tilde{x} \sim p_{\theta}$ |

A **discriminative model**, in contrast, would only be an **authenticity checker** — it takes a note and outputs "Real" or "Fake," without ever understanding *how* a real note is constructed.

## 1.3 Why Generative Modeling Matters

- **Synthetic Data Generation:** Creates realistic data when real data is scarce (medical scans, rare-event simulation).
- **Density Estimation:** Estimates the probability that a sample belongs to the data distribution (anomaly detection).
- **Representation Learning:** Learns meaningful latent features useful for downstream tasks.
- **Conditional Generation:** Generates samples conditioned on labels, text, or images (text-to-image, image inpainting).
- **Simulation & Planning:** Used in reinforcement learning, autonomous systems, and digital twins.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> The course PCCMT602 follows the NEP 2020 outcome-based education model. Generative Modeling is the *foundational concept* upon which GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models, and LLMs are built. Mastering this module is mandatory for understanding the remaining 4 modules.

## 1.4 Generative vs. Discriminative Models — The Fundamental Divide

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor Point**
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between generative and discriminative models."* This is a guaranteed 7-to-14 mark question in Part B.

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|--------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Goal** | Learn decision boundary | Learn data distribution |
| **Probability** | Models $P(y \mid x)$ | Models $P(x)$ or $P(x, y)$ |
| **Use Case** | Classification, regression | Sample generation, density estimation |
| **Output** | Label / class probability | New data sample |
| **Examples** | Logistic Regression, SVM, ResNet | Naive Bayes, GAN, VAE, Diffusion |
| **Question Answered** | "Which class does this $x$ belong to?" | "How is this $x$ generated, and can I make a new one?" |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Generative Models

The entire theoretical scaffold of generative modeling rests on **two fundamental probability rules**. These rules appear in nearly every KTU question paper and must be memorized verbatim.

### 2.1.1 The Product Rule of Probability (Chain Rule)

$$P(X, Y) = P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)$$

> **Interpretation:** The joint probability of two events can always be decomposed into a conditional times a marginal.

### 2.1.2 The Sum Rule of Probability (Marginalization)

$$P(X) = \sum_{Y} P(X, Y) = \sum_{Y} P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$$

> **Interpretation:** To find the probability of $X$ alone, sum (or integrate) over all possible values of $Y$.

### 2.1.3 The Bayes Theorem (Direct Consequence)

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

> **Interpretation:** A generative model learns $P(X \mid Y)$ — i.e., how data $X$ is distributed **within each class** $Y$. Once this is known, Bayes' theorem can convert it to the discriminative quantity $P(Y \mid X)$ by simply multiplying by the class prior $P(Y)$ and normalizing.

## 2.2 The Core Generative Modeling Objective

Every generative model, no matter how complex, attempts to solve one underlying optimization problem:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log p_{\theta}(x) \right]$$

where:
- $\theta$ = parameters of the model (weights of the neural network)
- $p_{\text{data}}$ = true (unknown) data distribution
- $p_{\theta}(x)$ = model's approximation of the data distribution
- $\mathbb{E}$ = expectation operator

This is the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** principle. The model is rewarded (high likelihood) for assigning high probability to real data points and implicitly penalized for assigning high probability to unrealistic ones.

## 2.3 Two Key Density Estimation Paradigms

### 2.3.1 Explicit Density estimation
The model provides a fully computable, closed-form (or tractable) expression for $p_{\theta}(x)$. Example: **PixelRNN, PixelCNN** (autoregressive), **Normalizing Flows** (invertible transforms), **Energy-Based Models**.

### 2.3.2 Implicit density estimation
The model never computes $p_{\theta}(x)$ directly. Instead, it provides a mechanism to **sample** from $p_{\theta}(x)$. Example: **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**.

## 2.4 Taxonomy of Generative Models (KTU High-Yield)

| Family | Core Idea | Density? | Sampling? | KTU Relevance |
|--------|----------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Naive Bayes** | Conditional independence assumption | Explicit | Easy | Foundational baseline |
| **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** | Weighted sum of Gaussians | Explicit | Easy | Clustering + generation |
| **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** | Latent Markov chain | Explicit | Easy | Speech / sequences |
| **Variational Autoencoders (VAE)** | Latent variable + variational bound | Approximate | Easy | Module 2 syllabus |
| **Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)** | Two-network min-max game | Implicit | Easy | Module 2 syllabus |
| **Autoregressive Models** | Predict next token pixel-by-pixel | Explicit | Slow | Module 3 syllabus |
| **Normalizing Flows** | Invertible transforms of noise | Explicit | Exact | Module 3 syllabus |
| **Diffusion Models** | Iterative denoising | Approximate | Slow but high quality | Module 4 syllabus |
| **LLMs (GPT, etc.)** | Large-scale autoregressive transformers | Implicit | Easy | Module 5 syllabus |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | $P(X,Y) = P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$ | Product rule | Decompose joint |
| 2 | $P(X) = \sum_{Y} P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$ | Sum rule | Marginalization |
| 3 | $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Posterior inference |
| 4 | $p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p_{\theta}(z) \, dz$ | Latent variable model | VAE foundation |
| 5 | $\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$ | MLE objective | Training any generative model |
| 6 | $\text{KL}(p \parallel q) = \int p(x) \log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \, dx$ | KL Divergence | Measures distribution match |
| 7 | $\text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - \text{KL}(q(z) \parallel p(z))$ | Evidence Lower Bound | VAE loss function |
| 8 | $\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | GAN objective | GAN training (Module 2) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Generative modeling is the engine behind:

- **Drug Discovery (Biotech):** Generate novel molecular structures with desired binding properties.
- **Engineering Design (CAD):** Propose new aerodynamic shapes within structural constraints.
- **Cybersecurity:** Generate synthetic attack traffic to train robust intrusion-detection systems.
- **Image Restoration:** Inpaint missing regions of satellite imagery.
- **Conversational AI:** Power the next-token prediction in LLMs like ChatGPT.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate**
> All algebraic steps below are shown in full, with text-level explanations between mathematical transitions. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Discriminative–Generative Bridge

**Step 1 — State the Bayes Theorem identity.**
We begin with the most general form of Bayes' theorem for two random variables $X$ (data) and $Y$ (label):

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the denominator using the sum rule.**
The denominator $P(X)$ is a marginal probability. Using the sum rule of probability, we expand it as:

$$P(X) = \sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(X, Y = y)$$

**Step 3 — Further expand the joint in the sum.**
By the product rule, $P(X, Y = y) = P(X \mid Y = y) P(Y = y)$. Substituting:

$$P(X) = \sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(X \mid Y = y) P(Y = y)$$

**Step 4 — Write the final discriminative–generative bridge.**

$$\boxed{\,P(Y = y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y = y) P(Y = y)}{\sum_{y' \in \mathcal{Y}} P(X \mid Y = y') P(Y = y')}\,}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A generative model learns the *numerator* $P(X \mid Y = y) P(Y = y)$ for every class $y$. A discriminative model skips this and directly fits the *whole left-hand side*. This is why generative models are far more powerful — they can also generate new samples — but require much more data and compute to train.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for Generative Models

**Step 1 — Assume a dataset of $N$ i.i.d. samples.**
Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ be drawn from an unknown distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$.

**Step 2 — Write the likelihood of the entire dataset.**
Since the samples are independent, the joint likelihood factorizes:

$$L(\theta) = p_{\theta}(\mathcal{D}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{\theta}(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Convert products into sums using the logarithm.**
The log-likelihood is preferred because it is monotonically equivalent and numerically stable:

$$\log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this as an empirical expectation.**
The sum is the empirical (sample-based) expectation of $\log p_{\theta}(x)$ over the dataset:

$$\log L(\theta) = N \cdot \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \hat{p}_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

where $\hat{p}_{\text{data}}$ is the empirical distribution placing mass $1/N$ on each observed sample.

**Step 5 — Drop the constant $N$ to form the training objective.**
Maximizing $\log L(\theta)$ is equivalent to maximizing the expectation:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \hat{p}_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

**Step 6 — In the limit of infinite data, the empirical distribution converges to the true distribution:**

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

This is the **canonical MLE objective** that drives training of every generative model.

## 3.3 Derivation: KL Divergence as MLE's Loss Function

**Step 1 — Start with the KL divergence between data and model distributions:**

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta}) = \int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)} \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Split the logarithm using $\log(a/b) = \log a - \log b$:**

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta}) = \int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log p_{\text{data}}(x) \, dx - \int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log p_{\theta}(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Identify the two integrals.**
The first integral does **not depend** on $\theta$. It is a constant of the true data distribution. The second integral is exactly the negative expected log-likelihood:

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta}) = \underbrace{\int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log p_{\text{data}}(x) \, dx}_{\text{Constant w.r.t. } \theta} - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to expose the MLE connection:**

$$\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)] = \text{Constant} - \text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta})$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
Minimizing KL divergence $\Leftrightarrow$ Maximizing expected log-likelihood. This is why MLE training is sometimes called *minimum KL divergence estimation*.

> **Conclusion:** Generative modeling under MLE is mathematically equivalent to making the model's distribution $p_{\theta}$ as close as possible to the true data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$ in the KL sense.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generative vs Discriminative Modeling Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete functional pipeline of both modeling paradigms, from training data to final output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Dataset X and labels Y] --> B{Modeling Paradigm}
    B -- Discriminative --> C[Learn P of Y given X]
    B -- Generative --> D[Learn P of X given Y and P of Y]
    C --> E[Decision Boundary]
    E --> F[Output: Class Label y]
    D --> G[Full Joint Distribution P of X and Y]
    G --> H1[Output: New Sample x synthesized]
    G --> H2[Output: Class via Bayes Rule]
    H1 --> I[Synthetic Data]
    H2 --> J[Prediction]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Latent Variable Generative Model Architecture

This diagram maps the *abstract* generative process used by VAEs, GANs, and Diffusion Models.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Latent Noise z ~ p z] --> G[Generator Network G theta]
    D[Real Data x ~ p data] --> S{Sampler Selector}
    G --> S
    S -- During Training --> Loss[Loss Computation: MLE / KL / Adversarial]
    Loss --> Opt[Optimizer: update theta]
    Opt --> G
    S -- After Training --> Out[Generated Sample x tilde]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Taxonomy of Generative Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Generative Models] --> Explicit[Explicit Density]
    Root --> Implicit[Implicit Density]
    Explicit --> Tract[Tractable Likelihood]
    Explicit --> Approx[Approximate Likelihood]
    Tract --> AR[Autoregressive: PixelRNN, PixelCNN]
    Tract --> NF[Normalizing Flows]
    Approx --> VAE[Variational Autoencoders]
    Approx --> EBM[Energy Based Models]
    Approx --> DM[Diffusion Models]
    Implicit --> GAN[Generative Adversarial Networks]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — The Generative Modeling Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Sample minibatch from real data] --> S2[Step 2: Forward pass through model]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Loss = minus log likelihood or surrogate]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Backpropagate gradients]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Update parameters theta]
    S5 --> S1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a generative model. How is it fundamentally different from a discriminative model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A generative model is one that learns the underlying data distribution $p(x)$ — or the joint distribution $P(x, y)$ — and can therefore produce *new* samples drawn from that distribution.
- (1 mark) A discriminative model, in contrast, learns the conditional probability $P(y \mid x)$ and only maps inputs to class labels.
- (1 mark) The key difference is that generative models capture *how data is generated* and can synthesize new data, while discriminative models only learn the *decision boundary* between classes.

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024]**
**State the Maximum Likelihood Estimation objective for a generative model and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) $\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$
- (1 mark) Explanation: $\theta$ = model parameters to be learned; $p_{\theta}(x)$ = the probability the model assigns to data point $x$.
- (1 mark) The objective is to find parameters that maximize the probability assigned to the observed data, effectively making $p_{\theta}$ as close to $p_{\text{data}}$ as possible.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Most students lose marks by (1) forgetting the empirical-vs-true distinction in MLE, (2) writing Bayes' theorem without normalizing by the sum rule, and (3) confusing joint and conditional probabilities. Always write the *full expanded* form. Marks are deducted for incomplete or rushed derivations.

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the two fundamental rules of probability on which generative models are built. Derive Bayes' theorem from these rules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the difference between generative and discriminative modeling approaches. Give two real-world examples of each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution A(a):**

The two fundamental rules are:

**Rule 1 — The Sum Rule (Marginalization):**
For discrete random variables, the marginal probability of $X$ is obtained by summing the joint probability over all values of $Y$:

$$P(X) = \sum_{Y} P(X, Y)$$

For continuous variables, the summation is replaced by an integral:

$$p(x) = \int p(x, y) \, dy$$

**Rule 2 — The Product Rule (Chain Rule):**
The joint probability factorizes as a product of a conditional and a marginal:

$$P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) P(Y) = P(Y \mid X) P(X)$$

**Derivation of Bayes' Theorem:**

Starting from the product rule, we have two equivalent expressions for the joint:

$$P(X, Y) = P(X \mid Y) P(Y) \quad \text{and} \quad P(X, Y) = P(Y \mid X) P(X)$$

Equating the right-hand sides:

$$P(X \mid Y) P(Y) = P(Y \mid X) P(X)$$

Dividing both sides by $P(X)$:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

This is the **Bayes' theorem**. The denominator $P(X)$ can be expanded using the sum rule:

$$P(X) = \sum_{Y} P(X \mid Y) P(Y)$$

Substituting yields the fully expanded form:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{\sum_{Y'} P(X \mid Y') P(Y')}$$

#### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).**

- **Discriminative Models:** Learn $P(Y \mid X)$ directly. They are *classifiers*. Examples: (i) Logistic Regression used for spam detection, (ii) CNN used for medical image diagnosis.
- **Generative Models:** Learn $P(X \mid Y)$ and $P(Y)$. They are *data synthesizers*. Examples: (i) GAN used to generate realistic human faces, (ii) VAE used to generate new drug-like molecules.

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) objective for a generative model. Show that minimizing KL divergence is equivalent to maximizing the log-likelihood. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a clear block diagram, classify and briefly describe any three major families of generative models used in modern AI. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution B(a):**

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ assumed to be i.i.d., the likelihood of the parameters is:

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{\theta}(x_i)$$

Taking the log:

$$\log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i)$$

This is $N$ times the empirical expectation:

$$\log L(\theta) = N \cdot \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \hat{p}_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

So MLE selects $\theta$ to maximize this expectation:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \hat{p}_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

**Equivalence to KL minimization:**
The KL divergence is:

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta}) = \int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)} \, dx$$

Splitting the log:

$$= \underbrace{\int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log p_{\text{data}}(x) \, dx}_{\text{constant in } \theta} - \int p_{\text{data}}(x) \log p_{\theta}(x) \, dx$$

So:

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta}) = \text{const} - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$$

Minimizing the left side $\Leftrightarrow$ Maximizing the right side.

#### **Model Solution B(b):**

(Refer to the Mermaid taxonomy in Section 4.3 for the diagram.)

**Family 1 — Variational Autoencoders (VAE):** Introduces a latent variable $z$ with prior $p(z)$. Uses an encoder $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ and a decoder $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$. Training maximizes the ELBO.

**Family 2 — Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN):** A two-player game between a generator $G$ and a discriminator $D$. $G$ tries to fool $D$; $D$ tries to detect fakes. Solves a min-max optimization.

**Family 3 — Diffusion Models:** Gradually add noise to data in a *forward process*, then learn to reverse it in a *backward process*. State-of-the-art for image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Generative Model Definition:** Learns $p(x)$ or $p(x, y)$ to *create* new data.
- **Discriminative Model Definition:** Learns $p(y \mid x)$ to *classify* data.
- **Two Fundamental Probability Rules:** Sum rule and Product rule. From these, derive Bayes' theorem.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ — the bridge between generative and discriminative.
- **MLE Objective:** $\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$
- **MLE ≡ KL Minimization:** Maximizing log-likelihood is mathematically identical to minimizing $\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_{\theta})$.
- **Latent Variable Form:** $p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z) \, dz$ — foundation of VAEs.
- **Two Paradigms:** Explicit density (compute $p_{\theta}(x)$) vs Implicit density (only sample).
- **Five Major Families:** VAE, GAN, Autoregressive, Normalizing Flow, Diffusion.
- **Key Engineering Insight:** Generative models answer "how is this $x$ generated?" while discriminative models answer "which class does this $x$ belong to?"
- **Exam Tip:** Always show the *empirical* expectation form of MLE, not just the abstract notation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generative vs. Discriminative Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generative vs. Discriminative Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the landscape of supervised and unsupervised machine learning, every probabilistic classifier fundamentally relies on estimating probability distributions from data. The two dominant paradigms that govern this estimation are **Generative Modeling** and **Discriminative Modeling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative Models** learn the **joint probability distribution** $P(X, Y)$, or equivalently $P(X \vert Y)$ and $P(X)$, by modeling how the data is generated. They attempt to understand *how* the data came into existence.
> 
> **Discriminative Models** learn the **conditional probability distribution** $P(Y \vert X)$, or directly learn a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ that produces a decision boundary. They focus purely on *separating* the classes.

Where:
- $X$ = the input features (data points)
- $Y$ = the target label or class

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Detective vs. The Artist Analogy:**
> 
> **Discriminative Model = The Detective** 🕵️: A detective examining a crime scene only cares about answering: *"Given this evidence (X), did the suspect (Y) do it?"* The detective draws a line between "guilty" and "innocent" without needing to know exactly what a "typical innocent person" looks like.
> 
> **Generative Model = The Artist** 🎨: An artist trying to forge a painting studies hundreds of Van Gogh works. By understanding the brush strokes, color palette, and subject matter, the artist can *generate* a new Van Gogh-like painting. The artist models how Van Gogh paintings are *created*.

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core Question** | Which class does this belong to? | How is this data generated? |
| **Learns** | Decision boundary $P(Y \vert X)$ | Data distribution $P(X, Y)$ |
| **Analogy** | Detective | Artist / Forger |
| **Goal** | Classification / Regression | Sampling / Generation |
| **Standard Metrics** | Accuracy, Precision, F1-Score, **Log-Likelihood** | **Log-Likelihood**, FID, Inception Score |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Two Paradigms

#### Discriminative Modeling — The "Boundary Drawer"
- **Step 1:** Take input features $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$.
- **Step 2:** Model the conditional probability $P(Y \vert X)$ directly.
- **Step 3:** Find a decision boundary that separates classes.
- **Why?** Focuses computational power on the *only thing needed* for classification — the boundary.
- **How?** Through methods like Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVMs), and Neural Networks used as classifiers.

#### Generative Modeling — The "Data Dreamer"
- **Step 1:** Learn the full data-generating process, i.e., $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X)$.
- **Step 2:** Use Bayes' theorem to derive $P(Y \vert X)$ when classification is needed.
- **Step 3:** Can synthesize new samples that resemble the training data.
- **Why?** Provides a holistic understanding of the data, enabling both classification AND generation.
- **How?** Through methods like Naive Bayes, Gaussian Mixture Models, Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Joint Probability** | $P(X, Y)$ | Foundation for generative modeling |
| **Conditional Probability** | $P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$ | Foundation for discriminative modeling |
| **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ | Links generative $P(X \vert Y)$ to discriminative $P(Y \vert X)$ |
| **Law of Total Probability** | $P(X) = \sum_{y} P(X \vert Y=y) P(Y=y)$ | Marginalization used in generative models |
| **Generative Objective** | $\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{\theta}(x_i, y_i)$ | Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) |
| **Discriminative Objective** | $\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{\theta}(y_i \vert x_i)$ | Conditional MLE |
| **Prior Probability** | $P(Y)$ | Class frequencies; assumption in generative models |
| **Likelihood** | $P(X \vert Y)$ | Class-conditional density in generative models |
| **Evidence** | $P(X)$ | Normalizing constant in Bayes' theorem |
| **Posterior** | $P(Y \vert X)$ | Decision rule for classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Any generative model *implicitly* contains a discriminative model via Bayes' rule. However, the reverse is not true. A discriminative model cannot generate new samples because it has never learned $P(X)$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis**: Discriminative models predict disease from symptoms; Generative models can simulate synthetic patient data for research.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Discriminative classifiers detect pedestrians; Generative models simulate rare edge-case scenarios for training.
- **Natural Language Processing**: Discriminative BERT classifies sentiment; Generative GPT creates human-like text.
- **Cybersecurity**: Discriminative models detect spam; Generative models can craft adversarial examples to test robustness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Joint Probability to Bayes' Decision Rule

**Objective:** Show mathematically how a generative model can be used to perform discriminative classification.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint distribution.**
A generative model learns the joint probability:
$$P(X, Y) = P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)$$

**Step 2 — Express the joint distribution symmetrically.**
By the chain rule of probability, we can also write:
$$P(X, Y) = P(Y \vert X) \cdot P(X)$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**
$$P(Y \vert X) \cdot P(X) = P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the posterior $P(Y \vert X)$.**
$$P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

This is the famous **Bayes' Theorem**, and it is the mathematical bridge that allows generative models to perform classification.

**Step 5 — Apply the Law of Total Probability to expand the denominator.**
$$P(X) = \sum_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(X \vert Y=y) \cdot P(Y=y)$$

**Step 6 — Define the Bayes' Optimal Classifier.**
The optimal decision rule is to pick the class $\hat{y}$ that maximizes the posterior:
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(Y=y \vert X=x)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute Bayes' theorem into the decision rule.**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \frac{P(X=x \vert Y=y) \cdot P(Y=y)}{P(X=x)}$$

**Step 8 — Drop the evidence term $P(X=x)$ since it is constant across all classes.**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(X=x \vert Y=y) \cdot P(Y=y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In a binary classification problem, you only need to compare $P(Y=1 \vert X) \cdot P(X \vert Y=1)$ versus $P(Y=0 \vert X) \cdot P(X \vert Y=0)$. KTU examiners often give a 7-mark question exactly on this derivation.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Generative Model | Discriminative Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **What it models** | $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X \vert Y) P(Y)$ | $P(Y \vert X)$ or $f(X)$ |
| **Can generate new data?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Handles missing inputs?** | ✅ Yes (natively) | ❌ Difficult |
| **Performance on small data** | Better (uses prior knowledge) | Often better with abundant data |
| **Asymptotic accuracy** | Approaches its ceiling earlier | Often achieves higher accuracy ceiling |
| **Computational cost** | Higher (model full distribution) | Lower (focused on boundary) |
| **Examples** | Naive Bayes, HMM, VAE, GAN | Logistic Regression, SVM, CRF, CNN |
| **Requires assumptions?** | Yes (distribution assumptions) | No (fewer assumptions) |
| **Out-of-distribution detection** | Strong | Weak |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Given a dataset where $P(Y=1) = 0.3$, $P(X \vert Y=1) = 0.8$, and $P(X \vert Y=0) = 0.2$, classify a new observation $X$.

**Step 1 — Compute the prior-weighted likelihoods.**

For class $Y=1$:
$$P(X \vert Y=1) \cdot P(Y=1) = 0.8 \times 0.3 = 0.24$$

For class $Y=0$:
$$P(X \vert Y=0) \cdot P(Y=0) = 0.2 \times 0.7 = 0.14$$

**Step 2 — Compare and decide.**

Since $0.24 > 0.14$, the observation is classified as $\hat{y} = 1$.

**Step 3 — (Optional) Compute the actual posterior using Bayes' theorem.**

First, the evidence $P(X)$:
$$P(X) = 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38$$

Then the posterior:
$$P(Y=1 \vert X) = \frac{0.24}{0.38} \approx 0.632$$

$$P(Y=0 \vert X) = \frac{0.14}{0.38} \approx 0.368$$

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example illustrates how generative models compute classification by first estimating the data distribution and then applying Bayes' rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generative vs. Discriminative Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput["Input Features X"] --> nodeChoice{"What does the model learn?"}
    
    nodeChoice -->|Learn Decision Boundary| nodeDisc["Discriminative Model P of Y given X"]
    nodeChoice -->|Learn Full Data Distribution| nodeGen["Generative Model P of X Y or P of X given Y"]
    
    nodeDisc --> nodeDiscSteps["1. Directly model posterior"]
    nodeDisc --> nodeDiscSteps2["2. Optimize boundary"]
    nodeDisc --> nodeDiscSteps3["3. Output class label"]
    nodeDiscSteps3 --> nodeDiscOut["Prediction: Class Label Y"]
    
    nodeDiscExamples["Examples: Logistic Regression, SVM, CNN Classifier"] --> nodeDisc
    
    nodeGen --> nodeGenSteps1["1. Learn class conditional P of X given Y"]
    nodeGen --> nodeGenSteps2["2. Learn prior P of Y"]
    nodeGen --> nodeGenSteps3["3. Apply Bayes Theorem"]
    nodeGenSteps3 --> nodeGenOut["Prediction: Class Label Y"]
    
    nodeGen --> nodeGenSampling["Additional Capability: Sample New X from P of X Y"]
    nodeGenSampling --> nodeGenSynth["Synthetic Data Generation"]
    
    nodeGenExamples["Examples: Naive Bayes, GMM, VAE, GAN"] --> nodeGen
    
    nodeDiscOut --> nodeCompare["Comparison"]
    nodeGenSynth --> nodeCompare
    nodeGenOut --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare --> nodeFinal["Choose based on task: Classification = Both, Generation = Generative only"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DISC["DISCRIMINATIVE PIPELINE"]
        discA["Raw Data X"] --> discB["Feature Extraction"]
        discB --> discC["Model P of Y given X directly"]
        discC --> discD["Decision Boundary"]
        discD --> discE["Output Y-hat"]
    end
    
    subgraph GEN["GENERATIVE PIPELINE"]
        genA["Raw Data X Y"] --> genB["Estimate P of X given Y"]
        genA --> genC["Estimate P of Y"]
        genB --> genD["Bayes Theorem Combination"]
        genC --> genD
        genD --> genE["Posterior P of Y given X"]
        genE --> genF["Output Y-hat"]
        genB --> genG["Optional: Sample Synthetic X"]
        genC --> genG
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:** The discriminative pipeline is *shorter* and more direct, going straight from features to boundary. The generative pipeline is *richer* — it learns the data-generating process, which can then either be used for classification (via Bayes' rule) OR for synthesizing new samples.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define generative and discriminative models. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Discriminative Models** learn the conditional probability $P(Y \vert X)$ or directly learn a decision boundary that maps inputs to outputs. They focus on distinguishing between classes without modeling how the data was generated. **Example:** Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine.
>
> **Generative Models** learn the joint probability distribution $P(X, Y)$ by modeling the data-generating process. They can generate new samples that resemble the training data and can also perform classification using Bayes' theorem. **Example:** Naive Bayes Classifier, Gaussian Mixture Model.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Bayes' theorem and explain how it connects generative and discriminative modeling.*

**Model Answer:**

> Bayes' theorem is stated as:
> $$P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$
>
> A **generative model** estimates $P(X \vert Y)$ and $P(Y)$ (the likelihood and prior), while a **discriminative model** directly estimates $P(Y \vert X)$ (the posterior). Bayes' theorem serves as the mathematical bridge — if you know the generative components, you can compute the discriminative posterior. Conversely, knowing the posterior alone does not let you recover the data distribution, which is why discriminative models cannot generate new data.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the difference between generative and discriminative models with a suitable example. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each approach. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Generative and discriminative models represent two fundamentally different approaches to probabilistic learning.

**Generative Models:**
A generative model learns the joint probability distribution $P(X, Y)$ by modeling how the data is generated. It typically estimates the class-conditional density $P(X \vert Y)$ and the prior $P(Y)$ separately. Given these, classification is performed using Bayes' theorem:
$$P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

*Example:* Naive Bayes classifier assumes that features are conditionally independent given the class label, allowing it to estimate $P(X \vert Y)$ as a product of individual feature probabilities.

**Discriminative Models:**
A discriminative model directly models the posterior $P(Y \vert X)$ or learns a decision boundary without modeling the input distribution. It focuses only on the boundary that separates classes.

*Example:* Logistic Regression models $P(Y=1 \vert X)$ using the sigmoid function:
$$P(Y=1 \vert X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$

**Advantages and Limitations:**

| Aspect | Generative | Discriminative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Advantage** | Can generate new data; handles missing inputs naturally; works with smaller datasets | Often higher classification accuracy; no distributional assumptions; computationally efficient |
| **Limitation** | Strong distributional assumptions; can be computationally expensive | Cannot generate new data; poor at handling missing inputs; requires more data |

---

**(b)** Derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Using Bayes' theorem, explain the Naive Bayes classification algorithm with a suitable numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Bayes' Theorem:**

Conditional probability is defined as:
$$P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Step 1 — Express $P(A \cap B)$ in two ways:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \vert B) \cdot P(B)$$
$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)$$

Step 2 — Equate the two expressions:
$$P(A \vert B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)$$

Step 3 — Solve for $P(A \vert B)$:
$$P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

In our ML context, $A = Y$ and $B = X$:
$$P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

**Naive Bayes Classification Algorithm:**

Naive Bayes applies Bayes' theorem with the "naive" assumption of conditional independence among features:
$$P(X \vert Y) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert Y)$$

The decision rule becomes:
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(Y=y) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert Y=y)$$

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose we want to classify an email as Spam ($Y=1$) or Not Spam ($Y=0$) based on two words: "offer" ($x_1$) and "free" ($x_2$).

Given:
- $P(Y=1) = 0.4$, $P(Y=0) = 0.6$
- $P(x_1=\text{offer} \vert Y=1) = 0.7$, $P(x_1=\text{offer} \vert Y=0) = 0.1$
- $P(x_2=\text{free} \vert Y=1) = 0.8$, $P(x_2=\text{free} \vert Y=0) = 0.05$

For $Y=1$:
$$P(Y=1) \cdot P(x_1 \vert Y=1) \cdot P(x_2 \vert Y=1) = 0.4 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.224$$

For $Y=0$:
$$P(Y=0) \cdot P(x_1 \vert Y=0) \cdot P(x_2 \vert Y=0) = 0.6 \times 0.1 \times 0.05 = 0.003$$

Since $0.224 > 0.003$, the email is classified as **Spam**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare and contrast generative and discriminative models in terms of the probability distributions they learn, their computational requirements, and their real-world applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Probability Distributions:**

- **Generative Models** learn the joint distribution $P(X, Y)$ or equivalently the class-conditional $P(X \vert Y)$ and the prior $P(Y)$. This is a "full probabilistic model" of the data.
- **Discriminative Models** learn only the conditional distribution $P(Y \vert X)$ or a direct discriminant function $f(X)$. They are "boundary-focused."

**Computational Requirements:**

- Generative models are typically more expensive because they must estimate the entire data distribution. For high-dimensional data, this becomes intractable without strong assumptions (e.g., Naive Bayes' independence assumption).
- Discriminative models are more efficient as they only model the boundary. Modern deep learning discriminative models (CNNs, Transformers) scale well to large datasets.

**Real-World Applications:**

| Domain | Generative Use Case | Discriminative Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Healthcare | Synthetic patient record generation | Disease diagnosis from X-rays |
| Finance | Fraud scenario simulation | Real-time fraud detection |
| NLP | Text generation (GPT, LLaMA) | Sentiment classification (BERT) |
| Computer Vision | Image synthesis (StyleGAN) | Object detection (YOLO) |
| Cybersecurity | Adversarial sample crafting | Malware classification |

**When to Use Each:**

- Use **generative** when you need to synthesize data, handle missing values, or work with limited labeled data.
- Use **discriminative** when classification accuracy is the primary goal and you have abundant labeled data.

---

**(b)** With the help of a labeled dataset containing two classes, demonstrate how a discriminative model (logistic regression) and a generative model (Gaussian discriminant analysis) would each approach the classification problem. Highlight the key mathematical difference between them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a 2D dataset with two classes: Class 0 and Class 1.

**Logistic Regression (Discriminative Approach):**

Logistic regression directly models:
$$P(Y=1 \vert X; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$$

It estimates the parameters $\theta = (\theta_0, \theta_1, \theta_2)$ by minimizing the cross-entropy loss:
$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Key Point:** Logistic regression does NOT model $P(X \vert Y)$ or $P(X)$. It only models the decision boundary.

**Gaussian Discriminant Analysis (Generative Approach):**

GDA assumes that $P(X \vert Y=y)$ follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution:
$$P(X \vert Y=y) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_y)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_y)\right)$$

It also models the prior $P(Y)$ as a Bernoulli distribution:
$$P(Y=1) = \phi, \quad P(Y=0) = 1 - \phi$$

The parameters $\phi, \mu_0, \mu_1, \Sigma$ are estimated using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).

**Classification** is then performed using Bayes' rule:
$$P(Y=1 \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y=1) P(Y=1)}{P(X \vert Y=1) P(Y=1) + P(X \vert Y=0) P(Y=0)}$$

**Key Mathematical Difference:**

| Aspect | Logistic Regression | GDA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Models $P(X \vert Y)$? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Gaussian assumption) |
| Models $P(Y)$? | Implicitly | ✅ Yes (Bernoulli prior) |
| Models $P(Y \vert X)$? | ✅ Yes (directly) | ✅ Yes (via Bayes' theorem) |
| Distribution assumption | None | Multivariate Gaussian |

**Conclusion:** Both models can produce a decision boundary, but GDA is *generative* because it learns the full data distribution, while logistic regression is *discriminative* because it skips the data modeling step entirely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "generative AI" with "generative models":** Students often assume that "generative models" in the ML context only refer to modern deep generative systems (GANs, VAEs, LLMs). In KTU Module 1, the term is **classical** and includes Naive Bayes, GMM, and HMM.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizing constant $P(X)$:** In Bayes' theorem derivations, students often drop $P(X)$ without justification. Always state that $P(X)$ is constant across classes and thus cancels out in $\arg\max$.
> 
> 3. **Claiming discriminative models are "always better":** This is false. Generative models excel at handling missing data, few-shot learning, and generation tasks. Examiners specifically look for nuanced answers.
> 
> 4. **Miscounting marks in Part B:** A 7-mark sub-question expects a *complete* solution — derivation + numerical example + conclusion. Skipping any one of these loses at least 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Confusing $P(Y \vert X)$ with $P(X \vert Y)$:** These are NOT the same. $P(Y \vert X)$ is the posterior (discriminative focus), while $P(X \vert Y)$ is the likelihood (generative focus). This is a favorite trick question by KTU examiners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Generative models** learn $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X \vert Y) P(Y)$; **Discriminative models** learn $P(Y \vert X)$ or a direct decision function.
- **Bayes' theorem** is the mathematical bridge: $P(Y \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$.
- **Discriminative** = Detective (draws boundary); **Generative** = Artist (models how data is created).
- The **evidence term $P(X)$** is constant across classes and can be dropped during $\arg\max$ classification.
- **Generative models can do everything discriminative models can** (via Bayes' rule), PLUS they can generate new samples.
- **Discriminative models cannot generate samples** because they never learned $P(X)$.
- **Generative examples:** Naive Bayes, GMM, HMM, VAE, GAN, Diffusion Models.
- **Discriminative examples:** Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, CNN, BERT (for classification).
- **Asymptotic behavior:** Generative models reach their accuracy ceiling faster (with less data), but discriminative models often achieve a *higher* ceiling asymptotically.
- **Naive Bayes assumption:** Conditional independence of features given the class, i.e., $P(X \vert Y) = \prod_i P(x_i \vert Y)$.
- **Logistic Regression** is a pure discriminative model that uses the sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- **GDA** is a generative model that assumes Gaussian class-conditionals.
- For KTU numericals, always show: (1) Bayes' formula, (2) Substituted values, (3) Comparison, (4) Final class decision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advances in Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advances in Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advances in Machine Learning (ML)** refer to the progressive evolution of computational algorithms, statistical models, and neural architectures that enable systems to automatically learn hierarchical representations from data, generalize across unseen distributions, and perform complex cognitive tasks without being explicitly programmed for every scenario. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this encompasses the transition from classical statistical learning (linear/logistic regression, SVMs, decision trees) to **Deep Learning (DL)**, **Representation Learning**, **Transfer Learning**, and **Foundation Models** that underpin modern Generative AI systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The course emphasizes the paradigm shift from *hand-crafted feature engineering* to *automatic feature learning* through deep neural networks, the rise of transformer-based architectures, self-supervised pretraining, and large-scale generative models (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models, LLMs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Evolution of a Cook":**
> 
> - **Classical ML (1990s–2000s):** Imagine a cook who can ONLY cook if you hand him an exact recipe and pre-measured ingredients. The "features" (ingredients) are *manually chosen by a human expert*. This is **feature engineering** — the model is shallow and rigid.
> 
> - **Deep Learning (2012–2017):** The cook now has a *blender and a stove that figure out the recipe themselves* by tasting thousands of dishes. He uses many layers (cutting → mixing → cooking → plating) to discover flavors automatically. This is **representation learning**.
> 
> - **Transformers & Foundation Models (2017–Present):** The cook has now read *every cookbook in the world*, understands context, can invent entirely new recipes, and even explain *why* a dish works. He shares this knowledge with junior cooks (transfer learning).
> 
> This is the journey of "Advances in Machine Learning."

### 1.3 Key Milestones in Advances of ML

| Era | Breakthrough | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **1958** | Perceptron (Rosenblatt) | First learning algorithm |
| **1986** | Backpropagation (Rumelhart) | Training multi-layer networks became feasible |
| **1990s** | SVMs, Random Forests | Dominant classical ML methods |
| **2012** | **AlexNet (Krizhevsky)** on ImageNet | Deep CNNs beat classical ML by 10.8% top-5 error |
| **2014** | **GANs (Goodfellow)**, **VAEs (Kingma)** | Generative modeling renaissance |
| **2015** | **ResNet (He et al.)** | Enabled 100+ layer networks via skip connections |
| **2017** | **Transformer ("Attention Is All You Need")** | Killed RNN dominance; birth of modern NLP |
| **2018–2020** | **BERT, GPT-2/3, T5** | Foundation Models, in-context learning |
| **2020–2022** | **Diffusion Models (DDPM, Stable Diffusion)** | State-of-the-art image generation |
| **2023+** | **LLMs, RLHF, Multimodal Models (GPT-4, Gemini)** | General-purpose generative AI |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Modern ML

- **Top-1 / Top-5 Accuracy** — classification correctness
- **BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, CIDEr** — text generation quality
- **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** — **≈ 0** means perfect image generation
- **IS (Inception Score)** — higher = better quality & diversity
- **Perplexity (PPL)** — language model fluency
- **Parameter Count** — model capacity (GPT-3 = **175B parameters**)
- **FLOPs** — computational cost

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Modern ML Advances

#### Pillar 1: Representation Learning (Deep Neural Networks)
- Eliminates manual feature engineering by learning **hierarchical features** automatically.
- Lower layers learn *low-level features* (edges, phonemes).
- Higher layers learn *high-level abstractions* (objects, semantics).

#### Pillar 2: Backpropagation + Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
- The *engine* that allows deep networks to train.
- Computes gradients of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. parameters $\theta$ using the chain rule, then updates via:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typical range: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$).

#### Pillar 3: Attention Mechanism (Transformer Core)
- Solves the *long-range dependency* problem of RNNs.
- Computes a weighted sum of values $\mathbf{V}$ using similarity between queries $\mathbf{Q}$ and keys $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$

- The $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor prevents softmax saturation in high dimensions.

#### Pillar 4: Transfer Learning & Pre-training
- Pre-train on a **large corpus** → fine-tune on a **small task-specific dataset**.
- Drastically reduces labeled data requirements and training cost.

#### Pillar 5: Generative Modeling
- **GANs** — adversarial training between Generator $G$ and Discriminator $D$.
- **VAEs** — probabilistic encoder-decoder maximizing ELBO.
- **Diffusion Models** — iterative denoising via reverse Markov chain.
- **Autoregressive LLMs** — predicting next token in a sequence.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Parameter optimization |
| 2 | **MSE Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression |
| 3 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification |
| 4 | **Sigmoid Activation** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Binary output |
| 5 | **Softmax Activation** | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability |
| 6 | **ReLU Activation** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Non-linearity in hidden layers |
| 7 | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $\text{Attn} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$ | Transformer core |
| 8 | **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MHA} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$ | Parallel attention subspaces |
| 9 | **Layer Norm** | $\hat{x} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}}$, $y = \gamma \hat{x} + \beta$ | Stabilizes training |
| 10 | **GAN Min-Max Objective** | $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Adversarial training |
| 11 | **VAE ELBO** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$ | Variational inference |
| 12 | **Diffusion Forward Step** | $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$ | Add noise gradually |
| 13 | **FID Score** | $\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | Image generation metric |
| 14 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\Vert \mathbf{a}\Vert \,\Vert \mathbf{b}\Vert}$ | Used in attention scoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *backbone of every modern AI product* — recommendation systems (Netflix, YouTube), language translation (Google Translate), medical imaging (tumor detection), autonomous driving (Tesla FSD), and content generation (ChatGPT, Midjourney).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation via Chain Rule

Consider a 2-layer neural network with input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, hidden layer $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$, output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{c}$, parameters $\{\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{W}_2, \mathbf{b}_2\}$, and sigmoid activation $\sigma$.

**Forward Pass:**

$$\mathbf{z}_1 = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1$$

$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}_1)$$

$$\mathbf{z}_2 = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z}_2)$$

**Loss (Cross-Entropy for true label $y$):**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$$

**Backward Pass — Derivation of Gradients:**

**Step 1:** Gradient of loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{z}_2$ (logistic derivative of softmax + cross-entropy is famously simple):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}_2} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y} \equiv \boldsymbol{\delta}_2$$

This is a *textbook result* — softmax + cross-entropy yields a clean difference.

**Step 2:** Gradients w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_2, \mathbf{b}_2$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_2} = \boldsymbol{\delta}_2 \, \mathbf{h}^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}_2} = \boldsymbol{\delta}_2$$

**Step 3:** Propagate error to hidden layer (chain rule):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}} = \mathbf{W}_2^\top \, \boldsymbol{\delta}_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \mathbf{h}(1 - \mathbf{h})$ element-wise:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}_1 = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}_1} = \left(\mathbf{W}_2^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}_2\right) \odot \mathbf{h} \odot (1 - \mathbf{h})$$

**Step 5:** Gradients w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_1, \mathbf{b}_1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_1} = \boldsymbol{\delta}_1 \, \mathbf{x}^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}_1} = \boldsymbol{\delta}_1$$

**Step 6:** Update via SGD with learning rate $\eta$:

$$\mathbf{W}_1 \leftarrow \mathbf{W}_1 - \eta \, \boldsymbol{\delta}_1 \mathbf{x}^\top$$

$$\mathbf{W}_2 \leftarrow \mathbf{W}_2 - \eta \, \boldsymbol{\delta}_2 \mathbf{h}^\top$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

Given input sequence representations $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, learn three projection matrices $\mathbf{W}^Q, \mathbf{W}^K, \mathbf{W}^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$.

**Step 1 — Project to queries, keys, values:**

$$\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}^V$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw similarity scores (dot product):**

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{K}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3 — Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?**

Assume $q_i, k_i$ are zero-mean, unit-variance. Then $s_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{d_k} q_{ik} k_{jk}$ has variance $d_k$. Large variance $\Rightarrow$ softmax gradients vanish.

Hence divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to restore unit variance:

$$\mathbf{S}' = \frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 4 — Softmax along rows (convert to attention weights):**

$$\mathbf{A} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{S}') \quad \text{where} \quad A_{ij} = \frac{e^{S'_{ij}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} e^{S'_{ik}}}$$

**Step 5 — Weighted sum of values:**

$$\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{V}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$

**Multi-Head Extension (used in all transformers):**

$$\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\mathbf{W}^O$$

where $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}_i^Q, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}_i^K, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}_i^V)$.

This allows the model to attend to information from **$h$ different representation subspaces** in parallel.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classical ML vs. Deep Learning vs. Generative AI

| Dimension | Classical ML | Deep Learning | Generative AI (LLMs / Diffusion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual (expert-driven) | Automatic (learned) | Automatic + emergent |
| **Data Requirement** | $10^2$ – $10^4$ samples | $10^4$ – $10^6$ samples | $10^9$ – $10^{12}$ tokens |
| **Model Capacity** | $10^2$ – $10^5$ params | $10^6$ – $10^8$ params | $10^9$ – $10^{12}$ params |
| **Hardware** | CPU | GPU | TPU / multi-GPU clusters |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low (black-box) |
| **Key Algorithms** | SVM, RF, XGBoost | CNN, RNN, LSTM | Transformer, Diffusion, GAN |
| **Training Objective** | Minimize loss on labels | Minimize loss on labels | Self-supervised + RLHF |
| **Output Type** | Prediction | Prediction | New data (text, image, audio) |
| **Inference Style** | Single forward pass | Single forward pass | Sequential (autoregressive) / Iterative (denoising) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Spam detection | Image classification | ChatGPT, DALL·E, Midjourney |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**  
*List and briefly explain any three major advances in Machine Learning that paved the way for modern Generative AI.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Deep Learning with CNNs & Backpropagation (2012):** The introduction of GPU-trained deep convolutional networks (e.g., **AlexNet**) demonstrated that hierarchical feature learning outperforms hand-crafted features, achieving a 10.8% reduction in ImageNet top-5 error.

2. **Transformer Architecture with Self-Attention (2017):** Vaswani et al.'s *"Attention Is All You Need"* replaced recurrence with parallel self-attention, enabling efficient training of very large models and forming the foundation of all modern LLMs (GPT, BERT, T5).

3. **Self-Supervised Pre-training & Foundation Models (2018+):** Models like **BERT** (Masked LM) and **GPT-2/3** (Causal LM) showed that pre-training on massive unlabeled corpora and fine-tuning on small labeled sets yields unprecedented generalization — the basis of today's Generative AI.

*(Award 1 mark per point with clear explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**  
*Compare Classical Machine Learning and Deep Learning with respect to (a) feature engineering, (b) data requirement, and (c) hardware.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical ML | Deep Learning |
|---|---|---|
| (a) **Feature Engineering** | Manual / domain-expert driven | Automatic representation learning |
| (b) **Data Requirement** | $10^2$ – $10^4$ samples | $10^4$ – $10^6$+ samples |
| (c) **Hardware** | CPU sufficient | GPU / TPU essential for parallelism |

*(Award 1 mark per correct comparison.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of the Transformer with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of self-attention. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Derive the scaled dot-product attention formula and justify the use of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transformer Architecture (7 marks):**

The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of an **encoder-decoder** structure built entirely on attention and feed-forward layers — *no recurrence or convolution*.

**Encoder Block** contains $N$ identical layers, each with:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — allows each token to attend to all tokens in the sequence.
2. **Add & Norm** — residual connection + Layer Normalization.
3. **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — two linear layers with ReLU: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, x\mathbf{W}_1 + b_1)\mathbf{W}_2 + b_2$.
4. **Add & Norm** — second residual + normalization.

**Decoder Block** additionally has **masked** multi-head self-attention (to prevent attending to future tokens) and **cross-attention** (decoder attends to encoder output).

**Role of Self-Attention:**
- Captures **long-range dependencies** in $O(1)$ path length (vs. $O(n)$ for RNNs).
- Provides **parallelization** across sequence positions.
- Yields **interpretable attention maps** showing which input tokens influence each output.
- Forms the **contextualized representation** that powers BERT, GPT, and all modern LLMs.

*(Award 2 marks for diagram description, 3 marks for components, 2 marks for role.)*

**(b) Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Attention (7 marks):**

Given inputs $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, learn projection matrices $\mathbf{W}^Q, \mathbf{W}^K, \mathbf{W}^V$.

**Step 1:** Compute query, key, value matrices:

$$\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^Q, \quad \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^K, \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^V$$

**Step 2:** Compute similarity via dot product:

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3:** Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent gradient vanishing — *justification below*.

**Step 4:** Apply row-wise softmax to get attention weights $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Step 5:** Weighted sum of values:

$$\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$

**Justification for $\sqrt{d_k}$:**  
Assume each component of $q_i$ and $k_i$ is independent with mean 0 and variance 1. Their dot product $s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ has mean 0 and variance $d_k$. When $d_k$ is large (e.g., 64 in BERT-base), $s$ takes large magnitudes, pushing softmax outputs to extremes (0 or 1) and causing **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1, keeping softmax in a smooth, trainable regime.

*(Award 2 marks each for forward projection, dot product, and softmax steps; 3 marks for scaling justification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the evolution of Machine Learning with reference to perceptron, backpropagation, CNN, RNN, and Transformer. (7 marks)**  
**(b) With relevant equations, explain the working of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). Compare it briefly with Variational Autoencoders (VAE). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evolution of ML (7 marks):**

1. **Perceptron (1958, Rosenblatt):** A linear binary classifier $\hat{y} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$. Limitation: cannot solve XOR (Minsky & Papert, 1969).

2. **Backpropagation (1986, Rumelhart et al.):** The chain-rule-based algorithm that enabled training of **multi-layer perceptrons**, overcoming the XOR limitation and giving birth to "connectionism."

3. **Convolutional Neural Networks (LeNet 1998, AlexNet 2012):** Used **convolutional layers** (weight sharing, translation invariance) and **pooling** to dominate image tasks. AlexNet used **ReLU** activation + **Dropout** + **GPU training** to win ImageNet 2012 by a huge margin.

4. **Recurrent Neural Networks & LSTM (1990s–2014):** Introduced **temporal recurrence** to handle sequences. **LSTM (Hochreiter, 1997)** solved vanishing gradient via gated memory cells ($\sigma$ forget/input/output gates). Used for translation, speech, time-series.

5. **Transformers (2017, Vaswani et al.):** Eliminated recurrence entirely. Used **self-attention** to model sequence dependencies in parallel. This enabled the training of **GPT, BERT, T5** and the entire LLM revolution.

*(Award ~1.4 marks per stage.)*

**(b) GAN Working and VAE Comparison (7 marks):**

**GAN Objective — Min-Max Game:**

The Generator $G$ maps noise $\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z})$ to fake samples $G(\mathbf{z})$. The Discriminator $D$ outputs probability that a sample is real.

$$\min_G \max_D \, V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- $D$ is trained to **maximize** — correctly classify real vs. fake.
- $G$ is trained to **minimize** — fool $D$ into believing fake samples are real.
- At equilibrium, $D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere and $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ (the generated distribution matches the real distribution).

**Training Algorithm (alternating SGD):**
1. Sample minibatch of real data $\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}$ and noise $\mathbf{z} \sim p_z$.
2. Update $D$: ascend on $\nabla_D [\log D(\mathbf{x}) + \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$.
3. Update $G$: descend on $\nabla_G [\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$.

**GAN vs. VAE Comparison:**

| Aspect | GAN | VAE |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Adversarial | Probabilistic / variational inference |
| **Loss** | Min-max game | **ELBO** = reconstruction $- D_{KL}(q(z\mid x)\,\Vert\,p(z))$ |
| **Latent Space** | Not necessarily regularized | Smooth, Gaussian-structured |
| **Sample Quality** | Sharper images | Blurrier images |
| **Training Stability** | Mode collapse, unstable | Stable |
| **Inference** | No encoder | Has explicit encoder $q(z\mid x)$ |
| **Use Case** | StyleGAN, BigGAN, DALL·E (early) | Anomaly detection, latent interpolation |

*(Award 3 marks for GAN equations, 2 marks for training, 2 marks for comparison table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling** in attention derivation — *loses 1–2 marks immediately*.
> 2. **Confusing encoder and decoder self-attention** — decoder is *masked* (causal); encoder is *bidirectional*.
> 3. **Writing GAN loss as a single objective** — must clearly split into $\min_G$ and $\max_D$.
> 4. **Skipping the chain rule steps** in backpropagation — KTU examiners expect *at least 4–5 explicit differentiation steps*.
> 5. **Writing "AI = ML = DL"** — these are *strictly nested sets*; ML ⊂ AI, DL ⊂ ML.
> 6. **Not mentioning softmax saturation** when justifying $\sqrt{d_k}$ — this is a 2-mark conceptual test point.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ML advances** span perceptron → backprop → CNN/RNN → Transformer → Foundation Models → Generative AI.
- **Backpropagation** is *the* training algorithm — derived via the chain rule; uses gradient descent update $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$.
- **CNNs** = spatial feature extraction (convolutions + pooling); **RNNs/LSTMs** = temporal sequence modeling; **Transformers** = universal sequence model via self-attention.
- **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is the *defining equation* of modern AI:

$$\text{Attn}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)\mathbf{V}$$

- **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ attentions in parallel, then concatenates — captures diverse relationships.
- **GANs** train a generator–discriminator pair via $\min_G \max_D$; **VAEs** maximize the ELBO; **Diffusion Models** iteratively denoise.
- **Transfer Learning** (pre-train + fine-tune) is the *default strategy* for all modern AI products.
- **Standard metrics**: FID ↓ for images, BLEU/ROUGE ↑ for text, Perplexity ↓ for LMs, Accuracy ↑ for classification.
- **Hardware shift**: CPU (classical) → GPU (deep learning) → TPU/cluster (LLMs).
- **Syllabus anchor**: This topic is the *foundation* for Modules 2–5 (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion, LLMs, Ethics).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
