# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define *mode collapse* in the context of Generative Adversarial Networks. State ONE remedy commonly used to mitigate it.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Mode collapse is a training pathology in GANs where the generator $G$ produces a **narrow subset** of the data distribution regardless of the input latent code $z$. That is, for many distinct $z_i \sim p_z(z)$, the model outputs $G(z_i)$ that cluster around one or a few modes of $p_{\text{data}}$, losing diversity.

**Remedies (any one of the following):**
1. **Unrolled GANs** — the generator is updated through several future discriminator steps to anticipate its response.
2. **Wasserstein loss with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP)** — provides informative gradients even when distributions are far apart.
3. **Mini-batch discrimination** — the discriminator inspects an entire batch for inter-sample similarity, penalizing low diversity.
4. **Conditional GANs** with class labels — explicitly forces one mode per class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"the GAN produces the same image"* without specifying the *mechanism* (i.e., the generator is exploited to fool the discriminator). For full 3 marks, **name the cause AND the remedy**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Write the formula for the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and state whether a lower or higher value indicates better generative performance.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
FID compares the statistics of real and generated images embedded in the Inception-v3 feature space. The two distributions are assumed Gaussian with mean vectors $\mu_r, \mu_g$ and covariance matrices $\Sigma_r, \Sigma_g$:

$$ \text{FID}(\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{G}) \;=\; \lVert \mu_r - \mu_g \rVert_2^{\,2} \;+\; \mathrm{Tr}\!\Bigl(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g \;-\; 2\,\bigl(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g\bigr)^{1/2}\Bigr) $$

A **lower** FID value indicates that the generated distribution is closer to the real distribution, hence **better** generative quality. A perfect generator would achieve FID = 0.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the matrix square-root term $(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}$. This is the **most-discriminating** part of the formula and earns 1 of the 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Generative Trilemma**. Discuss how GAN, VAE, and Diffusion models each address (or fail to address) the three competing properties of *high sample quality*, *high sample diversity*, and *fast sampling*. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** used in Variational Autoencoders. Explain how an excessive weight on the KL-divergence term leads to **posterior collapse**, and suggest TWO remedies. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the Trilemma.**
The Generative Trilemma (Xiao et al., 2021) states that a deep generative model can simultaneously optimize **at most two** of the following three properties:
- **Quality** — visual / semantic fidelity of generated samples.
- **Diversity** — coverage of the data distribution's modes.
- **Sampling Speed** — low wall-clock time per generated sample.

**Step 2 — How GANs Address It.**
- GANs produce *high quality* (sharp images) and are *fast* (one forward pass through $G$).
- However, they suffer from **mode collapse** → *low diversity*.
- GANs thus occupy the **(Quality, Speed) vertex** of the trilemma.

**Step 3 — How VAEs Address It.**
- VAEs have a tractable likelihood and *high diversity* (latent prior covers all modes).
- Sampling requires a single decoder pass → *fast*.
- However, samples are *blurry* (decoder optimizes pixel-wise Gaussian likelihood) → *low quality*.
- VAEs occupy the **(Diversity, Speed) vertex**.

**Step 4 — How Diffusion Models Address It.**
- Diffusion achieves *high quality* AND *high diversity* (state-of-the-art FID on ImageNet).
- However, sampling requires **iterative denoising** over hundreds to thousands of steps → *slow*.
- Diffusion occupies the **(Quality, Diversity) vertex** — modern research (DDIM, DPM-Solver) tries to break the trilemma by distilling diffusion into few-step models.

**Conclusion:** The trilemma is a useful lens for *categorizing* model families and *motivating* architectural choices in modern research.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Start with the marginal log-likelihood:**

$$ \log p_\theta(x) \;=\; \log \int p_\theta(x, z)\, dz $$

**Step 2 — Introduce variational distribution $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ and apply the log-of-expectation bound via Jensen's inequality:**

$$ \log p_\theta(x) \;\ge\; \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\bigl[\log p_\theta(x, z)\bigr] \;-\; \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\bigl[\log q_\phi(z\mid x)\bigr] $$

**Step 3 — Decompose the joint $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)$ and isolate the KL term:**

$$ \log p_\theta(x) \;\ge\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{reconstruction}} \;-\; \underbrace{\text{KL}\bigl(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\bigr)}_{\text{regularization}} \;\equiv\; \text{ELBO} $$

**Step 4 — Posterior Collapse Mechanism.**
The full training objective with a KL weight $\beta$ is:

$$ \mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] \;-\; \beta \cdot \text{KL}\bigl(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\bigr) $$

If $\beta$ is too large (or if the decoder is too expressive), the optimizer reduces the KL term to **near zero**. This forces:

$$ q_\phi(z \mid x) \;\longrightarrow\; p(z) \;=\; \mathcal{N}(0, I) $$

In this limit, $z$ becomes **statistically independent of $x$**, and the decoder learns to output the data distribution *unconditionally* — the latent $z$ is ignored. Generated samples lose semantic content.

**Step 5 — Remedies.**

| Remedy | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **KL Annealing** | Start with $\beta = 0$ (pure autoencoder) and gradually raise $\beta$ to 1, giving the encoder time to learn a useful mapping. |
| **Free Bits** | Set a minimum threshold $\lambda$ on the KL reduction per dimension: if $\text{KL}_j < \lambda$, the gradient on that dimension is zeroed. Prevents collapse of any single latent dimension. |
| **Weaker Decoder** | Use a less expressive decoder (e.g., shallower, smaller receptive field) so that it *cannot* model $p(x)$ without using $z$. |
| **Skip Connections from $x$ to Decoder** | Concatenate $x$ partially into the decoder, forcing $z$ to encode information not already in $x$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently write the ELBO formula but **omit the Jensen's inequality justification** ("because $\log$ is concave"). You will lose 2 of 7 marks if this step is missing. Also, do **not** confuse *posterior collapse* with *vanishing gradients* in GANs — they are different pathologies in different model families.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **vanishing gradient problem** in the original GAN formulation. Show mathematically why it arises from the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD)**, and explain how the **Wasserstein-1 distance** with the **Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality** resolves it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Inception Score (IS)** and the **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** as evaluation metrics for generative models. Highlight the formula, interpretation, and one limitation of each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Original GAN Objective.**

$$ \min_G \max_D V(D, G) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$

**Step 2 — Optimal Discriminator Derivation.** For fixed $G$, differentiating $V$ w.r.t. $D(x)$:

$$ \frac{\partial V}{\partial D(x)} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} \;-\; \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} $$

Setting to zero:

$$ D^*(x) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} $$

**Step 3 — Plug Back and Identify JSD.** Substituting $D^*$ yields:

$$ V(D^*, G) \;=\; -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}\bigl(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g\bigr) $$

where:

$$ \text{JSD}(P \,\|\, Q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\text{KL}\bigl(P \,\|\, M\bigr) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{KL}\bigl(Q \,\|\, M\bigr), \quad M = \tfrac{1}{2}(P + Q) $$

**Step 4 — The Saturation Problem.** JSD is **bounded above by $\log 2$**. Crucially, when $P$ and $Q$ have **disjoint supports** (a common situation in early GAN training), JSD is *identically* $\log 2$, regardless of how far apart the distributions are.

Therefore the generator gradient vanishes:

$$ \nabla_{\theta_G}\,V(D^*, G) \;=\; \nabla_{\theta_G}\bigl[2 \log 2\bigr] \;=\; 0 $$

The generator **cannot learn** because the loss surface is flat.

**Step 5 — Wasserstein-1 to the Rescue.**
The Wasserstein-1 (Earth-Mover) distance is:

$$ W_1(P_r, P_g) \;=\; \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P_r, P_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}\bigl[\lVert x - y \rVert\bigr] $$

By the **Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality**:

$$ W_1(P_r, P_g) \;=\; \sup_{\lVert f \rVert_L \le 1} \Bigl(\mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_r}[f(x)] \;-\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_g}[f(x)]\Bigr) $$

Parametrizing $f$ as a neural network $f_w$ constrained to be 1-Lipschitz (via weight clipping or gradient penalty) gives a **trainable critic**. Unlike JSD, $W_1$ provides **smooth, non-zero gradients** even for disjoint distributions — the gradient magnitude scales with the *physical distance* between samples.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Marks are split as follows — (i) 2 marks for deriving $D^*$, (ii) 2 marks for showing $V$ reduces to JSD, (iii) 1 mark for the saturation argument, (iv) 2 marks for the W-1 + KR duality resolution. **Don't skip the derivation steps.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Inception Score (IS) | Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\text{IS} = \exp\bigl(\mathbb{E}_x[\text{KL}(p(y\mid x)\,\vert\, p(y))]\bigr)$ | $\text{FID} = \vert\vert\mu_r - \mu_g\vert\vert_2^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ |
| **Direction** | Higher is better | Lower is better |
| **Reference Dataset** | Not required (uses marginal class entropy) | **Required** (compares to real set) |
| **Statistical Assumption** | None explicit | Both feature distributions are Gaussian |
| **Detects Mode Collapse?** | Weakly (only via low marginal entropy) | **Yes** (covariance mismatch reveals it) |
| **Sample Sensitivity** | Low | High (needs ~10k–50k samples) |
| **Limitation** | Cannot detect if generated distribution is *mismatched* to real data (e.g., a model generating all cats still gets high IS if labels are confident) | Assumes Gaussianity of Inception features; biased for small samples |

**Comparison Summary:**
- IS measures **how confident AND diverse** the generator is, but does not anchor to real data.
- FID measures the **distance to the real distribution** in feature space — much stronger signal of perceptual quality, at the cost of needing real reference samples.
- FID is the **de facto gold standard** in modern image generation research (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, StyleGAN papers all report FID).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often confuse the *direction* of the two metrics (lower vs. higher). A common 1-mark deduction. Also, do not write "FID uses Inception-v3" without noting it operates on the **penultimate layer activations**, not the softmax output.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield Bullets**

- ⭐ **Generative modeling = learning $P_{\text{data}}(x)$ to synthesize new samples.** The 5-pillar taxonomy is: *Data, Optimization, Evaluation, Architecture, Sociotechnical*.

- ⭐ **Mode collapse** is a GAN-specific pathology where the generator outputs low-diversity samples. **Causes:** discriminator weakness, lack of diversity penalty. **Fixes:** WGAN, mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs, conditional GANs.

- ⭐ **Vanishing gradients** in vanilla GANs come from **JSD saturating at $\log 2$** when $p_g$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ are disjoint. **Wasserstein-1** fixes this via the **Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality**.

- ⭐ **Posterior collapse** is a VAE-specific pathology where $q_\phi(z \mid x) \to p(z)$ and $z$ becomes uninformative. **Fixes:** KL annealing, free bits, weaker decoder.

- ⭐ **Inception Score (IS)** = $\exp(\mathbb{E}_x[\text{KL}(p(y\mid x)\,\vert\, p(y))])$, **higher is better**, no real data needed.

- ⭐ **FID** = $\lVert \mu_r - \mu_g \rVert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2})$, **lower is better**, requires real reference set, current gold standard.

- ⭐ **Manifold Hypothesis:** Real data lies on a low-dim manifold $\mathcal{M} \subset \mathbb{R}^D$ with $d \ll D$. Good generators learn $\mathcal{M}$, not the full ambient space.

- ⭐ **Generative Trilemma:** Quality, Diversity, Speed — pick any two. GAN ≈ (Q,S); VAE ≈ (D,S); Diffusion ≈ (Q,D).

- ⭐ **Evaluation Paradox:** No universal scalar loss correlates with human perception — must combine proxy metrics (FID, IS) + human evaluation.

- ⭐ **Log-likelihood can mislead:** A blurry "mean" image model can have *higher* log-likelihood than a sharp generative model that occasionally fails.

- ⭐ **Intractable partition function** in EBMs prevents exact likelihood computation → must use MCMC / contrastive divergence (slow, noisy).

- ⭐ **Sociotechnical challenges** include hallucination (LLMs), deepfakes, copyright memorization, and the alignment problem. Mention at least one in any 14-mark answer for full marks.

- ⭐ **Standard notation trap:** In KTU answer sheets, write norms as $\lVert x \rVert$ (double bar) — never as $|x|$, which is reserved for scalars.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on "Challenges of Generative Modeling," structure your answer as: (1) **Definition** (2 marks), (2) **Mathematical formulation** (4 marks), (3) **Worked numerical or derivational example** (4 marks), (4) **Comparison with another model family** (2 marks), (5) **Real-world implication** (2 marks). Following this template consistently across modules is what separates 12-marks from 14-marks answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Considerations in Generative AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations in Generative AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethical Considerations in Generative AI** refer to the *systematic moral, legal, and societal evaluation* of risks, obligations, and rights that arise from the design, training, fine-tuning, deployment, and downstream consumption of generative models (LLMs, diffusion models, GANs, VAEs, and multimodal foundation models). They span **bias & fairness**, **transparency & explainability**, **accountability & governance**, **privacy**, **intellectual property**, **misinformation**, **environmental sustainability**, and **dual-use safety**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is treated as a *cross-cutting concern* (i.e., a non-functional requirement that must be addressed alongside every functional module of a generative system). It is grounded in globally recognized frameworks such as the **OECD AI Principles**, the **UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)**, the **EU AI Act (2024)**, the **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)**, and **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dual-Use Power Tool"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine generative AI as a *high-pressure industrial water jet cutter*. It can flawlessly slice titanium (cure cancer, write code, design drugs) but can equally dismember a human hand (deepfakes, phishing at scale, automated disinformation). Because the *exact same tool* is used for both, society cannot ban the tool itself — instead it installs **safety interlocks, operator licensing, usage audits, and emergency shutoffs**. Ethics in Generative AI is precisely the engineering discipline that designs and validates these "safety interlocks."

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a High-Yield KTU Focus

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Regulatory urgency** | EU AI Act (in force 2024–2025), India's Digital India Act, US Executive Order 14110 |
| **Real-world harm** | Hallucinations in medical/legal chatbots, deepfake fraud, copyrighted training data lawsuits (NYT v. OpenAI) |
| **Mandatory CO mapping** | Maps to CO5 (Ethics, Privacy & Social Impact) in PCCMT602 |
| **Board question frequency** | High — typically a 7-mark sub-question in Part B |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two anchor definitions every KTU student must memorize verbatim:**
> 1. **Hallucination** — *confident generation of factually incorrect or fabricated outputs by a generative model.*
> 2. **Algorithmic Bias** — *systematic and repeatable errors in AI outputs that create unfair outcomes for specific demographic groups.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Generative AI Ethics

The KTU 2024 syllabus collapses the wide ethical landscape into **seven high-yield pillars**. Each is explained with the *Why* and *How* the engineer must address it.

### Pillar 1 — Bias & Fairness

- **Why:** Training corpora (Common Crawl, Wikipedia, Reddit) over-represent certain demographics, dialects, and viewpoints, embedding *historical and societal bias* into the model's parameter space.
- **How to mitigate:** dataset auditing, debiasing embeddings (e.g., projection-based methods), reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with diverse annotators, and post-hoc fairness audits using formal metrics (see §3).

### Pillar 2 — Transparency & Explainability

- **Why:** Generative models are essentially *opaque stochastic black boxes*. End-users and regulators cannot validate outputs.
- **How:** attention visualization, chain-of-thought prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) provenance logs, model cards, system cards, and datasheets for datasets (Gebru et al.).

### Pillar 3 — Accountability & Governance

- **Why:** When a model produces harmful output, who is legally responsible — the *user, developer, deployer, fine-tuner, or model provider*?
- **How:** contractual AI liability frameworks, audit trails, human-in-the-loop (HITL) sign-off for high-risk outputs, ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System).

### Pillar 4 — Privacy & Data Protection

- **Why:** LLMs can memorize and regurgitate verbatim training data (e.g., emails, phone numbers, medical records).
- **How:** differential privacy (DP-SGD), federated learning, data anonymization, consent-based data licensing, and adherence to **DPDP Act 2023** (India) and **GDPR Art. 22**.

### Pillar 5 — Intellectual Property & Copyright

- **Why:** Outputs may closely resemble copyrighted training inputs (memorization attacks). Conversely, AI-generated works raise authorship questions.
- **How:** training data licensing (e.g., OpenAI's deals with AP, Shutterstock), output filters, watermarking, and adherence to evolving fair-use jurisprudence.

### Pillar 6 — Misinformation, Deepfakes & Dual-Use Risk

- **Why:** Generative models lower the cost of producing convincing fake text, images, audio, and video.
- **How:** provenance mechanisms (C2PA standard), watermark embedding (e.g., SynthID by Google), content credentials, and platform-side detection classifiers.

### Pillar 7 — Environmental & Societal Sustainability

- **Why:** Training a frontier LLM can emit **>500 tonnes of CO₂-eq**; inference at scale multiplies this. Also: labor displacement in content/creative industries.
- **How:** efficient architectures (Mixture-of-Experts, distillation), green-AI metrics (kWh/token), renewable-powered data centers, and just-transition reskilling programs.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as the single-page revision sheet for any Part-A or Part-B question on this topic.**

| # | Ethical Pillar | Core Risk | Canonical Mitigation | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias & Fairness | Discriminatory outputs | Dataset balancing, RLHF, debiasing | IEEE 7003-2024 |
| 2 | Transparency | Opaque decisions | Model cards, RAG, attention viz | EU AI Act Art. 13 |
| 3 | Accountability | Liability vacuum | HITL, audit trails | ISO/IEC 42001 |
| 4 | Privacy | Data memorization | DP-SGD, federated learning | GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 |
| 5 | IP / Copyright | Training-data infringement | Licensing, watermark | Berne Convention |
| 6 | Misinformation | Deepfakes | C2PA, SynthID watermark | EU AI Act Art. 50 |
| 7 | Environment | Carbon footprint | Distillation, MoE | UN SDG 13 |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

These pillars are not abstract philosophy — they translate into **concrete engineering requirements (NFRs)** written into the Software Requirements Specification (SRS) of any KTU final-year project that uses a generative model. A student must therefore learn to write ethical requirements as testable specifications (e.g., *"the chatbot shall not reveal any PII token present in its training corpus with probability > 0.01"*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Mathematical Derivations

Because KTU 2024 marks ethics as an *engineer's responsibility*, certain ethical guarantees must be expressed as **formal mathematical metrics**. Below are the derivations a student must be able to reproduce in the exam.

## 3.1 Mathematical Formalization of Fairness (Group Fairness)

Let the protected attribute be $A \in \{0,1\}$ (e.g., $A=1$ = female, $A=0$ = male), the true label $Y \in \{0,1\}$, and the model prediction $\hat{Y} \in \{0,1\}$.

### Step 1 — Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity)

The *positive prediction rate* must be independent of $A$:

$$
P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \;=\; P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)
$$

$$
\therefore\; \bigl\vert\, P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) \,\bigr\vert \;\leq\; \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the *fairness tolerance* (typically $\epsilon = 0.05$ or $0.10$).

### Step 2 — Equalized Odds (Hardt et al., 2016)

Both the true-positive rate and the false-positive rate must be equal across groups:

$$
P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=1) \;=\; P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=0)
$$

$$
P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=1) \;=\; P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=0)
$$

### Step 3 — Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)

$$
\text{DIR} \;=\; \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "four-fifths rule" (EEOC, U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission) states: a DIR below $0.80$ ($4/5$) is presumptively *discriminatory*.

### Step 4 — Disparate Impact Reweighing (Kamiran & Calders, 2012)

To satisfy demographic parity on a training set, assign each tuple $(x,y)$ the weight:

$$
W(x,y) \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{exp}}(A=a, Y=b)}{P_{\text{obs}}(A=a, Y=b)}
$$

where $a = A(x)$, $b = y$, and the numerator is the joint probability if the dataset were perfectly balanced.

## 3.2 Privacy Guarantee via Differential Privacy

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for any two datasets $D, D'$ that differ in a single record:

$$
\Pr\bigl[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S\bigr] \;\leq\; e^{\epsilon}\,\Pr\bigl[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S\bigr]
$$

In **DP-SGD** (Abadi et al., 2016), the gradient $g_t$ at step $t$ is clipped to norm $\leq C$ and Gaussian noise of scale $\sigma$ added:

$$
\tilde{g}_t \;=\; \frac{g_t}{\max\!\bigl(1,\, \Vert g_t\Vert_2 / C\bigr)} \;+\; \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(0,\,\sigma^{2}C^{2}I\bigr)
$$

For a model trained for $T$ steps, the **privacy budget** is governed by the *moments accountant*:

$$
\epsilon \;\leq\; \frac{T}{\sigma^{2}}\,\sqrt{\log\!\bigl(1/\delta\bigr)}
$$

Hence, *more training steps with the same noise scale erode privacy* — the canonical privacy–utility tradeoff.

## 3.3 Environmental Cost (Strubell et al., 2019 — canonical KTU reference)

The carbon footprint in lbs of $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}$ for training a transformer is approximated by:

$$
\mathrm{CO}_{2\text{-eq}} \;\approx\; \mathrm{kWh}_{\text{total}} \;\times\; \mathrm{CI}_{\text{grid}} \;\times\; 1.1242
$$

where:
- $\mathrm{kWh}_{\text{total}}$ = power draw $\times$ training hours
- $\mathrm{CI}_{\text{grid}}$ = grid carbon intensity (kg $\mathrm{CO_2}$/kWh)
- $1.1242$ = lbs-per-kg conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (numerical):** Training a model for $336$ hours on a cluster drawing $100$ kW in a coal-heavy region ($\mathrm{CI}=0.9$ kg/kWh):

$$
\mathrm{kWh}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 100 \times 336 \;=\; 33{,}600 \;\text{kWh}
$$

$$
\mathrm{CO}_{2\text{-eq}} \;=\; 33{,}600 \times 0.9 \times 1.1242 \;\approx\; 34{,}001 \;\text{lbs} \;\approx\; 15{,}423 \;\text{kg}
$$

This equals roughly **6 years of one average Indian passenger car's emissions** — a striking figure often asked in viva voce.

## 3.4 Master Analytical Matrix — Ethics-to-Requirement Mapping

| Pillar | Formal Metric | Engineering NFR (testable) | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fairness | Demographic parity gap | $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) \vert$ | $\leq 0.05$ |
| Fairness | DIR | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) / P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)$ | $\in [0.8, 1.25]$ |
| Privacy | DP budget | $\epsilon$ from moments accountant | $\leq 1.0$ |
| Privacy | Memorization rate | Fraction of training strings leaked | $\leq 0.01$ |
| Environment | Carbon per 1M tokens | kg $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}$ / 1M tokens | $\leq 0.5$ |
| Transparency | Card completeness | % fields filled in Model Card | $= 100\%$ |
| Misinformation | Watermark detectability | AUC of detector on outputs | $\geq 0.95$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Ethical Risk Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataIn[Raw Web Corpus] --> filter1[Consent and Licensing Filter]
    filter1 --> filter2[PII Scrubber and DP Noise]
    filter2 --> train[Model Training]
    train --> eval[Bias and Fairness Audit]
    eval --> redTeam[Red-Team Adversarial Eval]
    redTeam --> guard[Output Guardrails]
    guard --> watermark[C2PA Watermark Embedding]
    watermark --> deploy[Deployment with Model Card]
    deploy --> monitor[Continuous Post-Market Monitoring]
    monitor --> incident[Incident Response and Rollback]
    incident --> retrain[Trigger Retraining Loop]
    retrain --> train
```

**How to read the diagram:** Every node is an *engineering checkpoint*. Skipping a checkpoint (e.g., red-teaming) is the most common reason projects fail ethical review — and the most common reason KTU viva examiners deduct marks.

## 4.2 EU AI Act Risk Classification Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RiskPyramid
        A1[Unacceptable Risk: Social Scoring, Manipulative AI] --> A2
        A2[High Risk: Hiring, Credit, Education, Biometric ID] --> A3
        A3[Limited Risk: Chatbots, Deepfakes must be labelled] --> A4
        A4[Minimal Risk: Spam Filters, AI in Video Games]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> Under the EU AI Act, **generative AI falls in the Limited Risk tier by default** (Art. 50 transparency duties) but instantly escalates to **High Risk** the moment it is used for hiring, credit scoring, medical diagnosis, or biometric identification.

## 4.3 Accountability Chain (Who is Liable?)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Provider: Trains and Releases the Model] --> D[Deployer: Integrates into a Product]
    D --> U[End User: Issues the Prompt]
    U --> H[Harmful Output]
    H --> Q{Who is Liable?}
    Q --> P1[Provider liable if defect in model]
    Q --> D1[Deployer liable if misuse by design]
    Q --> U1[User liable if used for illegal purpose]
```

## 4.4 Mitigation Stack (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Data Curation] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: Training-Time Alignment: RLHF and Constitutional AI] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3: Inference-Time Guardrails: Classifiers and Moderation] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4: Output Watermarking and Provenance] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5: Post-Market Monitoring and Audits]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]
**Define algorithmic bias in the context of generative AI. List any two sources of bias and one mitigation technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Algorithmic bias* is the systematic and repeatable error in a generative model's outputs that produces unfair outcomes for specific demographic groups (1 mark).
Two sources:
1. *Historical bias* in the training corpus (e.g., Wikipedia over-represents Western, male viewpoints) (1 mark).
2. *Annotation bias* introduced by homogeneous RLHF raters (0.5 marks).

Mitigation: demographic-parity-aware dataset re-sampling combined with RLHF using a diverse rater pool (0.5 marks).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]
**What is differential privacy? State its mathematical definition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Differential privacy is a *mathematical framework* that bounds the influence of any single training record on a model's output (1 mark).
A mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\epsilon$-DP if for neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ and any output set $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\leq\; e^{\epsilon}\,\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

Smaller $\epsilon$ means stronger privacy (1 mark). In DP-SGD this is achieved by *gradient clipping* plus *Gaussian noise* (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Do NOT write only philosophy.** You will lose 4–5 marks for every question that lacks engineering mechanisms (datasets, metrics, code references).
> 2. **Always pair each ethical risk with a quantifiable metric.** "Fairness" alone gets 1 mark; "demographic-parity gap $\leq 0.05$" gets 4 marks.
> 3. **Never quote a regulation by name without stating which Article or Section governs it.** Generic "GDPR compliance" gets 0.5 marks; "GDPR Art. 22 right to explanation" gets 2 marks.
> 4. **Use the §3 mathematical formulation when the question is 14 marks.** Skipping the math loses ~3 marks even in an ethics question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q3-A]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven pillars of ethical considerations** specific to generative AI. For each pillar, state **one engineering mitigation** and **one governing standard**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **demographic-parity metric** and the **disparate-impact ratio (DIR)**. A hiring classifier is evaluated on 1,000 candidates, of which 400 are women. The model approves 200 men and 60 women. Compute the DIR and state whether the system passes the four-fifths rule. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Seven pillars (1 mark each — but compact in table form):

| Pillar | Mitigation | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Bias & Fairness | Dataset reweighting | IEEE 7003-2024 |
| Transparency | Model Card + RAG | EU AI Act Art. 13 |
| Accountability | Human-in-the-loop | ISO/IEC 42001 |
| Privacy | DP-SGD | GDPR, DPDP 2023 |
| IP / Copyright | Output watermark + filter | Berne Convention |
| Misinformation | C2PA provenance | EU AI Act Art. 50 |
| Environment | Distillation, MoE | UN SDG 13 |

*(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for naming each Article/Standard correctly — examiner allocates 6 marks across rows + 1 mark for cohesion.)*

**(b)** *Demographic parity gap*:

$$
\Delta \;=\; \bigl\vert\, P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) \,\bigr\vert
$$

*Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)*:

$$
\text{DIR} \;=\; \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}
$$

**Numerical substitution (given: total = 1000, women = 400 → men = 600):**

Approval rate among women: $\dfrac{60}{400} = 0.150$

Approval rate among men: $\dfrac{200}{600} = 0.333$

$$
\text{DIR} \;=\; \frac{0.150}{0.333} \;\approx\; 0.45
$$

> [!WARNING]
> $0.45 < 0.80$ → the system **FAILS** the four-fifths rule and is *presumptively discriminatory*. The company must either reweight the training set, adjust the decision threshold per group, or apply Kamiran–Calders reweighing before deployment.

*(Allocation: 2 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical substitution, 1 mark interpretation, 2 marks mitigation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q3-B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental and privacy concerns** of training large generative models. Compute the carbon footprint (in lbs of $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}$) for a model trained on a 50 kW cluster for 200 hours in a coal-heavy region where the carbon intensity is $1.0$ kg $\mathrm{CO_2}$/kWh. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **EU AI Act's risk classification** for generative AI with a neat sketch. Why does a generative model used for medical diagnosis escalate to *high-risk* even if the base model is low-risk? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Privacy risks (2 marks): training data memorization (verbatim regurgitation of emails, PII); membership-inference attacks; prompt-injection exfiltration.

Environmental risks (2 marks): power-hungry training (frontier LLM ≈ 500 t $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}$); water cooling; e-waste from accelerator turnover.

**Carbon computation (3 marks):**

$$
\mathrm{kWh}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 50 \;\text{kW} \times 200 \;\text{h} \;=\; 10{,}000 \;\text{kWh}
$$

$$
\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}\;(\text{kg}) \;=\; 10{,}000 \times 1.0 \;=\; 10{,}000 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}\;(\text{lbs}) \;=\; 10{,}000 \times 1.1242 \;\approx\; 11{,}242 \;\text{lbs}
$$

Mitigations (incorporated for partial credit): renewable-powered data centres, mixture-of-experts (MoE) for sparse activation, model distillation, and green-AI metrics like kg $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq}$/million tokens.

**(b)** Risk pyramid sketch (refer §4.2) — 3 marks for correct drawing with the four tiers. Discussion (4 marks):

- The **base generative model** (e.g., GPT-4, Stable Diffusion) is *Limited Risk* under EU AI Act Art. 50 — meaning the deployer must label AI-generated content and inform users they are interacting with a machine.
- However, the **moment the same model is fine-tuned or wrapped for medical diagnosis**, it inherits the *High-Risk* classification because the EU AI Act Annex III lists medical/health applications as inherently high-risk. This triggers obligations: conformity assessment, post-market monitoring, fundamental-rights impact assessment, registration in the EU AI database, and human oversight by a qualified clinician.
- Escalation logic: *Use-case governs risk-tier, not the model.* This is why a general-purpose LLM can simultaneously be Limited Risk (for a customer-service chatbot) and High Risk (for triage advice).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision (read 5 minutes before exam):**

- ☐ Memorize the **seven pillars**: Bias, Transparency, Accountability, Privacy, IP, Misinformation, Environment.
- ☐ Memorize **two metrics with formulas**: Demographic Parity Gap and Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR); DIR must lie in $[0.8, 1.25]$ for fairness.
- ☐ Memorize the **differential-privacy inequality** $P[M(D)\in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} P[M(D')\in S]$ and the DP-SGD noise formulation.
- ☐ Memorize the **environmental formula**: $\mathrm{CO_2\text{-}eq} = \mathrm{kWh} \times \mathrm{CI} \times 1.1242$ (for lbs) — plus a worked number like $11{,}242$ lbs.
- ☐ Memorize the **EU AI Act tiers**: Unacceptable → High → Limited → Minimal, and that **use-case governs risk-tier**.
- ☐ Remember the **four-fifths rule (DIR < 0.80)** — it is asked almost every year.
- ☐ Always cite **Article numbers** (EU AI Act Art. 50, GDPR Art. 22, DPDP Act 2023) — vague citations lose marks.
- ☐ Ethics questions require **engineering mechanisms + metrics + standards**, not philosophy.
- ☐ Hallucination + Algorithmic Bias are the two **must-know definitions** (Section 1).
- ☐ Mitigation is **defense-in-depth**: Data → Training Alignment → Inference Guardrails → Watermark → Monitoring.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Ethical Considerations in Generative AI (PCCMT602)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bias and Fairness in Generative AI Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bias and Fairness in Generative AI Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias in Generative AI** refers to systematic, repeatable errors in the output of a generative model (GAN, VAE, Diffusion Model, LLM) that produce distributions or representations skewed against specific demographic, social, semantic, or contextual groups due to imbalances in training data, model architecture, or optimization objectives.

**Fairness in Generative AI** is the formal, measurable property of a generative system whose output distribution, conditional likelihoods, and representational geometry do not systematically disadvantage individuals or groups based on protected or sensitive attributes (race, gender, age, religion, dialect, geography, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Protected / Sensitive Attributes (A):** Variables legally or ethically considered sensitive — e.g., gender, race, caste, age, religion, sexual orientation, disability status, socio-economic class, native language, accent, region.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generative Model Class (G_θ):** A parameterized function $G_\theta$ that maps a prior $p(z)$ to a learned data distribution $p_\theta(x)$, where $\theta$ represents the model parameters learned via maximum likelihood, adversarial training, or score matching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias Amplification (BA):** A phenomenon in which a generative model exaggerates statistical regularities (stereotypes) present in the training set, producing outputs where the skew ratio between privileged and unprivileged groups is *higher* in generated samples than in real samples.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Generative AI as a **chefs apprentice** who has only ever cooked in one specific household.

- The apprentice has tasted **10,000 dishes**, but 8,000 of them were from North Indian kitchens. So when asked to "cook something for dinner," the apprentice almost always produces North Indian food — even if the guest explicitly asks for South Indian or Chinese. That is **data bias (underrepresentation)**.
- The apprentice also noticed that in that household, *men always cooked the meat and women always cooked the salad*. So when told "generate a typical chef," the apprentice draws a man cooking meat. This is **stereotypical bias amplification**.
- Now the homeowner is upset and says "make the dish fair." But the apprentice has to invent a new metric: *How do we measure fairness in a plate of food?* Do we ensure equal proportions of vegetables and meat? Equal spice level for everyone? This is the **fairness metric problem** — there is no single, universally correct definition.

In real Generative AI:
- **The household kitchen** = the training dataset (Common Crawl, LAION, COYO, etc.)
- **The apprentice** = the model (Stable Diffusion, GPT, DALL·E, StyleGAN)
- **The guests** = end-users and marginalized communities
- **The recipe book** = the loss function and optimization objective

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Bias = the model learned skewed patterns. Fairness = the deliberate effort to measure, quantify, and correct those skewed patterns using formal mathematical definitions and engineering interventions.

---

## 1.3 Foundational Terminology Glossary

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Protected Attribute | $A$ | Variable defining the sensitive group (e.g., gender, race) |
| Privileged Group | $A = a_{priv}$ | Group historically advantaged in the data |
| Unprivileged Group | $A = a_{unpriv}$ | Group historically disadvantaged |
| Favorable Outcome | $Y = +1$ | Positive label (e.g., "professional," "leader") |
| Unfavorable Outcome | $Y = -1$ | Negative label (e.g., "criminal," "assistant") |
| Generator | $G_\theta$ | Function producing synthetic samples $x' = G_\theta(z)$ |
| Discriminator | $D_\phi$ | Function distinguishing real vs. generated samples |
| Latent Space | $\mathcal{Z}$ | Probability space $\mathbb{R}^d$ from which $z$ is sampled |
| Data Distribution | $p_{data}(x)$ | True real-world distribution of $x$ |
| Model Distribution | $p_\theta(x)$ | Distribution learned by the generator |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Bias in Generative AI Systems

Bias in generative systems originates at **four distinct stages** of the ML pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to identify *which stage* a bias is sourced from.

### 2.1.1 Historical Bias
- Exists in the world *before* any data is collected.
- Reflects pre-existing societal inequalities (e.g., historically fewer CEOs are women).
- Cannot be removed by data cleaning — only by **causal intervention or counterfactual data generation**.

### 2.1.2 Representation Bias
- Underrepresentation or overrepresentation of certain groups in training data.
- Quantitative metric: $\rho_a = \frac{N_a}{N_{total}}$ where $N_a$ is the count of samples from group $a$.
- If $\rho_a \ll \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ for any group $a$, the data is imbalanced.

### 2.1.3 Measurement Bias
- Arises when proxies used for labels are systematically biased.
- Example: facial recognition systems misclassifying darker-skinned females at ~34.7% error vs. ~0.8% for lighter-skinned males (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018 study cited in KTU reference material).

### 2.1.4 Algorithmic / Learning Bias
- Induced by the optimization objective, loss landscape, or model inductive biases.
- Generative models **amplify** training skew — this is the **Bias Amplification Factor (BAF)**.

### 2.1.5 Generative-Specific Bias Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latent Space Bias:** Even when sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, the *post-generation* attribute distribution is non-uniform. Certain attribute directions (e.g., "gender") in $\mathcal{Z}$ are entangled with semantic concepts ("leadership," "beauty").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Bias in Diffusion Models:** Classifier-free guidance amplifies demographic skew. The amplification factor scales with the guidance weight $w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GAN Mode Collapse Bias:** Minority modes may be entirely lost — generator collapses to producing only the majority sub-distribution.

---

## 2.2 Fairness Definitions (Formal)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with **six core fairness criteria**, originating from the fairness-in-ML literature (Hardt, Chouldechova, Verma & Rubin, Barocas et al.).

### 2.2.1 Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity)
The probability of a favorable generated outcome must be independent of the protected attribute $A$:

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = +1) \quad \forall a \in \mathcal{A}$$

### 2.2.2 Equalized Odds
The true positive and false positive rates must be equal across groups:

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = y, A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = y) \quad \forall a, y$$

### 2.2.3 Equal Opportunity
A relaxed version of equalized odds — only true positive rates must be equal:

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = +1, A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = +1) \quad \forall a$$

### 2.2.4 Counterfactual Fairness
A decision is fair if it remains the same in a counterfactual world where the protected attribute were different:

$$\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U) \quad \forall a, a' \in \mathcal{A}$$

where $U$ denotes the latent background factors (non-protected).

### 2.2.5 Individual Fairness
Similar individuals must receive similar outcomes (Lipschitz condition on the outcome mapping):

$$\vert f(x_i) - f(x_j) \vert \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$$

where $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a task-specific similarity metric.

### 2.2.6 Procedural Fairness
The algorithm's *process* — not just its output — must not rely on protected attributes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2016):** It is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy **Demographic Parity**, **Equalized Odds**, and **Predictive Parity** *unless* base rates across groups are exactly equal. KTU students must acknowledge this trade-off explicitly in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.3 Bias Quantification Metrics

### 2.3.1 Disparate Impact (DI)
$$\text{DI} = \frac{P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a_{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a_{priv})}$$

A system is considered fair under the "80% rule" if $\text{DI} \geq 0.8$.

### 2.3.2 Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)
$$\text{SPD} = P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a_{unpriv}) - P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a_{priv})$$

Fairness: $\text{SPD} \in [-\epsilon, \epsilon]$, typically $\epsilon = 0.1$.

### 2.3.3 Bias Amplification Score
Measured by computing the ratio of skew in generated vs. real data for co-occurrence of (group, attribute):

$$\text{BA} = \frac{\text{Skew}_{gen}(a, c)}{\text{Skew}_{real}(a, c)}$$

where $c$ is a contextual attribute (e.g., "doctor") and $a$ is the protected attribute (e.g., "female").

### 2.3.4 KL-Divergence of Group-Conditional Distributions
$$D_{KL}\left(p_\theta(x \mid A = a_1) \parallel p_\theta(x \mid A = a_2)\right)$$

A perfectly fair generator should produce *statistically indistinguishable* outputs across groups.

### 2.3.5 CLIP-Based Bias Score
For text-to-image models:
$$\text{Score}_{\text{bias}} = \frac{\cos(E_{img}, E_{text\_masc})}{\cos(E_{img}, E_{text\_fem})}$$

Deviation from 1.0 indicates skewed alignment.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Cheat Sheet:** Memorize the formulas below — they are *frequently* tested in KTU ESE Part A (3 marks) and Module 1 of PCCMT602.

| # | Concept | Formula | Threshold / Fair Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Demographic Parity | $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=+1)$ | Exact equality | Group-fair generation |
| 2 | Disparate Impact | $\frac{P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{priv})}$ | $\geq 0.8$ | Legal/regulatory audit |
| 3 | Statistical Parity Diff. | $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{unpriv}) - P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{priv})$ | $\leq 0.1$ | Industry fairness benchmark |
| 4 | Equal Opportunity | $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid Y=+1, A=a)$ = const | Exact | Hiring/recruitment systems |
| 5 | Bias Amplification | $\frac{\text{Skew}_{gen}}{\text{Skew}_{real}}$ | $\leq 1.0$ | GAN/Diffusion evaluation |
| 6 | KL-Fairness | $D_{KL}(p_\theta(x \mid a_1) \parallel p_\theta(x \mid a_2))$ | $\leq 0.05$ nats | Distribution comparison |
| 7 | Individual Fairness | $\vert f(x_i) - f(x_j) \vert \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ | Lipschitz const. $L$ | Recommendation systems |
| 8 | Representation Ratio | $\rho_a = \frac{N_a}{N_{total}}$ | $\approx \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ | Data audit |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Pipeline of Bias Propagation

Bias propagates through **five sequential stages** in any generative AI system:

1. **Society** → produces historical inequities.
2. **Data Collection** → encodes inequities as statistical skew.
3. **Model Training** → loss function reinforces skew (especially with long-tail data).
4. **Sampling / Inference** → classifier-free guidance, truncation tricks, temperature all amplify skew.
5. **Deployment** → end-users and downstream applications inherit the bias.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Bias mitigation is most *cost-effective* at the data and loss-function stages. Post-hoc filtering of outputs is the *least* effective and most expensive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides exhaustive mathematical derivations of fairness metrics, bias amplification quantification, and demographic parity constraints in generative models. Every transition is explicitly written out, as required by KTU valuation keys.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Demographic Parity Constraint as a Lagrangian Penalty

**Setup:** Consider a generator $G_\theta$ that produces outputs $x' = G_\theta(z)$ with $z \sim p(z)$. A downstream classifier $C_\psi$ produces predictions $\hat{Y} = C_\psi(x')$. We wish to enforce demographic parity.

**Step 1 — Define the base training objective.**

The standard maximum-likelihood objective for the generator is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{gen}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D_\phi(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p(z)}[\log(1 - D_\phi(G_\theta(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Express demographic parity as a constraint.**

Demographic parity requires that for every $a \in \mathcal{A}$:

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a) - P(\hat{Y} = +1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the conditional probability empirically.**

Using a generated batch of $M$ samples with $M_a$ belonging to group $a$:

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a) \approx \frac{1}{M_a} \sum_{i: A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = +1]$$

**Step 4 — Construct the augmented Lagrangian.**

The bias-penalized training objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{total}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{gen}(\theta) - \lambda \cdot \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left( \frac{1}{M_a} \sum_{i: A_i = a} \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = +1] - \bar{p} \right)^2$$

where $\bar{p} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \sum_a P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a)$ and $\lambda$ is the fairness-strength hyperparameter.

**Step 5 — Gradient update.**

The parameter update for the generator becomes:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \left[ \mathcal{L}_{gen}(\theta) - \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{fair}(\theta) \right]$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \left( \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{gen} - \lambda \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{fair} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Convergence condition.**

Training converges when both $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{gen} \approx 0$ and $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{fair} \approx 0$, meaning the generator simultaneously matches real data distribution and respects group-level parity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bias Amplification Factor (BAF)

**Step 1 — Define skew in real data.**

For a (group, context) pair $(a, c)$, define the co-occurrence skew in real data:

$$\text{Skew}_{real}(a, c) = \frac{P(A = a, C = c)}{P(A = a) \cdot P(C = c)}$$

**Step 2 — Define skew in generated data.**

$$\text{Skew}_{gen}(a, c) = \frac{P_{G}(A = a, C = c)}{P_{G}(A = a) \cdot P_{G}(C = c)}$$

where $P_{G}$ denotes the joint distribution measured on samples produced by $G_\theta$.

**Step 3 — Form the amplification ratio.**

$$\text{BAF}(a, c) = \frac{\text{Skew}_{gen}(a, c)}{\text{Skew}_{real}(a, c)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the BAF.**

- $\text{BAF} = 1$ → no amplification (ideal).
- $\text{BAF} > 1$ → generator amplifies the existing skew.
- $\text{BAF} < 1$ → generator *corrects* the skew (rare, but desirable for unprivileged groups).

**Step 5 — Worked Example (Numerical).**

Suppose in real COCO data, $\text{Skew}_{real}(\text{woman, kitchen}) = 1.20$. After training a Stable Diffusion model and sampling 10,000 images of "a person in a kitchen":

- Empirical joint: $P_{G}(\text{woman, kitchen}) = 0.18$
- Marginals: $P_{G}(\text{woman}) = 0.30$, $P_{G}(\text{kitchen}) = 0.50$

Compute:

$$\text{Skew}_{gen} = \frac{0.18}{0.30 \times 0.50} = \frac{0.18}{0.15} = 1.20$$

$$\text{BAF} = \frac{1.20}{1.20} = 1.00$$

No amplification. But if $\text{Skew}_{gen} = 1.80$, then $\text{BAF} = 1.50$ — a 50% amplification.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Counterfactual Fairness (Causal Formulation)

**Step 1 — Build the Structural Causal Model (SCM).**

Let $\mathcal{G} = (U, V, F)$ be the SCM with:
- $U$: exogenous noise (background factors).
- $V = \{A, X, Y\}$: endogenous variables (attribute, features, outcome).
- $F$: structural equations.

Structural equations:

$$A = f_A(U_A)$$
$$X = f_X(A, U_X)$$
$$Y = f_Y(A, X, U_Y)$$

**Step 2 — Counterfactual intervention.**

Set $A$ to value $a'$ via $do(A = a')$:

$$X_{A \leftarrow a'} = f_X(a', U_X)$$
$$Y_{A \leftarrow a'} = f_Y(a', X_{A \leftarrow a'}, U_Y)$$

**Step 3 — Fairness condition.**

$$\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U) \quad \forall a, a' \in \mathcal{A}$$

The outcome is invariant under counterfactual flipping of the protected attribute.

**Step 4 — Apply to a generative model.**

For a text-to-image model with prompt $p$, the counterfactual pair is:

- $p_1$: "A *female* doctor."
- $p_2$: "A *male* doctor."

If the generated images differ *only* in features causally linked to $A$ (e.g., facial features), the model is counterfactually fair. If they differ in *unrelated* features (clothing, posture, lighting), bias exists.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fairness Criteria vs. Use-Case Suitability

| Use Case | Best Fairness Criterion | Reason | Engineering Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Face generation (StyleGAN) | Demographic Parity | Equal representation matters | Medium |
| Resume screening (LLM) | Equal Opportunity | True positives (qualified) must be equal | High |
| Loan approval (GenAI docs) | Equalized Odds | Both TPR and FPR parity required | High |
| Recommendation systems | Individual Fairness | Similar users get similar recs | Low |
| Content moderation | Procedural Fairness | Process must be attribute-blind | Medium |
| Synthetic medical data | Counterfactual Fairness | Causal invariance critical | Very High |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem: Disparate Impact Calculation

**Problem:** A generative recruitment-AI model produces 1,000 CVs. Of these, 600 are classified as "shortlisted." Of 400 female-generated CVs, 200 are shortlisted. Of 600 male-generated CVs, 400 are shortlisted. Compute the Disparate Impact.

**Step 1 — Compute $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A = \text{female})$.**

$$P_{fem} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.50$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A = \text{male})$.**

$$P_{male} = \frac{400}{600} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DI formula.**

$$\text{DI} = \frac{P_{fem}}{P_{male}} = \frac{0.50}{0.667} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Verdict.**

Since $\text{DI} = 0.75 < 0.80$, the model **fails** the 80% rule → **legally and ethically non-compliant** under EEOC guidelines.

---

## 3.6 Bias Mitigation Strategy Derivation: Reweighting Approach

**Step 1 — Compute desired parity.**

For group pair $(a, a')$, we want:

$$\frac{\sum_{i: A_i=a} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_i = +1]}{\sum_{i: A_i=a} w_i} = \frac{\sum_{j: A_{j}=a'} w_j \cdot \mathbb{1}[\hat{Y}_j = +1]}{\sum_{j: A_j=a'} w_j}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for weights.**

$$w_i = \frac{P(A = a_i) \cdot P(Y = y_i)}{P(A = a_i, Y = y_i)} \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

This is the *Kamiran-Calders reweighting formula*.

**Step 3 — Update loss.**

$$\mathcal{L}_{reweighted} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \ell(x_i, y_i)$$

This forces the model to treat statistically equivalent groups identically.

---

## 3.7 Bias Amplification in Diffusion Models (Guidance-Wise)

**Step 1 — Classifier-free guidance formulation.**

The guided output is:

$$\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w) \cdot \epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w \cdot \epsilon_\theta(x_t)$$

where $w$ is the guidance weight, $c$ is the conditioning, and $\epsilon_\theta$ is the noise predictor.

**Step 2 — Conditional attribute probability under guidance.**

$$P_{\text{guided}}(A = a \mid c) \propto P(A = a \mid c) \cdot e^{w \cdot s(a, c)}$$

where $s(a, c)$ is the classifier logit alignment between attribute $a$ and condition $c$.

**Step 3 — Bias amplification as function of $w$.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w} \log \frac{P_{\text{guided}}(A = a_{priv} \mid c)}{P_{\text{guided}}(A = a_{unpriv} \mid c)} = s(a_{priv}, c) - s(a_{unpriv}, c) > 0$$

This proves **higher guidance weights → higher amplification**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid diagrams below use **alphanumeric prefixed node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **clean uppercase labels** to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 anti-parser-failure protocol.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sources of Bias in Generative AI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[SOCIETAL BIAS] --> node2[DATA COLLECTION BIAS]
    node2 --> node3[REPRESENTATION BIAS]
    node2 --> node4[MEASUREMENT BIAS]
    node3 --> node5[MODEL TRAINING BIAS]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6[GENERATIVE LEARNING BIAS]
    node6 --> node7[GAN MODE COLLAPSE]
    node6 --> node8[DIFFUSION GUIDANCE BIAS]
    node6 --> node9[VAE LATENT BIAS]
    node7 --> node10[DEPLOYMENT BIAS]
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[HARM AND DISCRIMINATION]
```

---

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Bias Mitigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[RAW DATASET] --> stepB[BALANCE AUDIT]
    stepB --> stepC{REPRESENTATION OK?}
    stepC -->|NO| stepD[RESAMPLE OR SYNTHESIZE]
    stepC -->|YES| stepE[DEBIAS EMBEDDINGS]
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[FAIR LOSS FUNCTION]
    stepF --> stepG[TRAIN GENERATOR]
    stepG --> stepH[BIAS METRIC EVALUATION]
    stepH --> stepI{MEETS THRESHOLD?}
    stepI -->|NO| stepJ[RETRAIN WITH HIGHER LAMBDA]
    stepI -->|YES| stepK[DEPLOY FAIR MODEL]
    stepJ --> stepG
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Fairness Criteria Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[FAIRNESS IN GENERATIVE AI] --> g1[GROUP FAIRNESS]
    root --> g2[INDIVIDUAL FAIRNESS]
    root --> g3[COUNTERFACTUAL FAIRNESS]
    g1 --> s1[DEMOGRAPHIC PARITY]
    g1 --> s2[EQUALIZED ODDS]
    g1 --> s3[EQUAL OPPORTUNITY]
    g2 --> s4[LIPSCHITZ SIMILARITY]
    g2 --> s5[FAIRNESS THROUGH AWARENESS]
    g3 --> s6[CAUSAL INVARIANCE]
    g3 --> s7[SCM BASED]
```

---

## 4.4 Flow Diagram: Bias Detection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp[GENERATED SAMPLES] --> pr1[ATTRIBUTE CLASSIFICATION]
    pr1 --> pr2[GROUP STATISTICS COMPUTATION]
    pr2 --> pr3[FAIRNESS METRIC EVALUATION]
    pr3 --> pr4[DI AND SPD CALCULATION]
    pr3 --> pr5[BIAS AMPLIFICATION FACTOR]
    pr3 --> pr6[KL DIVERGENCE ACROSS GROUPS]
    pr4 --> out{FAIR?}
    pr5 --> out
    pr6 --> out
    out -->|YES| pass[REPORT FAIR MODEL]
    out -->|NO| fail[TRIGGER MITIGATION]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Fairness Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ctrl[FAIRNESS CONTROLLER] --> mon[BIAS MONITOR]
    mon --> measure[COMPUTE SPD DI BAF]
    measure --> compare{CONSTRAINTS SATISFIED?}
    compare -->|YES| hold[MAINTAIN LAMBDA]
    compare -->|NO| adapt[INCREASE LAMBDA]
    adapt --> ctrl
    hold --> ctrl
    ctrl --> gen[GENERATOR G THETA]
    gen --> mon
```

---

## 4.6 Architecture Diagram: Fair Generative Model Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bottom[RAW DATA] --> layer1[DATA PREPROCESSING]
    layer1 --> layer2[BALANCED BATCH SAMPLER]
    layer2 --> layer3[FAIR LATENT SPACE LEARNING]
    layer3 --> layer4[REGULARIZED LOSS WITH FAIRNESS PENALTY]
    layer4 --> layer5[GUIDANCE WEIGHT CONTROLLER]
    layer5 --> layer6[GENERATIVE MODEL]
    layer6 --> layer7[BIAS POST PROCESSING FILTER]
    layer7 --> output[FAIR GENERATED OUTPUT]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define bias amplification in generative AI. Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Bias Amplification (BA)** is the phenomenon in which a generative model exaggerates the statistical skew present in its training data, producing outputs where the imbalance between privileged and unprivileged groups is *greater* in synthetic samples than in real samples.

**Quantitative Form:** $\text{BA} = \frac{\text{Skew}_{gen}(a,c)}{\text{Skew}_{real}(a,c)}$, where $a$ is a protected attribute and $c$ is a contextual attribute.

**Example:** A text-to-image model trained on COCO captions may generate *"a person cooking"* as predominantly female 78% of the time, even though real-world cooking imagery shows a roughly 50-50 gender distribution. Here, the model's bias amplification factor exceeds 1.5, perpetuating a stereotype.

**(3 marks: Definition 1, Formula 1, Example 1)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between Demographic Parity and Equalized Odds with a mathematical formulation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Demographic Parity | Equalized Odds |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | Equal *positive* prediction rates | Equal TPR and FPR |
| Requires Labels? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Formula | $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=+1)$ | $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid Y=y, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid Y=y)$ |
| Strength | Simple, label-free | Accounts for true outcomes |
| Weakness | Ignores base-rate differences | Requires accurate labels |

**Key Distinction:** Demographic parity is a *marginal* condition, while equalized odds is a *conditional* condition given the true outcome $Y$.

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for definition + 1 for distinction)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part B question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. KTU valuation allows the student to attempt **either** Question A or Question B, not both. The Examiner typically checks the *first attempted* option only.

---

### Question A: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **taxonomy of bias** in generative AI systems. Discuss the five major sources of bias with examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Bias Amplification Factor (BAF)** with a mathematical derivation. Compute BAF for a numerical case. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**1. Introduction to Bias Taxonomy**

Bias in generative AI systems is a **multi-stage phenomenon** that arises from the entire ML pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes five principal categories:

**2. Detailed Classification**

**(i) Historical Bias (1.5 marks)**
- Pre-exists in society and is *encoded* in the data collection process.
- Example: Historically, only ~10% of CEOs globally are women. An LLM trained on web text learns this skew and may associate "CEO" predominantly with male pronouns.

**(ii) Representation Bias (1.5 marks)**
- Quantitative imbalance in training data: $\rho_a = \frac{N_a}{N_{total}}$ deviates from $\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$.
- Example: LAION-5B contains ~1.2B images with English-language captions but only ~50M with Bengali captions, causing poor generative quality for non-Western cultures.

**(iii) Measurement Bias (1 mark)**
- Labels or features are measured with systematic error.
- Example: Emotion recognition systems underperform on darker-skinned faces because training datasets had imbalanced skin-tone labels.

**(iv) Algorithmic/Learning Bias (1.5 marks)**
- Induced by the loss function or optimization dynamics.
- Example: GAN mode collapse causes minority sub-distributions to be unrepresented in generated samples.

**(v) Generative-Specific Biases (1.5 marks)**
- Latent space entanglement, classifier-free guidance amplification, sampling temperature bias.
- Example: Increasing guidance weight $w$ in Stable Diffusion amplifies the "doctor = male" association by a measurable factor.

**3. Conclusion (Optional)**
Bias mitigation requires a *layered* approach addressing all five categories simultaneously.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**1. Definition of Bias Amplification (2 marks)**

Bias Amplification Factor (BAF) is the **ratio of co-occurrence skew in generated data to the corresponding skew in real data** for a (group, context) pair. It quantifies how much the generator *exaggerates* real-world statistical regularities.

**2. Mathematical Derivation (3 marks)**

**Step 1:** Define joint probability of (group $A = a$, context $C = c$) in real data:

$$P_{\text{real}}(A = a, C = c) = \frac{N_{a,c}^{\text{real}}}{N_{\text{total}}^{\text{real}}}$$

**Step 2:** Define skew in real data:

$$\text{Skew}_{\text{real}}(a, c) = \frac{P_{\text{real}}(A=a, C=c)}{P_{\text{real}}(A=a) \cdot P_{\text{real}}(C=c)}$$

**Step 3:** Analogously, compute skew in generated data from $G_\theta$:

$$\text{Skew}_{\text{gen}}(a, c) = \frac{P_{G}(A=a, C=c)}{P_{G}(A=a) \cdot P_{G}(C=c)}$$

**Step 4:** Form the BAF:

$$\boxed{\text{BAF}(a, c) = \frac{\text{Skew}_{\text{gen}}(a, c)}{\text{Skew}_{\text{real}}(a, c)}}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- $\text{BAF} = 1$: No amplification.
- $\text{BAF} > 1$: Amplification (skew worsened).
- $\text{BAF} < 1$: Correction (skew reduced).

**3. Numerical Worked Example (2 marks)**

**Given:** In a real dataset, $P(\text{female, doctor}) = 0.10$, $P(\text{female}) = 0.50$, $P(\text{doctor}) = 0.20$.

**Compute real skew:**

$$\text{Skew}_{\text{real}} = \frac{0.10}{0.50 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.10}{0.10} = 1.00$$

**Generated data:** $P_{G}(\text{female, doctor}) = 0.04$, $P_{G}(\text{female}) = 0.50$, $P_{G}(\text{doctor}) = 0.20$.

**Compute generated skew:**

$$\text{Skew}_{\text{gen}} = \frac{0.04}{0.50 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.04}{0.10} = 0.40$$

**Compute BAF:**

$$\text{BAF} = \frac{0.40}{1.00} = 0.40$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{BAF} = 0.40 < 1$ means the generator has *under*-represented the female-doctor association by 60% — a *de-biasing* effect (which is desirable in this case).

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **fairness criteria** used in generative AI systems. Discuss Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, and Counterfactual Fairness with mathematical formulations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Impossibility Theorem** of fairness. How does this impact real-world generative AI deployments? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**1. Need for Fairness Criteria (1 mark)**

Generative models can perpetuate or amplify social biases. **Fairness criteria** provide a formal, mathematical way to define and enforce equitable behavior. There is **no single universally correct definition**; choice depends on application context.

**2. Demographic Parity (2 marks)**

**Definition:** The probability of a favorable generated outcome must be **independent** of the protected attribute.

**Formula:**

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = +1) \quad \forall a \in \mathcal{A}$$

**Example:** In a text-to-image model generating "a successful entrepreneur," demographic parity requires that the fraction of generated images depicting women equals the fraction depicting men (i.e., 50% each).

**Strength:** Simple, label-free, easy to audit.
**Weakness:** Ignores base-rate differences between groups.

**3. Equalized Odds (2 marks)**

**Definition:** The **true positive rate** and **false positive rate** must be equal across groups.

**Formula:**

$$P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = y, A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = +1 \mid Y = y) \quad \forall a, y$$

**Example:** In a resume-screening GenAI, qualified male and qualified female candidates must be shortlisted at the *same rate*.

**Strength:** Accounts for actual outcomes, more nuanced.
**Weakness:** Requires accurate ground-truth labels.

**4. Counterfactual Fairness (2 marks)**

**Definition:** An outcome is fair if it remains unchanged in a **counterfactual world** where the protected attribute were different.

**Formula:**

$$\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U) \quad \forall a, a' \in \mathcal{A}$$

where $U$ are exogenous background factors.

**Example:** Generate images for "a doctor" and "a female doctor" — the only difference should be in features causally linked to gender, not in unrelated features (clothing, posture, setting).

**Strength:** Causally robust, philosophically grounded.
**Weakness:** Requires a fully specified causal model — often impractical.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**1. Statement of the Impossibility Theorem (2.5 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Fairness Impossibility Theorem** (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan 2016) states that **it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy** all three of the following fairness criteria *unless* the base rates $P(Y=+1 \mid A=a)$ are exactly equal across groups:
> 1. Demographic Parity
> 2. Equalized Odds (or Equal Opportunity)
> 3. Predictive Parity (calibration)

**2. Mathematical Sketch (2.5 marks)**

Suppose $\hat{Y}$ is a binary predictor and the base rates differ:
$$P(Y=+1 \mid A = a_1) = p_1 \quad ; \quad P(Y=+1 \mid A = a_2) = p_2 \quad ; \quad p_1 \neq p_2$$

If Equalized Odds holds (i.e., TPR and FPR are equal across groups), then the positive prediction rates must differ:

$$P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a) = \text{TPR} \cdot p_a + \text{FPR} \cdot (1 - p_a)$$

Since $p_1 \neq p_2$, we have $P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_1) \neq P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_2)$, **violating Demographic Parity**.

Conversely, enforcing Demographic Parity forces unequal TPR or FPR, **violating Equalized Odds**.

**3. Real-World Engineering Impact (2 marks)**

- **Trade-off awareness:** Practitioners must *choose* which fairness criterion is most important for their domain (e.g., Equal Opportunity for hiring, Demographic Parity for face generation).
- **Legal vs. ethical alignment:** EEOC uses 80% rule (DI ≥ 0.8) — a Demographic Parity proxy. NIST and EU AI Act may use different standards.
- **Product design:** Teams must document the *choice* of fairness metric and justify it.
- **No "fair" model exists** — only models with *explicit*, *transparent* fairness trade-offs.

---

## Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PCCMT602 Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing Demographic Parity with Equal Opportunity** — many students write the same formula for both. Remember: DP ignores $Y$, EO conditions on $Y=+1$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Impossibility Theorem** — 14-mark answers that enumerate fairness criteria *without* acknowledging the trade-offs lose 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Disparate Impact and Statistical Parity Difference** — DI is a *ratio*; SPD is a *difference*. The 80% rule applies to DI, not SPD.
> 4. **Omitting quantitative worked examples** — KTU valuation explicitly awards 1-2 marks for numerical computation. Always include at least one worked numerical problem in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Not distinguishing bias *sources* from bias *types*** — Sources = where bias enters (data, model, sampling). Types = how bias manifests (amplification, mode collapse, entanglement).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Bias** = systematic, repeatable error favoring one group over another.
- ✅ **Fairness** = the formal, mathematical property of non-discrimination.
- ✅ **5 sources of bias:** Historical, Representation, Measurement, Algorithmic, Generative-specific.
- ✅ **6 fairness criteria:** Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, Equal Opportunity, Counterfactual, Individual, Procedural.
- ✅ **BAF formula:** $\text{BAF} = \frac{\text{Skew}_{gen}(a,c)}{\text{Skew}_{real}(a,c)}$
- ✅ **Disparate Impact formula:** $\text{DI} = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y}=+1 \mid A=a_{priv})}$; threshold = **0.8**.
- ✅ **SPD threshold:** typically $\leq 0.1$.
- ✅ **Impossibility Theorem:** DP + EO + Predictive Parity cannot all hold unless base rates are equal.
- ✅ **80% Rule** is the legal/regulatory benchmark for disparate impact.
- ✅ **Bias amplification in diffusion models** scales with classifier-free guidance weight $w$.
- ✅ **GAN mode collapse** is a generative-specific bias that erases minority sub-distributions.
- ✅ **Latent space entanglement** causes protected attributes to be statistically coupled with unrelated semantic features.
- ✅ **Counterfactual fairness** requires a complete SCM — often impractical in real deployment.
- ✅ **Mitigation hierarchy:** Data-level → Loss-level → Latent-level → Post-hoc filtering.
- ✅ **Reweighting formula (Kamiran-Calders):** $w_i = \frac{P(A=a_i) \cdot P(Y=y_i)}{P(A=a_i, Y=y_i)} \cdot \frac{1}{N}$
- ✅ **Engineering reality:** *Every* fairness choice is a trade-off — document it transparently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models** refers to the systematic practice of designing, developing, integrating, operating, and retiring generative AI systems (such as Large Language Models, Diffusion Models, and GANs) in a manner that is **ethically aligned, legally compliant, socially beneficial, and technically robust**. As per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) outcomes for PCCMT602, this discipline encompasses the governance frameworks, technical safeguards, risk mitigation strategies, and evaluation protocols required to ensure that generative AI does not cause unintended harm to individuals, communities, or societal structures.

The discipline operates across four intersecting pillars:
1. **Ethical Alignment** — ensuring outputs respect human values, dignity, and rights.
2. **Technical Robustness** — guaranteeing reliability, accuracy, and resistance to adversarial manipulation.
3. **Legal & Regulatory Compliance** — adhering to data protection laws, copyright statutes, and emerging AI-specific regulations.
4. **Societal Beneficence** — maximizing positive impact while minimizing displacement, misinformation, and exclusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 framework explicitly mandates that students understand *not just how generative models function, but the governance, safety, and ethical scaffolding required to deploy them in real-world engineering contexts.* Questions frequently appear on bias auditing, hallucination mitigation, and deployment risk frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Generative Model as a **newly invented super-powerful industrial machine** — say, a 3D molecular printer that can synthesize any chemical compound on demand. The machine itself is technologically brilliant, but its *responsible use* depends entirely on the surrounding ecosystem:

| Real-World Machine Analogy | Generative AI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Industrial printer | Generative Model (LLM/Diffusion/GAN) |
| Operator training & license | Model alignment, RLHF, safety fine-tuning |
| Ventilation & containment | Output filters, guardrails, content moderation |
| Toxic waste disposal protocol | Bias auditing, hallucination detection, PII redaction |
| Government safety inspection | AI regulatory audits (e.g., EU AI Act) |
| Emergency shutdown button | Red-teaming, kill-switch, model rollback |
| Public awareness labels | Model cards, system cards, datasheets |

Just as society does not permit the deployment of a chemical synthesizer without safety protocols, **no responsible engineer deploys a generative model without a corresponding governance stack.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Hallucination:** A *hallucination* is a confident, fluent, but factually incorrect or fabricated output produced by a generative model. It is the single most cited failure mode in deployed LLM systems and is a primary focus of KTU Module 1 evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Alignment:** *Alignment* is the process of ensuring that a model's behavior and outputs are consistent with human intentions, values, and ethical principles. Techniques include Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), Constitutional AI, and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).

## 1.3 Foundational Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Perplexity (PPL):** Standard measure of how well a probability model predicts a sample. Lower is better.
- **BLEU / ROUGE scores:** Quantitative metrics for text generation quality and reference similarity.
- **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR):** Legal fairness metric comparing selection rates across demographic groups.
- **Hallucination Rate:** Fraction of generated outputs containing fabricated or unsupported claims.
- **Toxicity Score:** Probability-weighted measure of harmful, offensive, or biased content in outputs.
- **Toxicity Threshold (commonly τ = 0.5):** Industry-standard cutoff above which content is flagged for human review.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Responsible Generative AI Deployment

A robust deployment framework rests on **four operational pillars**. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate and explain each.

### Pillar 1: Fairness and Bias Mitigation
Generative models inherit and amplify biases from training data. Responsible deployment requires:
- **Pre-processing:** Rebalancing training corpora, removing demographic proxies.
- **In-processing:** Adversarial debiasing, fairness-constrained loss functions.
- **Post-processing:** Output filtering, re-ranking by fairness scores.

### Pillar 2: Transparency and Explainability
Stakeholders must understand, at minimum, *what the model does, what data it was trained on, and what its known limitations are*. Mechanisms include:
- **Model Cards** (Mitchell et al., 2019) — structured documentation of intended use, performance, and ethical considerations.
- **System Cards** — operational documentation for end-to-end AI systems.
- **Datasheets for Datasets** (Gebru et al.) — provenance and characteristics of training data.

### Pillar 3: Privacy and Data Protection
Generative models can memorize and regurgitate training data, creating privacy vulnerabilities:
- **Memorization attacks** can extract verbatim PII (Personally Identifiable Information).
- Mitigation: differential privacy, federated learning, output scrubbing, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to decouple memory from parametric storage.

### Pillar 4: Accountability and Governance
- Establish clear **ownership chains** (data curators, model trainers, deployers, end-users).
- Maintain **audit trails** for inputs, outputs, and overrides.
- Implement **human-in-the-loop (HITL)** checkpoints for high-stakes decisions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Range / Threshold | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i \vert x_{<i})\right)$ | Lower is better; $\text{PPL} \geq 1$ | Language model fluency evaluation |
| **Demographic Parity** | $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \vert \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon = 0.05$ (industry) | Group fairness check |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{minority})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{majority})}$ | $0.8 \leq \text{DIR} \leq 1.25$ | Legal compliance (EEOC 4/5 rule) |
| **Equalized Odds** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a, Y=y) \text{ equal } \forall a$ | Difference $\leq 0.1$ | Equal TPR and FPR across groups |
| **Hallucination Rate** | $H = \frac{\text{Number of unsupported claims}}{\text{Total claims}}$ | $H \leq 0.05$ for production | Factual reliability check |
