<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence. State any two of its key mathematical properties. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, PCCMT602]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
KL Divergence is an information-theoretic, asymmetric measure of the difference between a true distribution $P(x)$ and an approximating distribution $Q(x)$. For discrete variables:

$$D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) \;=\; \sum_{x} P(x) \log\!\left(\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right)$$

**Two Properties (1 Mark — ½ Mark each):**

1. **Non-negativity (Gibbs' Inequality):** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$, with equality if and only if $P = Q$ almost everywhere.
2. **Asymmetry:** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)$ in general, hence it is *not* a true distance metric.

---

### Q2. Show that minimising KL Divergence is equivalent to minimising Cross-Entropy when the true distribution $P$ is fixed. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, PCCMT602]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

We start from the cross-entropy definition $H(P, Q) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log Q(x)$ and KL Divergence $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \log P(x) - \sum_{x} P(x) \log Q(x)$. 

Rewriting:

$$D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) \;=\; H(P, Q) - H(P)$$

Since $H(P) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log P(x)$ depends **only on the data** and is constant w.r.t. model parameters, the minimisation problems $\min_Q D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ and $\min_Q H(P, Q)$ share the **same optimum** $Q^{*}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 📝 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `Choose either A or B`

#### Q.A (a) Derive the closed-form expression for KL Divergence between two univariate Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^{2})$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^{2})$. Discuss its role in the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) loss. `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Write the Gaussian densities:**

$$p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_1)^{2}}{2\sigma_1^{2}}\right), \quad q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_2)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Form the log-ratio:**

$$\log\frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \;=\; \log\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} - \frac{(x-\mu_1)^{2}}{2\sigma_1^{2}} + \frac{(x-\mu_2)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks) — Take the expectation under $p(x)$:** Using $\mathbb{E}_p[X] = \mu_1$ and $\mathbb{E}_p[(X-\mu_1)^{2}] = \sigma_1^{2}$:

$$\mathbb{E}_p\!\left[\frac{(X-\mu_1)^{2}}{2\sigma_1^{2}}\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{2}, \qquad \mathbb{E}_p\!\left[(X-\mu_2)^{2}\right] \;=\; \sigma_1^{2} + (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^{2}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks) — Assemble the closed form:**

$$D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^{2}) \,\Vert\, \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^{2})\right) \;=\; \log\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} + \frac{\sigma_1^{2} + (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}} - \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Role in VAE Loss:** In a VAE, the encoder outputs $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$ parameterising $q_\phi(z \vert x)$. The KL term in the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) penalises deviation from the standard normal prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. The total VAE loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} \;=\; \underbrace{-\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction Loss}} \;+\; \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z \vert x) \,\Vert\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regulariser}}$$

Minimising this KL forces the latent representation to remain close to a tractable prior, enabling generation by ancestral sampling.

---

#### Q.A (b) Compute $D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ in **nats** for $P = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$ and $Q = (0.4, 0.4, 0.2)$. Verify Gibbs' inequality. `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Sanity-Check Normalisation:** $\sum P_i = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓; $\sum Q_i = 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Compute each pointwise ratio:**

$$\frac{P_1}{Q_1} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25, \quad \frac{P_2}{Q_2} = \frac{0.3}{0.4} = 0.75, \quad \frac{P_3}{Q_3} = \frac{0.2}{0.2} = 1.0$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Apply natural logarithms:**

$$\ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231, \quad \ln(0.75) \approx -0.2877, \quad \ln(1.0) = 0$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Multiply by $P_i$ and sum:**

$$D_{KL} = 0.5(0.2231) + 0.3(-0.2877) + 0.2(0) = 0.1116 - 0.0863 + 0 = 0.0253 \text{ nats}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Verify Gibbs' Inequality:** The result $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \approx 0.0253 > 0$, which is **strictly positive because $P \neq Q$**. This confirms the non-negativity property.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q.B (a) Explain the concept of Forward KL $D_{KL}(P \| Q)$ and Reverse KL $D_{KL}(Q \| P)$. Illustrate with a bimodal target distribution. Why is the choice of direction critical in generative modelling? `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Define both directions:**
- **Forward KL:** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum P(x) \log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ — penalises $Q$ being small wherever $P$ is non-zero. Forces $Q$ to **cover all modes** of $P$ ("mass-covering" or "zero-avoiding").
- **Reverse KL:** $D_{KL}(Q \| P) = \sum Q(x) \log\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}$ — penalises $Q$ placing mass where $P$ is small. Encourages $Q$ to **lock onto a single mode** ("mode-seeking").

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Bimodal Illustration:**
Let $P$ be a mixture of two Gaussians centred at $-3$ and $+3$. 
- **Minimising $D_{KL}(P \| Q)$** ⇒ $Q$ spreads out, placing density under *both* peaks (e.g., a wide single Gaussian). 
- **Minimising $D_{KL}(Q \| P)$** ⇒ $Q$ collapses onto *one* of the two peaks (e.g., a sharp Gaussian near $+3$).

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Generative Modelling Implications:**
- **VAEs** use the *forward* direction (relative to data) — i.e., $D_{KL}(q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z))$ — promoting diverse but possibly blurry samples.
- **GANs / implicit generative models** can be interpreted as minimising a *reverse* KL or its f-divergence variants — yielding sharper but potentially mode-collapsed samples.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Conclusion:** The direction of KL is a **modelling design decision** that trades off *sample diversity* against *sample fidelity*.

---

#### Q.B (b) State and prove Gibbs' inequality: $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$, with equality if and only if $P = Q$. What is the significance of this inequality in the training of Generative AI models? `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 Mark):** For any two probability distributions $P$ and $Q$ defined on the same support, $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$, with equality iff $P = Q$ almost everywhere.

**Proof (4 Marks):** Starting from the definition:

$$D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}$$

Apply **Jensen's inequality** to the convex function $-\log$:

$$-\sum_{x} P(x) \log\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \;\ge\; -\log\!\left(\sum_{x} P(x) \cdot \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}\right) = -\log\!\left(\sum_{x} Q(x)\right) = -\log(1) = 0$$

Hence $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0$. Equality requires $Q(x)/P(x) = c$ almost surely; with both summing to 1, $c = 1$, giving $P = Q$.

**Significance in Generative AI (2 Marks):**
1. **Loss Validity:** Guarantees the loss is non-negative, so a model can be meaningfully optimised toward a *zero* target.
2. **Identifiability:** A zero loss is *achieved only* when the model perfectly matches the data — a clean stopping criterion.
3. **Foundation for ELBO:** In VAEs, $D_{KL}$ non-negativity underpins the variational lower bound on log-likelihood.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KL Divergence Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the asymmetry** — Students write $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = D_{KL}(Q \| P)$. This is **false** and the KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating KL with cross-entropy** — In derivations, students often skip the $H(P)$ term, leading to incorrect sign. Always show the full identity $D_{KL} = H(P, Q) - H(P)$.
> 3. **Wrong base of logarithm** — When the question specifies "bits", use $\log_2$; for "nats", use $\ln$. Mixing them leads to unit errors.
> 4. **Improper handling of zero probability** — The convention $0 \log(0/0) = 0$ must be **explicitly mentioned** in derivations.
> 5. **Skipping the equality condition** in Gibbs' inequality proof — Examiners expect the "iff $P = Q$" clause; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Gaussian KL closed-form** — Students often miss the constant $-\frac{1}{2}$ term; a partial formula loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KL Divergence (Module 2 — Generative AI)**
> 
> - ✅ **Definition:** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_x P(x) \log\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ — discrete; replace $\sum$ with $\int$ for continuous.
> - ✅ **Asymmetry:** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)$ in general — *not a metric*.
> - ✅ **Non-Negativity:** $D_{KL} \ge 0$, equality iff $P = Q$ a.e. (Gibbs' Inequality — proven via Jensen's).
> - ✅ **Cross-Entropy Link:** $D_{KL}(P \| Q) = H(P, Q) - H(P)$; thus minimising KL ⇔ minimising cross-entropy (for fixed $P$).
> - ✅ **Units:** **nats** if $\ln$; **bits** if $\log_2$.
> - ✅ **Forward KL** = mass-covering (VAEs); **Reverse KL** = mode-seeking (implicit models).
> - ✅ **Gaussian Closed Form:** $D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^{2}) \| \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^{2})\right) = \log\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} + \frac{\sigma_1^{2} + (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}} - \frac{1}{2}$ — used per-dim in VAE loss.
> - ✅ **VAE Loss:** $\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = \text{Reconstruction Loss} + D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z)\right)$.
> - ✅ **ELBO:** Maximising the Evidence Lower Bound ⇔ Minimising KL between approximate and true posterior.
> - ✅ **Information-Theoretic Meaning:** Expected extra number of bits/nats needed to encode samples from $P$ using a code optimised for $Q$.
> - ✅ **Convention:** $0 \log(0/0) = 0$; $P(x) > 0, Q(x) = 0 \Rightarrow D_{KL} = +\infty$.
> - ✅ **Connection to KL-Conditional:** $D_{KL}(q(z|x) \| p(z))$ is the per-sample regulariser in VAEs.
> - ✅ **Jensen's Inequality:** Convexity of $-\log$ is the workhorse behind non-negativity proofs.
> - ✅ **Higher-Order Use:** KL generalises to **f-divergences** and **Jensen-Shannon divergence** (symmetric variant used in GAN training).
> - ✅ **Practical Trick:** When training, always clip $Q(x)$ away from 0 (e.g., $Q(x) + \varepsilon$) to avoid $\log(0) = -\infty$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generative Adversarial Network: Introduction to GANs, The Discriminator, The Generator, Training the GAN, GAN Challenges, Oscillating Loss, Mode Collapse, Uninformative Loss, Hyperparameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generative Adversarial Network: Introduction to GANs, The Discriminator, The Generator, Training the GAN, GAN Challenges, Oscillating Loss, Mode Collapse, Uninformative Loss, Hyperparameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a class of deep learning framework introduced by **Ian Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014**, in which two neural networks — a **Generator ($G$)** and a **Discriminator ($D$)** — are trained simultaneously in a competitive, zero-sum game. The Generator learns to produce synthetic data samples that mimic the real data distribution, while the Discriminator learns to distinguish between real samples drawn from the training dataset and fake samples produced by the Generator. The two networks are locked in an adversarial min–max optimization that drives both to improve iteratively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A GAN is a *generative* model (as opposed to a *discriminative* model). It learns the underlying data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ implicitly, without ever needing an explicit likelihood function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter and the Police**
> Imagine a **counterfeiter** (the Generator) who tries to forge currency, and a **police detective** (the Discriminator) who tries to tell real notes from fake ones.
> - Initially, the counterfeit's work is crude → the detective easily catches it.
> - The counterfeiter studies the detective's feedback and improves the forgery.
> - The detective, in turn, sharpens his skill as fakes get better.
> - Over time, the counterfeit's notes become indistinguishable from real currency.
> In GAN terms, "indistinguishable" means the Discriminator's output probability $D(x)$ converges to **0.5** (pure guess).

## 1.2 The Discriminator ($D$)

The **Discriminator** $D(x; \theta_d)$ is a binary classifier (typically a Convolutional Neural Network for images) that takes an input $x$ and outputs a scalar probability representing the likelihood that $x$ is a real sample (drawn from $p_{\text{data}}$) rather than a fake sample (drawn from the Generator's distribution $p_g$).

$$D(x) = P(\text{real} \mid x) \in [0, 1]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The Discriminator is the *critic* — its job is **NOT to generate** anything, only to **judge**. During training, it is shown two kinds of inputs:
> 1. **Real samples** $x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)$ → label = **1**
> 2. **Fake samples** $G(z), \; z \sim p_z(z)$ → label = **0**
> It is trained using standard **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** loss.

## 1.3 The Generator ($G$)

The **Generator** $G(z; \theta_g)$ is a neural network (often a transposed-CNN / deconvolution network) that maps a random noise vector $z$, sampled from a simple prior distribution (typically a uniform or standard Gaussian $p_z(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$), into a synthetic data sample $G(z)$ living in the same space as the real data.

$$G: z \mapsto x_{\text{fake}}, \quad z \in \mathbb{R}^d \sim p_z(z)$$

The Generator's goal is to **fool** the Discriminator, i.e., to make $D(G(z))$ as close to **1** as possible, even though the sample is fake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The Generator is the *artist*. It starts by producing random noise-shaped images and, driven by gradients flowing back from the Discriminator, slowly learns to sculpt outputs that the Discriminator can no longer differentiate from real.

## 1.4 Training the GAN — The Adversarial Loop

The training procedure alternates between updating $D$ and updating $G$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step A — Freeze $G$, update $D$:** Feed a batch of real samples and a batch of $G(z)$. Train $D$ to classify them correctly.
>
> **Step B — Freeze $D$, update $G$:** Generate $G(z)$, pass through $D$, and update $G$ to push $D(G(z))$ toward **1** (using $-\log D(G(z))$ rather than $\log(1-D(G(z))$ for stronger gradients early on).
>
> **Step C — Repeat** for many iterations until $D(x) \approx 0.5$ globally.

The classical objective is the **value function** $V(D, G)$:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

## 1.5 GAN Challenges (Overview)

| Challenge | Symptom |
|---|---|
| **Oscillating Loss** | Loss curves zig-zag without convergence |
| **Mode Collapse** | $G$ outputs nearly identical samples |
| **Uninformative Loss** | Loss values do not reflect sample quality |
| **Hyperparameter Sensitivity** | Performance heavily depends on $lr$, $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$ |

These will be analyzed rigorously in the next sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Min–Max Objective Function (Value Function)

The cornerstone of GAN theory is the **value function** $V(D, G)$ proposed by Goodfellow et al. (2014):

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- The **Discriminator** wants to **maximize** this expression → it should assign $D(x) \to 1$ for real and $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fake.
- The **Generator** wants to **minimize** this expression → it should make $D(G(z)) \to 1$, so the second term $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \to -\infty$.

This is a **two-player zero-sum game** with no closed-form equilibrium solution for arbitrary neural networks, but a *theoretical* equilibrium exists at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

## 2.2 Discriminator Update — Step by Step

For a **fixed** $G$, the optimal $D$ is found by maximizing $V(D, G)$ w.r.t. $D$ pointwise:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx$$

Differentiating the integrand w.r.t. $D(x)$ and setting it to zero yields the **optimal discriminator** (derived fully in Section 3):

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

At global equilibrium $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, we get $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ — the Discriminator becomes a random guesser, which is the operational definition of a successfully trained GAN.

## 2.3 Generator Update — Why $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ Fails Early

In early training, $D$ easily rejects the Generator's poor samples, so $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ and $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ **saturates** (its gradient w.r.t. $G$'s parameters is vanishingly small). Goodfellow's practical fix: train $G$ to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why you will see $-\log(D(G(z)))$ written as the **Generator's loss** in most modern PyTorch / TensorFlow GAN implementations.

## 2.4 Hyperparameters

GANs are notoriously sensitive to hyperparameter selection. The table below summarizes the key hyperparameters and typical KTU / industry-standard ranges.

| Hyperparameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Role / Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generator learning rate | $\eta_G$ | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | Step size for $G$'s optimizer |
| Discriminator learning rate | $\eta_D$ | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | Step size for $D$'s optimizer |
| Batch size | $B$ | $64, 128, 256$ | Affects gradient noise & stability |
| Latent dimension | $d_z$ | $100, 128, 256$ | Dimensionality of noise vector $z$ |
| Adam $\beta_1$ | $\beta_1$ | $0.0$ to $0.9$ | First-momentum decay; **$0.0$ recommended for GANs** (Radford et al., 2015 DCGAN) |
| Adam $\beta_2$ | $\beta_2$ | $0.9$ to $0.999$ | Second-momentum decay |
| Discriminator updates per $G$ update | $k$ | $1$ to $5$ | More $D$ updates → stronger critic; can cause $G$ collapse |
| Label smoothing | $\epsilon$ | $0.0$ to $0.1$ | Replaces hard $1.0$ with $0.9$ for real labels → regularizes $D$ |
| Epochs | $E$ | $50$ to $300+$ | Training duration |
| Activation (Generator output) | — | `tanh` | Pixels scaled to $[-1, 1]$ |
| Activation (Discriminator) | — | `LeakyReLU(0.2)` | Prevents dying ReLU in $D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** $\beta_1 = 0$ (instead of the default $0.9$) was found empirically by Radford et al. (DCGAN, 2015) to dramatically stabilize GAN training. This is a frequent **2-mark viva question**.

## 2.5 Loss Variants Used in Practice

| Loss Name | $D$ Update | $G$ Update |
|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla GAN (min–max)** | $\max \log D(x) + \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ | $\min \log(1 - D(G(z)))$ |
| **Non-saturating GAN** | Same as above | $\max \log D(G(z))$ |
| **Least-Squares GAN (LSGAN)** | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}(D(x) - 1)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}D(G(z))^2$ | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}(D(G(z)) - 1)^2$ |
| **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** | $\max D(x) - D(G(z))$ | $\max D(G(z))$ |
| **Hinge-loss GAN** | $\max \min(0, 1 - D(x)) - D(G(z))$ | $\max D(G(z))$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

GANs power **deepfakes, super-resolution, image-to-image translation (Pix2Pix, CycleGAN), medical image synthesis, data augmentation for low-resource datasets, drug discovery, and 3D shape generation** — making this topic a high-yield area for both KTU exams and industry interviews.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$

**Given:** For a fixed Generator $G$, the Discriminator maximizes:

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 1 — Rewrite the expectation over $x$ instead of $z$.**

Since $G$ is a deterministic function, the change-of-variables formula gives a *pushforward* distribution $p_g(x)$ over the data space induced by $z \sim p_z$. The second expectation becomes an integral over $x$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx$$

**Step 2 — Combine the integrals into a single integrand.**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \Big[\, p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \,\Big] dx$$

**Step 3 — Maximize pointwise.** Since the integrand depends on $D(x)$ independently at every $x$, we can maximize the integrand for each $x$ separately. Define:

$$f(D) = p_{\text{data}} \log D + p_g \log(1 - D)$$

Dropping the $(x)$ dependence for clarity.

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $D(x)$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} - \frac{p_g}{1 - D} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically.**

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} = \frac{p_g}{1 - D}$$

$$p_{\text{data}}(1 - D) = p_g \cdot D$$

$$p_{\text{data}} - p_{\text{data}} D = p_g D$$

$$p_{\text{data}} = D \left( p_{\text{data}} + p_g \right)$$

$$\boxed{\,D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Verify it's a maximum (second derivative).**

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial D^2} = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D^2} - \frac{p_g}{(1 - D)^2} < 0$$

The second derivative is strictly negative, confirming that $D^*$ is indeed a **maximum**. ✓

## 3.2 Value of $V$ at the Optimal Discriminator

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*$ back into $V$.**

$$V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $\log 2$ to expose KL divergences.**

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{2 \cdot p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{2 \cdot p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Jenson–Shannon Divergence (JSD).**

The JSD between two distributions $P$ and $Q$ is:

$$\text{JSD}(\mathbb{P} \,\|\, \mathbb{Q}) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \text{KL}(\mathbb{P} \,\|\, \mathbb{M}) + \text{KL}(\mathbb{Q} \,\|\, \mathbb{M}) \right)$$

where $\mathbb{M} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbb{P} + \mathbb{Q})$.

Therefore:

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.** The Generator's objective reduces to **minimizing $2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$**, which is non-negative and equals zero **iff** $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$.

$$\boxed{\,\min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{achieved when} \quad p_g = p_{\text{data}}\,}$$

This is a foundational result: the global optimum of the GAN game corresponds to a JSD of **zero** between the data and generator distributions.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Suppose $p_{\text{data}}(x) = 0.7$ and $p_g(x) = 0.3$ at some point $x_0$. Compute $D^*(x_0)$ and the contribution to $V$ from $x_0$.

**Solution:**

$$D^*(x_0) = \frac{0.7}{0.7 + 0.3} = \frac{0.7}{1.0} = 0.7$$

$$V \text{ contribution} = 0.7 \log(0.7) + 0.3 \log(1 - 0.7) = 0.7 \log(0.7) + 0.3 \log(0.3)$$

$$= 0.7 \times (-0.1549) + 0.3 \times (-0.5229)$$

$$= -0.1084 - 0.1569 = -0.2653$$

The negative value is expected because $-\log 4 \approx -1.386$ is the maximum possible (i.e., least negative) value of $V$ across all $x$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Behavior Diagnosis

| Symptom | Loss Behavior | Likely Root Cause | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oscillating Loss** | $D$ and $G$ losses zig-zag | Imbalance in capacity / learning rates | Use **TTUR** (Two-Time-Scale Update Rule) |
| **Mode Collapse** | $G$ loss drops, sample diversity collapses | $D$ too weak, $G$ finds one "trick" | Use **minibatch discrimination** or **unrolled GANs** |
| **Uninformative Loss** | $D$ loss stuck at $\log 2 \approx 0.693$ | $D$ cannot distinguish $p_g$ from $p_{\text{data}}$ | Check data preprocessing, scale, normalize |
| **D loss → 0** | $D$ overpowers $G$ | $D$ too strong / $G$ too weak | Reduce $\eta_D$, add label noise |
| **G loss → 0** | $G$ fools $D$ trivially | $G$ collapsed to one mode | Restart from checkpoint, use WGAN-GP |

## 3.5 Hyperparameter Sensitivity — Derivation Insight

The standard GAN update uses **Adam** with two momentum terms. For the **Generator's** parameters $\theta_g$:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_g^{(t)} &= \beta_1 \, m_g^{(t-1)} + (1 - \beta_1) \, \nabla_{\theta_g} V \\
v_g^{(t)} &= \beta_2 \, v_g^{(t-1)} + (1 - \beta_2) \left( \nabla_{\theta_g} V \right)^2 \\
\theta_g^{(t+1)} &= \theta_g^{(t)} - \eta_G \cdot \frac{m_g^{(t)}}{\sqrt{v_g^{(t)}} + \epsilon}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\beta_1 = 0$, the momentum term $m_g^{(t)}$ reduces to the **raw gradient** at the current step, which behaves like **SGD with adaptive per-parameter learning rates**. This is empirically more stable for GANs because it prevents the discriminator from "running away" via accumulated momentum.

The choice of $\beta_1 = 0$ is the single most impactful GAN hyperparameter change, and is routinely tested in KTU vivas.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAN Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Noise prior pz z uniform or normal"] --> nodeB["Generator G z transposed CNN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Fake sample Gz"]
    nodeD["Real data distribution pdata x"] --> nodeE["Real sample x"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Discriminator D CNN"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Probability Dx or DGz 0 to 1"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Binary cross entropy loss"]
    nodeH -->|backprop into D| nodeF
    nodeH -->|backprop into G via non saturating loss| nodeB

    classDef gen fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef dis fill:#a8d8ff,stroke:#003d80,color:#000
    classDef dat fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
    classDef los fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#800000,color:#000
    class nodeA gen
    class nodeB gen
    class nodeC gen
    class nodeD dat
    class nodeE dat
    class nodeF dis
    class nodeG los
    class nodeH los
```

## 4.2 Training Loop — Iterative Update Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1 Sample mini batch of real x from pdata"] --> step2["Step 2 Sample mini batch of noise z from pz"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3 Generate fake Gz"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4 Compute D loss BCE on real and fake"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5 Update D weights only via Adam"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6 Compute G loss using non saturating log DGz"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7 Update G weights only via Adam"]
    step7 --> step8{"Step 8 Epoch complete"}
    step8 -->|No| step1
    step8 -->|Yes| step9["Step 9 Save checkpoint and visualize samples"]

    classDef real fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
    classDef fake fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef dnode fill:#a8d8ff,stroke:#003d80,color:#000
    classDef gnode fill:#f4d4dd,stroke:#800040,color:#000
    class step1 real
    class step2 fake
    class step3 fake
    class step4 dnode
    class step5 dnode
    class step6 gnode
    class step7 gnode
    class step8 real
    class step9 real
```

## 4.3 Mode Collapse — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    targetA["True data has 6 modes cluster centers"] --> resultA["Healthy GAN samples spread across all 6 modes"]
    targetB["Same true data 6 modes"] --> resultB["Mode collapsed GAN samples concentrate on 1 or 2 modes"]
    resultA --> good["High diversity high FID"]
    resultB --> bad["Low diversity high FID mode collapse"]

    classDef goodcls fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q1.** Define a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). List its two main components and state the objective of each. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep learning framework consisting of two neural networks trained in competition with each other. The two components are:
1. **Generator ($G$):** Takes random noise $z \sim p_z$ as input and outputs synthetic data $G(z)$ that mimics real samples. Its objective is to **fool** the Discriminator.
2. **Discriminator ($D$):** Takes a sample $x$ as input and outputs $D(x) \in [0, 1]$, the probability that $x$ is real. Its objective is to **correctly classify** real vs fake samples.

The two networks are trained to play a **min–max game** on the value function $V(D, G)$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q2.** What is **mode collapse** in GANs? State two techniques to mitigate it. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Mode collapse** is a failure mode where the Generator learns to produce only a *limited subset* (often a single mode) of the true data distribution, even though training loss appears low. The Generator has found one or a few samples that consistently fool the Discriminator and exploits them.

**Two mitigation techniques:**
1. **Minibatch Discrimination** (Salimans et al., 2016) — Allow the Discriminator to compare samples within a batch, so it can detect lack of diversity.
2. **Unrolled GANs** (Metz et al., 2016) — Backpropagate the Discriminator's future response through $k$ update steps so the Generator anticipates and avoids collapse.
3. *(Bonus)* Use **Wasserstein loss with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP)** which provides a smoother gradient signal.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Derive the optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a fixed Generator $G$, starting from the GAN value function. Show that the resulting Generator objective corresponds to minimizing the Jensen–Shannon Divergence between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the **non-saturating loss** trick used to train GANs. Why does the original $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ loss fail in early training? How is the alternative loss $-\log D(G(z))$ derived from first principles? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the value function (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite in integral form (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $D(x)$ and set to zero (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D} \left[ p_{\text{data}} \log D + p_g \log(1 - D) \right] = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} - \frac{p_g}{1 - D} = 0$$

Solving:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify second-order maximum condition (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial D^2} = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D^2} - \frac{p_g}{(1 - D)^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back and recognize JSD (2 marks):**

After substitution, $\log 2$ is added and subtracted to obtain:

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$$

The Generator's minimization objective therefore reduces to **minimizing $2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$**, which is non-negative and equals zero iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$. ✓

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Recall Generator's original loss (1 mark):**

The Generator's objective from the min–max game is:

$$\min_G \, \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Analyze the gradient behavior (2 marks):**
The derivative of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ w.r.t. the Generator's parameters $\theta_g$ is:

$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \log(1 - D(G(z))) = -\frac{1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z))$$

In early training, $D$ is strong, so $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ and $1 - D(G(z)) \approx 1$. The gradient magnitude is therefore **very small** (saturates) → the Generator learns extremely slowly.

**Step 3 — The non-saturating trick (2 marks):**
Goodfellow proposed training $G$ to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead, i.e., minimizing $-\log D(G(z))$. Its gradient is:

$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \big[ -\log D(G(z)) \big] = -\frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z))$$

When $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ (early training), this gradient is **large**, providing strong learning signal.

**Step 4 — Justification that this preserves the same fixed point (2 marks):**
At the global optimum $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, both $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ and $-\log D(G(z))$ have the same value $-\log 2$ and the same derivative. They are *equivalent at convergence* but differ in the dynamics near the start of training. This is the **theoretical justification** for the non-saturating loss.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2025, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the role of the **Discriminator** and **Generator** in a GAN with a clear block diagram. Describe the training procedure step-by-step, including the loss functions used for each network. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Discuss the major **challenges in training GANs** — oscillating loss, mode collapse, uninformative loss, and hyperparameter sensitivity. For each, explain the cause and suggest one mitigation strategy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Role of Generator (1 mark):**
The Generator $G(z; \theta_g)$ takes noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$) and produces a fake sample $G(z)$ in the data space. Its weights are updated so that $D(G(z)) \to 1$.

**Step 2 — Role of Discriminator (1 mark):**
The Discriminator $D(x; \theta_d)$ outputs $D(x) \in [0, 1]$, the probability that $x$ is real. Trained as a binary classifier on real (label 1) and fake (label 0) samples.

**Step 3 — Block diagram (2 marks):**
A textual ASCII block diagram is acceptable; for full credit draw a flowchart showing:
- Real data $x \to D$ with target label 1
- Noise $z \to G \to G(z) \to D$ with target label 0
- Loss flows back to $D$ and then (only via the non-saturating loss) to $G$.

**Step 4 — Training procedure (2 marks):**

> **(i)** For each minibatch, draw $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ from training set.
> **(ii)** Draw $m$ noise vectors $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ from prior $p_z$.
> **(iii)** Update $D$ by ascending its stochastic gradient:
> $$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right]$$
> **(iv)** Update $G$ by ascending its stochastic gradient using the non-saturating loss:
> $$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m \log D(G(z^{(i)}))$$

**Step 5 — Loss functions summary (1 mark):**
- **Discriminator loss (BCE):** $-\big[\log D(x) + \log(1 - D(G(z)))\big]$
- **Generator loss (non-saturating):** $-\log D(G(z))$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

| # | Challenge | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oscillating Loss** (1.5 marks) | $D$ and $G$ improve at incompatible rates; gradients chase a moving target | **TTUR** — use a higher learning rate for $D$ than for $G$ (e.g., $\eta_D = 2\eta_G$); also add label smoothing |
| 2 | **Mode Collapse** (2 marks) | $G$ finds a single sample that always fools $D$ and exploits it; gradient signal becomes degenerate | **Minibatch Discrimination** — let $D$ see the whole batch and penalize low inter-sample diversity |
| 3 | **Uninformative Loss** (1.5 marks) | $D$ loss stuck at $\log 2 \approx 0.693$ because $D$ is at chance; loss value no longer correlates with sample quality | Switch to **Wasserstein loss** (WGAN), which has a meaningful, monotonic gradient with respect to sample quality |
| 4 | **Hyperparameter Sensitivity** (2 marks) | GANs are non-convex; small changes in $\eta$, $\beta_1$, batch size cause divergent training trajectories | Use $\beta_1 = 0$ (DCGAN), spectral normalization in $D$, learning rate warmup, and grid search with FID as the selection metric |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing the two loss signs.** A common mistake is writing the Generator loss as $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ without explaining the *non-saturating trick*. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for missing this nuance in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise prior.** Always mention the noise distribution $p_z(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ explicitly when describing the Generator. A vague "random vector" loses marks.
> 3. **Mixing up mode collapse with overfitting.** Mode collapse is the Generator producing *little variety*; overfitting is the Generator *memorizing* training samples. These are distinct concepts.
> 4. **Missing the JSD link.** When deriving $D^*(x)$, you MUST show the substitution step that converts $V(D^*, G)$ into $-\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}$. Skipping this loses 2 marks in derivations.
> 5. **Hyperparameter values.** Always state a *range* (e.g., $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4}$), not a single number. Examiners expect the range because GAN tuning is empirical.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ A **GAN** has two networks: **Generator $G$** (forger) and **Discriminator $D$** (detective), trained in a **min–max game**.
- ✅ The value function is $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- ✅ Optimal Discriminator: $D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
- ✅ At global equilibrium, $D^*(x) = 0.5$ and $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.
- ✅ Generator's global optimum value is $-\log 4 \approx -1.386$.
- ✅ GAN objective = minimize $2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$.
- ✅ Use the **non-saturating loss** $-\log D(G(z))$ to avoid vanishing gradients in early training.
- ✅ **Oscillating loss** → fix with TTUR, label smoothing, or smaller $\eta_G$.
- ✅ **Mode collapse** → fix with minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs, or WGAN-GP.
- ✅ **Uninformative loss** → $D$ loss stuck at $\log 2$; switch to Wasserstein loss.
- ✅ Critical hyperparameter: **Adam $\beta_1 = 0$** (not $0.9$) for stable GAN training (DCGAN, 2015).
- ✅ Typical learning rates: $\eta_D, \eta_G \in [1 \times 10^{-4}, \, 2 \times 10^{-4}]$; batch size $64$–$256$.
- ✅ Generator's final-layer activation: **tanh** (data scaled to $[-1, 1]$).
- ✅ Discriminator's intermediate activation: **LeakyReLU(0.2)**.
- ✅ GANs are **generative** (model $p(x)$), not discriminative (model $p(y \mid x)$).
- ✅ **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** is the standard quantitative metric for GAN sample quality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN): Architecture of RNN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Network (RNN): Architecture of RNN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks specifically engineered to process **sequential or time-series data** by maintaining an internal **hidden state (memory)** that is recursively updated at each time step. Unlike feedforward neural networks, RNNs possess **directed cyclic (recurrent) connections** that allow information to persist across temporal positions in the input sequence.

Mathematically, an RNN computes a parameterized mapping:

$$
F_{\theta}: (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) \mapsto (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)
$$

where the function $F_{\theta}$ is applied recursively at every time step $t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}$ using **shared parameters** $\theta = \{W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}, b_h, b_y\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RNNs are the foundational architecture for Generative AI tasks involving **language modeling, sequence generation, machine translation, and speech synthesis**. They are explicitly listed under the Module 3 syllabus as the precursor to advanced architectures like **LSTM, GRU, and Transformers**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Reading a Story with Memory"**
>
> Imagine you are reading a novel. To understand the **current sentence**, you don't just look at the words on the current page — you also **remember the plot, characters, and previous events** from earlier pages. Your brain maintains a *running summary* of what has happened so far.
>
> An RNN works exactly like this:
> - The **current input** ($x_t$) = the new sentence you're reading.
> - The **hidden state** ($h_t$) = the running summary in your memory.
> - The **output** ($y_t$) = your interpretation or prediction at that moment.
>
> When you move to the next sentence, your memory gets **updated** by combining the previous memory ($h_{t-1}$) with the new sentence ($x_t$). This is precisely what a recurrent neuron does at every time step $t$.

---

## 1.3 Why Standard Feedforward Networks Fail for Sequential Data

| Limitation | Consequence for Sequential Data |
|:---|:---|
| Fixed-size input vector | Cannot handle variable-length sequences |
| No memory mechanism | Past context is completely discarded |
| No parameter sharing across positions | Cannot generalize to longer sequences than seen in training |
| Stateless computation | Each input processed in isolation |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The two critical properties that distinguish RNNs from MLPs/ANNs are **(1) parameter sharing across time steps** and **(2) maintenance of a hidden state that summarizes past information**.

---

## 1.4 One-Line Definitions (KTU Examiner's Quick-Fire Vocabulary)

- **Hidden State ($h_t$):** The compressed memory vector summarizing information from all previous time steps up to time $t$.
- **Weight Sharing:** The same weight matrices ($W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$) are reused at every time step, enabling the network to generalize across sequence lengths.
- **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time):** The training algorithm that unrolls the RNN and applies standard backpropagation across the temporal dimension.
- **Unrolling:** The process of converting the recurrent computational graph into an equivalent feedforward graph across $T$ time steps.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient:** The pathological decay or blow-up of gradients during BPTT due to repeated multiplication through time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RNN Computational Graph — Step-by-Step Logic

An RNN processes a sequence by performing the **same computation** at every time step, using three learnable weight matrices:

### Step 1: Hidden State Update (The Core Recurrence)

At every time step $t$, the hidden state $h_t$ is computed as a function of:
- The current input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$
- The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$
- Two weight matrices: $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ and $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$
- A bias vector $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$

$$
h_t = \phi_h\!\left(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h\right)
$$

where $\phi_h$ is the **hidden activation function** — typically $\tanh(\cdot)$ or $\text{ReLU}(\cdot)$.

### Step 2: Output Generation

The output at time $t$, $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$, is computed from the current hidden state:

$$
y_t = \phi_y\!\left(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y\right)
$$

where $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$ is the output weight matrix and $\phi_y$ is the **output activation function** — typically **softmax** for classification or **linear** for regression.

### Step 3: Initialization

The hidden state at $t=0$ is typically initialized to zero:

$$
h_0 = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\tanh$ for hidden state?** The $\tanh$ activation keeps the hidden state bounded in $[-1, 1]$, which empirically helps control the magnitude of activations during long sequences and partially mitigates numerical instabilities.

---

## 2.2 Vectorized Implementation Across a Full Sequence

For computational efficiency, an entire mini-batch of sequences is processed using tensor operations. Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_x}$ denote the input batch at time $t$ (where $n$ is the batch size). Then:

$$
H_t = \phi_h\!\left(X\, W_{xh}^\top + H_{t-1}\, W_{hh}^\top + b_h\right)
$$

$$
Y_t = \phi_y\!\left(H_t\, W_{hy}^\top + b_y\right)
$$

This formulation allows **GPU parallelization** over the batch dimension while preserving the sequential dependency in the time dimension.

---

## 2.3 Types of RNN Architectures (KTU High-Yield Diagram Topic)

RNNs can be configured into several structural patterns based on input-output cardinalities:

| Type | Input | Output | Typical Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **One-to-One** | Single vector $x$ | Single vector $y$ | Standard classification (essentially an MLP) |
| **One-to-Many** | Single vector $x$ | Sequence $(y_1, \ldots, y_T)$ | Image captioning, music generation |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_T)$ | Single vector $y$ | Sentiment analysis, video classification |
| **Many-to-Many (Synced)** | Sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_T)$ | Sequence $(y_1, \ldots, y_T)$ | POS tagging, frame-level video labeling |
| **Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder)** | Sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_T)$ | Sequence $(y_1, \ldots, y_{T'})$ | Machine translation, speech recognition |

---

## 2.4 The Loss Function for RNNs

For sequence-level prediction, the **total loss** is the sum (or average) of per-time-step losses:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\theta)
$$

For classification tasks, $\mathcal{L}_t$ is the **cross-entropy loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{k=1}^{C} y_t^{(k)} \log\!\left(\hat{y}_t^{(k)}\right)
$$

where $C$ is the number of classes and $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Dimensions |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Hidden state recurrence | $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 2 | $y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$ | Output generation | $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$ |
| 3 | $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ | Initial hidden state | — |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$ | Total sequence loss | scalar |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{k} y_t^{(k)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(k)}$ | Cross-entropy per step | scalar |
| 6 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | BPTT gradient aggregation | matrix |
| 7 | $\prod_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ | Jacobian chain product (BPTT) | matrix product |
| 8 | $\|\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}\| \leq \lambda_{\max}(W_{hh}) \cdot \phi_h'$ | Gradient magnitude bound | scalar |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Equations 1, 2, 6, and 7 are the most frequently tested. Memorize the **dimensions** of every weight matrix — KTU examiners often award marks for correct dimensional consistency.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

RNNs underpin critical Generative AI applications:

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Language modeling (predicting the next word in a sentence) — foundational for GPT-style models.
- **Speech Recognition:** Mapping audio waveforms to phonetic sequences (used in early versions of Siri, Google Assistant).
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather modeling, energy load forecasting.
- **Music Generation:** Composing melodies token-by-token (e.g., Google's Magenta project).
- **Video Frame Prediction:** Forecasting future frames in autonomous driving and surveillance systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the RNN Forward Pass

### Given
- Input sequence: $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, x_3)$ with $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$
- Initial state: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$
- Weight matrices: $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$, $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$
- Biases: $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$, $b_y \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$
- Activation: $\phi_h = \tanh$, $\phi_y = \text{softmax}$

### Step-by-Step Computation

**Time step $t=1$:**

$$
z_1 = W_{xh}\, x_1 + W_{hh}\, h_0 + b_h
$$

Since $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$, this simplifies to:

$$
z_1 = W_{xh}\, x_1 + b_h
$$

Apply the hidden activation:

$$
h_1 = \tanh(z_1) = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_1 + b_h)
$$

Compute the output:

$$
o_1 = W_{hy}\, h_1 + b_y
$$

$$
y_1 = \text{softmax}(o_1) = \frac{\exp(o_1)}{\sum_{k} \exp(o_{1,k})}
$$

**Time step $t=2$:**

$$
z_2 = W_{xh}\, x_2 + W_{hh}\, h_1 + b_h
$$

$$
h_2 = \tanh(z_2)
$$

$$
o_2 = W_{hy}\, h_2 + b_y
$$

$$
y_2 = \text{softmax}(o_2)
$$

**Time step $t=3$:**

$$
z_3 = W_{xh}\, x_3 + W_{hh}\, h_2 + b_h
$$

$$
h_3 = \tanh(z_3)
$$

$$
o_3 = W_{hy}\, h_3 + b_y
$$

$$
y_3 = \text{softmax}(o_3)
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of BPTT Gradients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Topic:** BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time) is the core training algorithm. You **must** be able to derive the gradient with respect to $W_{hh}$, as it is the parameter most affected by vanishing/exploding gradients.

### Objective
Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial W_{hh}}$ for a single time step loss at $t=3$.

### Step 1: Chain Rule Setup

By the chain rule applied through the recurrent path:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_3}{\partial h_3} \cdot \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

This is because $W_{hh}$ is **reused** at every time step $k \in \{1, 2, 3\}$.

### Step 2: Expand the Jacobian Product

$$
\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{3} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}
$$

For our 3-step sequence:

$$
\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_1} = \frac{\partial h_2}{\partial h_1} \cdot \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_2} = \frac{\partial h_3}{\partial h_2}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Each Jacobian

From the hidden state equation:

$$
h_j = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_j + W_{hh}\, h_{j-1} + b_h)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $h_{j-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) \cdot W_{hh}^\top
$$

where $z_j = W_{xh}\, x_j + W_{hh}\, h_{j-1} + b_h$.

### Step 4: Final Gradient Expression

Putting it all together for $\mathcal{L} = \sum_t \mathcal{L}_t$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \left(\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}^\top \right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Vanishing Gradient Origin:** The product $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(z_j))\, W_{hh}^\top$ involves $T$ matrix multiplications. Since $\vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert \leq 1$, this product **decays exponentially** with sequence length $T$, making $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} \to 0$ for early time steps. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**, motivating LSTM and GRU architectures.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** Given $d_x = 2$, $d_h = 2$, $d_y = 1$, and the following parameters:

$$
W_{xh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
W_{hh} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.3 & 0.0 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
b_h = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
x_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
h_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Compute $h_1$:**

$$
W_{xh}\, x_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.1 \cdot 0 \\ 0.2 \cdot 1 + 0.4 \cdot 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
W_{hh}\, h_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
z_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
h_1 = \tanh\!\begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4621 \\ 0.1974 \end{pmatrix}
$$

This numerical pattern is what students should practice for KTU Part B problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: RNN vs. Feedforward MLP

| Property | Feedforward MLP | Recurrent Neural Network |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Input type | Fixed-length vector | Variable-length sequence |
| Memory mechanism | None | Hidden state $h_t$ |
| Parameter sharing | None across inputs | Same $W$ reused at all $t$ |
| Computational graph | Acyclic (DAG) | Contains cycles (or unrolled DAG) |
| Gradient algorithm | Standard backprop | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) |
| Suitable for sequences | No | Yes |
| Training stability | Generally stable | Suffers vanishing/exploding gradients |
| Generative capability | Limited | Strong (can autoregressively generate) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RNN Unrolled vs. Folded Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below shows the **compact (folded) recurrent view** on the left and the **time-unrolled view** on the right — the two equivalent representations that KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw or explain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FOLDED["FOLDED RECURRENT VIEW"]
        direction LR
        X1F["INPUT x_t"] --> RNNNODE["RNN CELL\nW_xh + W_hh + W_hy"]
        HFB["PREVIOUS h_t-1"] --> RNNNODE
        RNNNODE --> Y1F["OUTPUT y_t"]
        RNNNODE --> HFN["NEXT h_t"]
        HFN -.feedback loop.-> HFB
    end

    subgraph UNROLLED["TIME UNROLLED VIEW"]
        direction LR
        X1["x_1"] --> R1["RNN\nCELL 1"]
        R1 --> Y1["y_1"]
        R1 --> H1["h_1"]
        H1 --> R2["RNN\nCELL 2"]
        X2["x_2"] --> R2
        R2 --> Y2["y_2"]
        R2 --> H2["h_2"]
        H2 --> R3["RNN\nCELL 3"]
        X3["x_3"] --> R3
        R3 --> Y3["y_3"]
        R3 --> H3["h_3"]
    end

    FOLDED ~~~ UNROLLED
```

---

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Diagram of a Single RNN Cell

This diagram maps the **internal data flow** inside a single recurrent neuron at time step $t$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT x_t\ndim d_x"] --> M1["MATRIX MULTIPLY\nW_xh * x_t"]
    B["PREVIOUS HIDDEN STATE h_t-1\ndim d_h"] --> M2["MATRIX MULTIPLY\nW_hh * h_t-1"]
    M1 --> ADD["VECTOR ADDITION\n+ bias b_h"]
    M2 --> ADD
    ADD --> TANH["ACTIVATION\ntanh function"]
    TANH --> HT["CURRENT HIDDEN STATE h_t\ndim d_h"]
    HT --> M3["MATRIX MULTIPLY\nW_hy * h_t"]
    HT --> FEED["FEED TO NEXT STEP\nas h_t for t+1"]
    M3 --> SOFT["OUTPUT ACTIVATION\nsoftmax or linear"]
    SOFT --> YOUT["OUTPUT y_t\ndim d_y"]
```

---

## 4.3 Five Canonical RNN Architecture Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["ONE-TO-ONE"]
        A1["x"] --> B1["RNN"] --> C1["y"]
    end
    subgraph T2["ONE-TO-MANY"]

    end

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RNN Training (BPTT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["INITIALIZE\nh_0 = zero vector\nrandom W and b"] --> S2["FORWARD PASS\ncompute h_t and y_t\nat each time step"]
    S2 --> S3["COMPUTE LOSS\ncross entropy per step\nsum to get total L"]
    S3 --> S4["UNROLL GRAPH\nacross T time steps"]
    S4 --> S5["BACKWARD PASS\nchain rule through time\nBPTT gradient"]
    S5 --> S6{"GRADIENT\nSTABLE?"}
    S6 -- NO --> S7["APPLY GRADIENT CLIPPING\nor use LSTM or GRU"]
    S6 -- YES --> S8["UPDATE WEIGHTS\nW := W - alpha * dL/dW"]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 --> S8
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). Why is parameter sharing important in RNNs?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is a neural network architecture designed for processing **sequential data** by maintaining a hidden state that is updated recursively at each time step using the same set of parameters.

The hidden state update is given by:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)
$$

**Importance of parameter sharing (2 marks):**
1. It allows the network to **generalize to sequence lengths** not seen during training.
2. It **reduces the number of learnable parameters**, improving computational efficiency.
3. It treats the sequence in a **position-independent** manner, ensuring consistent feature extraction across time.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**
**What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How does it affect long-sequence learning?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during BPTT when the gradients of the loss with respect to early time-step weights become exponentially small (approach zero) due to repeated multiplication by Jacobian matrices.

Mathematically, the gradient involves the product:

$$
\prod_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} = \prod_{k=1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_k)\right) W_{hh}^\top
$$

Since $\vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert \leq 1$, this product decays exponentially with sequence length $T$.

**Effect on long sequences:**
- Early time-step weights **receive negligible updates**, preventing the network from learning long-range dependencies.
- The RNN effectively becomes "short-sighted," capturing only recent context.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network with a neat block diagram. Derive the forward propagation equations for a basic RNN cell. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm. Show the derivation of the gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$. Mention the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Architecture and Forward Propagation (7 Marks):**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
An RNN consists of:
1. **Input layer** that receives sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T$.
2. **Hidden layer** with recurrent connections that maintains state $h_t$.
3. **Output layer** that produces $y_t$ at each time step.
4. **Shared weight matrices**: $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden), $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output).

(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 for the visual.)

**Forward Propagation Equations (4 marks):**

For each time step $t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}$:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)
$$

$$
y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)
$$

with initial condition $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Numerical Illustration:** For $t=1$, with $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$:
$$
h_1 = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_1 + b_h)
$$

The same recurrence applies for $t=2, 3, \ldots, T$ with updated hidden states.

---

**(b) BPTT and Vanishing Gradient (7 Marks):**

**BPTT Concept (2 marks):**
BPTT unrolls the RNN across all $T$ time steps and applies standard backpropagation on the resulting feedforward graph. The total loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t
$$

**Gradient Derivation (4 marks):**
Using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

where:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}^\top
$$

For each $j$:
$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}^\top
$$

**Vanishing Gradient (1 mark):**
Because the Jacobian product decays exponentially with $t$, early time-step gradients approach zero, hindering long-range learning. This motivates LSTM and GRU.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the various types of RNN architectures (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many) with suitable examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast RNNs with standard feedforward neural networks. Explain with equations how RNNs handle sequential data and why parameter sharing is essential. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Types of RNN Architectures (7 Marks):**

| Type | Structure | Example Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **One-to-One** | Single input → single output | Image classification (no sequence) |
| **One-to-Many** | Single input → sequence output | Image captioning, music generation |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence input → single output | Sentiment analysis, spam detection |
| **Many-to-Many (Synced)** | Sequence input → sequence output (aligned) | POS tagging, named entity recognition |
| **Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder)** | Sequence input → sequence output (different lengths) | Machine translation, speech-to-text |

**Detailed Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **One-to-Many:** The single input $x$ initializes $h_0$, and the network generates outputs $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T$ at each subsequent step. Example: feeding an image and generating a caption word-by-word.

2. **Many-to-One:** The entire sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_T)$ is processed, and only the final hidden state $h_T$ is used to produce a single output $y$. Example: classifying a movie review as positive or negative.

3. **Many-to-Many (Encoder-Decoder):** An encoder RNN compresses the input sequence into a context vector $c = h_T^{\text{enc}}$. A decoder RNN then generates the output sequence from $c$. Used extensively in machine translation.

---

**(b) RNN vs. Feedforward Networks (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Feedforward NN | RNN |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Input | Fixed-size | Variable-length sequence |
| Connections | Acyclic | Cyclic (recurrent) |
| Memory | None | Hidden state |
| Parameter sharing | No | Yes (across time) |
| Sequence modeling | Poor | Excellent |

**How RNNs Handle Sequential Data (3 marks):**

RNNs maintain a **hidden state** $h_t$ that is updated recursively:

$$
h_t = f_W(h_{t-1}, x_t)
$$

The same function $f_W$ (with shared weights $W$) is applied at every step, allowing the network to process sequences of arbitrary length.

**Why Parameter Sharing is Essential (1 mark):**
Without parameter sharing, the network would require a separate set of weights for each possible sequence length, making it impossible to generalize. Sharing enables the network to apply learned transformations consistently across all positions in the sequence — a property known as **translation invariance in time**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing dimensions:** Students frequently write $h_t = W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1}$ without verifying that matrix multiplication is valid. Always check: $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$, so $W_{xh}\, x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$. **Marks lost: 1–2 per question.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term $b_h$:** A complete RNN equation MUST include $b_h$. Omitting it loses at least **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Not stating $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the initialization condition. Missing it = **0.5 marks lost**.
>
> 4. **Confusing "many-to-many" types:** The synced and encoder-decoder variants are DIFFERENT architectures. Mislabeling them loses **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the activation function:** Writing $h_t = W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h$ without $\tanh$ is incomplete. RNNs are NON-LINEAR precisely because of $\tanh$.
>
> 6. **Hand-waving BPTT:** KTU expects the chain-rule product notation $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$. Writing only "we apply backpropagation" earns **zero marks** for the derivation.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🎯 **RNN = Neural Network with memory** (hidden state $h_t$).
- 🎯 **Core equations:** $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$ and $y_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$.
- 🎯 **Parameter sharing** across time steps is the defining feature that enables sequence generalization.
- 🎯 **Unrolling** converts the cyclic graph into a feedforward DAG of depth $T$.
- 🎯 **BPTT** = backpropagation on the unrolled graph; total loss = $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$.
- 🎯 **Vanishing gradient** arises from the Jacobian product $\prod \text{diag}(1-\tanh^2(z_j)) W_{hh}^\top$ decaying exponentially.
- 🎯 **Five RNN types:** one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many (synced), many-to-many (encoder-decoder).
- 🎯 **Hidden activation:** $\tanh$; **Output activation:** softmax (classification) or linear (regression).
- 🎯 **Initialization:** $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
- 🎯 **Limitations:** vanishing/exploding gradients → led to LSTM, GRU, and eventually Transformers.
- 🎯 **Weight matrix dimensions to memorize:** $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$, $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$.
- 🎯 **Loss function:** cross-entropy per time step, summed over the sequence.
- 🎯 **Why RNNs matter for Generative AI:** they form the autoregressive backbone of language models and sequence generators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, formally introduced by *Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997)*, engineered to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in conventional RNNs. LSTMs achieve this by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms** (forget, input, output) that selectively control the flow, retention, and emission of information across long temporal sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LSTM is a foundational deep-learning primitive that underpins modern sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) models, machine translation, speech recognition, time-series forecasting, and **Generative AI** architectures such as text generation and language modeling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Selective Librarian

Imagine a **wise librarian** managing an enormous library of books (your data stream):
- Every day, a new book arrives (the input $x_t$).
- The librarian decides **which old books to throw away** (the **forget gate**).
- The librarian decides **which new books to add to the collection** (the **input gate**).
- When a reader asks for a recommendation, the librarian decides **which books to actually show** (the **output gate**).

The librarian's **backroom archive** is the **cell state $C_t$** — the long-term memory. What the librarian *hands you* is the **hidden state $h_t$** — the short-term, working memory. LSTMs work exactly this way: they decide *what to remember*, *what to forget*, and *what to expose* at every time step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Whereas a vanilla RNN squeezes all past context into a single hidden vector (and gradually forgets it), an LSTM carries a **separate, protected conveyor belt** of memory (the cell state) that information can be added to or removed from with minimal interference.

### 1.3 Salient Properties of LSTM

- **Persistent memory** through the cell state $C_t$, allowing gradients to flow over hundreds of time steps.
- **Three sigmoid gates**, each producing values in the open interval $(0, 1)$, which act as differentiable "knobs."
- **Tanh activations** in the candidate cell and output, bounded in $(-1, 1)$ for numerical stability.
- **Parameter sharing** across time steps, mirroring the recurrent inductive bias of standard RNNs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Gates — Operational Logic

The LSTM cell at time step $t$ receives three inputs:
1. The previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$
2. The previous cell state $C_{t-1}$
3. The current input vector $x_t$

It then computes three gate activations in parallel.

#### (a) Forget Gate $f_t$ — *What to discard?*
- Reads $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$, passes them through a sigmoid.
- Output $f_t \in (0,1)^d$ is **pointwise-multiplied** with the previous cell state.
- A value of **0** means *completely forget*; a value of **1** means *fully retain*.

#### (b) Input Gate $i_t$ and Candidate Cell $\tilde{C}_t$ — *What to store?*
- $i_t$ (sigmoid) decides *which values to update*.
- $\tilde{C}_t$ (tanh) creates a vector of *new candidate values* that could be added.
- Their product $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the *new information*.

#### (c) Output Gate $o_t$ — *What to emit?*
- $o_t$ (sigmoid) decides *which parts of the cell state* become the next hidden state.
- The hidden state $h_t$ is the **filtered, squashed** version of the cell state.

### 2.2 Why LSTM Solves the Vanishing Gradient Problem

In vanilla RNNs, gradients are multiplied by the same recurrent weight matrix at every time step, leading to exponential decay (vanish) or growth (explode). In LSTMs, the gradient flowing back through the cell state $C_t$ involves the **forget gate $f_t$** as a multiplicative factor. Since $f_t$ is *learned* (not fixed), the network can learn to keep $f_t \approx 1$ for important long-range dependencies, allowing gradients to propagate across **hundreds of time steps** without vanishing.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** LSTMs are the workhorse behind **Google Translate (pre-Transformer era)**, **Apple Siri's speech recognizer**, **autocomplete keyboards**, and **financial time-series forecasters**. Any time an engineer needs to model *temporal causality with long memory*, LSTM is a default candidate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Symbol | Equation | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forget Gate | $f_t$ | $\sigma\!\left(W_f\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)$ | $\vert f_t \vert$ vector in $(0,1)$ |
| Input Gate | $i_t$ | $\sigma\!\left(W_i\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right)$ | $\vert i_t \vert$ vector in $(0,1)$ |
| Candidate Cell | $\tilde{C}_t$ | $\tanh\!\left(W_C\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right)$ | $\vert \tilde{C}_t \vert$ in $(-1, 1)$ |
| Cell State Update | $C_t$ | $f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Long-term memory |
| Output Gate | $o_t$ | $\sigma\!\left(W_o\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right)$ | $\vert o_t \vert$ vector in $(0,1)$ |
| Hidden State | $h_t$ | $o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Output for next step |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Activation in $(0,1)$ |
| Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(z)$ | $\dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Activation in $(-1,1)$ |
| Concatenation | $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ | Vector stacking of hidden + input | Dimension: $d_h + d_x$ |
| Hadamard Product | $\odot$ | Element-wise multiplication | Same-shape operands |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note for KTU Scripts:** $\odot$ denotes **Hadamard (element-wise) product**, not matrix multiplication. This is the single most common point of confusion in LSTM derivations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Forward Pass — Exhaustive Derivation

We begin with the **concatenation vector** that combines the previous hidden state and the current input. This is the master input to every gate.

$$
z_t \;=\; [\,h_{t-1},\; x_t\,] \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{d_h + d_x}
$$

#### Step 1 — Compute the Forget Gate

The forget gate is a single-layer feedforward neural network with sigmoid activation, parametrized by weight matrix $W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times (d_h + d_x)}$ and bias $b_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$.

$$
f_t \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\,W_f\, z_t + b_f\,\right) \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\,W_f\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\,\right)
$$

**Logic:** Each element of $f_t$ is in $(0,1)$. When multiplied element-wise with $C_{t-1}$, it acts as a "soft mask" — keeping dimensions where $f_t \approx 1$ and erasing dimensions where $f_t \approx 0$.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Input Gate and Candidate Cell Values

The input gate decides *which positions* of the cell state will be updated. The candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ decides *what new values* are proposed.

$$
i_t \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\,W_i\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\,\right)
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t \;=\; \tanh\!\left(\,W_C\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\,\right)
$$

**Logic:** The element-wise product $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the *gated update* — only the candidate values whose corresponding input gate is "open" will be written into the cell state.

#### Step 3 — Update the Cell State (Long-Term Memory)

The new cell state is the sum of (a) the *retained old memory* and (b) the *gated new candidate values*.

$$
C_t \;=\; f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

**Logic:** This is the *only* linear (additive) update in the entire cell. Because of this additive structure, gradients flow through $C_t$ as a near-identity mapping when $f_t \approx 1$, which is what makes long-range credit assignment tractable.

#### Step 4 — Compute the Output Gate

$$
o_t \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\,W_o\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\,\right)
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the New Hidden State (Short-Term Output)

The hidden state is the **filtered, squashed** version of the cell state — the part of memory the LSTM chooses to *expose* to the outside world and to the next time step.

$$
h_t \;=\; o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

**Logic:** Note the **two-fold nonlinear transformation** — $\tanh$ compresses $C_t$ into $(-1,1)$, and $o_t$ filters which components survive. This makes $h_t$ a *gated, bounded* view of $C_t$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Summary

| Step | Compute | Output Shape | Activation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $z_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h+d_x}$ | None | Concatenate inputs |
| 1 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f z_t + b_f)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | Sigmoid | Decide what to forget |
| 2a | $i_t = \sigma(W_i z_t + b_i)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | Sigmoid | Decide what to write |
| 2b | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C z_t + b_C)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | Tanh | Propose new values |
| 3 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | None | Update long-term memory |
| 4 | $o_t = \sigma(W_o z_t + b_o)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | Sigmoid | Decide what to emit |
| 5 | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | Tanh (post-$\odot$) | Output short-term memory |

### 3.3 Parameter Count Derivation

Let $d_h$ = hidden dimension, $d_x$ = input dimension. Each of the four weight matrices is of shape $d_h \times (d_h + d_x)$, and each has a bias of length $d_h$.

$$
N_{\text{params}} \;=\; 4 \times \left[\,d_h(d_h + d_x) + d_h\,\right] \;=\; 4 d_h (d_h + d_x + 1)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** If $d_h = 128$ and $d_x = 64$, then $N_{\text{params}} = 4 \times 128 \times (128 + 64 + 1) = 4 \times 128 \times 193 = \mathbf{98{,}816}$ trainable parameters in a single LSTM cell. Remember that these weights are **shared across all time steps**.

### 3.4 Gradient Flow — Why LSTM Doesn't Vanish

Consider the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \;=\; \mathrm{diag}(f_t)
$$

The full gradient is then:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_{t-1}} \;=\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t}\,\mathrm{diag}(f_t) \;+\; \text{(other terms)}
$$

**Logic:** Because the multiplicative factor is $\mathrm{diag}(f_t)$ (a learned, sigmoid-bounded matrix), the network can keep its eigenvalues near 1 across many time steps, ensuring **stable gradient flow** — the very property vanilla RNNs lack.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LSTM Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ht_prev["h t minus 1"]:::input
    xt["x t"]:::input
    Ct_prev["C t minus 1"]:::memory

    concat["Concat z t"]:::node

    fgate["Forget Gate sigmoid Wf dot zt plus bf"]:::gate
    igate["Input Gate sigmoid Wi dot zt plus bi"]:::gate
    cgate["Candidate tanh WC dot zt plus bC"]:::gate
    ogate["Output Gate sigmoid Wo dot zt plus bo"]:::gate

    mul1["Hadamard ft times Ct_minus_1"]:::op
    mul2["Hadamard it times C_tilde_t"]:::op
    add["Add C t new memory"]:::op
    tanhC["tanh C t"]:::op
    mul3["Hadamard ot times tanhCt"]:::op

    Ct_new["C t long term memory"]:::memory
    ht_new["h t short term output"]:::output

    ht_prev --> concat
    xt --> concat

    concat --> fgate
    concat --> igate
    concat --> cgate
    concat --> ogate

    fgate --> mul1
    Ct_prev --> mul1

    igate --> mul2
    cgate --> mul2

    mul1 --> add
    mul2 --> add

    add --> Ct_new
    add --> tanhC

    ogate --> mul3
    tanhC --> mul3

    mul3 --> ht_new
    ht_new --> concat

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0a2540
    classDef gate fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#5a3e00
    classDef op fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#5a0010
    classDef memory fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0a3622
    classDef output fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#2e1065
    classDef node fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#212529
```

### 4.2 Unrolled LSTM Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["x 1"] --> A1["LSTM Cell t=1"]
    x2["x 2"] --> A2["LSTM Cell t=2"]
    x3["x 3"] --> A3["LSTM Cell t=3"]
    x4["x 4"] --> A4["LSTM Cell t=4"]

    A1 -- "h0, C0" --> A1
    A1 -- "h1, C1" --> A2
    A2 -- "h2, C2" --> A3
    A3 -- "h3, C3" --> A4

    A1 --> h1o["h 1"]
    A2 --> h2o["h 2"]
    A3 --> h3o["h 3"]
    A4 --> h4o["h 4"]

    classDef cell fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#5a3e00
    classDef inp fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0a2540
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0a3622

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 cell
    class x1,x2,x3,x4 inp
    class h1o,h2o,h3o,h4o out
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Narrative

The diagram above maps the **architectural data flow**:
- The **horizontal conveyor belt at the top** ($C_{t-1} \rightarrow C_t$) is the long-term memory, which the forget gate can selectively clear and the input gate can selectively extend.
- The **three yellow gate blocks** act as differentiable, learnable filters that are trained via Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).
- The **bottom red multiplication** shows the *output projection* — the hidden state that flows to the next time step and also to any downstream task (classification, generation, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**List and briefly explain the three gates of an LSTM cell. State the activation function used in each gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An LSTM cell uses three gating mechanisms to regulate information flow:

1. **Forget Gate $f_t$** — Uses the **sigmoid** activation function. It decides what fraction of the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ to retain. Output values in $(0,1)$ act as a soft mask.
