2. **Input Gate $i_t$** — Uses the **sigmoid** activation function. It decides which new candidate values should be written into the cell state.
3. **Output Gate $o_t$** — Uses the **sigmoid** activation function. It decides which parts of the cell state are emitted as the next hidden state $h_t$.

The candidate cell $\tilde{C}_t$ uses the **tanh** activation to propose new values in $(-1, 1)$.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Why does LSTM overcome the vanishing gradient problem faced by vanilla RNNs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a vanilla RNN, gradients are repeatedly multiplied by the same recurrent weight matrix, causing them to decay (or explode) exponentially over long sequences.

In an LSTM, the cell state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is **additive** rather than purely multiplicative. The gradient with respect to $C_{t-1}$ is $\mathrm{diag}(f_t)$, where the forget gate $f_t$ is **learned** (not fixed). The network can therefore learn to keep $f_t \approx 1$ along dimensions that carry long-range dependencies, allowing gradients to flow back through hundreds of time steps without vanishing.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\odot$ (Hadamard product) with $\cdot$ (matrix multiplication).** A single slip costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always specify the activation function** (sigmoid for gates, tanh for cell/output projection) — vague statements lose marks.
> 3. **Distinguish between $C_t$ (long-term memory) and $h_t$ (short-term output).** Many students write the wrong equation for the wrong variable.
> 4. **In numerical problems, show the parameter-count formula derivation**, not just the final number. Partial marks are awarded for setup.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the complete forward-pass equations of an LSTM cell at time step $t$, clearly defining the **forget gate**, **input gate**, **output gate**, **candidate cell state**, **cell state update**, and **hidden state output**. Mention the activation function used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain how LSTM solves the **vanishing gradient problem** in standard RNNs. Derive the expression for the gradient of the loss with respect to the previous cell state and justify why it does not vanish. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Concatenation (1 mark):**

$$
z_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]
$$

**Step 2 — Three Gates and Candidate (3 marks):**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(sigmoid)}
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(sigmoid)}
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \quad \text{(tanh)}
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(sigmoid)}
$$

**Step 3 — Cell State Update and Hidden State (2 marks):**

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Note activation functions (1 mark):**
Sigmoid $\sigma$ for all three gates; tanh for candidate cell and post-cell projection.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Recap the problem (1 mark):**
In vanilla RNNs, $h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$. The gradient flowing back involves repeated multiplication by $W_h^T$, leading to exponential decay if its spectral radius is less than 1.

**Step 2 — LSTM gradient through cell state (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \mathrm{diag}(f_t)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} \, \mathrm{diag}(f_t) + \text{downstream terms}
$$

**Step 3 — Why this avoids vanishing (2 marks):**
The factor $\mathrm{diag}(f_t)$ is a **learned, sigmoid-bounded diagonal matrix**. The network can set $f_t \approx 1$ along dimensions representing long-range dependencies, keeping the gradient magnitude close to 1 across many steps. The **additive** structure of the cell state update (versus purely multiplicative) further aids gradient flow.

**Step 4 — Block diagram (1 mark):**
Draw a horizontal cell-state conveyor belt with the forget gate controlling retention and the input gate controlling new information, demonstrating that gradients ride along the same belt in the backward pass.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare **vanilla RNN**, **LSTM**, and **GRU** with respect to gating mechanisms, number of parameters, memory duration, and handling of vanishing gradients. Present the comparison in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LSTM network has hidden dimension $d_h = 100$ and input dimension $d_x = 50$. Compute:
  - (i) The number of trainable parameters in a **single LSTM cell**. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) The number of trainable parameters if the cell is **unrolled for 10 time steps** with weight sharing. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Justify why weight sharing is beneficial for sequence modeling. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Memory mechanism | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Cell state $C_t$ + hidden $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| Activation in gate | N/A | Sigmoid (gates), tanh (cell) | Sigmoid (gates), tanh (candidate) |
| Parameters per cell | $d_h(d_h + d_x) + d_h$ | $4 d_h(d_h + d_x + 1)$ | $3 d_h(d_h + d_x + 1)$ |
| Long-term memory | Poor (vanishing gradient) | Excellent (additive cell state) | Good (gated hidden state) |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Mitigated | Mitigated (slightly less than LSTM) |
| Training time | Fast | Slowest of the three | Intermediate |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row, with a bonus 1 mark for overall presentation/comparison summary.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Single cell parameter count (3 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{cell}} = 4 d_h (d_h + d_x + 1)
$$

$$
N_{\text{cell}} = 4 \times 100 \times (100 + 50 + 1) = 4 \times 100 \times 151 = \mathbf{60{,}400 \text{ parameters}}
$$

**(ii) Unrolled for 10 time steps with weight sharing (2 marks):**

Since the same set of weights $\{W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o\}$ and biases $\{b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o\}$ is reused at every time step, the total parameter count is **unchanged**:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{60{,}400 \text{ parameters}}
$$

**(iii) Justification for weight sharing (2 marks):**

Weight sharing enforces the **temporal inductive bias** — the same dynamics should govern the relationship between $x_t$ and $h_t$ regardless of absolute time position. It drastically reduces parameter count, prevents overfitting on long sequences, and allows the network to generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LSTM = RNN + Protected Cell State + 3 Gates.** The cell state $C_t$ is the "highway" that carries long-term memory with minimal gradient interference.
- **Three Gates:**
  - **Forget Gate $f_t$** → sigmoid → *what to throw away*
  - **Input Gate $i_t$** → sigmoid → *what to write*
  - **Output Gate $o_t$** → sigmoid → *what to expose*
- **Candidate Cell $\tilde{C}_t$** uses **tanh**; gates use **sigmoid**.
- **Update Rule (memorize verbatim):** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ and $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- **Hadamard product $\odot$** is **element-wise multiplication**, NOT matrix multiplication.
- **Parameter Formula:** $N = 4 d_h (d_h + d_x + 1)$ — for **every** LSTM cell, regardless of unrolling depth, due to weight sharing.
- **LSTM vs GRU:** GRU combines forget + input into a single update gate and merges cell state with hidden state, giving fewer parameters but slightly less expressive long-term memory.
- **Vanishing Gradient Resolution:** The gradient $\partial L / \partial C_{t-1}$ involves the learned factor $\mathrm{diag}(f_t)$, allowing the network to preserve gradient magnitude across hundreds of time steps.
- **Common Exam Traps:**
  - Mixing up $C_t$ and $h_t$ formulas.
  - Forgetting the **tanh** on the cell state before the output gate multiplication.
  - Stating "LSTM has no vanishing gradient" — correction: it *mitigates*, does not *eliminate*, the problem.
- **Real-world Generative AI Use Case:** LSTMs were the backbone of early **text generation, machine translation, and music generation** systems before Transformers (2017) took over. They are still preferred in low-resource, low-latency, or streaming settings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of LSTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of LSTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of the Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**, engineered to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in standard RNNs. It achieves this by introducing a sophisticated **memory cell** governed by three multiplicative gating units — the **forget gate**, **input gate**, and **output gate** — that explicitly regulate the flow, retention, and emission of information across arbitrary temporal sequences.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCMT602 – Generative AI)**, LSTM is treated as the foundational sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) backbone that powered the first generation of generative language and time-series models, sitting architecturally **before** GRU, encoder-decoder stacks, and modern Transformer-based generators.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of LSTM Design

| Pillar | Purpose | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell State (Cₜ)** | Long-term memory conveyor belt | The "permanent record" of everything the network has decided to remember |
| **Hidden State (hₜ)** | Short-term working memory / output | The "currently focused thought" that is exposed to the next layer |
| **Gates (fₜ, iₜ, oₜ)** | Information traffic controllers | Three security checkpoints deciding what passes, what is stored, and what is output |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: LSTM as a Library Archivist

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine an extremely meticulous Library Archivist (the LSTM cell) sitting at a desk.**
>
> 1. **The Inbox (Input xₜ):** Every day, a new book chapter (the current input) arrives on the desk.
> 2. **The Master Ledger (Cell State Cₜ):** This is the giant, hand-bound ledger the archivist maintains for decades. It contains the entire library's catalogue — the long-term memory.
> 3. **The Three Assistants (Gates):**
>    - The **Forgetting Assistant (Forget Gate fₜ)** scans the ledger every morning and erases entries that are no longer relevant (e.g., "the user mentioned 1995 — no longer needed").
>    - The **Writing Assistant (Input Gate iₜ)** decides which *new* parts of today's chapter are worth copying into the master ledger.
>    - The **Speaking Assistant (Output Gate oₜ)** translates today's relevant ledger entries into a short spoken summary handed to the next archivist.
> 4. **The Handover Note (Hidden State hₜ):** This is the short spoken summary — it tells the *next* archivist (next time step) what was important about today.

The genius of the design is that the master ledger (**cell state**) can flow through hundreds of time steps with only **linear** modifications (addition + multiplication by a gate value between 0 and 1), which is exactly why the gradient does not vanish.

### 1.4 Why Standard RNNs Fail — The Motivation for LSTM

In a vanilla RNN, the hidden state update is:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$$

During backpropagation through time (BPTT), the gradient is multiplied by the same weight matrix $W_h$ at every step. If the largest singular value of $W_h$ is:

- **< 1** → gradient shrinks exponentially → **Vanishing Gradient**
- **> 1** → gradient blows up exponentially → **Exploding Gradient**

LSTM solves this by replacing the single multiplicative chain with an **additive cell-state update pathway**, allowing gradients to flow across long unrolled sequences without decay.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The LSTM Cell — Component-wise Decomposition

The LSTM cell at time step $t$ accepts **three inputs** and emits **two outputs**:

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape (for hidden size H) |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Current input vector | $(B, D)$ where $B$ = batch, $D$ = input dim |
| $h_{t-1}$ | Previous hidden state | $(B, H)$ |
| $C_{t-1}$ | Previous cell state | $(B, H)$ |
| $h_t$ | New hidden state | $(B, H)$ |
| $C_t$ | New cell state | $(B, H)$ |

### 2.2 The Four Core Equations (Gates + Candidate)

These four equations are executed **in parallel** within a single LSTM cell. All four are essentially a **logistic-regression-style linear projection** of the same concatenated input $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right) &\quad &\text{(Forget Gate)} \\
i_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right) &\quad &\text{(Input Gate)} \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh\!\left(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right) &\quad &\text{(Candidate Values)} \\
o_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right) &\quad &\text{(Output Gate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) activation**, $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, producing values strictly in $(0,1)$ — making them act as soft **switches**.
- $\tanh$ is the **hyperbolic tangent**, producing values in $(-1, 1)$ — squashing the candidate values symmetrically.
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times (H+D)}$ and $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ are **learnable parameters**.
- $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes the **concatenation** of the previous hidden state and current input.

### 2.3 The Two State-Update Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.
- The first equation is the **additive memory update** — it is the architectural innovation that cures vanishing gradients.
- The second equation is the **read-out** — the cell's memory is filtered through the output gate before being exposed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU Board Exam (Module 3).**

| # | Equation | Function | Activation | Output Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Forget Gate | **Sigmoid** $\sigma$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| 2 | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Input Gate | **Sigmoid** $\sigma$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| 3 | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | Candidate Memory | **Tanh** | $(-1, 1)$ |
| 4 | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Output Gate | **Sigmoid** $\sigma$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| 5 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Cell State Update | — (linear additive) | $(-2, 2)$ |
| 6 | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Hidden State Output | **Tanh** then gate | $(-1, 1)$ |

**Parameter count per LSTM cell:** $4 \times \left[(H + D) \times H + H\right] = 4H^2 + 4HD + 4H$

**Where:**
- $H$ = hidden state size
- $D$ = input feature dimension
- The factor **4** comes from the four gates/candidates (forget, input, candidate, output).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Generative AI (course context):** First-generation text generators, music composition (e.g., early Magenta models), and image captioning pipelines.
- **Time-series forecasting:** Stock prediction, weather modeling, energy load forecasting.
- **Speech & audio:** Pre-transformer speech recognition (DeepSpeech 1/2), TTS systems.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein secondary structure prediction, DNA sequence modeling.
- **Anomaly detection:** Industrial IoT sensor streams, network intrusion detection.

### 2.6 Gradient Flow — Why LSTM Survives Long Sequences

The gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to an earlier cell state $C_k$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} \;=\; \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t}
$$

For a vanilla RNN this product contains **matrix multiplications by $W_h$** at every step. For the LSTM:

$$
\frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}} \;=\; \text{diag}(f_j) \;+\; \text{(lesser, gated correction terms)}
$$

Since $f_j \in (0, 1)$ element-wise, the gradient becomes a **product of bounded diagonal matrices** — not unbounded full matrix multiplications. When $f_j \approx 1$, the gradient passes through almost unchanged → **error carousel mechanism (Gers et al., 2000)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shape and Dimensionality of the Forget Gate

**Problem.** Given an LSTM with hidden size $H = 4$ and input dimension $D = 3$, derive the exact shape of the weight matrix $W_f$ and bias vector $b_f$, and verify the dimensionality of $f_t$ for a batch of $B = 2$ sequences.

**Step 1 — Form the concatenated input vector.**

The previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ has shape $(B, H) = (2, 4)$. The current input $x_t$ has shape $(B, D) = (2, 3)$. Concatenation along the feature axis yields:

$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{\,B \times (H+D)} = \mathbb{R}^{\,2 \times 7}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the shape of $W_f$.**

The forget gate equation requires a linear transformation followed by sigmoid. The output $f_t$ must be a $(B, H)$ matrix (one forget-value per hidden unit, per sample). Therefore:

$$W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{\,H \times (H+D)} = \mathbb{R}^{\,4 \times 7}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the shape of $b_f$.**

The bias is broadcast across the batch, so it must have one entry per hidden unit:

$$b_f \in \mathbb{R}^{\,H} = \mathbb{R}^{\,4}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the matrix multiplication.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^{\top}_{\text{per-sample}} &\in \mathbb{R}^{\,4 \times 7} \cdot \mathbb{R}^{\,7 \times 1} = \mathbb{R}^{\,4 \times 1} \\
\text{Add bias } b_f \text{ (broadcast)} &\in \mathbb{R}^{\,4 \times 1} \\
\text{Apply } \sigma \text{ element-wise} &\in \mathbb{R}^{\,4 \times 1} = (H,)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Reassemble across the batch.**

For all $B = 2$ samples, the final forget gate tensor is:

$$f_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\,B \times H} = \mathbb{R}^{\,2 \times 4}$$

> **Conclusion.** A hidden-size-4, input-dim-3 LSTM has a forget-gate weight tensor of $4 \times 7 = 28$ scalar parameters plus a $4$-element bias, giving **32 learnable scalars per forget gate alone**. Multiplied by the four gate-equations → **128 learnable scalars per cell per time step** (not counting the candidate's separate transformation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Forward Pass — Toy Single-Cell Walkthrough

**Problem.** Let $H = 1$, $D = 1$. Suppose at time $t$:
- $h_{t-1} = 0.5$
- $x_t = 0.8$
- $C_{t-1} = 1.2$

Let the learnable parameters (we will treat them as fixed, given) be:

$$
W_f = 0.4,\quad b_f = 0.1,\quad W_i = 0.3,\quad b_i = -0.2
$$
$$
W_C = 0.5,\quad b_C = 0.0,\quad W_o = 0.6,\quad b_o = 0.05
$$

Compute $C_t$ and $h_t$.

**Step 1 — Compute the forget gate.**

The concatenated input is $[h_{t-1}, x_t] = [0.5, 0.8]$. The forget gate's linear pre-activation is:

$$z_f = W_f \cdot [0.5, 0.8] + b_f$$

Since $W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 2}$, the product $W_f \cdot [0.5, 0.8]$ is a scalar weighted sum. Following the **standard PyTorch-style ordering** where $W_f$ is shaped $(4H, H+D)$ and operates on the concatenation in that order, and given the toy scalar reduction, we treat it as a dot product:

$$z_f = 0.4 \cdot (0.5 + 0.8) + 0.1 = 0.4 \cdot 1.3 + 0.1 = 0.52 + 0.1 = 0.62$$

$$f_t = \sigma(0.62) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.62}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5379} = \frac{1}{1.5379} \approx 0.6503$$

> **Interpretation:** The cell is being told "forget about 35% of the old memory."

**Step 2 — Compute the input gate.**

$$z_i = 0.3 \cdot (0.5 + 0.8) - 0.2 = 0.3 \cdot 1.3 - 0.2 = 0.39 - 0.2 = 0.19$$

$$i_t = \sigma(0.19) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.19}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.8270} = \frac{1}{1.8270} \approx 0.5473$$

**Step 3 — Compute the candidate memory.**

$$z_C = 0.5 \cdot (0.5 + 0.8) + 0.0 = 0.5 \cdot 1.3 = 0.65$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(0.65) = \frac{e^{0.65} - e^{-0.65}}{e^{0.65} + e^{-0.65}} = \frac{1.9155 - 0.5220}{1.9155 + 0.5220} = \frac{1.3935}{2.4375} \approx 0.5717$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new cell state.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
&= 0.6503 \times 1.2 + 0.5473 \times 0.5717 \\
&= 0.7804 + 0.3129 \\
&\approx 1.0933
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The previous cell state $1.2$ was scaled down by $0.6503$ (preserving 78%), and the new candidate contributed an additional $0.3129$. The new long-term memory is $\approx 1.09$.

**Step 5 — Compute the output gate and hidden state.**

$$z_o = 0.6 \cdot (0.5 + 0.8) + 0.05 = 0.6 \cdot 1.3 + 0.05 = 0.78 + 0.05 = 0.83$$

$$o_t = \sigma(0.83) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.83}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4360} = \frac{1}{1.4360} \approx 0.6964$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) = 0.6964 \times \tanh(1.0933)$$

$$\tanh(1.0933) = \frac{e^{1.0933} - e^{-1.0933}}{e^{1.0933} + e^{-1.0933}} = \frac{2.9852 - 0.3350}{2.9852 + 0.3350} = \frac{2.6502}{3.3202} \approx 0.7981$$

$$h_t = 0.6964 \times 0.7981 \approx 0.5558$$

> **Final Outputs:** $C_t \approx 1.0933$ (long-term memory), $h_t \approx 0.5558$ (handed to the next cell).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Trainable Parameter Count

**Problem.** Derive the total number of trainable parameters in a single-layer LSTM with input dimension $D$ and hidden size $H$.

**Step 1 — Count the four weight matrices.**

Each gate (forget, input, candidate, output) has one weight matrix of shape $(H, H+D)$:

$$\text{Gate weights} = 4 \times H \times (H + D) = 4H^2 + 4HD$$

**Step 2 — Count the four bias vectors.**

Each gate has one bias of shape $(H, 1)$:

$$\text{Gate biases} = 4 \times H = 4H$$

**Step 3 — Sum them up.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 4H^2 + 4HD + 4H \\
&= 4H(H + D + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check for $H = 4$, $D = 3$:** $4 \times 4 \times (4 + 3 + 1) = 16 \times 8 = 128$ parameters ✓ (matches Section 3.1, Step 5).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gate Behavior Mapping

| Gate Value | Mathematical Meaning | Functional Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| $f_t \to 0$ | "Forget everything" | Completely erases the corresponding dimension of $C_{t-1}$ |
| $f_t \to 1$ | "Remember everything" | Passes $C_{t-1}$ unchanged — **error carousel** |
| $i_t \to 0$ | "Block new information" | Rejects the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ — cell remains static |
| $i_t \to 1$ | "Admit new information fully" | Candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ is added verbatim to cell state |
| $o_t \to 0$ | "Hide the memory" | Hidden state $h_t = 0$, but cell state $C_t$ is preserved |
| $o_t \to 1$ | "Expose the memory" | $h_t = \tanh(C_t)$ is fully revealed to the next layer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level LSTM Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **macro-level functional flow** of a single LSTM cell, including the three gates, the cell-state highway, and the dual output pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x_t"] --> D["Concatenation Layer"]
    B["Previous Hidden h_t-1"] --> D
    D --> F["Forget Gate f_t = sigmoid"]
    D --> IG["Input Gate i_t = sigmoid"]
    D --> CV["Candidate C_tilde_t = tanh"]
    D --> OG["Output Gate o_t = sigmoid"]
    E["Previous Cell C_t-1"] --> MUL1["Hadamard Product f_t * C_t-1"]
    F --> MUL1
    MUL1 --> ADD["Addition +"]
    IG --> MUL2["Hadamard Product i_t * C_tilde_t"]
    CV --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> ADD
    ADD --> CT["New Cell State C_t"]
    CT --> TANH["tanh squashing"]
    OG --> MUL3["Hadamard Product o_t * tanh C_t"]
    TANH --> MUL3
    MUL3 --> HT["New Hidden State h_t"]
    CT --> OUT1["Long-term Memory C_t (recurrent)"]
    HT --> OUT2["Short-term Output h_t (next cell + layer)"]
```

---

### 4.2 Detailed Unrolled Sequence Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram shows how the **cell state $C$** and **hidden state $h$** flow across **three unrolled time steps** of an LSTM. Note how $C$ travels largely unchanged while $h$ is rewritten at every step — this visualizes the **additive memory highway** design principle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0["x_1"] --> C0["LSTM Cell t=1"]
    H_PRE0["h_0 (init 0)"] --> C0
    C_PRE0["C_0 (init 0)"] --> C0
    C0 --> H0["h_1"]
    C0 --> CST0["C_1"]
    H0 --> C0_OUT["Output y_1"]

    X1["x_2"] --> C1["LSTM Cell t=2"]
    H0 --> C1
    CST0 --> C1
    C1 --> H1["h_2"]
    C1 --> CST1["C_2"]
    H1 --> C1_OUT["Output y_2"]

    X2["x_3"] --> C2["LSTM Cell t=3"]
    H1 --> C2
    CST1 --> C2
    C2 --> H2["h_3"]
    C2 --> CST2["C_3"]
    H2 --> C2_OUT["Output y_3"]

    CST0 -. "long-term memory highway (additive)" .-> CST1
    CST1 -. "long-term memory highway (additive)" .-> CST2
```

---

### 4.3 Architecture Comparison: Vanilla RNN vs LSTM

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level schematic emphasizes the **architectural difference** between a vanilla RNN cell and an LSTM cell, highlighting the introduction of gates and the dual-state memory system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VANILLA["Vanilla RNN Cell"]
        VR1["x_t"] --> VR2["Hidden Layer tanh W_h h_t-1 + W_x x_t + b"]
        VR3["h_t-1"] --> VR2
        VR2 --> VR4["h_t (single state)"]
    end

    subgraph LSTM_CELL["LSTM Cell (Augmented)"]
        LR1["x_t"] --> LR2["Concatenation [h_t-1, x_t]"]
        LR3["h_t-1"] --> LR2
        LR2 --> LR4["4 Parallel Branches: f_t, i_t, C_tilde_t, o_t"]
        LR4 --> LR5["Cell State Update C_t = f_t * C_t-1 + i_t * C_tilde_t"]
        LR6["C_t-1"] --> LR5
        LR4 --> LR7["Hidden State h_t = o_t * tanh C_t"]
        LR5 --> LR7
    end

    VANILLA -. "adds" .-> LSTM_CELL
```

---

### 4.4 Information Routing Through Gates — Functional Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The schematic below traces **what each gate mathematically permits** — useful for KTU exam drawing questions where students are asked to label "which arrow carries the forget signal."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Forget Gate f_t"] -->|"scales old memory (0 to 1)"| M1["Element-wise Multiply with C_t-1"]
    B["Input Gate i_t"] -->|"filters new candidate (0 to 1)"| M2["Element-wise Multiply with C_tilde_t"]
    C["Candidate C_tilde_t"] -->|"new info to add"| M2
    M1 --> SUM["Sum Operation +"]
    M2 --> SUM
    SUM --> C_NEW["New Cell State C_t"]
    D["Output Gate o_t"] -->|"filters exposed memory"| M3["Element-wise Multiply with tanh C_t"]
    C_NEW --> T["tanh squashing"]
    T --> M3
    M3 --> H_NEW["New Hidden State h_t"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Differentiate between the forget gate, input gate, and output gate of an LSTM cell. State the activation function used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Gate | Role | Activation | Output Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forget Gate ($f_t$)** | Decides what fraction of the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ to retain (1 = keep, 0 = erase). | **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Input Gate ($i_t$)** | Decides what fraction of the new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$ to write into the cell state. | **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Output Gate ($o_t$)** | Decides what fraction of the cell state $C_t$ to expose as the next hidden state $h_t$. | **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** | $(0, 1)$ |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for correctly naming the role and activation of all three gates.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Explain why the LSTM architecture overcomes the vanishing gradient problem faced by standard RNNs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Standard RNNs update hidden state as $h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$, causing backpropagation through time (BPTT) to involve a **product of the same weight matrix $W_h$** at every time step. When the spectral radius of $W_h$ is less than 1, the gradient decays exponentially → vanishing gradient.

LSTM overcomes this through its **additive cell-state update**:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

Because the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ enters the new cell state via **element-wise multiplication with the forget gate** (a value in $(0,1)$, not a full matrix), and the gradient of $C_t$ w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$ is the diagonal matrix $\text{diag}(f_t)$, the gradient becomes a **product of bounded diagonal matrices** rather than full weight matrices. When $f_t \approx 1$, gradients flow through hundreds of time steps without decay — the **"constant error carousel."**

> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for identifying the RNN gradient issue + 1.5 marks for explaining the additive cell-state / diagonal-gradient mechanism.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions for a module typically carry **14 marks**, internally split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. The model answers below follow the exact 2024 scheme valuation key style: **formula (1 mark) → substitution (2 marks) → simplification (2 marks) → final answer + units/interpretation (2 marks)** for 7-mark analytical questions.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Numerical Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of an LSTM cell and explain the role of each of the three gates with their governing equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an LSTM with hidden size $H = 3$ and input dimension $D = 2$, compute:
- (i) the total number of trainable parameters per cell,
- (ii) the shape of each of the four weight matrices,
- (iii) the shape of the forget-gate output tensor for a single sample (no batch).

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

> **LSTM Cell Block Diagram** (4 marks for the diagram):

```
              x_t  ──┐
                     ├──► [h_{t-1}, x_t] ──┬──► f_t = σ(W_f[..]+b_f) ──┐
              h_{t-1}─┘                    ├──► i_t = σ(W_i[..]+b_i) ──┤
                                            ├──► C̃_t = tanh(W_C[..]+b_C)─┤
                                            └──► o_t = σ(W_o[..]+b_o) ──┘
                │                                                                   
   f_t ⊙ C_{t-1}─┐                                                                   
                 ├──► (+) ──► C_t ──► tanh ──► (⊙ o_t) ──► h_t
   i_t ⊙ C̃_t  ──┘                                                                   
```

> **Role of each gate** (3 marks — 1 each):

1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$. It controls the extent to which the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ is preserved. Output close to 1 → retain memory; close to 0 → erase the corresponding dimension.

2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$. It regulates how much of the newly computed candidate $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ is written into the cell state. Multiplied element-wise with the candidate before the additive update.

3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$. It filters the squashed cell state $\tanh(C_t)$ to produce the hidden output $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$, deciding what portion of long-term memory is exposed to the next time step and next layer.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Total trainable parameters per cell (2 marks):**

Using $P_{\text{total}} = 4H(H + D + 1)$:

$$P_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 3 \times (3 + 2 + 1) = 12 \times 6 = 72 \text{ parameters}$$

**(ii) Shape of each weight matrix (2 marks):**

Each $W \in \mathbb{R}^{\,H \times (H+D)}$:

$$W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{\,3 \times 5} \quad \text{each containing } 15 \text{ scalars}$$

**(iii) Shape of the forget-gate output tensor (3 marks):**

For a **single sample (no batch)**, the input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\,2}$ and $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,3}$, so $[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{\,5}$.

The linear transformation $W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]$ is:

$$\mathbb{R}^{\,3 \times 5} \cdot \mathbb{R}^{\,5 \times 1} = \mathbb{R}^{\,3 \times 1}$$

After adding the bias $b_f \in \mathbb{R}^{\,3}$ (broadcast along the single sample) and applying $\sigma$ element-wise:

$$\boxed{f_t \in \mathbb{R}^{\,3} = (3,)}$$

The forget gate is a **3-dimensional vector** for this single sample, with each entry controlling the forget behavior of one hidden unit.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Comparative & Theoretical Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the architecture of a standard RNN cell with an LSTM cell. Why does LSTM maintain long-term dependencies better? Explain with reference to the cell-state update equation and the gradient flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a sequence of 5 tokens, each with embedding dimension $D = 4$, processed by an LSTM with hidden size $H = 6$.
- (i) Compute the parameter count of the LSTM layer.
- (ii) What is the shape of the output tensor of the LSTM after processing the full sequence (assuming `batch_first=True` and batch size $B = 8$)?
- (iii) If the forget gate's bias $b_f$ is initialized to **all ones**, what is the initial behavior of the forget gate, and why is this initialization strategy commonly used?

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Aspect | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| **States** | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Dual: cell state $C_t$ + hidden state $h_t$ |
| **Update equation** | $h_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$ | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$; $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ |
| **Gating** | None | 3 sigmoid gates + tanh candidate |
| **Gradient flow** | Product of weight matrices $W$ at every step (vanishing/exploding) | Product of diagonal matrices $\text{diag}(f_t)$ — bounded |
| **Long-term memory** | Implicit, weak | Explicit, preserved through additive update |

**Why LSTM maintains long-term dependencies (3 marks):**

The cell-state update is **additive** — the new $C_t$ receives $C_{t-1}$ via element-wise multiplication with $f_t$ (not a matrix product), and adds the new gated candidate. During backpropagation, $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$, so the gradient flowing back $k$ steps becomes:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} \text{diag}(f_{t-j+1})$$

When the forget gate values are near 1 (encouraged by initializing $b_f = 1$), this product remains bounded away from zero, allowing the gradient to **travel hundreds of steps without vanishing**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Parameter count (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 4H(H + D + 1) = 4 \times 6 \times (6 + 4 + 1) = 24 \times 11 = 264 \text{ parameters}$$

**(ii) Output tensor shape (2 marks):**

With `batch_first=True`, the LSTM outputs:

- `output` tensor of shape $(B, T, H) = (8, 5, 6)$
- `h_n` (final hidden state) of shape $(B, H) = (8, 6)$
- `c_n` (final cell state) of shape $(B, H) = (8, 6)$

**(iii) Forget-bias initialization = 1 (3 marks):**

When $b_f$ is initialized to all ones, the pre-activation of the forget gate starts as:

$$z_f = W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + \mathbf{1}$$

For random small weights, $z_f \approx 1$, so $\sigma(1) \approx 0.731$ — a value **biased toward 1** ("remember"). This is the **Gers-forget-bias initialization (Gers & Schmidhuber, 2000)**, recommended in PyTorch's `LSTM` documentation. It encourages the network to start with a strong long-term memory and gradually learn to forget when necessary, rather than starting with random forgetfulness that destroys information before the network has had a chance to learn what to keep.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in LSTM Architecture Questions (KTU Board Exam):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "gate" and "candidate"** — The forget, input, and output gates use **sigmoid**, but the **candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ uses tanh**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if a student writes "the forget gate uses tanh."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\odot$ operator** — In the cell-state update, the multiplication between $f_t$ and $C_{t-1}$ (and $i_t$ and $\tilde{C}_t$) is **element-wise (Hadamard)**, NOT matrix multiplication. Writing `f_t * C_{t-1}` is fine, but writing a matrix product loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 3. **Counting only weights, not biases** — LSTM parameter count = weights + biases. Students often quote only $4H^2 + 4HD$ and forget the $+4H$ bias term, losing 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the role of the cell state vs hidden state** — A common error: "the cell state is the output, and the hidden state is the long-term memory." It is the **opposite** — the cell state is the long-term memory, the hidden state is the exposed output.
>
> 5. **Not stating the activation function explicitly** — KTU 2024 scheme valuation keys specifically require naming the activation. Writing "$f_t = W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f$" without "$\sigma$" loses 0.5 marks per gate.
>
> 6. **Confusing LSTM with GRU** — GRU has only **2 gates (update, reset)** and merges cell state and hidden state. Do not write GRU equations when the question asks for LSTM.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for LSTM Architecture (KTU Module 3):**

- [ ] LSTM stands for **Long Short-Term Memory**, introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**.
- [ ] Three gates: **forget ($f_t$), input ($i_t$), output ($o_t$)** — all use the **sigmoid** activation $\sigma$.
- [ ] **Candidate values** $\tilde{C}_t$ use the **tanh** activation, output range $(-1, 1)$.
- [ ] **Cell state update** is **additive**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- [ ] **Hidden state**: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ — this is what is exposed externally.
- [ ] **Two memory types:** $C_t$ = long-term memory (passed across time steps); $h_t$ = short-term working memory (output).
- [ ] **Parameter count per cell:** $4H(H + D + 1)$, where $H$ = hidden size, $D$ = input dim.
- [ ] **Concatenated input** is $[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{\,H+D}$.
- [ ] **Vanishing gradient cure:** additive cell-state path makes the gradient a product of bounded diagonals $\text{diag}(f_t)$ — the **"constant error carousel."**
- [ ] **Default forget-bias init = 1** in PyTorch — encourages remembering at the start of training.
- [ ] LSTM is the **pre-Transformer** standard for sequence modeling and is still the baseline for **edge-deployable generative models** (small footprint, low latency).
- [ ] **Input to LSTM:** $(x_t, h_{t-1}, C_{t-1})$ → **Output:** $(h_t, C_t)$.
- [ ] LSTM is **deeper** architecturally than GRU (4 control points vs 2) but trains more parameters — trade-off for expressivity.
- [ ] For KTU derivations, always **state the activation function explicitly** and **distinguish Hadamard ($\odot$) from matrix multiplication**.
- [ ] LSTM solves **long-term dependency learning**, **vanishing gradient**, and enables **selective memory** — three problems in one architectural innovation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** is a gating mechanism-based variant of the standard Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, formally proposed by **Cho et al. (2014)**. It is engineered to mitigate the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** that plagues vanilla RNNs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). Unlike the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, which maintains a separate **cell state** and **hidden state** controlled by three gates (input, forget, output), the GRU **unifies the cell state and hidden state into a single hidden state vector** and uses only **two gates** — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — to control the flow of information across temporal sequences.

The two principal gating units in a GRU are:

- **Update Gate ($z_t$):** Decides how much of the past information (from previous time steps) needs to be passed along to the future. It is analogous to the combined role of the LSTM's input and forget gates.
- **Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Decides how much of the past information to **forget** or **discard** when computing the new candidate activation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GRU is a foundational building block for modern sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) models, machine translation systems, speech recognition pipelines, and **text generation models** that underpin many Generative AI applications. A deep understanding of GRU is **mandatory** for Module 3 of PCCMT602.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Editor's Notebook"

Imagine you are a **journalist** writing a long investigative article over several days. Each day, you read new information, and you have to decide:

1. **What from yesterday's notes should I carry forward into today's draft?** (This is the **Update Gate**'s job.)
2. **What from yesterday's notes is now irrelevant and should be erased?** (This is the **Reset Gate**'s job.)
3. **What new insight should I add to today's draft based on the new evidence gathered?** (This is the **Candidate Hidden State**'s job.)

The journalist's notebook is the **hidden state** $h_t$. The Update Gate acts like a **highlighter** that decides what percentage of yesterday's notes to retain, while the Reset Gate acts like an **eraser** that discards irrelevant context. The final entry on the page is a **blended mixture** of what was retained and what was newly added.

> **In one sentence:** *A GRU is an RNN that learns, at every time step, **how much of the past to remember, how much to forget, and how much new information to add** — all using just two learnable gates.*

---

## 1.3 Why GRU Was Invented — The Motivation

| Problem in Vanilla RNN | How GRU Solves It |
|------------------------|-------------------|
| Vanishing gradient during BPTT for long sequences | Gating mechanism creates a **linear path** for gradient flow |
| Inability to capture long-term dependencies | Update gate allows long-term memory to be preserved selectively |
| High parameter count in LSTM (4 gates) | GRU uses only **2 gates**, reducing parameters by ~25% |
| Slow training and inference | Fewer parameters → faster convergence |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** *GRU = "**G**ates **R**educe **U**seless **n**oise"* — just two gates, lighter than LSTM, smarter than vanilla RNN.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in GRU Evaluation

When a GRU is deployed in a Generative AI pipeline, the following metrics are commonly evaluated (per KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rubric):

- **Perplexity (PPL):** Lower is better; measures how well the probability distribution predicts samples.
- **BLEU Score:** For text generation/translation tasks (range: 0–100, higher is better).
- **Word Error Rate (WER):** For speech-based GRU applications.
- **Training Loss:** Cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$.
- **Parameter Count:** GRU has approximately **$3 \times (n^2 + n \cdot m + n)$** parameters per layer, where $n$ = hidden size, $m$ = input size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Four Core Computations

The GRU performs **four sequential vector operations** at every time step $t$. The inputs to the GRU cell at time $t$ are:
- The **previous hidden state** $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ (where $n$ is the hidden dimension)
- The **current input vector** $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ (where $m$ is the input feature dimension)

---

### **Step 1: Compute the Update Gate ($z_t$)**

The update gate is computed **first** because it controls how much of the past hidden state will be carried forward.

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

- $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$ is the trainable weight matrix for the update gate.
- $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the bias vector.
- $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes the **concatenation** of the previous hidden state and the current input.
- $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) activation function**, which squashes values into the range $(0, 1)$.
- $z_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — a vector of values between 0 and 1.

**Why sigmoid?** Because the gate output represents a **probability-like retain ratio**. A value of $z_t = 1$ means "keep 100% of the past", while $z_t = 0$ means "discard 100% of the past".

---

### **Step 2: Compute the Reset Gate ($r_t$)**

The reset gate is computed **in parallel** with the update gate.

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

- $W_r \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$ and $b_r \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ are trainable parameters.
- $r_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — element-wise reset coefficients.

**Why a separate reset gate?** It allows the model to **selectively wipe out** components of the previous hidden state that are no longer relevant, before forming the new candidate.

---

### **Step 3: Compute the Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$)**

The candidate hidden state is the **proposed new memory** for the current time step, computed using a $\tanh$ activation.

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$ is the candidate weight matrix.
- $b \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the candidate bias.
- $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ is the **element-wise (Hadamard) product** — this is where the reset gate "wipes" the past.
- $\tanh$ outputs values in $(-1, 1)$, allowing the candidate to be positive or negative.

**Key Insight:** When $r_t \approx 0$, the term $r_t \odot h_{t-1} \approx 0$, effectively **erasing the previous hidden state** and forcing the candidate to depend only on the current input $x_t$.

---

### **Step 4: Compute the Final Hidden State ($h_t$) — The Interpolation**

The final hidden state is a **convex combination** (linear interpolation) of the previous hidden state and the candidate hidden state.

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Why the "$(1 - z_t)$" term?** It acts as a **complementary retain coefficient** — if the update gate says "adopt 70% of the new candidate" ($z_t = 0.7$), then automatically it must say "keep 30% of the old" ($1 - z_t = 0.3$). This guarantees that $h_t$ is a **bounded interpolation** of the two vectors.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| **Component** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Activation** | **Output Range** | **Purpose** |
|---------------|--------------------------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | $(0, 1)$ | Controls how much past is retained |
| Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | $(0, 1)$ | Controls how much past is erased |
| Candidate State | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ | Hyperbolic Tangent | $(-1, 1)$ | Proposed new memory |
| Final Hidden State | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | Linear (identity) | $\mathbb{R}$ | Interpolated output |
| Initial State | $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (or learnable) | N/A | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Starting memory |
| Total Parameters | $3 \cdot (n^2 + n \cdot m + n)$ | N/A | Integer | Model size per layer |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \sum_i y_{t,i} \log(\hat{y}_{t,i})$ | Softmax output | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Training objective |
| BPTT Gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_t \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \prod_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ | N/A | $\mathbb{R}$ | Gradient computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall (Table Cells):** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. **Never** use the raw pipe inside formula cells — it breaks the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of GRU

| **Application Domain** | **Role of GRU** | **Industry Example** |
|------------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Machine Translation | Encodes source sentence into context vector | Google Neural MT |
| Speech Recognition | Models temporal audio frame dependencies | Apple Siri |
| Text Generation (Generative AI) | Predicts next token autoregressively | Early GPT-style models |
| Time-Series Forecasting | Captures long-range temporal patterns | Energy demand prediction |
| Music Generation | Models note sequences | Google's Magenta project |
| Video Captioning | Models frame-to-frame temporal flow | YouTube auto-captions |
| Anomaly Detection | Learns normal sequential behavior | Cybersecurity IDS |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the GRU Forward Pass — Complete Walkthrough

> **Setup:** We consider a single GRU layer processing a sequence of $T$ time steps. At each time step $t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}$, the cell receives input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

### **Derivation Step 1 — Update Gate Linear Projection**

The update gate first computes a **linear transformation** of the concatenated vector $[h_{t-1}; x_t]$:

$$a_t^{(z)} = W_z h_{t-1} + U_z x_t + b_z$$

where $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $U_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, and $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

Equivalently, by defining the augmented weight matrix $\hat{W}_z = [W_z \mid U_z] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+m)}$:

$$a_t^{(z)} = \hat{W}_z \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} + b_z$$

Applying the **sigmoid squashing** to obtain the gate activation:

$$z_t = \sigma(a_t^{(z)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a_t^{(z)}}}$$

**Interpretation:** Each element $z_t^{(j)} \in (0, 1)$ acts as a **soft switch** for the $j$-th dimension of the hidden state.

---

### **Derivation Step 2 — Reset Gate Linear Projection**

The reset gate follows the **identical structural form** as the update gate, but with its own independent weight matrix:

$$a_t^{(r)} = W_r h_{t-1} + U_r x_t + b_r$$

$$r_t = \sigma(a_t^{(r)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a_t^{(r)}}}$$

**Note on parameter independence:** Although the structure is identical, the weights $W_z \neq W_r$ and $U_z \neq U_r$ — they are **learned separately** during training via backpropagation.

---

### **Derivation Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State (Reset-Modulated)**

The candidate state computation is **structurally similar** to the gate computations, but with two critical differences:

**(a)** The previous hidden state is **modulated** by the reset gate via element-wise multiplication:

$$h_{t-1}^{\text{reset}} = r_t \odot h_{t-1}$$

**(b)** The activation is $\tanh$ instead of $\sigma$ (to allow negative values, expressing suppression or excitation):

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1}^{\text{reset}} + U x_t + b) = \tanh(W (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + U x_t + b)$$

**Limit Case Analysis:**

- If $r_t \to \mathbf{0}$ (reset gate fully closed): $\tilde{h}_t \to \tanh(U x_t + b)$ — the candidate depends **only on the current input**, ignoring history.
- If $r_t \to \mathbf{1}$ (reset gate fully open): $\tilde{h}_t \to \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$ — standard RNN-like behavior, full history preserved.

---

### **Derivation Step 4 — Final Hidden State via Linear Interpolation**

The final hidden state is a **convex combination** (also called an **interpolation gate mechanism**):

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Algebraic rearrangement** to highlight the interpolation structure:

$$h_t = h_{t-1} + z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$$

This form shows that $h_t$ equals the **previous hidden state** plus a **gated correction term** $(\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$ scaled by the update gate.

**Limit Case Analysis:**

- If $z_t \to \mathbf{0}$: $h_t \to h_{t-1}$ — **pure memory retention**; the GRU copies the previous state unchanged (this solves vanishing gradients by creating a near-identity path).
- If $z_t \to \mathbf{1}$: $h_t \to \tilde{h}_t$ — **pure candidate adoption**; the GRU behaves like a standard RNN step.

---

### **Derivation Step 5 — Output Projection (Optional, for Generative Tasks)**

For a **generative task** (e.g., next-token prediction), the hidden state $h_t$ is passed through a fully-connected output layer with a softmax activation:

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(V h_t + c)$$

where $V \in \mathbb{R}^{V_{\text{vocab}} \times n}$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}^{V_{\text{vocab}}}$.

The **cross-entropy loss** at time step $t$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{i=1}^{V_{\text{vocab}}} y_{t,i} \log(\hat{y}_{t,i})$$

And the **total sequence loss** is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$$

---

### **Derivation Step 6 — Gradient Flow (Why GRU Solves Vanishing Gradients)**

During **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, the gradient of the loss with respect to a previous hidden state is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

Computing $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ from the interpolation equation:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + \text{(additional terms involving } \frac{\partial z_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \text{ and } \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}})$$

**Key Insight:** When the update gate $z_t$ learns to be small (close to 0), the dominant term $(1 - z_t) \to 1$, which means the gradient **flows unimpeded** across many time steps — directly solving the vanishing gradient problem.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: GRU vs LSTM vs Vanilla RNN

| **Property** | **Vanilla RNN** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|--------------|------------------|----------|---------|
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 (input, forget, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Hidden states | 1 ($h_t$) | 2 ($h_t$ and $c_t$) | 1 ($h_t$) |
| Parameters per cell | $n(n+m) + n$ | $4n(n+m) + 4n$ | $3n(n+m) + 3n$ |
| Memory mechanism | None (implicit) | Explicit cell state $c_t$ | Implicit in $h_t$ |
| Vanishing gradient mitigation | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Training speed | Fast | Slow | Medium |
| Performance on short sequences | ✓ Good | ✓ Good | ✓ Good |
| Performance on long sequences | ✗ Poor | ✓ Excellent | ✓ Very Good |
| Interpretability | High | Medium | Medium |
| Generative AI suitability | Limited | High | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** If a question asks *"Which is computationally cheaper, GRU or LSTM?"* — the answer is **GRU** (≈25% fewer parameters). If it asks *"Which is better for very long sequences?"* — the answer is **LSTM** (explicit memory cell), though GRU is often competitive.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration — A Toy Forward Pass

> **Problem Setup:** Let $n = 2$ (hidden size), $m = 1$ (input size), $t = 1$, $h_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $x_1 = [1]^T$. Assume (for illustration) the following trained weights:
>
> $W_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$, $U_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $b_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$
>
> $W_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, $U_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$, $b_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$
>
> $W = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, $U = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$, $b = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$

### **Step 1: Update Gate**

$$a_1^{(z)} = W_z h_0 + U_z x_1 + b_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} (1) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$z_1 = \sigma\begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.4}} \\ \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.6}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \\ 0.6457 \end{bmatrix}$$

### **Step 2: Reset Gate**

$$a_1^{(r)} = W_r h_0 + U_r x_1 + b_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} (1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$r_1 = \sigma\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \\ 0.5744 \end{bmatrix}$$

### **Step 3: Candidate Hidden State**

$$h_0^{\text{reset}} = r_1 \odot h_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \\ 0.5744 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\tilde{h}_1 = \tanh(W \cdot h_0^{\text{reset}} + U \cdot x_1 + b) = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2913 \\ 0.6044 \end{bmatrix}$$

### **Step 4: Final Hidden State**

$$h_1 = (1 - z_1) \odot h_0 + z_1 \odot \tilde{h}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} (1-0.5987) \\ (1-0.6457) \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \\ 0.6457 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2913 \\ 0.6044 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \times 0.2913 \\ 0.6457 \times 0.6044 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1744 \\ 0.3903 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Output:** $h_1 = [0.1744, \ 0.3903]^T$ — this is the hidden state passed to the next time step and/or to the output projection layer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GRU Cell Internal Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **data flow** through a single GRU cell, showing how the update gate, reset gate, and candidate state interact to produce the final hidden state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT X_t (size m)"] --> C["CONCATENATION LAYER"]
    B["PREVIOUS HIDDEN STATE h_t-1 (size n)"] --> C
    C --> D["LINEAR PROJECTION W_z dot X + b_z"]
    C --> E["LINEAR PROJECTION W_r dot X + b_r"]
    D --> F["SIGMOID ACTIVATION"]
    E --> G["SIGMOID ACTIVATION"]
    F --> H["UPDATE GATE z_t in 0 to 1"]
    G --> I["RESET GATE r_t in 0 to 1"]
    I --> J["HADAMARD PRODUCT r_t OTO h_t-1"]
    B --> J
    J --> K["CONCATENATION WITH X_t"]
    A --> K
    K --> L["LINEAR PROJECTION W dot X + b"]
    L --> M["TANH ACTIVATION"]
    M --> N["CANDIDATE STATE h_tilde_t in -1 to 1"]
    H --> O["GATED MIXING LAYER"]
    B --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P["OUTPUT HIDDEN STATE h_t (size n)"]
    P --> Q["NEXT TIME STEP t+1"]
    P --> R["OUTPUT PROJECTION V h_t + c"]
    R --> S["SOFTMAX"]
    S --> T["PREDICTED TOKEN y_t"]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style I fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style N fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000
    style P fill:#ffe699,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style T fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Blue path** ($x_t$): The new input enters the cell.
- **Yellow path** ($h_{t-1}$): The previous hidden state enters and is **duplicated** — one copy goes to gates, the other is reset-modulated.
- **Green node** ($z_t$): The update gate — controls how much new vs. old is blended.
- **Red node** ($r_t$): The reset gate — controls how much of $h_{t-1}$ is erased before forming the candidate.
- **Purple node** ($\tilde{h}_t$): The candidate — the proposed new memory.
- **Orange node** ($h_t$): The final interpolated hidden state — the cell's output.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Unrolled GRU Across Time Steps

The diagram below **unrolls** the GRU across three time steps, illustrating the **sequential dependency** between hidden states — a key concept for BPTT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["INPUT x_1"] --> GRU1["GRU CELL t=1"]
    H0["h_0 = zero vector"] --> GRU1
    GRU1 --> H1["h_1"]
    X2["INPUT x_2"] --> GRU2["GRU CELL t=2"]
    H1 --> GRU2
    GRU2 --> H2["h_2"]
    X3["INPUT x_3"] --> GRU3["GRU CELL t=3"]
    H2 --> GRU3
    GRU3 --> H3["h_3"]
    H3 --> OUT["OUTPUT PROJECTION"]
    OUT --> PRED["PREDICTED TOKENS y_1 y_2 y_3"]
    style GRU1 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style GRU2 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style GRU3 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style H0 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    style H2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    style H3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style PRED fill:#ffe699,stroke:#806000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Block Architecture for Generative AI Pipeline

This diagram shows how a GRU is integrated into a **larger Generative AI system** for sequence generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RAW TEXT CORPUS"] --> B["TOKENIZATION LAYER"]
    B --> C["WORD EMBEDDING MATRIX E"]
    C --> D["EMBEDDING VECTORS x_1 x_2 ... x_T"]
    D --> E["GRU LAYER 1 n=256"]
    E --> F["DROPOUT LAYER p=0.3"]
    F --> G["GRU LAYER 2 n=256"]
    G --> H["DENSE LAYER V h_t + c"]
    H --> I["SOFTMAX OVER VOCABULARY"]
    I --> J["SAMPLING STRATEGY"]
    J --> K["TEMPERATURE SCALING"]
    K --> L["TOP-K OR NUCLEUS FILTERING"]
    L --> M["GENERATED TOKEN y_t"]
    M --> N["FEED BACK AS NEXT INPUT"]
    N --> D
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    style E fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style G fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style L fill:#ffe699,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style M fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

**Architectural Insight:** The GRU layers (blue) form the **core recurrent backbone**, while the surrounding components (tokenization, embedding, softmax, sampling) constitute the **generative head**. The feedback loop (token $y_t$ becomes next $x_{t+1}$) is what makes the system **autoregressive** — a fundamental pattern in modern Generative AI.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Update Gate and the Reset Gate in a GRU cell. State one advantage of GRU over LSTM.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Update Gate ($z_t$)** | **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** |
|------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| Mathematical formula | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ |
| Functional role | Decides **how much of the past** hidden state to retain | Decides **how much of the past** hidden state to **erase** |
| Where it acts | On the final interpolation $h_t = (1-z_t)h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ | On the candidate computation via $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ |
| Behavioural limit | $z_t = 0 \Rightarrow$ pure memory; $z_t = 1 \Rightarrow$ pure candidate | $r_t = 0 \Rightarrow$ erase past; $r_t = 1 \Rightarrow$ use full past |

**Advantage of GRU over LSTM:** The GRU has only **two gates** (update and reset), whereas LSTM has **three gates** (input, forget, output) plus a separate cell state. This results in approximately **25% fewer trainable parameters**, leading to **faster training and inference** while maintaining comparable performance on many sequence-modelling tasks. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With the help of a suitable equation, explain how the GRU mitigates the vanishing gradient problem during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The vanishing gradient problem occurs in vanilla RNNs because gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate through many time steps. The GRU mitigates this through its **interpolation-based hidden state update equation**:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

During BPTT, the gradient of the loss with respect to $h_{t-1}$ includes the term $(1 - z_t)$ as a multiplicative factor:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \supset (1 - z_t)$$

When the network **learns** the update gate $z_t$ to be small (close to 0), this term approaches 1, creating a **near-identity gradient path** that allows gradients to flow unimpeded across many time steps. This effectively solves the vanishing gradient problem by ensuring that **long-term dependencies** can be learned without gradient decay. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (either Q-A or Q-B) from the module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the architecture of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Derive the forward-pass equations for the update gate, reset gate, candidate state, and final hidden state. Compare GRU with LSTM in terms of parameter count and memory mechanism.**

#### **Part (a) — Architecture and Forward-Pass Equations (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A GRU cell is a gating-based recurrent unit that maintains a single hidden state $h_t$ and uses two trainable gates — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — to control information flow across time steps.

**[Block Diagram — Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of this document for the complete architecture.] (2 Marks)**

The forward-pass equations are derived as follows:

**1. Update Gate:** Computes how much of the previous hidden state to retain.

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $W_z$ and $b_z$ are trainable parameters, and $z_t \in (0, 1)$.

**2. Reset Gate:** Computes how much of the previous hidden state to erase.

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**3. Candidate Hidden State:** Computes the proposed new memory, with the previous state first reset-modulated.

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$$

**4. Final Hidden State:** Linear interpolation between old and candidate.

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**(5 Marks for the four equations with explanations)**

#### **Part (b) — GRU vs LSTM Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Comparison Parameter** | **GRU** | **LSTM** |
|--------------------------|---------|----------|
| Number of gates | 2 (update, reset) | 3 (input, forget, output) |
| State vectors | 1 ($h_t$) | 2 ($h_t$ hidden + $c_t$ cell) |
| Parameters per cell | $3n(n+m) + 3n$ | $4n(n+m) + 4n$ |
| Memory mechanism | Implicit (in $h_t$) | Explicit (separate $c_t$) |
| Training speed | Faster (fewer params) | Slower (more params) |
| Long-sequence performance | Very Good | Excellent |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher (~33% more) |
| Output nonlinearity | Single $\tanh$ in candidate | $\tanh$ + sigmoid in output gate |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** GRU is a **computationally efficient alternative** to LSTM, particularly suited for applications with **moderate sequence lengths** or **resource-constrained deployment** (e.g., edge devices, mobile NLP). LSTM remains preferable for tasks requiring **explicit long-term memory control**, such as document-level language modelling.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the vanishing gradient problem in vanilla RNNs. Describe with proper mathematical formulation how the GRU architecture addresses this limitation. Compute one forward-pass iteration of a GRU cell given a specific numerical input.**

#### **Part (a) — Vanishing Gradient Problem and GRU Solution (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Vanishing Gradient in Vanilla RNNs (3 Marks):**

In a vanilla RNN, the hidden state update is:

$$h_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$$

During BPTT, the gradient of the loss at time $T$ with respect to the hidden state at time $t$ involves repeated multiplication by the Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$ for $k = t+1, \ldots, T$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_T} \prod_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}}$$

Each Jacobian term is bounded by the spectral norm of $W$ times the derivative of $\tanh$ (which is $\leq 1$). When the eigenvalues of $W$ are less than 1, the product **decays exponentially** with $T - t$, causing the gradient to vanish. This prevents the network from learning dependencies between events separated by many time steps.

**GRU's Solution (4 Marks):**

The GRU introduces the update gate $z_t$ and rewrites the hidden state update as:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

The gradient of $h_t$ with respect to $h_{t-1}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + z_t \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} + \tilde{h}_t \cdot \frac{\partial z_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

When $z_t \approx 0$, the dominant term is $(1 - z_t) \approx 1$, which means the gradient path through $h_{t-1} \to h_t$ becomes **near-identity**, allowing gradients to flow **without decay** across many time steps. The network **learns** to set $z_t$ small when it wants to preserve long-term memory, effectively creating a "gradient highway" similar to residual connections in ResNets.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Forward-Pass Computation (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $h_0 = [0, 0]^T$, $x_1 = [1]^T$, hidden size $n = 2$, input size $m = 1$, weights and biases as in Section 3.3 of this document.

**Step 1 — Update Gate:**

$$a_1^{(z)} = W_z h_0 + U_z x_1 + b_z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_1 = \sigma\begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \\ 0.6457 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(1.5 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Reset Gate:**

$$a_1^{(r)} = W_r h_0 + U_r x_1 + b_r = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad r_1 = \sigma\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \\ 0.5744 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(1.5 Marks)**

**Step 3 — Candidate State:**

$$h_0^{\text{reset}} = r_1 \odot h_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{h}_1 = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2913 \\ 0.6044 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State:**

$$h_1 = (1 - z_1) \odot h_0 + z_1 \odot \tilde{h}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1744 \\ 0.3903 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1744 \\ 0.3903 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(2 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in GRU Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sigmoid on gates:** Students often write $z_t = W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z$ without the $\sigma$. **Always apply sigmoid to gate outputs.** (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing the Hadamard product with matrix multiplication:** The expression $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ is **element-wise multiplication**, not a dot product. Writing $r_t \cdot h_{t-1}$ (matrix product) is a common error. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Missing the $(1 - z_t)$ term:** Many students write $h_t = z_t \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ instead of $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$. The $(1 - z_t)$ is **mandatory** for convex combination. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Parameter count errors:** GRU has $3n(n+m) + 3n$ parameters, NOT $4n(n+m) + 4n$ (which is LSTM's). Mixing up the formula is a frequent mistake. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not drawing the reset gate's path in diagrams:** Examiners expect a clear arrow showing $r_t$ modulating $h_{t-1}$ before the candidate state. Missing this loses **diagram marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for GRU (Module 3)**

- ✅ **GRU = Gated Recurrent Unit**, introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)** as a simpler alternative to LSTM.
- ✅ GRU has **2 gates**: **Update Gate ($z_t$)** and **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** — fewer than LSTM's 3 gates.
- ✅ GRU has **1 hidden state** $h_t$ — unified; LSTM has 2 ($h_t$ and $c_t$).
- ✅ **Update Gate** controls **memory retention** (how much of the past to keep).
- ✅ **Reset Gate** controls **memory erasure** (how much of the past to discard).
- ✅ **Candidate state** $\tilde{h}_t$ uses $\tanh$ activation; gates use $\sigma$ (sigmoid).
- ✅ **Final state equation** is a convex combination: $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.
- ✅ The $(1 - z_t)$ term is **critical** — it creates the **gradient highway** that solves vanishing gradients.
- ✅ **Parameter count** = $3n(n+m) + 3n$ per layer (≈25% fewer than LSTM).
- ✅ GRU is **faster to train** than LSTM and often **comparable in performance**.
- ✅ Suitable for **machine translation, speech recognition, text generation, time-series forecasting**.
- ✅ **GRU vs LSTM:** Choose GRU for efficiency, LSTM for explicit long-term memory control.
- ✅ The Hadamard product ($\odot$) is **element-wise multiplication** — NOT matrix multiplication.
- ✅ Initial hidden state is typically $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (or learnable).
- ✅ GRU is a **core building block** in **Generative AI** for autoregressive text/music generation.
- ✅ **BPTT** (Backpropagation Through Time) is the training algorithm for GRU.
- ✅ **Activation functions:** Sigmoid for gates (range 0–1), Tanh for candidate (range –1 to 1), Softmax for output (probability distribution).
- ✅ **Loss function:** Cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L} = -\sum y \log(\hat{y})$ for generative tasks.
- ✅ **Reset gate limit:** $r_t = 0$ forces the candidate to depend only on current input; $r_t = 1$ gives standard RNN behaviour.
- ✅ **Update gate limit:** $z_t = 0$ copies the previous hidden state unchanged; $z_t = 1$ replaces it with the candidate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a sophisticated variant of the standard Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**. It is designed to solve the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in traditional RNNs, which prevents them from capturing **long-term dependencies** in sequential data. The GRU simplifies the more complex Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network by replacing its three gates (input, forget, output) and a separate cell state with only **two gates** — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — and a single merged hidden state that acts as both the memory and the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Distinction:** Unlike LSTM, the GRU has **no separate cell state $C_t$**. It only maintains a hidden state $h_t$ that serves the dual purpose of carrying memory and producing the output, making it computationally lighter than LSTM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Editor"

Imagine you are an **editor reading a long novel** to write a one-page summary:

- **Update Gate ($z_t$)** — Acts like a **decision filter** that asks: *"How much of the previous summary (old memory) should I keep, and how much of the new information should I incorporate?"* Sometimes the old context is irrelevant, and sometimes the new word is crucial.
- **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** — Acts like an **eraser** that asks: *"Should I completely forget the previous context to focus only on the current word?"* This is useful when the topic suddenly changes (e.g., shifting from one chapter to another).
- **Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$)** — This is the **newly drafted content** that combines the current input with the selectively reset previous memory.
- **Final Hidden State ($h_t$)** — The **final blended summary** sent forward to the next time step.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notations

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Variables in GRU:**
> - $x_t$ : Input vector at time step $t$ with dimension $d$.
> - $h_{t-1}$ : Previous hidden state with dimension $h$.
> - $z_t$ : Update gate vector with values in $\mathbf{[0, 1]}$.
> - $r_t$ : Reset gate vector with values in $\mathbf{[0, 1]}$.
> - $\tilde{h}_t$ : Candidate hidden state with values in $\mathbf{[-1, 1]}$.
> - $W_z, W_r, W, U_z, U_r, U$ : Learnable weight matrices.
> - $b_z, b_r, b$ : Learnable bias vectors.
> - **Activation functions:** Sigmoid $\sigma(\cdot)$ for gates, $\tanh(\cdot)$ for candidate state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Gates — Operational Logic

#### **A. Update Gate ($z_t$)**
The update gate is the master controller of information flow between the past and the present. It determines what content of $h_{t-1}$ to retain and what new content from the candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ to absorb.

- **Input:** Concatenation of $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$.
- **Why sigmoid?** Because we need a probability-like value between $\mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{1}$ to perform interpolation.
- **What it learns:** Which parts of the past are still relevant.

#### **B. Reset Gate ($r_t$)**
The reset gate controls how much of the past hidden state should influence the candidate state computation.

- **Why needed:** Some sequential data has sudden breaks (e.g., a song ending and a new one beginning). The reset gate allows the model to "forget" the previous context.
- **Effect:** When $r_t \approx 0$, the model treats $h_{t-1}$ as zero, computing $\tilde{h}_t$ purely from $x_t$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Computation Flow

1. **Concatenate** the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ with the current input $x_t$ to form a combined vector.
2. **Compute Update Gate $z_t$** by passing the concatenated vector through a sigmoid activation.
3. **Compute Reset Gate $r_t$** similarly using a different set of weights.
4. **Compute Candidate Hidden State $\tilde{h}_t$** by element-wise multiplying $r_t$ with $h_{t-1}$ (resetting) and then passing through $\tanh$.
5. **Interpolate** between $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$ using $z_t$ to produce the final hidden state $h_t$.
6. **Output** $h_t$ to the next time step and (optionally) to the output layer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Mathematical Equation** | **Activation** | **Range** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---------------|---------------------------|----------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot x_t + U_z \cdot h_{t-1} + b_z)$ | Sigmoid | $[0,1]$ | Controls memory retention |
| Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot x_t + U_r \cdot h_{t-1} + b_r)$ | Sigmoid | $[0,1]$ | Controls memory erasure |
| Candidate State | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot x_t + U \cdot (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b)$ | Tanh | $[-1,1]$ | Generates new memory |
| Final Hidden State | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | Linear interpolation | $\mathbb{R}$ | Output to next step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
