> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-Attention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Self-Attention** (also called *intra-attention* or *scaled dot-product attention*) is a sequence-to-sequence modeling mechanism that computes a contextualized representation of every element in an input sequence by attending to **all other elements of the same sequence**, including the element itself. Each output position is formed as a *weighted sum* of the value vectors of all positions, where the weights are derived from a compatibility function between the **Query** and the **Key** of the corresponding positions.

In the canonical Transformer formulation (introduced in *"Attention Is All You Need"*, Vaswani et al., 2017), self-attention is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

where $Q$, $K$, and $V$ are the **Query**, **Key**, and **Value** matrix projections of the same input sequence, and $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Self-Attention is the foundational building block of the Transformer architecture, which powers every modern Generative AI model (GPT, LLaMA, Gemini, Stable Diffusion's text encoder, etc.). Expect at least one full 14-mark question on this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Research Intuition

Imagine you are writing a research summary from **10 different books stacked on a desk**. For the sentence you are currently writing, every book has a different level of relevance.

- You formulate a **question** in your mind: *"Which books talk about neural networks?"* — this is the **Query (Q)**.
- Each book has a **title and index** that hints at its content — these are the **Keys (K)**.
- The **actual paragraphs** you will quote from each book are the **Values (V)**.

You **score** every book against your question (Query $\cdot$ Key), **normalize** the scores so they sum to 1 (softmax), and then take a **weighted mix of paragraphs** from all 10 books. The book most relevant to your current sentence gets the highest weight.

**Self-attention does exactly this — but every word in a sentence is simultaneously a question, an index, and a paragraph about every other word.** It is the model asking: *"For the word I am currently encoding, which other words in this sentence should I focus on, and by how much?"*

## 1.3 Why Self-Attention Replaced Recurrence

Before Transformers, models processed sequences word-by-word (RNNs/LSTMs). Self-attention offers three paradigm-shifting advantages:

1. **Parallelization** — All positions are processed simultaneously, unlike RNNs which are inherently sequential.
2. **Long-Range Dependencies** — The path length between any two tokens is **O(1)**, vs **O(n)** in RNNs.
3. **Interpretability** — The attention weights form a transparent "which-word-looks-at-which" matrix, which is a powerful debugging tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently tested):** *Self-attention* $\neq$ *Cross-attention*. In **self-attention**, Q, K, V all come from the **same** sequence. In **cross-attention**, Q comes from one sequence (e.g., decoder state) while K and V come from another (e.g., encoder output).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Projections: Q, K, V

Given an input sequence represented as a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ (where $n$ is the sequence length and $d_{\text{model}}$ is the embedding dimension), the input is projected into three different learned linear spaces using three different weight matrices:

$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

where:
- $W_Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$
- $W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$
- $W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$

These matrices are the **only learnable parameters** inside a self-attention layer (excluding the output projection). They are initialized randomly and updated via backpropagation.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Linear Projection:** Each token embedding $x_i$ is multiplied by $W_Q$, $W_K$, $W_V$ to produce query $q_i$, key $k_i$, and value $v_i$ vectors.
2. **Score Computation:** For every pair of positions $(i, j)$, compute the dot product $q_i \cdot k_j$. This measures how much token $i$ should attend to token $j$.
3. **Scaling:** Divide every score by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent the dot products from growing too large in magnitude, which would push the softmax into regions of vanishing gradients.
4. **Masking (Optional):** In causal (autoregressive) language models, future positions are masked out by adding $-\infty$ before softmax.
5. **Softmax Normalization:** Each row of the score matrix is converted into a probability distribution that sums to 1.
6. **Weighted Aggregation:** Multiply the attention weight matrix by the value matrix $V$ to produce the contextualized output.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Shape (per token) |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Q = X W_Q$ | Query projection | $d_k$ |
| 2 | $K = X W_K$ | Key projection | $d_k$ |
| 3 | $V = X W_V$ | Value projection | $d_v$ |
| 4 | $S = Q K^{\top}$ | Raw compatibility scores | $n \times n$ |
| 5 | $S' = S / \sqrt{d_k}$ | Scaled scores (controls variance) | $n \times n$ |
| 6 | $A = \text{softmax}(S')$ | Attention weight matrix | $n \times n$ |
| 7 | $Z = A V$ | Self-attention output | $d_v$ |
| 8 | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W_O$ | Multi-head attention | $d_{\text{model}}$ |
| 9 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_Q^{(i)}, K W_K^{(i)}, V W_V^{(i)})$ | Single attention head | $d_{\text{model}}/h$ |
| 10 | $\Omega(n^2 \cdot d)$ | Self-attention time complexity | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variance Justification for $\sqrt{d_k}$:** If all components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1, then their dot product $q \cdot k$ has variance $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ brings the variance back to **1**, keeping the softmax inputs in a well-behaved range. **This is a guaranteed KTU question.**

## 2.4 Multi-Head Self-Attention

Instead of computing a single attention function, the model projects Q, K, V into **$h$ different subspaces** and runs attention in parallel on each. This allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions** (e.g., one head may focus on syntactic dependencies, another on semantic similarity).

$$d_k = d_v = \frac{d_{\text{model}}}{h}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Self-attention has revolutionized:
- **Large Language Models (LLMs):** GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Claude.
- **Vision Transformers (ViT):** Patches of an image are treated as tokens.
- **Text-to-Image Generation:** Stable Diffusion and DALL-E use cross-attention to bind text tokens to image regions.
- **Speech, Genomics, Code, Time-Series:** Wherever sequential/structured data exists.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Mathematical Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention

We derive the output $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ for an input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

**Step 1 — Parameterize the learnable projections.**

Let $W_Q, W_K, W_V$ be the trainable weight matrices of shapes $(d_{\text{model}} \times d_k)$, $(d_{\text{model}} \times d_k)$, and $(d_{\text{model}} \times d_v)$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Project the input into Q, K, V.**

$$Q = X W_Q \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$$

$$K = X W_K \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$$

$$V = X W_V \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

Each row $i$ of $Q$ (denoted $q_i$) is the query vector of token $i$, etc.

**Step 3 — Compute the raw attention score matrix $S$.**

For every ordered pair $(i, j)$ of positions, the score is the dot product $q_i \cdot k_j^{\top}$. Writing this compactly:

$$S = Q K^{\top} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling.**

To stabilize gradients, divide element-wise:

$$S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply row-wise softmax.**

For each row $i$ of $S'$, the attention weights are:

$$A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ik})} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j} A_{ij} = 1$$

This yields the **attention matrix** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, where row $i$ is a probability distribution telling us "where token $i$ should look."

**Step 6 — Aggregate values.**

Each output row $z_i$ is a convex combination of the value rows of $V$:

$$Z = A V \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

**Step 7 — Consolidate into the canonical formula.**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Hall Exam Style)

Suppose we have $n = 3$ tokens with $d_{\text{model}} = 4$, and let $d_k = d_v = 2$. For pedagogical clarity, use pre-learned projection weights.

**Input matrix $X$ (3 tokens × 4 dims):**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Projection weights (assume $2 \times 4$ each):**

$$W_Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W_K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W_V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step A — Compute $Q = X W_Q^{\top}$.**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step B — Compute $K = X W_K^{\top}$.**

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Compute $V = X W_V^{\top}$.**

$$V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^{\top} \text{ applied to } X \Rightarrow V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step D — Raw scores $S = Q K^{\top}$.**

$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 4 & 12 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step E — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.**

$$S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{2}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.414 & 2.828 & 2.828 \\ 2.828 & 5.657 & 5.657 \\ 2.828 & 8.485 & 7.071 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step F — Row-wise softmax.** For row 1: $e^{1.414} = 4.113$, $e^{2.828} = 16.905$, $e^{2.828} = 16.905$. Sum $= 37.923$.

$$A_{1} = \left[ 0.108, \; 0.446, \; 0.446 \right]$$

Continuing similarly for all rows gives the attention matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.108 & 0.446 & 0.446 \\ 0.001 & 0.500 & 0.500 \\ 0.001 & 0.933 & 0.066 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step G — Compute output $Z = A V$.**

$$Z = \begin{bmatrix} 0.108 & 0.446 & 0.446 \\ 0.001 & 0.500 & 0.500 \\ 0.001 & 0.933 & 0.066 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.108 & 2.784 \\ 1.001 & 3.001 \\ 0.133 & 3.798 \end{bmatrix}$$

Each row of $Z$ is the contextualized representation of the corresponding input token.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?"*, the answer is: *To keep the variance of the dot product at 1. If $q, k$ have i.i.d. zero-mean unit-variance components, then $\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = d_k$. Large variance pushes softmax into saturated regions, killing gradients.*

## 3.3 Complexity Analysis

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| $Q K^{\top}$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d_k)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Softmax | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| $A V$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d_v)$ | $O(n \cdot d_v)$ |
| **Total Self-Attention** | $\mathbf{O(n^2 \cdot d)}$ | $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$ |
| RNN (per layer) | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(d^2)$ |
| **Bottleneck** | $n^2$ | $n^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **quadratic $n^2$** cost is the principal motivation for efficient attention variants like **Linformer**, **Performer**, **Longformer**, and **FlashAttention** (which is hardware-aware, not algorithmic).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Token Embeddings X] --> A1[Linear Projection W_Q]
    A0 --> A2[Linear Projection W_K]
    A0 --> A3[Linear Projection W_V]
    A1 --> B1[Query Matrix Q]
    A2 --> B2[Key Matrix K]
    A3 --> B3[Value Matrix V]
    B1 --> C1[Matrix Multiply Q x K transpose]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Raw Score Matrix S]
    C2 --> C3[Scale by 1 over sqrt d_k]
    C3 --> C4[Optional Mask add negative infinity]
    C4 --> C5[Row-wise Softmax]
    C5 --> C6[Attention Weight Matrix A]
    C6 --> C7[Matrix Multiply A x V]
    B3 --> C7
    C7 --> D1[Self-Attention Output Z]
    D1 --> E1[Add and Norm Residual Connection]
    E1 --> E2[Feed Forward Network]
    E2 --> E3[Add and Norm Residual Connection]
    E3 --> F1[Final Contextualized Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Head Self-Attention Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input X] --> P1[Project to Q]
    In --> P2[Project to K]
    In --> P3[Project to V]
    P1 --> H1[Head 1 Attention]
    P1 --> H2[Head 2 Attention]
    P1 --> H3[Head h Attention]
    P2 --> H1
    P2 --> H2
    P2 --> H3
    P3 --> H1
    P3 --> H2
    P3 --> H3
    H1 --> C[Concatenate Heads]
    H2 --> C
    H3 --> C
    C --> WO[Output Linear W_O]
    WO --> Out[Multi-Head Output]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Architecture of a Transformer Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InBlock[Input Stage]
        In[Token plus Positional Embedding]
    end
    subgraph SABlock[Self-Attention Sublayer]
        SA[Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention]
        AN1[Add and LayerNorm]
    end
    subgraph FFB[Feed-Forward Sublayer]
        FF1[Linear to 4 times d_model]
        R[ReLU or GELU Activation]
        FF2[Linear back to d_model]
        AN2[Add and LayerNorm]
    end
    Out[Output to Next Layer]
    In --> SA --> AN1 --> FF1 --> R --> FF2 --> AN2 --> Out
    In -.Residual.-> AN1
    AN1 -.Residual.-> AN2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Causal Masking for Autoregressive Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Compute Scaled Scores S prime] --> R2[Add Causal Mask Matrix M]
    R2 --> R3[Set upper triangle to negative infinity]
    R3 --> R4[Apply Softmax]
    R4 --> R5[Each Token attends only to past and present]
    R5 --> R6[Multiply by V]
    R6 --> R7[Autoregressive Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **causal (masked) self-attention**, used in GPT-style decoders, position $i$ can only attend to positions $\leq i$. The mask is an upper-triangular matrix filled with $-\infty$ added to $S'$ before softmax, which makes the corresponding attention weights exactly 0 after normalization.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Self-Attention. Mention its two key advantages over RNNs. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Self-Attention is a mechanism in which every position in a sequence attends to all other positions of the **same** sequence to compute a contextualized representation. Formally, given a sequence $X$, it produces:

$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

where $Q = XW_Q$, $K = XW_K$, $V = XW_V$ are projections of the same input.

**Two advantages over RNNs:**
1. **Parallelization:** All positions are processed simultaneously, whereas RNNs require sequential computation across time steps.
2. **Long-range dependencies:** Path length between any two tokens is $O(1)$ vs $O(n)$ in RNNs, eliminating the vanishing-gradient bottleneck.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for advantages)*

---

### Question 2: Why is the dot-product scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ inside the attention computation? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

If the components of the query and key vectors are independent random variables with zero mean and unit variance, then the dot product $q \cdot k$ has variance $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows, the magnitude of the dot products grows, pushing the softmax function into regions where it produces outputs very close to 0 or 1. This results in **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation.

By dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$, we normalize the variance of the dot products back to **1**, keeping the softmax inputs in a numerically stable range and ensuring smooth gradient flow.

$$\text{Scaled Scores} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

*(3 marks — 1 for variance issue, 1 for solution, 1 for formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention** mechanism. Derive the mathematical expression and explain the role of Q, K, V matrices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention mechanism takes an input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ and produces an output $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ where every position has been contextualized by attending to all other positions.

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

The input $X$ is fed in parallel to three different learnable linear layers producing $Q$, $K$, and $V$. The scores are computed as $Q K^{\top}$, scaled, optionally masked, normalized via softmax, and finally multiplied with $V$ to yield the output.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Project input:

$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

Step 2 — Compute raw scores: $S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$

Step 3 — Scale: $S' = S / \sqrt{d_k}$

Step 4 — Softmax: $A = \text{softmax}(S')$, ensuring rows sum to 1.

Step 5 — Aggregate: $Z = A V$

**Role of Q, K, V:**
- **Query (Q):** Represents "what information am I looking for?" — a token's question against all other tokens.
- **Key (K):** Represents "what information do I contain?" — used by other tokens to score relevance against their queries.
- **Value (V):** Represents "the actual content I will contribute" if selected by the attention weights.

The asymmetric roles of Q and K (despite both being projections of the same input) are what allow every token to *interrogate* every other token in a learnable, content-dependent manner. *(7 marks — 2 diagram, 3 derivation, 2 role explanation)*

---

**Q. (b)** What is **Multi-Head Self-Attention**? Explain with equations. How does it differ from single-head attention, and why is it beneficial? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs **$h$ parallel self-attention operations**, each on a different learned linear projection of Q, K, V. The outputs are concatenated and linearly projected:

$$\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W_O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_Q^{(i)}, K W_K^{(i)}, V W_V^{(i)})$$

Typically, $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$.

**Difference from Single-Head:**

| Aspect | Single-Head | Multi-Head |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Subspaces | 1 | $h$ parallel |
| Total params (Q,K,V) | $3 \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_{\text{model}}$ | Same total, split across $h$ heads |
| Information captured | One type of dependency | Multiple types simultaneously |
| Output dim | $d_v$ | $d_{\text{model}}$ (after concat) |

**Benefits:**
1. Each head can specialize — one head may learn syntactic relations, another coreference, another positional proximity.
2. The model attends to information from **different representation subspaces** at different positions.
3. Empirically yields significantly better performance at the same computational cost as a single head with full dimensionality.

*(7 marks — 2 for equations, 2 for differences, 3 for benefits)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between **Self-Attention** and **Cross-Attention**. Explain the role of masking in causal self-attention with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Self vs. Cross Attention:**

| Feature | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Source of Q | From sequence A | From sequence A (decoder) |
| Source of K, V | From sequence A (same as Q) | From sequence B (encoder) |
| Use case | Encoder/Decoder of Transformer | Decoder attending to encoder memory |
| Example | BERT, GPT encoder layer | Machine translation decoder, Stable Diffusion U-Net |

In **self-attention**, Q, K, V all derive from the same input. In **cross-attention**, Q comes from one modality and K, V from another — this is the mechanism by which text conditions an image in diffusion models.

**Causal Masking:**

In autoregressive language models (GPT), the prediction of token $t_i$ must depend only on tokens $t_1, \ldots, t_{i-1}$, never on future tokens. To enforce this, an **upper-triangular mask** $M$ is added to the scaled scores before softmax:

$$S'' = S' + M, \quad \text{where } M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases}$$

After softmax, the $-\infty$ entries become exactly 0, ensuring token $i$ cannot attend to any future token.

**Example:** Consider the sentence *"The cat sat on the ___"* while predicting the next word. Causal masking ensures the model sees only *"The cat sat on the"* and not the answer itself during training. *(7 marks — 3 for difference, 2 for masking formula, 2 for example)*

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **computational complexity** of self-attention. Explain how it scales with sequence length and embedding dimension. Why is the quadratic complexity a bottleneck, and what are the standard remedies? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Complexity Derivation:**

For an input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$:
- Computing $Q$, $K$, $V$: three matrix multiplications of cost $O(n \cdot d \cdot d_k)$, $O(n \cdot d \cdot d_k)$, $O(n \cdot d \cdot d_v)$. Combined: $O(n \cdot d^2)$.
- Computing $Q K^{\top}$: $O(n^2 \cdot d_k)$.
- Softmax over $n \times n$ matrix: $O(n^2)$.
- Multiplying $A$ by $V$: $O(n^2 \cdot d_v)$.

**Total: $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ time, $O(n^2)$ space.**

**Scaling Behavior:**
- Linear in embedding dimension $d$.
- **Quadratic in sequence length $n$** — the dominant cost for long sequences.

**Why Quadratic is a Bottleneck:**
- A sequence of length $n = 8192$ already needs an $8192 \times 8192 = 67$ million entry attention matrix.
- A context of $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ tokens (a long document) becomes infeasible at standard precision.

**Standard Remedies:**

1. **Sparse Attention (Longformer, BigBird):** Attend only to a fixed window + global tokens. Reduces to $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n \sqrt{n})$.
2. **Linear Attention (Performer, Linformer):** Approximate softmax with kernel feature maps, achieving $O(n)$.
3. **FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022):** Hardware-aware tiling that never materializes the full $n \times n$ matrix in HBM, giving $O(n^2)$ FLOPs but with $5\text{–}10\times$ wall-clock speedup and reduced memory.
4. **Multi-Query / Grouped-Query Attention (MQA, GQA):** Share K, V heads across attention heads, slashing KV-cache memory during inference.

*(7 marks — 2 for derivation, 2 for scaling explanation, 3 for remedies)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** — students frequently write $\text{softmax}(Q K^{\top}) V$ and lose 2 marks. The scaling is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V** — students sometimes write $Q = X W_V$ or swap dimensions. Remember: $W_Q$ and $W_K$ project to $d_k$, while $W_V$ projects to $d_v$.
> 3. **Mixing Self-Attention with Cross-Attention** — this is the #1 conceptual error. Self-attention has Q, K, V from the **same** source.
> 4. **Skipping the variance justification** for $\sqrt{d_k}$ — examiners award marks for explaining *why*, not just *what*.
> 5. **Not specifying shapes of matrices** — a complete answer always includes dimensions like $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$.
> 6. **Omitting the residual connection + LayerNorm** when drawing a Transformer block diagram — this is part of the standard answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Self-Attention** lets every token in a sequence look at every other token in the same sequence.
- The mechanism is parameterized by three learnable matrices: $W_Q$, $W_K$, $W_V$.
- **Canonical formula:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$.
- **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to keep dot-product variance at 1 and avoid softmax saturation.
- **Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ parallel heads, concatenates outputs, and projects via $W_O$.
- **Complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ time, $O(n^2)$ space — the quadratic $n^2$ term is the bottleneck.
- **Causal masking** uses an upper-triangular $-\infty$ mask to prevent attending to future tokens.
- **Self vs. Cross:** Self-attention uses Q, K, V from the same sequence; cross-attention uses Q from one source, K, V from another.
- **Key advantages** over RNNs: full parallelization, $O(1)$ path length between any two tokens, interpretable attention weights.
- **Practical impact:** Forms the backbone of GPT, BERT, ViT, Stable Diffusion, and every modern Generative AI system.
- **Efficient variants to remember:** Longformer (sparse), Performer (linear), FlashAttention (hardware-aware), MQA/GQA (inference-friendly).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of the Transformer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of the Transformer

# ⚙️ Analysis of the Transformer Architecture

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017):** A deep neural network architecture that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute representations of its inputs and outputs, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. It is the foundational backbone of modern Generative AI models such as **GPT, BERT, T5, and LLaMA**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine reading a sentence in a noisy classroom. Your brain does not process each word one-by-one in sequence. Instead, it **simultaneously focuses on the most relevant words** in the sentence to understand the meaning of any given word. The Transformer mimics this cognitive process.

Consider the sentence: *"The animal didn't cross the street because **it** was too tired."*

- To figure out what **"it"** refers to, your mind instantly links it back to **"animal"**, ignoring "street".
- A Transformer does this mathematically using **Self-Attention**: for every word, it computes a weighted "attention score" against every other word in the sequence, **in parallel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from RNNs/LSTMs:**
> - RNNs process tokens **sequentially** (slow, vanishing gradient).
> - Transformers process tokens **in parallel** (fast, captures long-range dependencies in **O(1)** path length).

### Core Components of a Transformer Block

| Component | Function | Role in Generative AI |
|---|---|---|
| **Query (Q)** | Represents "what information am I looking for?" | Drives generation/prediction |
| **Key (K)** | Represents "what information do I contain?" | Used for matching with queries |
| **Value (V)** | Represents "the actual content to be aggregated" | The information passed forward |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | Runs multiple attention sub-mechanisms in parallel | Captures diverse relationships |
| **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** | Position-wise non-linear transformation | Adds representational depth |
| **Positional Encoding** | Injects sequence order information | Compensates for lack of recurrence |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Self-Attention Mechanism — Logical Breakdown

The Transformer's core is **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**. The steps are:

1. **Input Projection:** Each input embedding $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ is linearly projected into three vectors: **Query** ($q_i$), **Key** ($k_i$), and **Value** ($v_i$).
   $$q_i = x_i W^Q, \quad k_i = x_i W^K, \quad v_i = x_i W^V$$
   where $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$.

2. **Compatibility Scoring:** Compute the dot product between the Query of token $i$ and the Keys of **all** tokens $j$. This gives a raw relevance score.
   $$e_{ij} = \frac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$
   The $\sqrt{d_k}$ **scaling factor** prevents the softmax from saturating into extremely small gradients when $d_k$ is large.

3. **Softmax Normalization:** Convert raw scores into a probability distribution (attention weights) that sums to **1**.
   $$\alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}_j(e_{ij}) = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(e_{ik})}$$

4. **Weighted Aggregation:** Multiply each attention weight by its corresponding Value vector and sum.
   $$z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} v_j$$

### 2.2 Multi-Head Attention — Why "Multi-Head"?

A single attention head can only capture **one type of relationship** per layer. Multiple heads allow the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions.

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{model}}$ is the final output projection matrix, and typically $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$.

### 2.3 Positional Encoding (Sinusoidal)

Since Transformers have no inherent notion of sequence order, positional information must be injected:

$$PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

$$PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sinusoid's wavelengths form a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$. This allows the model to **easily learn to attend by relative positions** because $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | Core attention equation |
| 2 | $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ | Projection matrices | Learned parameters |
| 3 | $h \cdot d_k = d_{model}$ | Head dimension constraint | Keeps total cost constant |
| 4 | $\Omega(\text{Attention}) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)$ | Per-layer complexity | $n$ = sequence length |
| 5 | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(\cdot)$, $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(\cdot)$ | Sinusoidal positional encoding | Adds order to parallel attention |
| 6 | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise feed-forward | Two linear layers + ReLU |
| 7 | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Layer normalization | Stabilizes training |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Generative AI:** Transformers power **text generation** (GPT), **image generation** (Vision Transformers, DALL-E), **code generation** (Codex, Copilot), **speech synthesis** (Whisper), and **multimodal reasoning** (CLIP, Flamingo). Understanding its analysis is essential for designing, fine-tuning, or compressing any modern foundation model.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Scaled Dot-Product Attention

We start from the raw scaled scores and walk through to the output vector. Let $n$ denote the sequence length.

**Step 1 — Compute raw dot products.**  
For each query $q_i$, score it against every key $k_j$:

$$S_{ij} = q_i \cdot k_j = \sum_{m=1}^{d_k} q_{im} \, k_{jm}$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$.**  
The variance of $q_i \cdot k_j$ (assuming $q_i, k_j$ have zero mean and unit variance) is $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to **1**, preventing softmax saturation.

$$\hat{S}_{ij} = \frac{S_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply row-wise softmax.**  
For each row $i$, exponentiate and normalize so the attention weights sum to 1:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{l=1}^{n} \exp(\hat{S}_{il})}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted sum of values.**  
The output $z_i$ is the convex combination of value vectors:

$$z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} \, v_j$$

**Step 5 — Compact matrix form.**  
Stacking all queries, keys, values into matrices $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V = Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

where the softmax is applied row-wise. This entire operation is **differentiable end-to-end**, enabling gradient-based training via backpropagation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Computational Complexity

Let us analyze the cost per layer:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost of } QK^{\top} & = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_k) \\
\text{Cost of softmax} & = \mathcal{O}(n^2) \\
\text{Cost of } (\cdot) V & = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_v) \\
\text{Total Attention cost} & = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{model}) \\
\text{FFN cost} & = \mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_{model}^2) \\
\text{Total per layer} & = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_{model} + n \cdot d_{model}^2)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bottleneck:** Self-attention is **quadratic in sequence length $n$**. This is the central engineering challenge in modern LLMs — motivating efficient variants like **Linformer** ($O(n)$), **Performer** (kernel-based), **Longformer** (sliding window), and **FlashAttention** (IO-aware tiling).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding Generalization

We want $PE_{pos+k}$ to be expressible as a **linear function** of $PE_{pos}$ for any offset $k$.

Using the identity $\sin(a+b) = \sin a \cos b + \cos a \sin b$:

$$\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i)} & = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos+k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \\
& = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **linear combination** of $PE_{(pos, 2i)}$ and $PE_{(pos, 2i+1)}$ with coefficients depending **only on $k$**. Hence the model can learn relative-position attention by a simple linear projection. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Transformer vs. RNN vs. CNN

| Property | Transformer | RNN / LSTM | CNN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parallelization** | Full (per layer) | None (sequential) | Full (per layer) |
| **Path length (long-range deps)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log_k n)$ |
| **Per-layer complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n \cdot d^2)$ |
| **Inductive bias** | Minimal (learned) | Sequential | Locality + translation |
| **Data efficiency (small data)** | Lower (data-hungry) | Higher | Higher |
| **Generative AI dominance** | **Dominant** (2017–today) | Legacy (2013–2017) | Hybrid use |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Token Embeddings X] --> B[Add Positional Encoding PE]
    B --> C[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    C --> D[Add and LayerNorm 1]
    D --> E[Feed Forward Network FFN]
    E --> F[Add and LayerNorm 2]
    F --> G[Encoder Output Nx times]

    H[Output Embeddings shifted right] --> I[Add Positional Encoding]
    I --> J[Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    J --> K[Add and LayerNorm 1]
    K --> L[Cross Attention with Encoder]
    L --> M[Add and LayerNorm 2]
    M --> N[Feed Forward Network]
    N --> O[Add and LayerNorm 3]
    O --> P[Linear and Softmax]
    P --> Q[Output Probabilities]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Scaled Dot-Product Attention Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input X] --> B[Linear WQ]
    A --> C[Linear WK]
    A --> D[Linear WV]
    B --> E[Queries Q]
    C --> F[Keys K]
    D --> G[Values V]
    E --> H[MatMul QKT]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Scale divide by sqrt dk]
    I --> J[Mask optional]
    J --> K[Softmax row wise]
    K --> L[MatMul with V]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Output Z]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Head Attention Internal Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input X] --> B1[Head 1 WQ1 WK1 WV1]
    A --> B2[Head 2 WQ2 WK2 WV2]
    A --> B3[Head h WQh WKh WVh]
    B1 --> C1[Scaled Dot Product Attention]
    B2 --> C2[Scaled Dot Product Attention]
    B3 --> C3[Scaled Dot Product Attention]
    C1 --> D[Concatenate]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Linear Projection WO]
    E --> F[Multi Head Output]
```

### 4.4 Token-Level Attention Weight Visualization (Conceptual Heatmap)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[The] -->|alpha=0.10| H((Attention Matrix))
    B[animal] -->|alpha=0.45| H
    C[didnt] -->|alpha=0.05| H
    D[cross] -->|alpha=0.08| H
    E[street] -->|alpha=0.12| H
    F[because] -->|alpha=0.04| H
    G[it] -->|alpha=0.16| H
    H --> I[Strong weight on animal confirms coreference]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**  
**Define self-attention and explain in one sentence why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of every position in a sequence by comparing its **Query** with the **Keys** of all other positions, then aggregating the corresponding **Values** using softmax-normalized weights.
> The scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary because the dot products $q_i \cdot k_j$ grow in magnitude as $d_k$ increases, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients and causing **vanishing gradient** during training. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
**What is positional encoding in a Transformer? Why can't the vanilla Transformer process ordered sequences without it?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Positional encoding is a vector (typically sinusoidal) that is **added** to input token embeddings to inject information about the position of each token in the sequence.
> A vanilla Transformer processes all tokens **in parallel** using attention, which is a **permutation-equivariant** operation — meaning it produces the same output regardless of input order. Without positional encoding, the sentences *"dog bites man"* and *"man bites dog"* would be indistinguishable, making sequence order inaccessible to the model.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q.A(a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**  
**(7 Marks)** Explain the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** mechanism. Derive the mathematical expression for $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V)$ and clearly state the role of the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
> Scaled Dot-Product Attention computes the output as a weighted sum of **Value** vectors, where the weights are determined by the compatibility between **Query** and **Key** vectors, normalized by a softmax function.
>
> **Step 2 — Mathematical Derivation (3 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Raw score: } & S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \\
> \text{Scaled score: } & \hat{S} = \dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \\
> \text{Attention weights: } & A = \text{softmax}(\hat{S}), \quad A_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{l=1}^{n}\exp(\hat{S}_{il})} \\
> \text{Output: } & \text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A \cdot V
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Role of $\sqrt{d_k}$ (2 marks):**
> Assuming $q_i, k_j$ are independent random variables with zero mean and unit variance, the dot product $q_i \cdot k_j$ has variance $d_k$. For large $d_k$ (e.g., 64 or 128), the dot products become very large in magnitude, pushing softmax into saturation regions where gradients vanish. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores variance to ~1, preserving stable gradients during backpropagation.

---

**Q.A(b) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**  
**(7 Marks)** Describe **Multi-Head Attention**. Explain why multiple heads are used instead of a single head, and derive the expression $\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O$.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Motivation (2 marks):**
> A single attention head can only attend to one type of dependency at a time (e.g., syntactic vs. semantic). Multiple heads allow the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions. For instance, one head may learn subject–verb agreement, while another learns coreference resolution.
>
> **Step 2 — Construction (3 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{head}_i & = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\, K W_i^K,\, V W_i^V), \quad i = 1,\dots,h \\
> W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}, & \quad W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}, \quad W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v} \\
> \text{where typically } d_k = d_v & = d_{model}/h
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Final Output (2 marks):**
> The $h$ head outputs (each of dimension $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$) are concatenated along the feature axis, producing a tensor of shape $\mathbb{R}^{n \times h d_v}$. A final linear projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{model}}$ maps this back to the model dimension:
>
> $$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O$$
>
> This concatenation preserves all $h$ sub-space representations while allowing the model to learn how to combine them via $W^O$.

---

**Q.B(a) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**  
**(7 Marks)** Analyze the **computational complexity** of a Transformer layer. Show that self-attention is $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)$ while the feed-forward network is $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d^2)$, and discuss the implications for long sequences.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Self-Attention Cost (3 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> QK^{\top} & : \mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_k \cdot n) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 d) \\
> \text{Softmax} & : \mathcal{O}(n^2) \\
> (\cdot) V & : \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d_v) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 d) \\
> \text{Total Attention} & = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 2 — FFN Cost (2 marks):**
> Each position independently passes through two linear layers of dimension $d_{model} \to d_{ff} \to d_{model}$ (typically $d_{ff} = 4 d_{model}$):
>
> $$\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_{model} \cdot d_{ff}) + \mathcal{O}(n \cdot d_{ff} \cdot d_{model}) = \mathcal{O}(n \cdot d^2)$$
>
> **Step 3 — Implications (2 marks):**
> For sequences longer than ~4096 tokens, the **quadratic $n^2$ term dominates**, making self-attention the bottleneck. This has motivated a family of **efficient Transformers**:
> - **Sparse Attention** (Longformer, BigBird) — restricts attention to local windows.
> - **Linear Attention** (Performer, Linformer) — kernel/low-rank approximations yielding $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
> - **Memory-efficient kernels** (FlashAttention) — reduces IO between HBM and SRAM.
> For typical LLM training, $d_{model} = 4096$–$12288$ and $n = 2048$–$32768$, putting extreme pressure on GPU memory and compute.

---

**Q.B(b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`**  
**(7 Marks)** Explain **sinusoidal positional encoding** with its full mathematical formulation. Show why it allows the model to attend by **relative position**.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Necessity (1 mark):**
> Self-attention is permutation-equivariant. Without positional information, two sentences differing only in word order yield identical outputs.
>
> **Step 2 — Formulation (2 marks):**
>
> $$PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$
>
> Each dimension $i$ of the encoding corresponds to a sinusoid of wavelength $10000^{2i/d_{model}} \cdot 2\pi$, forming a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$.
>
> **Step 3 — Relative Position Property (4 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i)} & = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos+k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \\
> & = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \\
> & = PE_{(pos,\,2i)} \cdot u_{2i}(k) + PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} \cdot u_{2i+1}(k)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> where $u_{2i}(k), u_{2i+1}(k)$ are functions **only of the offset $k$**. Therefore $PE_{pos+k}$ is a **linear function of $PE_{pos}$**, and the attention layer can learn to translate between positions using only a learned linear projection — enabling relative-position attention naturally.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor:** Many students write $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$ instead of $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. You **lose 1–2 marks** for omitting $\sqrt{d_k}$ because the rationale is the *most-tested* part of the formula.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V dimensions:** $W^Q$ and $W^K$ project to $d_k$, while $W^V$ projects to $d_v$. These are *not* all equal to $d_{model}$.
> 3. **Skipping the complexity analysis:** Simply stating $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ without showing the matrix-multiply cost derivation loses marks in Part B.
> 4. **Omitting LayerNorm / Residual connections:** A Transformer *block* is incomplete without Add & Norm; mentioning them shows architectural depth.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Transformer** replaces recurrence with **self-attention**, enabling full parallelization and $\mathcal{O}(1)$ path length for long-range dependencies.
- ✅ Core equation: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.
- ✅ Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is essential — without it, softmax saturates and gradients vanish.
- ✅ **Multi-Head Attention** captures diverse relational subspaces; heads run in parallel and are concatenated, then projected by $W^O$.
- ✅ **Positional encoding** (sinusoidal or learned) injects order; sin/cos enables relative-position attention via linear transforms.
- ✅ **Computational bottleneck** is the $\mathcal{O}(n^2 d)$ attention cost for long sequences — motivating efficient variants (FlashAttention, Longformer, Performer).
- ✅ Each sub-layer has a **residual connection + LayerNorm** for stable training.
- ✅ Transformers are the **backbone of every modern Generative AI system** — GPT, BERT, T5, LLaMA, Stable Diffusion, Sora.
- ✅ Encoder–Decoder vs. Decoder-only vs. Encoder-only architectures serve different generative tasks (translation, generation, understanding).
- ✅ Remember the KTU 2024 CO mapping: this topic directly maps to **CO3 — Analyze advanced deep learning architectures for generative tasks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BERT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BERT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a deep bidirectional, pre-trained natural language processing (NLP) model introduced by Devlin et al. (Google, 2018). Architecturally, BERT is built by stacking multiple **Transformer Encoder** blocks (originally $L=12$ for BERT-Base and $L=24$ for BERT-Large). Unlike unidirectional (left-to-right) language models, BERT is pre-trained on two unsupervised tasks simultaneously:
1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** — predicting randomly masked tokens using bidirectional context.
2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** — predicting whether sentence B logically follows sentence A.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> BERT is the canonical *encoder-only* Transformer. It generates **contextualized embeddings** — the same word in different sentences gets a different vector representation, which is a high-weight exam concept.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE] **The "Fill-in-the-Blank Detective" Analogy**
> Imagine a student who reads an entire detective novel in **one go**, both forwards and backwards simultaneously, rather than page by page. Whenever the author blankens out a key word (e.g., "The ____ was found guilty"), the student uses the **entire surrounding story** (left context + right context) to guess the word. That is exactly how BERT works:
> - **Bidirectionality** = seeing words from both sides (left and right of the masked token).
> - **Pre-training** = reading millions of sentences beforehand to learn grammar, semantics, and world knowledge.
> - **Fine-tuning** = specializing that "reading skill" for a specific exam question (e.g., sentiment analysis, question answering).

### 1.3 Core Variants (Standard Metrics)
| Variant | Layers ($L$) | Hidden Size ($H$) | Attention Heads ($A$) | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BERT-Base** | **12** | 768 | 12 | $\approx 110$ M |
| **BERT-Large** | **24** | 1024 | 16 | $\approx 340$ M |

> [!TIP] A single Transformer encoder block in BERT has a parameter count given approximately by $12 H^2 \times L$ — a key relationship examiners love to test for size estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Input Representation
Every BERT input is a sequence composed of three distinct embedding components that are **summed element-wise**:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$$

- **Token Embeddings**: Sub-word units via **WordPiece** tokenizer (vocabulary size $\approx 30{,}000$).
- **Segment Embeddings**: Distinguishes Sentence A from Sentence B (values $E_A$ or $E_B$).
- **Position Embeddings**: Learned absolute position vectors (max length **512 tokens**).

> [!NOTE] **Special Tokens**:
> - `[CLS]` — Classification token placed at position 0. Its final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^H$ serves as the aggregate sequence representation.
> - `[SEP]` — Sentence separator token.
> - `[MASK]` — The placeholder token used for MLM pre-training.

### 2.2 Transformer Encoder Internals
Each encoder layer applies **Layer Normalization** after every sub-block (a hallmark of BERT vs. the original Transformer, which used Post-LN).

$$\text{Output}_{\text{block}} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$$

The two sublayers are:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA)**
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**

### 2.3 Pre-Training Task #1: Masked Language Modeling (MLM)
- **15%** of input tokens are randomly selected for prediction.
- Of those 15%:
  - **80%** → replaced with `[MASK]`
  - **10%** → replaced with a random token
  - **10%** → kept unchanged

> [!IMPORTANT] The 10% noise injection prevents a mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning (because `[MASK]` never appears during fine-tuning). This is a frequently-asked **2-mark KTU question**.

### 2.4 Pre-Training Task #2: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
- Input pairs (A, B) sampled with a **50/50** distribution: 50% true consecutive, 50% random.
- Binary classification on the `[CLS]` output vector.
- Later variants like **RoBERTa** showed NSP offers marginal benefit, leading to its removal.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Token-to-token contextual mixing |
| Multi-Head | $\text{MHSA} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | Parallel attention subspaces |
| FFN Block | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Per-token non-linear transform |
| Total Params (approx) | $P \approx 12 L H^2$ | Size estimation for variants |
| MLM Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \text{mask}} \log P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i})$ | Pre-training objective 1 |
| NSP Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}} = -\log P(\text{IsNext} \mid C)$ | Pre-training objective 2 |
| Joint Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{NSP}}$ | Optimized during pre-training |
| Scaling factor | $\sqrt{d_k}$ | Prevents softmax saturation in attention |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
We begin with raw similarity scores between a query and all keys.

**Step 1 — Raw Score Matrix:**
$$S = QK^{\top}, \quad S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$
where $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$. The dot product measures how strongly the $i$-th query attends to the $j$-th key.

**Step 2 — Variance Stabilization:**
Each entry $S_{ij}$ is a sum of $d_k$ products of zero-mean unit-variance terms, so $\text{Var}(S_{ij}) = d_k$. We divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to normalize variance back to 1:
$$S' = \frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Scores to Probabilities (Softmax over keys):**
$$A = \text{softmax}(S') = \frac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ik})}$$
Here $A$ is the attention weight matrix with $\sum_j A_{ij} = 1$ for every row $i$.

**Step 4 — Weighted Sum of Values:**
$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

> [!NOTE] The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling is **critical**: without it, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into regions of near-zero gradient, blocking learning. This is a classic 3-mark KTU question.

### 3.2 Derivation: BERT-Base Parameter Count (Approximate)
We estimate the trainable parameters in the **self-attention + FFN** of one encoder layer.

**Step 1 — Self-Attention Projections ($W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$):**
$$P_{\text{attn}} = 4 \times (H \times H) = 4 H^2$$

**Step 2 — Feed-Forward Network ($W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times 4H}$ and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{4H \times H}$):**
$$P_{\text{FFN}} = H(4H) + (4H)H = 8 H^2$$

**Step 3 — Per-Layer Total:**
$$P_{\text{layer}} = 4 H^2 + 8 H^2 = 12 H^2$$

**Step 4 — Stack Over $L$ Layers:**
$$P_{\text{enc}} \approx 12 L H^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute BERT-Base Values ($L=12$, $H=768$):**
$$P_{\text{enc}} = 12 \times 12 \times 768^2 = 144 \times 589{,}824 \approx 84.9 \text{ M parameters}$$

**Step 6 — Add Embedding Matrix + Pooler:**
The token embedding is a $V \times H$ matrix. With $V \approx 30{,}522$ and $H=768$:
$$P_{\text{embed}} = 30{,}522 \times 768 \approx 23.4 \text{ M}$$
Grand total: $P_{\text{total}} \approx 110$ M, matching the published figure. ✅

### 3.3 Derivation: MLM Loss for a Single Masked Token
Cross-entropy between the predicted distribution $P_\theta(\cdot \mid x_{\setminus i})$ and the one-hot ground truth $e_{x_i}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}}^{(i)} &= - \sum_{v=1}^{V} e_{x_i}[v] \log P_\theta(v \mid x_{\setminus i}) \\
&= - \log P_\theta(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i}) \\
&= - \log \frac{\exp(\text{logit}_{x_i})}{\sum_{v=1}^{V} \exp(\text{logit}_v)}
\end{aligned}$$

The total loss is summed over **only the masked positions**, then averaged over the batch.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BERT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Input Tokens with CLS and SEP"]
    tokEmb["Token Embedding Layer"]
    segEmb["Segment Embedding A or B"]
    posEmb["Positional Embedding 0 to 511"]
    sumOp["Elementwise Sum Operator"]
    encStack["Stacked Transformer Encoders L equals 12 or 24"]
    mhsa["Multi Head Self Attention Sublayer"]
    ffn["Feed Forward Network Sublayer"]
    layNorm["Layer Normalization"]
    outVec["Contextualized Token Vectors"]
    clsOut["CLS Vector for Classification"]
    mlmHead["MLM Prediction Head Softmax over V"]
    nspHead["NSP Classification Head Sigmoid"]

    inputA --> tokEmb
    inputA --> segEmb
    inputA --> posEmb
    tokEmb --> sumOp
    segEmb --> sumOp
    posEmb --> sumOp
    sumOp --> encStack
    encStack --> mhsa
    mhsa --> layNorm
    layNorm --> ffn
    ffn --> layNorm
    layNorm --> outVec
    outVec --> clsOut
    outVec --> mlmHead
    clsOut --> nspHead
```

### 4.2 End-to-End Pre-training then Fine-tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    corpus["Large Unlabeled Corpus Books and Wikipedia"]
    mlm["Masked Language Modeling 15 percent masking"]
    nsp["Next Sentence Prediction 50 percent true pairs"]
    pretrained["Pretrained BERT Backbone"]

    corpus --> mlm
    corpus --> nsp
    mlm --> pretrained
    nsp --> pretrained

    pretrained --> ft1
    pretrained --> ft2
    pretrained --> ft3
    pretrained --> ft4

    ft1["Finetune for Sentiment Classification"]
    ft2["Finetune for Question Answering SQuAD"]
    ft3["Finetune for Named Entity Recognition"]
    ft4["Finetune for Textual Entailment NLI"]
```

### 4.3 Attention Pattern Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1["Query Token He"]
    k1["Key Token likes"]
    k2["Key Token coffee"]
    k3["Key Token MASK"]
    k4["Key Token today"]
    a1["Attention Weight High"]
    a2["Attention Weight Medium"]
    a3["Attention Weight Highest"]
    a4["Attention Weight Low"]
    out["Context Vector for MASK"]

    q1 --> a3
    k3 --> a3
    a3 --> out
    k1 --> a2 --> out
    k2 --> a1 --> out
    k4 --> a4 --> out
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three distinguishing features of BERT compared to a traditional left-to-right language model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Bidirectional context**: BERT uses self-attention over the full sequence, so each token attends to both left and right context simultaneously. (1 mark)
2. **Pre-training via MLM and NSP** instead of next-token prediction, enabling deeper semantic understanding. (1 mark)
3. **Transformer-Encoder-only architecture** with no decoder, optimized for producing representations rather than generating text. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Why does BERT replace 10% of the selected masked tokens with a random word instead of leaving all of them as `[MASK]`?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Because `[MASK]` never appears during downstream fine-tuning, leaving all 15% as `[MASK]` causes a **train-test distribution mismatch**. Replacing 10% with random tokens and keeping 10% unchanged forces the model to rely on **contextual information** rather than the explicit `[MASK]` symbol, producing representations that generalize well to fine-tuning. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mark-losing mistakes in BERT questions:
> 1. Confusing **BERT (encoder)** with **GPT (decoder)** — examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. Forgetting the **$\sqrt{d_k}$** in attention — KTU has standardized on this scaling.
> 3. Writing that BERT is **unidirectional** — instant 0 for "explain bidirectionality".
> 4. Missing the **80/10/10 split** in MLM masking strategy.
> 5. Stating BERT can do **text generation** natively — wrong, it is representation-focused.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — *Architecture Deep-Dive***

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the complete input representation pipeline of BERT with the special tokens and the three embedding types. Show the final summation formula.

**Model Solution:**
BERT accepts a single sequence of tokens (single or paired sentences) capped at **512 tokens**. Three embeddings are constructed and summed:
1. **Token Embedding $E_{\text{tok}}$** — Sub-word units from **WordPiece** (vocab $\approx 30$k). The sequence is prefixed with `[CLS]` and separated by `[SEP]`. (2 marks)
2. **Segment Embedding $E_{\text{seg}}$** — A learned vector per sentence (E_A for sentence A, E_B for sentence B). (1 mark)
3. **Position Embedding $E_{\text{pos}}$** — Learned absolute position vectors for indices $0$ to $511$. (1 mark)

The total input is:
$$E = E_{\text{tok}} + E_{\text{seg}} + E_{\text{pos}} \quad (1)$$
followed by LayerNorm and dropout. (1 mark)
Mentioning `[CLS]` and `[SEP]` with their roles. (2 marks)

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the scaled dot-product attention formula and justify the role of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ term. Show its effect on gradient flow.

**Model Solution:**
Step 1: Compute raw scores $Q K^{\top}$. (1 mark)
Step 2: Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to normalize variance. (2 marks)
Step 3: Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights. (1 mark)
Step 4: Multiply by $V$ to obtain contextualized outputs. (1 mark)
Final formula:
$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$
Justification (2 marks): Without scaling, dot-products grow as $\mathcal{O}(d_k)$, pushing softmax into saturation zones where gradients vanish. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the softmax input variance at 1, preserving smooth gradient flow for stable training of large encoder stacks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — *Pre-training & Fine-tuning Application***

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe BERT's pre-training tasks (MLM and NSP) in detail. Include the **80/10/10** strategy and justify why NSP helps downstream sentence-pair tasks.

**Model Solution:**
- **MLM (4 marks):** 15% of tokens are candidates. Of those: 80% → `[MASK]`, 10% → random token, 10% → unchanged. The model predicts the original token at every masked position using **both left and right context**. The 10% noise prevents the model from learning to rely on the `[MASK]` symbol alone.
- **NSP (3 marks):** Sentence pairs (A, B) are formed — 50% with B being the *true* next sentence, 50% with B being a *random* sentence. The `[CLS]` final hidden state $C$ is fed to a binary classifier predicting `IsNext`. This teaches BERT **inter-sentence coherence**, vital for tasks like Question Answering and Natural Language Inference.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain BERT fine-tuning for a **text classification** task. Show the architectural changes and describe the loss function.

**Model Solution:**
- Replace BERT's pre-training heads with a randomly-initialized classification layer on top of the `[CLS]` vector $C \in \mathbb{R}^H$. (2 marks)
- Compute class probabilities:
$$P(y \mid x) = \text{softmax}(W C + b), \quad W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times H}$$
where $K$ is the number of classes. (2 marks)
- Cross-entropy loss:
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(y_i \mid x_i)$$
- All BERT weights are updated with a **low learning rate** (e.g., $2 \times 10^{-5}$). (1 mark)
- Discuss benefits: only a small classifier is added, yet the entire 110M-parameter model adapts to the task. (2 marks)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BERT = Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers** — encoder-only.
- Pre-trained on a huge **BookCorpus + English Wikipedia** corpus using two objectives: **MLM** and **NSP**.
- Input = `[CLS] tokens_A [SEP] tokens_B [SEP]` with summed Token + Segment + Position embeddings.
- **MLM masking rule (high-yield):** 15% of tokens are selected → of those 15%, **80% MASK / 10% random / 10% unchanged**.
- **NSP** is a binary classifier on `[CLS]` (50/50 true/false pairs).
- **Bidirectionality** comes from the self-attention mask being all-ones (no causal mask).
- **Attention formula** is the standard scaled dot-product with the $\sqrt{d_k}$ term.
- **Architecture variants:** BERT-Base ($L=12, H=768, A=12, 110$M params) and BERT-Large ($L=24, H=1024, A=16, 340$M params).
- **Fine-tuning** typically uses learning rate in the range $2\text{e-}5$ to $5\text{e-}5$ for 2–4 epochs.
- **Successor models to remember:** **RoBERTa** (removes NSP, dynamic masking), **ALBERT** (parameter sharing, factorized embeddings), **DistilBERT** (40% smaller, 60% faster, retains 97% performance), **SpanBERT** (span-based MLM).
- BERT is **not** a generative model — it produces embeddings, not free-form text (unlike GPT).
- **Application domains:** Sentiment analysis, NER, Question Answering (SQuAD), Search Ranking, Token Classification, Sentence Pair tasks.
- Total parameter approximation: $P \approx 12 L H^2$ for encoder weights.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPT-2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GPT-2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GPT-2 (Generative Pre-trained Transformer 2)** is a large-scale, unsupervised **autoregressive language model** released by **OpenAI in February 2019**. It belongs to the family of **decoder-only Transformer** architectures and is pre-trained on a massive corpus called **WebText (40 GB of text from 8 million web pages)**. GPT-2 is fundamentally a **next-token prediction model** that estimates the conditional probability distribution:

$$P(x_t \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

where $x_t$ is the next token given the preceding context. The full joint probability of a sequence is decomposed via the **chain rule of probability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GPT-2 is studied as the *pivotal transition* from task-specific NLP models to **prompt-based, zero-shot, multi-task learners**. It is the conceptual predecessor of GPT-3, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and modern LLMs.

GPT-2 was released in **four sizes** to enable comparative analysis of model scaling:

| Variant | Parameters | Layers ($n_{layer}$) | $d_{model}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GPT-2 Small** | **117 M** | 12 | 768 |
| **GPT-2 Medium** | 345 M | 24 | 1024 |
| **GPT-2 Large** | 762 M | 36 | 1280 |
| **GPT-2 XL (GPT-2)** | **1.54 B** | 48 | 1600 |

> [!NOTE]
> The flagship 1.5B model is often referred to simply as "GPT-2." The paper is *"Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"* (Radford et al., 2019).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Infinite Autocomplete"

Imagine you open a chat application on your phone. As you type *"The weather in Kerala today is"*, your phone suggests the next word — maybe *"sunny"*, *"rainy"*, or *"humid"*. 

GPT-2 is essentially an **infinitely powerful version of this autocomplete**, but:
- It has read **40 GB of internet text** (billions of webpages, books, articles, Reddit links).
- It considers **the last 1024 tokens (~1500 words)** as context before predicting the next token.
- It can generate **paragraphs, essays, stories, code, summaries, translations, and even Q&A** — purely from being asked the right way, *without any retraining*.

**Analogy in another frame:** Think of GPT-2 as a *brilliant mimic*. It has absorbed so much human writing that when you give it a prompt, it can statistically reproduce whatever *style, format, or task* the prompt implies — a phenomenon called **in-context learning** (zero-shot).

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors (KTU-Standard Terminology)

- **Autoregressive Model:** Generates output one token at a time, feeding each prediction back as input.
- **Unsupervised:** Pre-trained on raw text with no human labels.
- **Zero-shot Learning:** Performs a task *at inference time* with **no examples and no gradient updates**.
- **Decoder-only Transformer:** Uses only the decoder stack of the original Vaswani et al. (2017) Transformer.
- **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE):** Subword tokenization algorithm used to handle vocabulary efficiently.
- **Causal Masking:** Masks future tokens to prevent the model from "cheating" during training.
- **Pre-Layer Normalization (Pre-Norm):** LayerNorm applied *before* attention/FFN sub-layers (a key GPT-2 architectural change from GPT-1).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GPT-2 Architecture — The Decoder-Only Stack

GPT-2's architecture is **a stack of $n_{layer}$ identical decoder blocks**. Each block contains two sub-layers wrapped in residual connections:

### 2.1.1 Sub-Layer 1: Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention

For an input embedding matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$, we project it into three matrices using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

The **scaled dot-product attention** is then computed with a **causal mask** $M$ that sets attention scores from future positions to $-\infty$:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V$$

where $M_{ij} = 0$ if $i \geq j$, else $M_{ij} = -\infty$. This **causal mask** is what makes GPT-2 a *left-to-right* language model.

**Multi-head** version concatenates $h$ parallel attention heads:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$$

### 2.1.2 Sub-Layer 2: Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)

Each position independently passes through a two-layer MLP with GELU activation:

$$\text{FFN}(x) = \text{GELU}(x W_1 + b_1)\, W_2 + b_2$$

with $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times 4 d_{model}}$ and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{4 d_{model} \times d_{model}}$ (the inner dimension is **4× the model dimension**).

### 2.1.3 Pre-Layer Normalization (Pre-Norm) — A Critical GPT-2 Innovation

Unlike the original Transformer (post-LN), GPT-2 applies LayerNorm **before** each sub-layer:

$$h = x + \text{Attention}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$$
$$h = h + \text{FFN}(\text{LayerNorm}(h))$$

This **Pre-Norm** arrangement dramatically stabilizes training of deep networks (48 layers in GPT-2 XL), where the residual stream $x$ accumulates without being repeatedly rescaled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Pre-Norm?** In deep Transformer stacks, post-LN causes the residual path's activation magnitudes to grow, leading to instability. Pre-Norm keeps the residual path "clean" (identity-like) so gradients flow easily, which is essential for training very deep models.

## 2.2 BPE Tokenization in GPT-2

GPT-2 uses a **Byte-Level BPE** tokenizer with a vocabulary of **50,257 tokens**. It operates directly on UTF-8 bytes (rather than Unicode characters), which gives it:
- **No "unknown token"** — every possible byte sequence is representable.
- **Compact vocabulary** — subword merging balances vocabulary size and sequence length.

The tokenization procedure:
1. Start with the **256 byte vocabulary**.
2. Repeatedly find the most frequent adjacent pair and merge it (standard BPE).
3. Continue until the vocabulary size reaches 50,257.

## 2.3 Pre-training Objective: Standard Language Modeling

GPT-2 is trained to minimize the **negative log-likelihood** of the next token given previous tokens. For a sequence $x_1, \ldots, x_n$:

$$L(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

The probability is produced by a **softmax over the vocabulary** applied to the final hidden state, passed through a linear projection $W^E \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times \vert V \vert}$:

$$P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(h_t \cdot W^E)$$

where $h_t$ is the final-layer hidden state at position $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on weight tying:** GPT-2 ties the input embedding matrix and the output projection matrix (i.e., $W^E$ is shared), reducing parameter count and improving generalization.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Autoregressive decomposition | $P(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Joint sequence probability |
| 2 | Scaled dot-product attention | $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V$ | Token mixing |
| 3 | Causal mask | $M_{ij} = 0$ if $i \geq j$ else $-\infty$ | Blocks future tokens |
| 4 | Multi-head concat | $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)W^O$ | Combine heads |
| 5 | GELU activation | $\text{GELU}(x) = x\,\Phi(x)$ | FFN non-linearity |
| 6 | Pre-Norm block | $h = x + \text{SubLayer}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$ | Stable deep training |
| 7 | Training loss | $L = -\sum_t \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Cross-entropy LM loss |
| 8 | Output projection | $P(x_t) = \text{softmax}(h_t W^E)$ | Next-token distribution |
| 9 | Context window | $n_{ctx} = 1024$ | Max input length |
| 10 | Vocabulary size | $\vert V \vert = 50{,}257$ | Byte-level BPE |
| 11 | Zero-shot prediction | $\hat{y} = \arg\max P(\text{token} \mid \text{prompt})$ | No fine-tuning needed |
| 12 | Top-k / nucleus sampling | Keep tokens with cumulative prob. $\leq p$ | Diverse generation |

## 2.5 Why GPT-2 Matters in Engineering (Real-World Utility)

- **Foundation of modern chatbots:** GPT-2 demonstrated the viability of the decoder-only stack, directly inspiring **GPT-3, ChatGPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen**, and all subsequent decoder-only LLMs.
- **Zero-shot task transfer:** It eliminated the need for thousands of labeled datasets; tasks are specified through natural language prompts.
- **Code generation precursor:** Follow-up work (GPT-2 variant Codex) led to GitHub Copilot.
- **Industry applications:** Customer-support automation, content drafting, summarization pipelines, SQL generation, and synthetic data creation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Causal Masking is Mandatory for Autoregressive Training

**Goal:** Show mathematically that masking future positions is necessary to keep training consistent with inference.

### Step 1 — Define the inference-time generation process
At inference, GPT-2 generates tokens one at a time from left to right. When predicting $x_t$, only $x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}$ are available. The model never sees the future. This gives the conditional:

$$P_{\text{infer}}(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

### Step 2 — Define the training-time objective
During training, we have the *entire sequence* $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ in parallel. If we naively let every position attend to every other position, the model at position $t$ would directly "see" $x_{t+1}, x_{t+2}, \ldots$ — information that will **never** be available at inference.

### Step 3 — Construct the mask matrix $M$
To enforce the inference-time constraint at training time, we add a mask to the pre-softmax logits. For all pairs $(i, j)$ with $j > i$ (future positions), we add $-\infty$:

$$M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0, & j \leq i \\ -\infty, & j > i \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Verify the masking kills future information
The pre-softmax attention score becomes:

$$S_{ij} = \frac{Q_i K_j^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M_{ij}$$

For $j > i$: $S_{ij} = \frac{Q_i K_j^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} - \infty = -\infty$

Applying softmax: $\text{softmax}(-\infty) = 0$, so the attention weight on any future position is **exactly zero**.

### Step 5 — Resulting attention weights
The attention weight from token $i$ to token $j$ is therefore:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S_{ik})} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\exp(Q_i K_j^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})}{\sum_{k \leq i} \exp(Q_i K_k^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})}, & j \leq i \\ 0, & j > i \end{cases}$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion
The output at position $i$ is a weighted sum of values $V_j$ with $j \leq i$ only:

$$\text{head}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \alpha_{ij}\, V_j$$

This matches the inference-time behavior, making the training objective a valid estimator of $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners frequently award a full 7-mark question on "Explain causal masking with equations." Writing the mask formula and showing $\text{softmax}(-\infty) = 0$ explicitly is worth at least 3 marks by itself.

## 3.2 Derivation: GPT-2 Pre-training Loss is Equivalent to Cross-Entropy

**Goal:** Show that the autoregressive negative log-likelihood equals a per-position categorical cross-entropy.

### Step 1 — Start from the chain rule decomposition
For a sequence of $n$ tokens with vocabulary $V$:

$$P_\theta(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$$

### Step 2 — Take the negative log

$$\mathcal{L} = -\log P_\theta(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = -\sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the softmax output

$$P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \frac{\exp(h_t^{\top} e_{x_t})}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp(h_t^{\top} e_v)}$$

where $e_{x_t}$ is the embedding of the *true* next token and $e_v$ ranges over all vocabulary items.

### Step 4 — Identify cross-entropy

$$-\log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = -h_t^{\top} e_{x_t} + \log \sum_{v \in V} \exp(h_t^{\top} e_v)$$

The right-hand side is precisely the **categorical cross-entropy** between the predicted logit vector $h_t W^E$ and the one-hot encoding of $x_t$. Hence:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} H\!\left(\text{onehot}(x_t),\, \text{softmax}(h_t W^E)\right)$$

### Step 5 — Per-token loss (averaging)
Most implementations average over tokens (and batches):

$$\bar{\mathcal{L}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{CE}\!\left(\text{onehot}(x_t),\, \text{softmax}(h_t W^E)\right)$$

This is the **perplexity** objective: $\text{PPL} = \exp(\bar{\mathcal{L}})$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Forward Pass Through One GPT-2 Block

**Setup:** Take $n = 4$ tokens with $d_{model} = 8$, $h = 2$ heads, $d_k = 4$.

### Step 1 — Embedding + Positional Encoding
$$X = \text{TokenEmbed}(x) + \text{PosEmb}, \quad X \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 8}$$

### Step 2 — Pre-LN
$$\tilde{X} = \text{LayerNorm}(X), \quad \tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 8}$$

### Step 3 — Q, K, V projections
$$Q = \tilde{X} W^Q, \quad K = \tilde{X} W^K, \quad V = \tilde{X} W^V$$

Each is $\mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$ (because $d_k = 4$).

### Step 4 — Apply causal mask to the attention matrix $Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$
Upper triangle (above the main diagonal) is set to $-\infty$.

### Step 5 — Softmax and weighted sum
$$A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{4}} + M\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$$
$$\text{head} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$$

### Step 6 — Concatenate both heads
$$\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2)\, W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 8}$$

### Step 7 — First residual
$$H_1 = X + \text{MHA}(\text{LN}(X))$$

### Step 8 — FFN sub-block with pre-norm
$$H_2 = H_1 + \text{FFN}(\text{LN}(H_1))$$

where $\text{FFN}(z) = \text{GELU}(z W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2$ with $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{8 \times 32}, W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{32 \times 8}$.

This output $H_2$ becomes the input of the next decoder block.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Pre-Norm vs. Post-Norm)

| Property | Post-LN (Original Transformer) | Pre-LN (GPT-2) |
|---|---|---|
