| **Safety & Alignment** | Reducing hallucinations, injecting refusal behaviour. |
| **Latency & Cost** | A fine-tuned small model often beats a prompted large model at lower inference cost. |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU 2024 exam trap: students confuse **fine-tuning** with **training from scratch**. Fine-tuning always **starts from a pre-trained checkpoint** $\theta_0$ and performs a small number of additional gradient steps on a comparatively tiny dataset.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Setting

Let:
- $\theta_0$ be the parameters of the pre-trained model (frozen baseline).
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{task}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ be the task-specific labelled dataset, where $N$ is typically $10^3$ to $10^6$ (versus $10^{11+}$ tokens for pre-training).
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta)$ be the task loss — usually cross-entropy for generative language models, MSE for regression heads, or diffusion noise-prediction loss for image models.

The fine-tuning objective is:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{task}}} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}\bigl(f_\theta(x),\, y\bigr) \right]$$

initialised at $\theta = \theta_0$, and solved via mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), Adam, or AdamW for $T$ additional steps where $T \ll T_{\text{pre-train}}$.

### 2.2 The Two Families of Fine-Tuning

**A. Full Fine-Tuning (FFT)** — All parameters $\theta$ are unfrozen and updated. Computationally expensive; risks **catastrophic forgetting** of pre-trained knowledge.

**B. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)** — Only a tiny subset $\phi \ll \theta$ of parameters is updated. The original weights $\theta_0$ remain frozen. Sub-families include:
- **Adapters** (Houlsby et al., 2019) — small bottleneck MLPs inserted into transformer layers.
- **LoRA** (Low-Rank Adaptation, Hu et al., 2021) — injects trainable low-rank matrices.
- **QLoRA** (Dettmers et al., 2023) — LoRA on a 4-bit quantised base model.
- **Prefix / Prompt Tuning** — prepended learnable virtual tokens.
- **IA³** — learned element-wise scaling vectors.

### 2.3 LoRA — The Crown Jewel of KTU Module 4

For a pre-trained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, LoRA freezes $W_0$ and learns a low-rank decomposition $\Delta W = B A$ where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, with rank $r \ll \min(d, k)$.

The forward pass becomes:

$$h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$$

where $\alpha$ is a constant scaling hyperparameter. Trainable parameter count is $r(d+k)$ instead of $dk$, yielding **>1000× reduction** for typical $r=8$ on large transformers.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\|` inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fine-tuning objective | $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x),y)]$ | $\theta$ model parameters | — |
| 2 | Cross-entropy loss (language) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{t} \log P_\theta(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ | per-token NLL | nats or bits |
| 3 | Diffusion fine-tuning loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t,x_0,\epsilon}\bigl[\, \Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}x_0+\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\epsilon,\,t) \Vert^{2}\,\bigr]$ | noise prediction MSE | — |
| 4 | LoRA forward pass | $h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$ | $r$ rank, $\alpha$ scale | $r=4,8,16,32,64$ |
| 5 | LoRA param count | $\Theta_{\text{LoRA}} = r(d+k)$ | vs $dk$ for full | ratio $r(d+k)/(dk)$ |
| 6 | QLoRA memory | $M \approx 4b \cdot \vert\theta\vert + 16b\cdot \vert\phi\vert$ | $b$ bits, $\vert\theta\vert$ base params | base in 4-bit |
| 7 | AdamW update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \frac{\hat m_t}{\sqrt{\hat v_t}+\epsilon} - \lambda\theta_t$ | $\eta$ LR, $\lambda$ WD | $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 5 \times 10^{-5}]$ |
| 8 | Effective batch size | $B_{\text{eff}} = B \cdot \text{grad\_accum} \cdot \text{GPUs}$ | gradient accumulation | $32$–$128$ typical |
| 9 | Cosine LR schedule | $\eta_t = \eta_{\min} + \tfrac{1}{2}(\eta_{\max}-\eta_{\min})(1+\cos(\pi t/T))$ | $t$ step, $T$ total | warm-up 3–10% |
| 10 | KL-regularised fine-tune | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \beta \cdot \mathrm{KL}(P_\theta \,\Vert\, P_{\theta_0})$ | anti-forgetting | $\beta \in [0.01, 0.1]$ |
| 11 | RLHF reward loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{policy}} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\pi_\theta}[R(x,y) - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y\mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y\mid x)}]$ | PPO-style | — |
| 12 | Catastrophic forgetting measure | $\Delta = \mathrm{Acc}_{\text{orig}}(\theta^{*}) - \mathrm{Acc}_{\text{orig}}(\theta_0)$ | drop on pre-train tasks | want $\Delta \to 0$ |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility Across Domains

- **Healthcare**: Fine-tune Llama-3-8B on de-identified clinical notes for SOAP-note generation.
- **Legal**: Adapt a base LLM on Indian Kanoon judgments for case-similarity retrieval.
- **Code**: Specialise on a single company's internal monorepo for autocomplete.
- **Vision**: Fine-tune Stable Diffusion XL on 200 curated product images for branded e-commerce generation.
- **Speech**: Fine-tune Whisper on Malayalam call-center audio for transcription accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — LoRA Gradient Flow

We derive why LoRA updates are mathematically equivalent to learning a full $\Delta W$ but at fraction of the cost. Start from the LoRA forward pass:

$$h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$$

Take the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the trainable parameters $A$ and $B$ (note that $W_0$ is frozen, so $\nabla_{W_0}\mathcal{L}$ is discarded in the parameter-update sense):

**Step 1.** Partial derivative with respect to $B$:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial B} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot (A x)^{\top}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial B} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h}\right) (A x)^{\top}$$

**Step 2.** Partial derivative with respect to $A$:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial A} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B \cdot x^{\top}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B^{\top} \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h}\right) x^{\top}$$

**Step 3.** AdamW update on $A$ and $B$:

$$A \leftarrow A - \eta \cdot \frac{\hat m_A}{\sqrt{\hat v_A}+\epsilon}, \quad B \leftarrow B - \eta \cdot \frac{\hat m_B}{\sqrt{\hat v_B}+\epsilon}$$

**Step 4.** Effective low-rank update assembled:

$$\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r} B A \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$$

**Step 5.** Rank analysis: $\mathrm{rank}(\Delta W) \le r$ by construction. Hence $\Delta W$ lies on a low-rank manifold of dimension $r(d+k) - r^{2}$, vastly smaller than the full $dk$ ambient space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $A$ is initialised to Gaussian and $B$ to zero:** With $B=0$ at $t=0$, the LoRA branch contributes nothing, so the model begins exactly at the pre-trained output: $f_{\theta_0 + \Delta W} = f_{\theta_0}$. This guarantees a smooth, stable starting point and is a common **KTU exam viva question**.

### 3.2 Derivation — Why Catastrophic Forgetting Happens in FFT

Consider the full fine-tuning gradient on the task loss:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta_t)$$

Expanding around $\theta_0$ via a first-order Taylor series gives:

$$\theta^{*} \approx \theta_0 - \eta \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta_i)$$

The cumulative drift from $\theta_0$ is:

$$\Vert \theta^{*} - \theta_0 \Vert_2 \;\le\; \eta T \cdot \max_{i}\Vert \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta_i)\Vert_2$$

Thus, large $T$ or large $\eta$ (typical when learning a highly specialised small dataset) drives the model far from $\theta_0$, destroying pre-trained features. PEFT sidesteps this by design.

### 3.3 Derivation — KL-Regularised Fine-Tuning

To mitigate forgetting, KTU 2024 expects familiarity with the **KL-anchored objective**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{KL}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta) + \beta \cdot \mathrm{KL}\bigl(P_{\theta}(\cdot \mid x) \,\Vert\, P_{\theta_0}(\cdot \mid x)\bigr)$$

The KL term expands as:

$$\mathrm{KL}\bigl(P_\theta \,\Vert\, P_{\theta_0}\bigr) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim P_\theta(\cdot \mid x)}\left[ \log \frac{P_\theta(y \mid x)}{P_{\theta_0}(y \mid x)} \right]$$

Differentiating and using the score-function identity yields the regularised gradient:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{KL}} = \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \beta \bigl( \nabla_\theta \log P_\theta(y \mid x) - \mathbb{E}_{P_\theta}[\nabla_\theta \log P_\theta] \bigr)$$

This pulls the fine-tuned distribution back toward $P_{\theta_0}$, preserving general capabilities. As $\beta \to \infty$, the model freezes at $\theta_0$; as $\beta \to 0$, we recover plain FFT and risk forgetting.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PEFT Method Comparison

| Method | Trainable Params / Layer | Memory | Quality vs FFT | Forgetting Risk | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | $100\%$ | Very High | Baseline | High | High |
| **Adapters (Houlsby)** | $\sim 0.5$–$5\%$ | Medium | 95–99% | Low | Medium |
| **LoRA** | $\sim 0.1$–$1\%$ | Low | 97–100% | Very Low | **Very High** |
| **QLoRA** | $\sim 0.1$–$1\%$ | Very Low (4-bit) | 95–99% | Very Low | **Very High** |
| **Prefix Tuning** | $\sim 0.01$–$0.1\%$ | Very Low | 90–97% | Very Low | Medium |
| **Prompt Tuning** | $\sim 0.001$% | Negligible | 85–95% | Very Low | Low |
| **IA³** | $\sim 0.01$% | Negligible | 95–99% | Very Low | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner's heuristic:** If the question mentions *GPU memory constrained*, the answer is **QLoRA**. If it mentions *inference latency critical* with a single deployment, the answer is **LoRA merged back into $W_0$**. If the dataset is < 500 samples, the answer is **prefix/prompt tuning** to avoid overfitting.

### 3.5 Hyperparameter Selection Heuristics (Engineering Best Practice)

| Hyperparameter | Typical Range | Rule of Thumb |
|---|---|---|
| Learning rate $\eta$ | $10^{-6}$ to $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | $\eta_{\text{PEFT}} \approx 10\times \eta_{\text{FFT}}$ |
| LoRA rank $r$ | $4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128$ | Start at $r=8$; increase if under-fitting |
| LoRA $\alpha$ | $2r$ or $r$ | Common pair: $\alpha=16, r=8$ |
| Batch size | $4$ to $128$ (per device) | Use gradient accumulation if VRAM-limited |
| Epochs | $1$ to $5$ | >3 epochs risks overfitting on small data |
| Warm-up ratio | $0.03$ to $0.1$ | Critical for transformer stability |
| Weight decay | $0.0$ to $0.1$ | Often $0.01$ with AdamW |
| KL coefficient $\beta$ | $0.01$ to $0.5$ | Tune to balance task vs retention |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — End-to-End Fine-Tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large Raw Corpus] --> B[Self-Supervised Pre-Training]
    B --> C[Pre-Trained Foundation Model theta_0]
    C --> D{Choose Customization Path}
    D --> E1[Prompt Engineering - No Weight Update]
    D --> E2[RAG - External Memory]
    D --> E3[Full Fine-Tuning - Update all theta]
    D --> E4[LoRA - Update only B and A]
    D --> E5[QLoRA - 4-bit base + LoRA adapters]
    D --> E6[RLHF - PPO alignment]
    F[Task Specific Curated Dataset D_task] --> E3
    F --> E4
    F --> E5
    F --> E6
    G1[Reference Documents] --> E2
    H[Human Preference Rankings] --> E6
    E1 --> I[Deployed Specialised Model]
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    E4 --> I
    E5 --> I
    E6 --> I
    J[Evaluation on Holdout + Pre-Train Probes] --> I
    I --> K{Catastrophic Forgetting Check}
    K -->|Drift Too Large| L[Re-tune with KL Regularisation]
    K -->|Acceptable Drift| M[Production Deployment]
    L --> E4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — LoRA Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Vector x] --> SPLIT{Split Path}
    SPLIT --> P1[Frozen Pre-Trained W_0]
    SPLIT --> P2[LoRA Branch]
    P1 --> ADD((Sum))
    P2 --> ADD
    P2 --> A[Matrix A: r by k]
    A --> M[Matrix Multiplication]
    M --> B[Matrix B: d by r]
    B --> S[Scale by alpha over r]
    S --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[Output h]
    A -.->|trainable| TRAIN[AdamW Optimizer]
    B -.->|trainable| TRAIN
    P1 -.->|frozen| FRZ[No Gradient Updates]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — PEFT Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Do you have labelled task data?] -->|No| RAG[Use RAG / Prompting Only]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[How many GPU hours available?]
    Q2 -->|>100 hours| FFT[Full Fine-Tuning + KL Regularisation]
    Q2 -->|10-100 hours| LORA[LoRA rank 16-64]
    Q2 -->|<10 hours| QLORA[QLoRA 4-bit + LoRA rank 8]
    Q3[Is dataset < 500 samples?] -->|Yes| PREFIX[Prefix Tuning]
    Q3 -->|No| LORA
    Q4[Need human preference alignment?] -->|Yes| RLHF[RLHF or DPO Pipeline]
    Q4 -->|No| LORA
    Q5[Inference latency critical?] -->|Yes| MERGE[Merge LoRA into W_0 then Serve]
    Q5 -->|No| DYNAMIC[Dynamic Adapter Loading]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — RLHF Three-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning SFT] --> S2[Stage 2: Reward Model Training]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: PPO Reinforcement Learning]
    S1 --> DM1[SFT Model pi_SFT]
    DM1 --> S2
    PREF[Human Preference Pairs chosen vs rejected] --> S2
    S2 --> RM[Reward Model R phi]
    RM --> S3
    DM1 --> S3
    KL[KL Penalty vs Reference pi_SFT] --> S3
    S3 --> POL[Aligned Policy pi_theta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-Fallback Note:** For physical drawings (e.g., GPU rack layouts, circuit-level adapter placement), the above block diagrams serve as functional architecture proxies — adequate for KTU 14-mark answers where schematic clarity matters more than photorealism.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
*Distinguish between Full Fine-Tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT). Give two examples of PEFT methods.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Full Fine-Tuning (FFT) updates **all** parameters $\theta$ of a pre-trained model on a downstream dataset, requiring large GPU memory and risking catastrophic forgetting. PEFT updates only a small subset $\phi \ll \theta$, keeping the base weights frozen, drastically reducing memory, training time, and storage per task. Two examples: **(i) LoRA** — learns low-rank matrices $A, B$ such that $\Delta W = BA$; **(ii) Adapters** — small bottleneck MLPs inserted inside transformer blocks. *(Award 1 mark for clean distinction, 1 mark for parameter-count contrast, 1 mark for two named PEFT examples.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest]**
*What is catastrophic forgetting in the context of fine-tuning? State one mitigation strategy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Catastrophic forgetting is the phenomenon where, during fine-tuning on a narrow task dataset, the model's performance on **previously learned general capabilities** degrades sharply, quantified as $\Delta = \mathrm{Acc}_{\text{orig}}(\theta^{*}) - \mathrm{Acc}_{\text{orig}}(\theta_0) \ll 0$. It arises because unconstrained gradient updates on a small dataset shift weights far from $\theta_0$. **Mitigation strategies** (any one for full marks): (i) KL-regularised objective $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \beta\,\mathrm{KL}(P_\theta\Vert P_{\theta_0})$; (ii) PEFT methods like LoRA that freeze $W_0$; (iii) Replay of pre-training data mixed into the fine-tuning batch. *(1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for cause, 1 mark for mitigation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **not** confuse **LoRA rank $r$** with the **number of LoRA modules per layer**. A common 2-mark deduction.
> - Always state the **initialisation** of $A$ (Gaussian) and $B$ (zero) in LoRA — missing this costs 1 mark.
> - In RLHF questions, students forget the **KL penalty term** in the PPO objective; this is a 2-mark deduction in most board evaluations.
> - For LoRA param-count questions, present the formula **and** the worked numerical example. Half-mark answers fail.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — LoRA Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set B, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and mathematical formulation of **Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)** for fine-tuning large language models. Derive the gradient flow with respect to the trainable matrices. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) freezes the pre-trained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ and learns a low-rank update $\Delta W = BA$ where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, with rank $r \ll \min(d, k)$. The forward pass is:

$$h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r}\,BA x$$

Trainable parameters per layer: $r(d+k)$ instead of $dk$ — a reduction of factor $\frac{dk}{r(d+k)} \approx \frac{d}{r}$ for $d \gg k$.

**Initialisation:** $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$, $B = 0$ so that at $t=0$, $h = W_0 x$ exactly (model output equals pre-trained output). This guarantees a smooth start.

**Gradient Derivation:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial B} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h}\right)(Ax)^{\top}, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A} = \frac{\alpha}{r} B^{\top}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h}\right) x^{\top}$$

These are updated via AdamW. The product $\Delta W = (\alpha/r)BA$ is at most rank $r$, lying on a low-rank manifold in $\mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.

**Merging at inference:** $W_{\text{final}} = W_0 + (\alpha/r)BA$ — no extra latency.

**Mark split:** 2 marks architecture, 2 marks formula + initialisation, 2 marks gradients, 1 mark merging.

**(b)** A 7-billion-parameter LLM has transformer attention matrices of dimension $d = k = 4096$. Compare the **trainable parameter count** and **approximate GPU memory** for (i) full fine-tuning in fp16, (ii) LoRA with $r=8$ in fp16, and (iii) QLoRA with 4-bit base + LoRA $r=8$ in fp16. Assume Adam optimiser states (2 moments per param) and 2 bytes per fp16 / 0.5 bytes per 4-bit-normal-int. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Full Fine-Tuning on one attention matrix:**

- Parameters in matrix: $dk = 4096 \times 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216 \approx 1.68 \times 10^{7}$
- Memory per matrix (weights + Adam moments + gradients): $2 + 4 + 4 + 2 = 12$ bytes/param
- Per-matrix memory: $1.68 \times 10^{7} \times 12 \approx 201$ MB
- Scaling to 32 transformer layers (32 attention matrices total): $32 \times 201 \approx 6.4$ GB just for attention
- Whole-model fp16 + Adam: roughly $2 \times 7\text{B} + 4 \times 7\text{B} + 2 \times 7\text{B} \approx 56$ GB

**(ii) LoRA, $r=8$:**

- Trainable per matrix: $r(d+k) = 8 \times (4096+4096) = 65{,}536$ parameters
- Ratio: $65{,}536 / 16{,}777{,}216 = 0.39\%$ — a **256× reduction**
- Trainable memory (full-precision AdamW): $65{,}536 \times 12 \approx 0.79$ MB per matrix, negligible
- Total: $32 \times 0.79 \approx 25$ MB trainable, but base model still loaded in fp16 (~14 GB)

**(iii) QLoRA, 4-bit base + LoRA $r=8$:**

- Base model in 4-bit normal-float: $7\text{B} \times 0.5 \text{ bytes} \approx 3.5$ GB
- Dequantisation scratch + LoRA adapters: ~2 GB
- **Total ≈ 5.5 GB**, enabling fine-tuning of 7B model on a single 24 GB consumer GPU

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | FFT fp16 | LoRA $r=8$ fp16 | QLoRA $r=8$ 4-bit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trainable params / matrix | $1.68 \times 10^{7}$ | $6.55 \times 10^{4}$ | $6.55 \times 10^{4}$ |
| Memory (full model) | ~56 GB | ~14 GB | ~5.5 GB |
| Hardware needed | 2× A100 80GB | 1× A100 40GB | 1× RTX 4090 24GB |
| Inference overhead | None | None (mergeable) | Tiny (dequant) |

**Mark split:** 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 2 marks (iii), 1 mark comparison table.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — RLHF & Alignment Customization**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set C, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **three stages of RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) used to align a generative language model. Write down the **PPO objective** used in Stage 3 and explain each term. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

RLHF (Christiano et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., InstructGPT 2022) consists of three sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):**
A pre-trained LLM $\pi_{\theta_0}$ is fine-tuned on high-quality human-written demonstrations $\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$ using standard cross-entropy:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{\text{SFT}}}\sum_{t} \log \pi_\theta(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$$

This yields $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$, the reference policy for subsequent stages.

**Stage 2 — Reward Modelling:**
Human annotators rank $K$ candidate completions $\{y^{(1)}, \ldots, y^{(K)}\}$ for prompt $x$, producing preference pairs $(y^{(w)}, y^{(l)})$ (winner, loser). A reward model $R_\phi(x, y)$ is trained via the Bradley-Terry loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}}(\phi) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\left[ \log \sigma\bigl(R_\phi(x, y_w) - R_\phi(x, y_l)\bigr) \right]$$

**Stage 3 — PPO Policy Optimisation:**
The SFT policy is further updated to maximise expected reward while staying close to the SFT reference:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\pi_\theta}\bigl[\,R_\phi(x,y) - \beta\,\mathrm{KL}\bigl(\pi_\theta(\cdot\mid x) \,\Vert\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot\mid x)\bigr)\bigr]$$

**Term-by-term:**
- $R_\phi(x, y)$: learned scalar reward from the reward model; higher = more aligned with human preference.
- $\beta > 0$: KL penalty coefficient preventing the policy from drifting too far from $\pi_{\text{SFT}}$, thereby mitigating reward hacking and catastrophic forgetting.
- $\mathrm{KL}(\pi_\theta\Vert\pi_{\text{SFT}})$: regulariser anchoring the optimised policy near the safe SFT checkpoint.
- The negative sign converts maximisation into minimisation for gradient descent.

**Mark split:** 1.5 marks SFT, 2 marks reward modelling, 2.5 marks PPO formula + terms, 1 mark for annotating the role of KL.

**(b)** Compare **RLHF** with **DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation, Rafailov et al., 2023)** as alignment strategies. State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of DPO relative to RLHF. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**DPO** eliminates the explicit reward model and PPO loop. It re-derives the optimal policy in closed form from the Bradley-Terry preference model:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\left[\log \sigma\bigl(\beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\bigr)\right]$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | RLHF | DPO |
|---|---|---|
| Reward model | Separate $R_\phi$ trained | Implicit; no separate network |
| Optimiser | PPO (on-policy, complex) | Standard SFT-style (off-policy, simple) |
| Compute cost | High (4 models in memory) | Low (policy + reference) |
| Stability | Sensitive to KL/clip hyperparams | More stable empirically |
| Sample efficiency | Requires many rollouts | Uses preference dataset directly |
| Quality ceiling | High with careful tuning | Competitive, sometimes better |

**Two advantages of DPO:**
1. **Simplicity & stability:** No PPO, no value head, no reward hacking — a single supervised loss on preference pairs, far easier to train and tune.
2. **Compute & memory efficiency:** Eliminates the reward model and the actor-critic infrastructure, cutting memory by ~4× and avoiding the brittleness of PPO.

**One disadvantage of DPO:**
1. **Static reference policy:** DPO's implicit reward depends on $\pi_{\text{ref}}$ frozen at SFT time; as the policy updates, the implicit reward drifts, sometimes causing the model to overfit to the preference distribution and produce lower-diversity outputs. RLHF, by contrast, can re-evaluate completions with a separately trained reward model that generalises beyond the SFT reference.

**Mark split:** 2 marks DPO formula, 2 marks comparison table, 2 marks two advantages, 1 mark one disadvantage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Fine-Tuning & Customizability**

- **Fine-Tuning** = gradient-based continued training of a pre-trained model on task data, **starting from $\theta_0$**, not from scratch.
- **Customizability Spectrum** (least to most invasive): Prompt Engineering → RAG → Prefix/Prompt Tuning → Adapters → LoRA/QLoRA → Full Fine-Tuning → RLHF/DPO.
- **LoRA Forward Pass:** $h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x$, with $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ and $B=0$ initially.
- **LoRA Param Count:** $r(d+k)$ per matrix; for $d=4096, r=8$ this is **0.39%** of full.
- **QLoRA** = 4-bit quantised base + LoRA adapters in fp16; fits 7B model on 24 GB GPU.
- **Catastrophic Forgetting** = loss of pre-trained capabilities; mitigated by **PEFT** or **KL-regularisation** with anchor $\theta_0$.
- **Adapter** = small bottleneck MLP inserted inside transformer FFN or attention sub-layer; original Houlsby design.
- **Prefix / Prompt Tuning** = learnable virtual tokens prepended to input; minimal parameters.
- **IA³** = element-wise learned scaling vectors; even smaller than LoRA.
- **RLHF Three Stages:** (1) SFT on demonstrations, (2) Reward Model on human preferences (Bradley-Terry), (3) PPO with KL penalty.
- **PPO Objective:** $\max_\theta \mathbb{E}[R_\phi(x,y)] - \beta\,\mathrm{KL}(\pi_\theta\Vert\pi_{\text{SFT}})$.
- **DPO** = reparameterised preference loss without explicit reward model or PPO; simpler but reference-policy drift is a known limitation.
- **Hyperparameter Heuristics:** $\eta_{\text{PEFT}} \approx 10\times\eta_{\text{FFT}}$; LoRA $r=8$ + $\alpha=16$ is a strong default; epochs 1–3 for small data; warm-up 3–10%.
- **Merging LoRA** at inference: $W_{\text{final}} = W_0 + (\alpha/r)BA$ — zero latency overhead.
- **Multi-task Serving:** Load many LoRA adapters dynamically per request for cheap multi-tenant deployment.
- **Common KTU Traps to Avoid:** (a) confusing LoRA rank with number of adapters, (b) forgetting the zero-initialisation of $B$, (c) omitting KL term in PPO objective, (d) calling QLoRA "quantisation" alone (it is **quantisation + LoRA**), (e) claiming PEFT requires retraining the base (it does **not**).
- **Engineering Decision Rule:** GPU-rich + large task data → FFT; GPU-poor + moderate data → QLoRA; tiny data + fast iteration → Prefix Tuning; alignment + preferences → RLHF or DPO; safety-critical + style control → RLHF; cost-sensitive + alignment needed → DPO.
- **Evaluation:** Always measure (i) task metric, (ii) pre-train task retention, (iii) inference latency, (iv) GPU memory peak, (v) training time per epoch. KTU board problems often ask you to reason over **multiple** of these trade-offs simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (As per 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)** is a class of deep-learning adaptation techniques that updates only a *small subset* (typically **< 1% to 5%**) of a pre-trained model's total parameters, or appends a tiny number of trainable parameters, while **freezing** the original weights. This dramatically reduces computational cost, GPU memory footprint, storage requirements, and prevents **catastrophic forgetting** during downstream task adaptation of large foundation models (LLMs, diffusion models, vision transformers).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Specialist Surgeon in a Big Hospital"

Imagine a massive, fully-trained university hospital with thousands of senior doctors (the **pre-trained base model** containing billions of parameters). A new disease (a **downstream task**, e.g., legal Q&A, medical report summarization) appears.

- **Full Fine-Tuning (FFT)** is like retraining *every single doctor* in the hospital from scratch on the new disease — extremely expensive, time-consuming, and the doctors may forget their general medicine.
- **PEFT** is like hiring a **single new specialist surgeon** and giving her a small *toolkit* (a few low-rank matrices, a few prefix tokens, or a small adapter). The original doctors keep doing their jobs (frozen weights), and only the small toolkit is trained. The hospital gains new expertise at **< 1%** of the cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Exams:**
> - **Frozen Weights ($\theta_{\text{frozen}}$):** Original pre-trained parameters that are kept constant.
> - **Trainable Parameters ($\theta_{\text{PEFT}}$):** The tiny new parameters added/selected for training.
> - **Downstream Task ($\mathcal{T}$):** The specific supervised task for which we adapt the model.
> - **Rank ($r$):** A small integer (e.g., 4, 8, 16) that controls capacity in low-rank methods.

### Why PEFT is Critical in the 2024 Generative AI Landscape
| Challenge in Full Fine-Tuning | PEFT Solution |
|---|---|
| Storing a full 70B-parameter checkpoint per task (≈ 140 GB in FP16) | Stores only ~20–200 MB per task |
| Requires multi-node GPU clusters | Often trainable on a **single consumer GPU** |
| Catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained knowledge | Original weights remain untouched |
| Slow iteration across many downstream tasks | Rapid "task-switching" by swapping small adapters |
| High carbon / energy cost | **10×–1000×** lower energy consumption |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PEFT is the **de-facto industry standard** for customizing open-weight LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma) and is a *guaranteed high-yield topic* for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of PEFT

Let a pre-trained model have parameter matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$. Full fine-tuning learns a *residual* update $\Delta W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, such that the new weight is:

$$W = W_0 + \Delta W$$

The number of trainable parameters in $\Delta W$ is $d \times k$, which for a transformer layer where $d = k = 4096$ gives **16,777,216** parameters per matrix — multiplied by hundreds of layers, this becomes huge.

**PEFT restricts the space from which $\Delta W$ is drawn**, making $\vert\theta_{\text{PEFT}}\vert \ll \vert\theta_{\text{frozen}}\vert$.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of PEFT Methods (KTU High-Yield Classification)

PEFT methods are broadly classified into three families:

**A. Additive Methods** — Inject *new* trainable parameters into the model.
- Adapter Layers (Houlsby et al., 2019)
- Prompt Tuning / Prefix Tuning (Lester et al., Li & Liang)
- P-Tuning v2 (Deep prompt tuning)

**B. Reparameterization-Based Methods** — Reparameterize $\Delta W$ using a low-rank decomposition.
- **LoRA** (Low-Rank Adaptation) — the most popular
- LoRA-FA (Frozen-A)
- QLoRA (Quantized LoRA)

**C. Selective / Sparse Methods** — Fine-tune only a *subset* of existing parameters.
- BitFit (bias terms only)
- Diff Pruning
- IA³ (Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Inner Activations)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Trainable Params per Layer | Core Formula / Mechanism | Typical Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | $d \times k$ | $W = W_0 + \Delta W$ | 0% (baseline) |
| **LoRA** | $r(d+k)$ | $W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$ | **~0.1% – 1%** |
| **Adapter (Houlsby)** | $2 d \cdot r + r \cdot d$ | Bottleneck MLP: $\text{down} \rightarrow \text{ReLU} \rightarrow \text{up}$ | ~3% – 5% |
| **Prefix Tuning** | $L \times n_{\text{tokens}} \times d$ | Prepends trainable $P_{\text{prefix}}$ to K, V | ~0.1% |
| **Prompt Tuning** | $n_{\text{tokens}} \times d$ | Soft prompts at input embedding only | **< 0.01%** |
| **P-Tuning v2** | $L \times n_{\text{tokens}} \times d$ | Deep prompt tuning at every layer | ~0.1% – 1% |
| **BitFit** | $d$ (biases only) | Updates $b$ of linear layers | ~0.05% – 0.1% |
| **IA³** | $L \times d$ (multiplicative vectors) | $W \leftarrow W \odot \ell$ | **< 0.01%** |
| **QLoRA** | $r(d+k)$ (on 4-bit base) | LoRA + NF4 quantization of $W_0$ | ~0.5% + 4× memory saving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU 2024:** When you state LoRA's parameter count, **always** use the formula $r \cdot (d + k)$, **not** $r \cdot d \cdot k$. This is the single most-missed mark in the board exam.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Multi-tenant LLM serving:** A single base LLM can serve thousands of customers, each with their own tiny LoRA adapter swapped in/out per request (e.g., Together AI, Hugging Face Inference Endpoints).
- **Edge deployment:** LoRA adapters for mobile/edge LLMs (≤ 50 MB).
- **Domain adaptation:** Medical, legal, financial LLMs (e.g., Med-PaLM, FinGPT).
- **Diffusion model personalization:** DreamBooth-LoRA for Stable Diffusion — teaches a new concept in ~10 minutes on a single GPU.
- **Federated & on-device fine-tuning** where bandwidth and privacy matter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) — *The 14-Mark Backbone*

**Step 1 — Problem Setup.**
Let $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ be a frozen pre-trained weight matrix of a transformer (e.g., the query projection $W_q$ or value projection $W_v$). For a downstream task, full fine-tuning would learn a dense update $\Delta W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$.

**Step 2 — The Aghajanyan et al. (2020) Empirical Finding (the "Why").**
Aghajanyan showed that the *intrinsic rank* of $\Delta W$ during fine-tuning is very low, even though the ambient dimension is high. Concretely, when adapting GPT-3 175B, $\Delta W$ can be approximated with rank $r \le 8$ for many tasks.

**Step 3 — Apply the Low-Rank Decomposition.**
We constrain $\Delta W$ to a low-rank factorization:

$$
\Delta W \approx B A, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}, \quad r \ll \min(d, k)
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Forward Pass.**
The output of the modified linear layer becomes:

$$
y = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + B A x
$$

**Step 5 — The Practical Scaling Factor.**
In practice, to control the magnitude of the LoRA update relative to the frozen weights, we scale by $\frac{\alpha}{r}$:

$$
W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A
$$

where $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter (often $\alpha = 2r$ or $\alpha = r$). At inference, the matrices can be *merged*: $W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$, so there is **zero additional latency**.

**Step 6 — Initialization.**
$A$ is initialized with **random Gaussian** (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma$ small), and $B$ is initialized to **zero**. This ensures that at the start of training, $\Delta W = 0$ and the model behaves *exactly* like the pre-trained model.

**Step 7 — Parameter Count Calculation (full derivation).**
Trainable parameters in LoRA per adapted matrix:
- $A$ has $r \times k$ entries.
- $B$ has $d \times r$ entries.
- Total: $r \cdot k + d \cdot r = r(d + k)$.

Compare to full fine-tuning of the same matrix: $d \times k$.

The **reduction ratio** is therefore:

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} \;=\; \frac{r}{d} + \frac{r}{k}
$$

For a typical 7B-parameter LLaMA layer with $d = k = 4096$ and $r = 8$:

$$
\rho = \frac{8}{4096} + \frac{8}{4096} = \frac{16}{4096} = 0.0039 \;\;(\approx 0.39\%)
$$

**Step 8 — Multi-Layer Extension.**
LoRA is typically applied to the four attention projection matrices: $W_q$, $W_k$, $W_v$, and $W_o$ (and sometimes to the feed-forward $W_{\text{up}}, W_{\text{down}}$). For an L-layer transformer:

$$
\text{Total LoRA params} = L \cdot 4 \cdot r(d + k) + L \cdot 2 \cdot r(d_{\text{ff}}, k_{\text{ff}})
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Prompt Tuning vs. Prefix Tuning

**Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021):**
Let the input embedding layer produce $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ for $n$ tokens. We prepend a *soft prompt* $P \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$ (where $m$ is the number of virtual tokens, e.g., $m = 10$ to $100$). Trainable parameters: $m \cdot d$.

$$
\text{Input to Transformer} = [P \,;\, E] \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+n) \times d}
$$

The base model is fully frozen; only $P$ is updated.

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):**
Soft prompts are added to the **Key** and **Value** matrices of *every* attention layer, not just the input embedding. For an $L$-layer model with $n_{\text{prefix}}$ prefix tokens:

$$
\text{Trainable params} = L \times n_{\text{prefix}} \times 2d
$$

**P-Tuning v2:** Equivalent to prefix tuning but with reparameterization removed (no MLP head) for efficiency and stability.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: QLoRA — Quantized LoRA

**Step 1:** Base model weights $W_0$ are quantized to **4-bit NormalFloat (NF4)**:
$$
W_0^{\text{NF4}} = \text{dequant}(W_0^{\text{stored}}), \quad W_0^{\text{stored}} \in \mathbb{F}_4
$$
This shrinks memory by **~4×** (e.g., 70B model fits in ~35 GB).

**Step 2:** LoRA adapters $B$ and $A$ are kept in **BF16** (higher precision) and trained.

**Step 3:** Forward pass:
$$
Y = W_0^{\text{NF4}} X + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A X
$$

**Step 4:** Loss is backpropagated **only** through $A$ and $B$; gradients with respect to $W_0$ are computed via *Straight-Through Estimator* (STE) through the quantization.

**Step 5:** Memory footprint of QLoRA ≈ (4-bit base model) + (16-bit adapters) + (16-bit optimizer states for adapters only). This enables fine-tuning of a **65B model on a single 48 GB GPU** — a hallmark result of the 2023 Dettmers et al. paper.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | LoRA | Adapter | Prefix Tuning | Prompt Tuning | BitFit | IA³ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inference Latency** | **0** (mergeable) | + (sequential) | + (extra K/V) | **0** | **0** | **0** |
| **Modifies Base Weights?** | No (frozen) | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Storage per Task** | ~MB | ~MB | ~MB | ~KB | ~KB | ~KB |
| **Task Quality vs FFT** | ≈ Match | ≈ Match | ≈ Match (large models) | Slightly lower | Lower | ≈ Match |
| **Training Compute** | Low | Medium | Low | **Very Low** | Low | **Very Low** |
| **Best For** | LLMs, Diffusion | NLP, vision | LLM generation | LLM at scale | Tiny budgets | Few-shot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam (Part A 3-mark):** *"PEFT is **A**lways **A**daptive, **L**ightweight, **I**terative, **S**elective, **A**ugmented, **T**ask-specific"* — the **A-LISTA** mnemonic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level PEFT Classification (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["PEFT METHODS"]:::root
    rootA --> B1["ADDITIVE"]:::branch
    rootA --> B2["REPARAMETERIZATION"]:::branch
    rootA --> B3["SELECTIVE"]:::branch

    B1 --> C1["Adapter Layers"]:::leaf
    B1 --> C2["Prompt Tuning"]:::leaf
    B1 --> C3["Prefix Tuning"]:::leaf
    B1 --> C4["P-Tuning v2"]:::leaf

    B2 --> C5["LoRA"]:::hot
    B2 --> C6["LoRA-FA"]:::leaf
    B2 --> C7["QLoRA NF4"]:::hot

    B3 --> C8["BitFit"]:::leaf
    B3 --> C9["Diff Pruning"]:::leaf
    B3 --> C10["IA3"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#3a4a7a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#dfe6f3,color:#000000,stroke:#3a4a7a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef hot fill:#ffd966,color:#000000,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 LoRA Internal Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp["Input x in R to the d"]:::io
    frozen["Frozen W0 in R d by k"]:::frozen
    A["Trainable A in R r by k init N(0,sigma)"]:::train
    B["Trainable B in R d by r init 0"]:::train
    scale["Scale by alpha over r"]:::scale
    add["Element-wise Add"]:::op
    out["Output y in R d"]:::io

    inp --> frozen
    frozen --> add
    inp --> A
    A --> B
    B --> scale
    scale --> add
    add --> out

    classDef io fill:#e8f0fe,color:#000,stroke:#1a73e8
    classDef frozen fill:#f5f5f5,color:#555,stroke:#888,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef train fill:#fff2cc,color:#000,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef scale fill:#fce5cd,color:#000,stroke:#e69138
    classDef op fill:#d9ead3,color:#000,stroke:#38761d
```

> The above block diagram maps the parallel paths of the **frozen** weight $W_0$ and the **trainable** low-rank bottleneck $BA$. The dashed border on `frozen` represents the parameters that are *never* updated.

### 4.3 Full PEFT Pipeline (Mermaid End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Pretrained Foundation Model LLM"]:::base
    S2["Select PEFT Strategy LoRA, Adapter, Prefix"]:::step
    S3["Inject / Attach Trainable Parameters"]:::step
    S4["Freeze Base Weights"]:::step
    S5["Train ONLY PEFT Parameters on Downstream Data"]:::train
    S6["Save Tiny Adapter Checkpoint MB scale"]:::step
    S7["Deploy: Merge Adapter into Base for Zero Latency"]:::deploy

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef base fill:#1f2a44,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#dfe6f3,color:#000
    classDef train fill:#ffd966,color:#000
    classDef deploy fill:#d9ead3,color:#000
```

### 4.4 QLoRA 4-bit Quantization + LoRA Adapter (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W0[("W0 in NF4 4-bit")]:::frozen
    deq["Dequantize to BF16"]:::op
    X["Input x in BF16"]:::io
    mul1["MatMul W0 BF16 times x"]:::op
    A["LoRA A in BF16"]:::train
    B["LoRA B in BF16"]:::train
    mul2["MatMul A times x"]:::op
    mul3["MatMul B times Ax"]:::op
    scale["Scale alpha over r"]:::scale
    add["Add residual to W0 times x"]:::op
    Y["Output y in BF16"]:::io

    W0 --> deq --> mul1
    X --> mul1
    X --> A --> mul2 --> B --> mul3 --> scale --> add
    mul1 --> add --> Y

    classDef frozen fill:#f5f5f5,color:#555,stroke:#888,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef train fill:#fff2cc,color:#000,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef io fill:#e8f0fe,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#d9ead3,color:#000
    classDef scale fill:#fce5cd,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT). List any two advantages of PEFT over full fine-tuning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** PEFT is a set of techniques that adapt a large pre-trained model to downstream tasks by training only a *small fraction* of parameters (typically **< 1% to 5%** of total) while keeping the base weights frozen.
- **Advantages (1.5 marks — any two):**
  1. **Storage efficiency** — stores only a few MB per task instead of a full multi-GB checkpoint.
  2. **Compute efficiency** — trainable on a single consumer GPU; no need for multi-node clusters.
  3. **Avoids catastrophic forgetting** — base knowledge is preserved.
  4. **Multi-task serving** — swap small adapters per request.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is LoRA? Write the mathematical expression for the LoRA-modified weight matrix.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **LoRA Definition (1.5 marks):** Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) injects trainable low-rank decomposition matrices $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ alongside the frozen weight $W_0$, where $r \ll \min(d, k)$.
- **Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A
$$

where $\alpha$ is a scaling hyperparameter, $A$ is initialized from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ and $B$ is initialized to **zero**, ensuring $W = W_0$ at the start of training.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) method in detail. Derive the formula for the number of trainable parameters in LoRA and compare it with full fine-tuning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the QLoRA technique. How does it reduce memory consumption further, and what is the role of the NormalFloat (NF4) quantization? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — LoRA Explanation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** LoRA, proposed by Hu et al. (2021), addresses the inefficiency of full fine-tuning by hypothesizing that the change in weights during adaptation has a **low intrinsic rank**.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** For a pre-trained matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, the update is constrained to a low-rank form:

$$
W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A
$$

with $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, $r \ll \min(d, k)$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Initialization: $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $B = 0$. Thus $\Delta W = 0$ at step 0 — model behaves identically to base.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Trainable parameter count derivation:

$$
\text{Params}_{\text{LoRA}} = \underbrace{d \cdot r}_{B} + \underbrace{r \cdot k}_{A} = r(d + k)
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** For $d = k = 4096$, $r = 8$:

$$
\text{Params}_{\text{LoRA}} = 8(4096+4096) = 65536
$$

Full fine-tuning of the same matrix: $4096 \times 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216$ parameters.
Reduction factor: $\rho = \frac{65536}{16777216} \approx 0.39\%$. At inference, $W$ is merged, so **no extra latency**.

---

**Part (b) — QLoRA (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) combines **4-bit quantization** of the base model with **LoRA adapters kept in BF16**. This enables fine-tuning a 65B-parameter model on a single 48 GB GPU.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Forward pass:

$$
Y = (W_0)^{\text{NF4-dequant}} X + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A X
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **NormalFloat (NF4) Quantization:** A novel 4-bit data type designed for *normally distributed weights*. It divides the quantization range into **16 quantile-based bins** that are equal-sized in terms of input probability mass (not uniform spacing). This is information-theoretically optimal for Gaussian-distributed neural-network weights and reduces quantization error compared to FP4/INT4.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Additional QLoRA innovations:
- **Double Quantization:** Quantizing the *quantization constants themselves* to save another ~3 GB.
- **Paged Optimizers:** Uses NVIDIA's unified memory to page optimizer states to CPU RAM during gradient spikes, preventing OOM errors.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Memory comparison for a 65B model:
- Full FT in FP16: ~780 GB
- LoRA in FP16: ~390 GB (full model + adapters)
- **QLoRA: ~48 GB** ✓

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the three main families of PEFT methods (Additive, Reparameterization, Selective) with at least one example technique from each family. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain Prefix Tuning. Compare Prefix Tuning with Prompt Tuning and P-Tuning v2. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Three PEFT Families (7 marks)**

**1. Additive Methods (2.5 marks):** Inject *new* trainable modules/tokens into the frozen model.
- **Examples:** Adapter Layers (Houlsby), Prefix Tuning, Prompt Tuning, P-Tuning v2.
- **Mechanism:** A bottleneck MLP $W_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, ReLU, $W_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}$ is inserted after the attention and feed-forward sub-layers.
- **Cost:** Adds inference latency unless reparameterized (e.g., Compacter, Pfeiffer adapters).

**2. Reparameterization-Based Methods (2.5 marks):** Re-express the weight update $\Delta W$ as a product of two low-rank matrices, with the *reparameterized* matrices trained and merged at inference.
- **Examples:** LoRA, LoRA-FA, QLoRA.
- **Mechanism:** $W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$, $r \ll d, k$.
- **Key advantage:** **Zero inference overhead** because $W$ can be merged back.

**3. Selective / Sparse Methods (2 marks):** Update only a *subset* of *existing* parameters without adding new ones.
- **Examples:** BitFit (bias terms only), Diff Pruning (learns sparse diff).
- **Mechanism:** BitFit updates *only* the bias vectors $b$ in all linear layers and LayerNorm scales, achieving surprisingly good results with only ~0.1% of parameters.

---

**Part (b) — Prefix Tuning (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

> In Prefix Tuning, trainable prefix vectors $P_K, P_V$ of length $n_{\text{prefix}}$ are prepended to the **Key** and **Value** matrices of every transformer attention layer.
> For layer $\ell$:
> - New $K^{(\ell)} = [P_K^{(\ell)}; K^{(\ell)}]$
> - New $V^{(\ell)} = [P_V^{(\ell)}; V^{(\ell)}]$
> Only $P_K^{(\ell)}, P_V^{(\ell)}$ are trainable; the entire base model is frozen.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Prompt Tuning | Prefix Tuning | P-Tuning v2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Where prompts are added | Input embedding only | K, V of **every** attention layer | K, V of every layer (deep prompts) |
| Trainable params | $m \cdot d$ | $L \cdot n \cdot 2d$ | $L \cdot n \cdot 2d$ |
| Reparameterization | No (direct) | Yes (MLP head, often removed later) | **No** (stable, like prefix) |
| Quality on small models | Poor | Good | **Good** |
| Quality on large models (>10B) | Good | Good | Good |
| Parameter count | **Lowest** | Higher | Higher |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** P-Tuning v2 essentially *removes* the reparameterization MLP from Prefix Tuning, making it more parameter-efficient and stable, while retaining the deep-prompt benefits across all layers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Parameter Count Mistake:** Writing LoRA's trainable params as $r^2$ or $r \cdot d \cdot k$ instead of $r(d+k)$ → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Initialization Confusion:** Saying $A$ is zero and $B$ is Gaussian — the *correct* order is **$A$ is Gaussian and $B$ is zero**, so that $BA = 0$ initially. Wrong direction = full mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the Scaling Factor:** Omitting $\frac{\alpha}{r}$ in the LoRA equation → -1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing PEFT families:** Writing "LoRA is an additive method" is **wrong** — it is reparameterization-based. Mixing families loses categorization marks.
> 5. **Inference Overhead:** Saying LoRA *increases* inference latency — incorrect. $W$ is merged with $W_0$, so **zero** extra latency.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PEFT Definition:** Adapt a frozen pre-trained model by training a tiny subset of parameters (typically **< 1% – 5%**).
- ✅ **Three Families:** **Additive** (Adapters, Prompt, Prefix), **Reparameterization** (LoRA, QLoRA), **Selective** (BitFit, IA³).
- ✅ **LoRA Equation (memorize verbatim):** $W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$.
- ✅ **LoRA Trainable Params:** $r(d + k)$, *not* $r \cdot d \cdot k$.
- ✅ **LoRA Initialization:** $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $B = 0$ (so $W = W_0$ at start).
- ✅ **Inference:** LoRA is *merged* into $W_0$ — **zero extra latency**.
- ✅ **QLoRA:** Base model in **NF4 (4-bit)**, adapters in **BF16**, enables 65B on one 48 GB GPU.
- ✅ **Prefix Tuning:** Adds trainable K, V prefixes in *every* layer; trainable params = $L \cdot n \cdot 2d$.
- ✅ **Prompt Tuning:** Adds soft prompts at input embedding only; trainable params = $m \cdot d$.
- ✅ **P-Tuning v2:** Prefix tuning *without* the reparameterization MLP.
- ✅ **BitFit:** Updates only bias terms; ~0.05% – 0.1% trainable params.
- ✅ **IA³:** Multiplicative learned vectors; even fewer params than LoRA, comparable quality.
- ✅ **Reduction Formula:** $\rho = \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} = \frac{r}{d} + \frac{r}{k}$.
- ✅ **Why LoRA works:** Empirical finding by Aghajanyan et al. (2020) that fine-tuning updates have **low intrinsic rank**.
- ✅ **Industry Standard:** LoRA / QLoRA are the **de-facto** PEFT methods for LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma.
- ✅ **DreamBooth-LoRA:** Personalization of Stable Diffusion in ~10 minutes using < 100 MB adapter.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Strategy for Module 4:**
> 1. Draw the LoRA block diagram first; examiners reward visual clarity.
> 2. Always quote the **trainable param formula** $r(d+k)$ explicitly.
> 3. State the **three PEFT families** by name — questions often begin with "Classify PEFT methods…".
> 4. Mention **QLoRA + NF4 + Single 48 GB GPU** as the headline result for memory-efficient fine-tuning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
