<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Prompt Tuning

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> **Prompt Tuning** is a *Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)* technique in which a small set of **continuous, learnable embedding vectors** (commonly called *soft prompts* or *prompt parameters*) are prepended to the input of a frozen, pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM). Only these prompt vectors are updated during training via backpropagation, while the entire backbone of the LLM remains **frozen**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cue Cards" Metaphor
Imagine a brilliant foreign-language expert (the **frozen LLM**) who has memorized an entire dictionary but sometimes answers questions in an inconsistent style. Instead of re-teaching the expert the whole language (which is **fine-tuning**, slow and expensive), you hand them a small stack of **cue cards** (the **soft prompts**) before every question. The expert reads the cue cards, understands the *context, format, and intent* you want, and then answers. The cue cards themselves get refined over time based on feedback — but the expert's knowledge never changes. This is exactly how **Prompt Tuning** works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard Prompts vs Soft Prompts — A Critical Distinction**
> - **Hard Prompt (Manual Prompt Engineering):** Human-written natural language instructions such as *"Translate the following English sentence to French:"*. The LLM tokenizer processes these as discrete tokens. They are *not trainable*.
> - **Soft Prompt (Prompt Tuning):** Continuous, real-valued vectors living in the LLM's *embedding space*. They are *not* required to correspond to any real word. They are entirely **trainable parameters**.

### Key Quantities (Highlighted for Quick Recall)
- **$n$** = number of prompt tokens (typical range: **10 – 100**)
- **$d$** = embedding dimension of the LLM (e.g., **4096** for LLaMA-7B, **5120** for LLaMA-13B)
- **$|P_{\text{train}}|$** = trainable parameter count = **$n \times d$**
- **$|M|$** = total LLM parameter count (e.g., **7 Billion**)
- **Efficiency Ratio** = **$|P_{\text{train}}|/|M| \approx 0.001\%$** to **$0.1\%$**

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** It tests your understanding of the *efficiency-vs-expressiveness trade-off* in modern LLM adaptation. Memorize the soft vs hard prompt distinction, the frozen-backbone principle, and the parameter-count formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Architecture of Prompt Tuning

The mechanics of prompt tuning can be broken down into a clean, sequential pipeline:

1. **Initialize a Learnable Prompt Tensor** $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, where each row $\mathbf{p}_i$ is a $d$-dimensional embedding vector. Initialization is typically performed by sampling from the LLM's existing vocabulary embedding distribution (Lester et al., 2021).

2. **Freeze the LLM Backbone** $\mathcal{M}_\theta$ where $\theta$ denotes the billions of parameters. Mathematically: $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}$ for every training step $t$.

3. **Construct the Augmented Input Sequence** by concatenating the soft prompt $\mathbf{P}$ with the tokenized input embeddings $\mathbf{E}_x$ from the user's text query.

4. **Forward Pass Through Frozen LLM:** The combined sequence $[\mathbf{P}; \mathbf{E}_x]$ propagates through all transformer layers. Only $\mathbf{P}$ influences gradient computation.

5. **Compute Loss** $\mathcal{L}$ (typically cross-entropy on the next-token prediction task) over the target tokens.

6. **Backpropagate and Update ONLY the prompt parameters** $\mathbf{P}$, leaving $\theta$ untouched.

7. **At Inference Time**, the trained $\mathbf{P}^*$ is prepended to any user input, *without any architectural change* to the deployed model.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Trainable parameter count | $\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert = n \cdot d$ | $n$: prompt length, $d$: embedding dim |
| 2 | Parameter efficiency ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert}{\vert M \vert}$ | Typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | Augmented input tensor | $\mathbf{Z}^{(0)} = [\mathbf{P}; \mathbf{E}_x] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+k) \times d}$ | $k$ = input token count |
| 4 | Frozen constraint | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}, \quad \forall t$ | No update on LLM weights |
| 5 | Soft prompt update rule | $\mathbf{P}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{P}^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{P}} \mathcal{L}$ | $\alpha$: learning rate |
| 6 | Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{T} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | $T$: target sequence length |
| 7 | Storage cost per task | $S_{\text{task}} = n \cdot d \cdot 4$ bytes (FP32) | E.g., $100 \times 4096 \times 4 = 1.6$ MB |
| 8 | Prefix Tuning parameter count | $\vert P_{\text{prefix}} \vert = L \cdot n \cdot d$ | $L$ = number of transformer layers |
| 9 | Adapter Tuning parameter count | $\vert P_{\text{adapter}} \vert = 2 \cdot d \cdot r + d + r$ | $r$ = bottleneck rank |
| 10 | Effective batch prompt size | $\mathbf{P}_{\text{eff}} = \mathbf{P} + \text{Reparam}(\mathbf{P}_0, W)$ | Used in **P-Tuning v2** |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Prompt Tuning in Engineering
- **Edge Deployment:** Store only ~1.6 MB of prompt parameters per task instead of 14 GB of full LLM weights.
- **Multi-Tenant Serving:** One frozen LLM serves thousands of customers, each with a private tiny prompt — privacy and scalability benefits.
- **Rapid Domain Adaptation:** Healthcare, Legal, Finance — adapt the base LLM to a domain in minutes on a single GPU.
- **Federated Learning Compatibility:** Only small gradients for $\mathbf{P}$ need to be transmitted, reducing bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Comparison Snapshot (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
> - **Fine-Tuning:** Updates all $\vert M \vert$ parameters. Highest accuracy. Highest cost.
> - **Prompt Tuning:** Updates $n \cdot d$ parameters. Slightly lower accuracy. **Cheapest storage.**
> - **Prefix Tuning:** Updates $L \cdot n \cdot d$ parameters (one prompt per layer). Better than prompt tuning at low $n$.
> - **LoRA:** Updates low-rank matrices. Most popular in industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Mathematical Formulation of Prompt Tuning

Let us define the problem rigorously. Given:

- A frozen LLM $\mathcal{M}_\theta$ with parameters $\theta$ kept constant.
- A learnable soft prompt matrix $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$.
- An input text sequence $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ tokenized and embedded as $\mathbf{E}_x \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$.
- A target output sequence $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$.

**Step 1 — Concatenation of Prompt and Input Embeddings**

The augmented input sequence is formed as:

$$
\mathbf{Z}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{p}_1 \\ \mathbf{p}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{p}_n \\ \mathbf{E}_{x,1} \\ \mathbf{E}_{x,2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{E}_{x,k} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+k) \times d}
$$

Here, $\mathbf{p}_i$ is the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{P}$, a learnable $d$-dimensional vector. The first $n$ rows constitute the soft prompt, while the remaining $k$ rows are derived from the input tokens.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass Through Transformer Blocks**

Each transformer block $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, L$ computes:

$$
\mathbf{Z}^{(\ell)} = \text{Block}_\ell^{(\theta)}\left(\mathbf{Z}^{(\ell-1)}\right)
$$

Since $\theta$ is frozen, only the values in $\mathbf{Z}^{(0)}$ derived from $\mathbf{P}$ are subject to gradient flow. The attention mechanism with the augmented sequence can be written as:

$$
\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \mathbf{V}
$$

where the queries, keys, and values are projected from $\mathbf{Z}^{(\ell-1)}$ using the frozen weight matrices $W_Q^{(\ell)}, W_K^{(\ell)}, W_V^{(\ell)}$.

**Step 3 — Output Logits and Loss Computation**

The transformer output corresponding to the last input position produces logits over the vocabulary:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}}_i = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{\text{LM}} \cdot \mathbf{z}_{i}^{(\text{last})}\right), \quad i = 1, \ldots, \vert V \vert
$$

The cross-entropy loss for next-token prediction is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{P}) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \hat{y}_{y_t}^{(t)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This loss is a *function of* $\mathbf{P}$ only, because $\theta$ is fixed. The gradients therefore flow exclusively back through the prompt positions.

**Step 4 — Gradient Computation with Respect to $\mathbf{P}$**

By the chain rule, for each prompt token $\mathbf{p}_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}_{i}^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{\ell=j+1}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}_{i}^{(\ell)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}_{i}^{(j)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}_{i}^{(j)}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Parameter Update (Adam / SGD)**

The soft prompt is updated as:

$$
\mathbf{P}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{P}^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{P}}
$$

For Adam optimizer, the moment estimates are maintained:

$$
\mathbf{m}^{(t+1)} = \beta_1 \mathbf{m}^{(t)} + (1 - \beta_1) \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{P}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}^{(t+1)} = \beta_2 \mathbf{v}^{(t)} + (1 - \beta_2) \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{P}}\right)^{\!2}
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{P}^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\hat{\mathbf{m}}^{(t+1)}}{\sqrt{\hat{\mathbf{v}}^{(t+1)}} + \epsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Training Termination**

The training loop terminates when the validation loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\mathbf{P})$ plateaus, yielding the optimal soft prompt $\mathbf{P}^*$.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Parameter Count Calculation

> **Problem:** A 7-billion-parameter LLM has an embedding dimension of $d = 4096$. You are performing prompt tuning with a prompt length of $n = 100$ tokens. Calculate the trainable parameter count and the efficiency ratio.

**Solution:**

$$
\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert = n \cdot d = 100 \times 4096 = 409{,}600 \text{ parameters}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert}{\vert M \vert} = \frac{4.096 \times 10^{5}}{7 \times 10^{9}} \approx 5.85 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\eta_{\%} = 0.00585\% \text{ of the model is trainable}
$$

Storage cost in FP32:

$$
S = 409{,}600 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 1.6384 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Insight:** A task-specific adapter for a 7B LLM fits in **under 2 MB**, while the full model requires **~28 GB** in FP32. This is why prompt tuning is the *de facto* standard for multi-task LLM deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Prompt Tuning Architecture Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User Input Text X] --> B[Tokenizer Module]
    B --> C[Input Token Embeddings E_X]
    D[Soft Prompt Tensor P_theta, n x d, Trainable] --> E[Concatenation Operator]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Augmented Sequence Z0 of shape n+k by d]
    F --> G[Frozen LLM Backbone M_theta, Billions of params, NO GRADIENT]
    G --> H[Output Logits Y_hat over Vocabulary]
    H --> I[Cross Entropy Loss L]
    I --> J{Backward Pass}
    J -- Gradients flow only to P_theta --> K[Update P using Adam or SGD]
    J -- Gradients blocked on M_theta --> L[LLM Weights Remain Frozen]
    K --> M[Trained Soft Prompt P_star, Stored per task]
    M --> N[Deployment: Prepend P_star to any new query]
```

### 4.2 Comparison: Prompt Tuning vs Prefix Tuning vs LoRA

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Full Fine-Tuning] --> A1[Updates: 100 percent of params, 7B params updated]
    A1 --> A2[Storage: 28 GB per task, Slow training, Highest accuracy]

    B[Prompt Tuning] --> B1[Updates: Input layer only, n x d params, e.g. 410K]
    B1 --> B2[Storage: 1.6 MB per task, Fast training, Good for large models]

    C[Prefix Tuning] --> C1[Updates: All L layers, L x n x d params]
    C1 --> C2[Storage: 50 MB per task, Better at small n than Prompt Tuning]

    D[LoRA] --> D1[Updates: Low rank matrices, 2 x d x r params]
    D1 --> D2[Storage: 8 to 30 MB per task, Industry standard, Best quality]
```

### 4.3 Lifecycle of a Soft Prompt — Training to Deployment

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Initialize P from Vocabulary Embeddings] --> S2[Step 2: Freeze M_theta]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Concat P and E_X to form Z0]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Forward Pass Through Frozen M_theta]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Cross Entropy Loss L]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Backprop Only into P]
    S6 --> S7{Convergence Check}
    S7 -- Not Converged --> S3
    S7 -- Converged --> S8[Step 7: Save P_star as Task Adapter]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Deploy with One Base LLM and N Task Prompts]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Multi-Tenant Inference at Scale]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams and Not Photographs?** Prompt tuning is a computational process — its *architecture*, *data flow*, and *lifecycle* are what examiners test. The Mermaid block diagrams above capture the precise engineering of the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **hard prompt** and **soft prompt** in the context of LLM adaptation. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A *hard prompt* is a human-crafted natural language instruction (e.g., *"Summarize the following article in 3 lines:"*) that is tokenized and fed to the LLM. It is **discrete, interpretable, and not trainable**. A *soft prompt*, in contrast, is a set of **continuous, real-valued embedding vectors** of dimension $d$ that are prepended to the input. Soft prompts are **trainable via backpropagation**, are not constrained to correspond to real words, and live in the LLM's embedding space. Soft prompts enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning without modifying the LLM.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** State the **trainable parameter count formula** for prompt tuning and explain why this method is termed "parameter-efficient." *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The trainable parameter count is given by $\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert = n \cdot d$, where $n$ is the prompt length (typically 10–100) and $d$ is the LLM embedding dimension. For a 7B model with $d = 4096$ and $n = 100$, only **409,600 parameters** are trainable — about **0.006%** of the total. This extreme reduction in trainable parameters — while preserving competitive downstream performance — is why the technique is termed *parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)*.

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper, 14 Marks, Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **Prompt Tuning** with a neat block diagram. Describe how the soft prompt interacts with the frozen LLM. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Prompt Tuning architecture consists of three primary components: a **trainable soft prompt tensor** $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, a **frozen LLM backbone** $\mathcal{M}_\theta$, and a **concatenation layer** that merges the two. The workflow is as follows:

1. The user input $x$ is tokenized into $k$ tokens and converted to embeddings $\mathbf{E}_x \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$ via the LLM's embedding matrix.
2. The soft prompt $\mathbf{P}$ is prepended to $\mathbf{E}_x$ to form the augmented input $\mathbf{Z}^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+k) \times d}$.
3. $\mathbf{Z}^{(0)}$ propagates through all $L$ transformer blocks of the frozen LLM.
4. The output corresponding to the last position is projected to vocabulary logits via $\mathbf{W}_{\text{LM}}$.
5. The cross-entropy loss is computed against the target sequence $y$.
6. Gradients are backpropagated *only* through the prompt positions, updating $\mathbf{P}$ via Adam.
7. After convergence, $\mathbf{P}^*$ is stored as a per-task adapter of size $n \cdot d \cdot 4$ bytes (FP32).

The key insight is that the soft prompt **steers the LLM's attention and hidden states** by influencing the key-value memory of every attention layer, even though the soft prompt vectors are only physically present in the input layer.

**(b)** Compare **Prompt Tuning**, **Prefix Tuning**, and **LoRA** in terms of parameter count, memory, and task performance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Prompt Tuning | Prefix Tuning | LoRA |
|--------|---------------|----------------|------|
| Where is adaptation added? | Input embeddings only | Every layer's keys and values | Weight matrices (low-rank) |
| Trainable param count | $n \cdot d$ | $L \cdot n \cdot d$ | $2 \cdot d \cdot r$ per matrix |
| Typical storage per task | ~1.6 MB | ~50–100 MB | ~8–30 MB |
| Best at small $n$? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Quality at scale ($>10$B) | Comparable to full FT | Comparable | Often surpasses full FT |
| Industry adoption | Moderate | Low | High (most popular) |

**Discussion:** Prompt tuning is the *lightest* in memory but requires sufficiently large LLMs ($>10$B params) to match full fine-tuning. Prefix tuning introduces prompts at every layer, improving low-$n$ behavior. LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices, providing the best quality-to-cost trade-off, which is why it dominates production systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** Derive the **gradient update rule** for the soft prompt $\mathbf{P}$ in a frozen LLM setting, and show that the LLM parameters $\theta$ receive zero gradient. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Let $\mathcal{L}$ be the cross-entropy loss. For a frozen $\theta$, the chain rule gives:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(L)}}{\partial \theta} = 0
$$

Because the forward pass is detached from $\theta$ (no gradient flows through frozen parameters), the gradient with respect to $\theta$ vanishes. Conversely, for $\mathbf{P}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{P}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{\ell=1}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(\ell)}}{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(\ell-1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{Z}^{(0)}}{\partial \mathbf{P}}
$$

Since $\mathbf{Z}^{(0)}$ is a linear function of $\mathbf{P}$ (concatenation), this gradient is non-zero. The Adam update then becomes:

$$
\mathbf{P}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{P}^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\hat{\mathbf{m}}^{(t+1)}}{\sqrt{\hat{\mathbf{v}}^{(t+1)}} + \epsilon}
$$

This proves that **only $\mathbf{P}$ is updated**, while $\theta$ remains unchanged.

**(b)** A team deploys a 13-billion-parameter LLM ($d = 5120$) for **50 different downstream tasks** using prompt tuning with $n = 50$. Calculate the **total storage overhead** for all task-specific prompts and compare it with the storage cost of **50 full fine-tuned copies** of the model. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Prompt Tuning Storage:**

$$
\vert P_{\text{task}} \vert = 50 \times 5120 = 256{,}000 \text{ parameters per task}
$$

$$
S_{\text{prompt}} = 256{,}000 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 1.024 \text{ MB per task}
$$

$$
S_{\text{total, prompt}} = 50 \times 1.024 \text{ MB} = 51.2 \text{ MB}
$$

**Full Fine-Tuning Storage (FP16, 2 bytes/param):**

$$
S_{\text{full}} = 13 \times 10^9 \times 2 \text{ bytes} = 26 \text{ GB per copy}
$$

$$
S_{\text{total, full}} = 50 \times 26 \text{ GB} = 1300 \text{ GB} = 1.3 \text{ TB}
$$

**Comparison Ratio:**

$$
\frac{S_{\text{total, full}}}{S_{\text{total, prompt}}} = \frac{1300 \text{ GB}}{51.2 \text{ MB}} \approx 25{,}390 \times
$$

> **Conclusion:** Prompt tuning reduces storage overhead by a factor of **~25,000×**, making it the only feasible strategy for large-scale multi-task LLM serving in production environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Prompt Tuning" with "Prompt Engineering":** These are *not* the same. Prompt engineering is manual, hard-prompt-based, and requires no training. Prompt tuning is automatic, soft-prompt-based, and involves gradient descent.
> 2. **Forgetting the Frozen Constraint:** Many students mistakenly write that "the model is also updated." This is a *guaranteed zero* on full-marks questions. Always state explicitly: *"The LLM backbone parameters $\theta$ remain frozen."*
> 3. **Mixing up Prefix Tuning and Prompt Tuning:** Prefix tuning introduces prompts at *every* layer, while prompt tuning only at the *input embedding layer*. The parameter count formulas differ by a factor of $L$.
> 4. **Unit Errors in Storage Calculation:** Remember FP32 = 4 bytes, FP16 = 2 bytes, INT8 = 1 byte. Forgetting to multiply by the byte-size is a frequent mark-deduction reason.
> 5. **Skipping the Initialization Strategy:** Examiners appreciate when you mention that soft prompts are typically initialized from the model's existing vocabulary embedding distribution, not random.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Prompt Tuning**
> - **Definition:** PEFT method that learns continuous prompt embeddings while keeping the LLM frozen.
> - **Soft vs Hard Prompt:** Soft = trainable continuous vectors; Hard = human-written discrete text.
> - **Parameter Count:** $\vert P_{\text{train}} \vert = n \cdot d$ (input-layer only).
> - **Efficiency Ratio:** Typically $0.001\%$ to $0.1\%$ of total LLM parameters.
> - **Storage per Task:** ~1–2 MB for a 7B-class LLM in FP32.
> - **Frozen Constraint:** $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}, \forall t$.
> - **Update Rule:** Adam or SGD applied to $\mathbf{P}$ only.
> - **Initialization:** Sample from LLM's vocabulary embedding distribution.
> - **Variants:** P-Tuning v1, P-Tuning v2 (deep prompt tuning), Prefix Tuning, Prompt-Tuning with Reparameterization.
> - **Scaling Insight:** Prompt tuning matches full fine-tuning only when LLM size is **large enough (typically $\geq 10$B params)**.
> - **Industrial Alternatives:** LoRA, QLoRA, Adapters — each with different cost-quality trade-offs.
> - **Key Use Cases:** Multi-tenant LLM serving, edge deployment, domain adaptation, federated learning.
> - **Examiner's Hot Keywords:** "Soft prompt," "Frozen backbone," "Parameter-efficient," "Embedding space," "PEFT," "Multi-task serving."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prefix Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prefix Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prefix Tuning** is a *parameter-efficient fine-tuning* (PEFT) technique for large pre-trained language models in which a short, learnable sequence of **continuous task-specific vectors** (called the *prefix*) is prepended to the **keys (K) and values (V) at every transformer layer**, while the original model parameters remain completely frozen. Formally introduced by Li & Liang (ACL 2021) in *"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation"*, the method optimizes only the prefix parameters $\theta_{P}$ (typically a tiny fraction, **< 0.1%**, of the total model size) and discards the rest of the backbone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – PEFT):**
> Prefix Tuning belongs to the family of *additive* parameter-efficient methods. Unlike prompt engineering, the "prompt" here is **not natural language** — it is a continuous embedding in the same latent space as the model's representations, making it differentiable and trainable via backpropagation.

### 1.2 The Intuition: A Real-World Analogy

Imagine a highly experienced **chartered accountant (the frozen LLM)** who knows every tax regulation by heart, but you want her to specialize in *cryptocurrency auditing*. You have three options:

| Option | Analogy | Problem |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | Send her back to university for 4 more years | Expensive (huge GPU + storage cost) |
| **Prompt Engineering** | Give her a sticky note with a verbal instruction | Lossy — the words are constrained, fragile, and limited |
| **Prefix Tuning** | Hand her a **small personalized "cheat card"** of carefully tuned *mental cues* she can use at *every* stage of her reasoning | She uses her original knowledge, but steers her thought process layer-by-layer |

The "cheat card" is the **prefix** — a sequence of trainable vectors that does not change the accountant's knowledge (frozen model) but **re-conditions how she attends to and processes** the input at every internal layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (from the original paper):**
> - Default prefix length $\vert P \vert = \mathbf{200}$ tokens
> - Achieves comparable performance to full fine-tuning on summarization (BART) and table-to-text (T5) generation
> - Parameter reduction: from **~100%** to **~0.1%** of model size

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step

1. **Freeze the Backbone:** All original transformer weights $W$ are frozen. Gradients flow *through* them but they are not updated.
2. **Initialize the Prefix:** Create two trainable matrices per layer $\ell$ — the key-prefix $P_{K}^{\ell} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d}$ and the value-prefix $P_{V}^{\ell} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d}$, where $d$ is the hidden dimension.
3. **Reparameterize (Training Time Only):** A small feed-forward MLP maps a lower-dimensional prefix $P' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d'}$ (with $d' \le d$) into the full prefix — this stabilizes training.
4. **Prepend at Every Layer:** The augmented keys and values become
$$K'_{\ell} = [P_{K}^{\ell} \ ; \ K_{\ell}] \quad , \quad V'_{\ell} = [P_{V}^{\ell} \ ; \ V_{\ell}]$$
   so that attention operates on a *longer* context consisting of prefix + input.
5. **Train via Standard LM Loss:** Compute cross-entropy on the target sequence; only the prefix parameters (and the reparameterization MLP) receive gradient updates.
6. **Discard the MLP, Keep the Prefix:** At inference, the MLP is dropped. The precomputed prefix $P$ is concatenated to each layer's K/V cache.

### 2.2 The "Why" and the "How"

- **Why prepend to K/V, not the input?** Prepending only at the input embedding forces the model to "squeeze" all conditioning information into one place. Prepending at *every* layer gives the prefix the ability to **steer attention at every depth** of the network — much more expressive.
- **Why reparameterize?** Direct training of $P$ is unstable because gradients must travel through many transformer layers to reach a *single* set of parameters. The MLP transformation creates a smoother optimization landscape.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Prefix Tuning is the **de-facto PEFT choice for autoregressive generation** in production — chatbots, code generators, RAG adapters — because the prefix can be **swapped at inference time per task** without reloading the base model.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_{\ell} = \text{TransformerBlock}_{\ell}(h_{\ell-1})$ | Standard layer recursion | Baseline |
| 2 | $K'_{\ell} = [P_{K}^{\ell} \ ; \ K_{\ell}]$ | Augmented key matrix | Prefix injection |
| 3 | $V'_{\ell} = [P_{V}^{\ell} \ ; \ V_{\ell}]$ | Augmented value matrix | Prefix injection |
| 4 | $\text{Attn}(Q,K',V') = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K'^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right)V'$ | Prefix-augmented attention | Forward pass |
| 5 | $P = W_{2}\,\tanh(W_{1}\,P')$ | Reparameterization MLP | Training stability |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log p_{\theta_{P}}(y_{t} \mid x, y_{<t})$ | LM loss over prefix params $\theta_{P}$ | Optimization |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\vert \theta_{P} \vert}{\vert \theta_{\text{model}} \vert} \approx \mathbf{0.1\%}$ | Parameter ratio | Efficiency metric |
| 8 | $\vert P \vert = \mathbf{200}$ | Default prefix length (paper) | Hyperparameter |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Prefix-Augmented Scaled Dot-Product Attention

We start from the standard scaled dot-product attention used inside every transformer block.

**Step 1 — Standard Attention (No Prefix):**
For a query $q_{i} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{k}}$ at position $i$ of input length $n$, the standard attention output is

$$\text{head}_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij}\, v_{j}$$

where the attention weight is

$$\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_{i}\cdot k_{j}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right)}{\sum_{m=1}^{n} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_{i}\cdot k_{m}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right)}$$

This is the softmax over input tokens only.

**Step 2 — Inject the Prefix into the Sequence Length:**

With prefix of length $\vert P \vert$, the new key and value sequences are

$$
\begin{aligned}
K'_{\ell} &= [\,P_{K}^{\ell} \ ; \ K_{\ell}\,] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P \vert + n) \times d_{k}} \\
V'_{\ell} &= [\,P_{V}^{\ell} \ ; \ V_{\ell}\,] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P \vert + n) \times d_{v}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The semicolon $;$ denotes *row-wise concatenation* along the sequence dimension.

**Step 3 — Rewrite the Attention Sum Over the Extended Index Set:**

Let the extended index set be $\mathcal{I} = \{\,1, 2, \dots, \vert P \vert, \vert P \vert+1, \dots, \vert P \vert + n\,\}$. The first $\vert P \vert$ indices correspond to prefix positions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{head}_{i}^{\text{(prefix)}} &= \sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}} \alpha'_{ij}\, v'_{j} \\[4pt]
\alpha'_{ij} &= \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_{i}\cdot k'_{j}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right)}{\sum_{m \in \mathcal{I}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q_{i}\cdot k'_{m}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Split the Sum into Prefix-Attention and Input-Attention:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{head}_{i}^{\text{(prefix)}} &= \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert P \vert} \alpha'_{ij}\, p^{V}_{j}}_{\text{attention to prefix}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{j=\vert P \vert+1}^{\vert P \vert+n} \alpha'_{ij}\, v_{j}}_{\text{attention to input}} \\[4pt]
&= A^{\text{prefix}}_{i}\,P_{V} \;+\; A^{\text{inp}}_{i}\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

where $A^{\text{prefix}}_{i}$ and $A^{\text{inp}}_{i}$ are the respective attention-weight vectors. This shows that the prefix acts as an *additional context* the query can read from, without changing the input embeddings themselves.

**Step 5 — Matrix Form of the Full Operation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attn}(Q,\,K',\,V') &= \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q\,K'^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) V' \\[4pt]
\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q\,[P_{K};K]^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) \,[P_{V};V] &= \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q P_{K}^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) P_{V} \;+\; \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) V
\end{aligned}
$$

(Shown conceptually — the softmax *does not* distribute additively, but the symbolic decomposition illustrates how the prefix contributes as a separate context block.)

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reparameterization Trick

**Step 1 — Naive Parameterization Fails:**

Directly training $P \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d}$ produces **high-variance gradients** and unstable loss curves, because $P$ sits at the input of *every* layer — small perturbations propagate and amplify.

**Step 2 — Introduce a Smaller Latent Prefix:**

Let $P' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d'}$ with $d' \le d$ be the *actual* trainable parameter. Apply a two-layer MLP with $\tanh$ non-linearity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 1:} \quad H &= \tanh(W_{1}\,P' + b_{1}) \\[4pt]
\text{Layer 2:} \quad P &= W_{2}\,H + b_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the complete transformation is

$$P = W_{2}\,\tanh(W_{1}\,P') + b_{2}$$

where $W_{1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d' \times d}$ and $W_{2} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d'}$. The bottleneck $d'$ reduces effective rank and stabilizes optimization.

**Step 3 — Forward Pass During Training:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= W_{2}\,\tanh(W_{1}\,P') + b_{2} \\[4pt]
K'_{\ell} &= [P_{K}^{\ell} \ ; \ K_{\ell}], \quad V'_{\ell} = [P_{V}^{\ell} \ ; \ V_{\ell}] \\[4pt]
\hat{y} &= f_{\text{LM}}\!\left(x \ ; \ P_{K}^{1:\ell_{\max}}, P_{V}^{1:\ell_{\max}}\right) \\[4pt]
\mathcal{L} &= -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p(y_{t} \mid x, y_{<t} \ ; \ P', W_{1}, W_{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Inference Simplification:**

After training, the MLP is **discarded** and $P$ is **precomputed once** and stored. At inference time, the prefix is constant per task and concatenation reduces to a simple lookup:

$$P^{\text{(final)}} = W_{2}^{*}\,\tanh(W_{1}^{*}\,P'^{*}) + b_{2}^{*} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{cached, MLP dropped}$$

This is what makes Prefix Tuning **fast at inference** — no extra forward passes, no extra layers.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Trainable Parameter Count

For a model with $L$ transformer layers, hidden dim $d$, prefix length $\vert P \vert$, and reparameterization dim $d'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{P}^{\text{total}} &= L \times \left( 2 \vert P \vert d \right) \quad \text{(raw prefix)} \\[4pt]
\theta_{P}^{\text{raw}} &= 2L \vert P \vert d \quad ; \quad \theta_{P}^{\text{reparam}} = 2L(\vert P \vert d' + d' d + d + d d' + d) \\[4pt]
\text{Ratio} \; r &= \dfrac{\theta_{P}^{\text{reparam}}}{\theta_{\text{model}}} \approx \dfrac{2 L \vert P \vert d'}{L \cdot 12 d^{2}} = \dfrac{\vert P \vert d'}{6 d^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For GPT-2 style: $L = 12$, $d = 768$, $\vert P \vert = 200$, $d' = 768 \Rightarrow r \approx \mathbf{2.7\%}$ (larger models drop to **< 0.1%**).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow (Prefix Tuning)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Input Text x"] --> stepB["Tokenizer"]
    stepB --> stepC["Token Embeddings E_x"]
    
    subgraph frozen[ "Frozen Transformer Backbone (Parameters NOT updated)" ]
        direction TB
        stepC --> stepD["Layer 1: Self-Attention"]
        stepD --> stepE["Layer 2: Self-Attention"]
        stepE --> stepF["Layer L: Self-Attention"]
        stepF --> stepG["Layer Norm + Output Head"]
    end
    
    subgraph trainP[ "Trainable Prefix Parameters" ]
        direction TB
        stepH["Init Small Prefix P prime"] --> stepI["MLP Reparameterization: P = W2 tanh W1 P prime"]
        stepI --> stepJ["Per-Layer Prefix Vectors P_K and P_V"]
    end
    
    stepJ -.->|"Prepended to K, V"| stepD
    stepJ -.->|"Prepended to K, V"| stepE
    stepJ -.->|"Prepended to K, V"| stepF
    
    stepG --> stepK["Output y hat"]
    
    stepL["Loss: Cross-Entropy vs y"] --> stepM["Gradient flows back ONLY through prefix"]
    stepK --> stepL
    stepM --> stepH
```

### 4.2 Attention-Layer Zoom-In (Prefix Injection Point)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pA["Trainable Prefix P_K ell"] --> concatK["Concat along seq dim"]
    pB["Frozen K ell from input"] --> concatK
    concatK --> augK["Augmented K prime ell"]
    
    pC["Trainable Prefix P_V ell"] --> concatV["Concat along seq dim"]
    pD["Frozen V ell from input"] --> concatV
    concatV --> augV["Augmented V prime ell"]
    
    qIn["Query Q ell from input"] --> attn["Scaled Dot-Product Attention: softmax Q K prime T over sqrt d k times V prime"]
    augK --> attn
    augV --> attn
    attn --> out["Head Output h ell"]
```

### 4.3 Inference-Time Flow (MLP Discarded)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    infA["Precomputed Prefix P (cached, no MLP)"] --> infB["Per-Layer K, V Augmentation"]
    infC["New Input x"] --> infD["Frozen LM Forward Pass"]
    infB --> infD
    infD --> infE["Generated Output y"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> - **Solid arrows** = forward data flow.
> - **Dotted arrows** = parameter injection at attention layers.
> - The *frozen* box contains all $\approx 99.9\%$ of parameters; the *trainable* box contains $\approx 0.1\%$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`**  
*Define Prefix Tuning. In what way does it differ from conventional full fine-tuning of a language model?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Prefix Tuning is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method in which a small set of **continuous, learnable vectors (the prefix)** is prepended to the keys and values of every transformer layer, while the **original model parameters remain frozen**. The key differences from full fine-tuning are:

| Aspect | Full Fine-Tuning | Prefix Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters updated | 100% of model weights | Only prefix ($\approx 0.1\%$) |
| Storage per task | Full model copy (~GBs) | Prefix only (~MBs) |
| Training cost | Very high | Drastically reduced |
| Risk of catastrophic forgetting | Yes | Minimal |

**(1 mark definition + 2 marks for comparison table)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**  
*What is the "reparameterization trick" used in Prefix Tuning, and why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The reparameterization trick in Prefix Tuning expresses the trainable prefix $P \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d}$ as the output of a small **MLP** applied to a lower-dimensional prefix $P' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d'}$, where $d' \le d$:

$$P = W_{2}\,\tanh(W_{1}\,P') + b_{2}$$

**Why it is necessary:** Direct training of $P$ causes high gradient variance and unstable loss curves, since $P$ influences every transformer layer. The MLP bottleneck creates a **smoother optimization landscape** and improves training stability. At inference, the MLP is discarded and $P$ is precomputed. **(1 mark for formula + 2 marks for justification)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of **Prefix Tuning** with a neat block diagram. Describe how the prefix interacts with the attention mechanism of a transformer layer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **reparameterization transformation** used during training of Prefix Tuning and explain why the MLP can be discarded at inference time.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture Explanation [7 marks]:**

1. **Freeze base model** — all original weights of the transformer are non-trainable. *(1 mark)*
2. **Prefix construction** — at every layer $\ell$, two trainable matrices $P_{K}^{\ell} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d_{k}}$ and $P_{V}^{\ell} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d_{v}}$ are maintained. *(1 mark)*
3. **Key/Value augmentation** — the prefix vectors are concatenated with the layer's original K and V matrices along the sequence dimension, giving $K'_{\ell} = [P_{K}^{\ell} \ ; \ K_{\ell}]$ and $V'_{\ell} = [P_{V}^{\ell} \ ; \ V_{\ell}]$. *(2 marks)*
4. **Attention recomputation** — the scaled dot-product is now computed over a sequence of length $\vert P \vert + n$, allowing each query token to attend to the prefix as virtual context. *(1 mark)*
5. **Layer-wise stacking** — the same prefix injection happens at *every* layer of the transformer, giving the prefix global steering power. *(1 mark)*
6. **Block diagram** — the standard diagram is: Input → Frozen Embeddings → For each layer: [Augmented K,V from Prefix + Frozen Attention + FFN] → Output Head. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Reparameterization Derivation [7 marks]:**

1. **Problem setup** — direct optimization of $P$ suffers from gradient instability. *(1 mark)*
2. **Define smaller latent prefix** $P' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P \vert \times d'}$ with $d' \le d$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Two-layer MLP transformation:**
   $$H = \tanh(W_{1} P' + b_{1}), \quad P = W_{2} H + b_{2}$$
   This is the full reparameterization. *(2 marks)*
4. **Training loss:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log p(y_{t} \mid x, y_{<t} \ ; \ P', W_{1}, W_{2})$ — only the small set $\{P', W_{1}, W_{2}\}$ receives gradients. *(1 mark)*
5. **Why MLP is discarded at inference:** After training, $P$ is **precomputed once** via $P^{*} = W_{2}^{*}\,\tanh(W_{1}^{*}\,P'^{*}) + b_{2}^{*}$ and stored as a constant tensor. At inference, the prefix is a fixed lookup — no extra FLOPs, no extra latency. This makes Prefix Tuning essentially **free at deployment**. *(2 marks)*

---

**Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Prefix Tuning**, **Prompt Tuning**, and **LoRA** along the dimensions of: (i) where the parameters are inserted, (ii) parameter count, (iii) expressivity, and (iv) typical use cases.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **three advantages** and **two limitations** of Prefix Tuning. Mention one real-world application where it is preferred over full fine-tuning.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Study [7 marks]:**

| Dimension | Prompt Tuning | Prefix Tuning | LoRA |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Insertion point | Input embedding only | K, V at every layer | Low-rank update to weight matrices |
| (ii) Parameter count | Very low | Low | Low (rank-dependent) |
| (iii) Expressivity | Low (one layer) | High (every layer) | High |
| (iv) Typical use | Simple classification | Generation, summarization | All tasks |

*(Allocate ~1.5–2 marks per row, with a short sentence justification)*

**(b) Advantages & Limitations [7 marks]:**

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Parameter efficiency** — only $\approx 0.1\%$ parameters trained, drastically reducing GPU memory and storage per task. *(2 marks)*
2. **Multi-task deployment** — one frozen base model + many small prefix files; tasks can be swapped instantly by loading a different prefix. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Preservation of base knowledge** — frozen backbone prevents catastrophic forgetting, retaining general language ability. *(1.5 marks)*

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Training instability** — prefix parameters sit at the input of every layer; without reparameterization, optimization can be unstable. *(1 mark)*
2. **Hyperparameter sensitivity** — performance depends on prefix length $\vert P \vert$ and the reparameterization dimensionality $d'$. *(1 mark)*

**Real-world application:** **Domain-adapted chatbots / RAG adapters** in production LLM serving — e.g., a base LLaMA-3 model with separate prefixes for medical-QA, legal-QA, and code-generation, served from a single base checkpoint. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Prefix Tuning with Prompt Tuning.** Prefix = prepended to **K and V at every layer**; Prompt = prepended to **input embedding only**. Mixing these up is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting that the MLP is *only* used during training.** Many students write that the MLP is part of inference — this is incorrect and costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating parameter ratio.** Examiner's checklist specifically looks for the phrase "$\approx 0.1\%$ of model parameters" — omitting it loses a quick mark.
> 4. **Skipping the reparameterization derivation.** A bare formula without the $W_{1}, W_{2}$ expansion and $\tanh$ is considered incomplete (loses 2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Prefix Tuning** is a PEFT method that **freezes the base model** and trains only a small set of **continuous prefix vectors** prepended to K and V at every transformer layer.
- The prefix is a sequence of length $\vert P \vert$ (default **200**), living in the same embedding space as the model's hidden states — *not* in natural language.
- The augmented attention formula: $\text{Attn}(Q, [P_{K};K], [P_{V};V])$ — semicolon means **row-wise concatenation** along sequence length.
- **Reparameterization:** $P = W_{2}\,\tanh(W_{1}\,P')$ — stabilizes training by adding a bottleneck MLP over a smaller latent prefix $P'$.
- At **inference**, the MLP is **discarded** and $P$ is **precomputed and cached** — zero additional latency.
- Parameter efficiency: prefix occupies **$\approx 0.1\%$** of total model parameters for large LMs.
- **Multi-task serving:** one frozen base model + many small per-task prefix files (each only a few MB).
- **Versus Prompt Tuning:** Prefix Tuning injects at *every* layer; Prompt Tuning injects only at the input embedding (less expressive).
- **Versus LoRA:** LoRA modifies existing weight matrices with low-rank updates; Prefix Tuning adds new key/value context. Both are PEFT but architecturally distinct.
- The training objective is the **standard language-model cross-entropy** — gradients flow back through the frozen LM to update *only* the prefix.
- Real-world sweet spot: **autoregressive generation tasks** (summarization, dialogue, code, table-to-text) where the prefix can steer multi-step generation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** P-Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of P-Tuning

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**P-Tuning (Prompt Tuning)** is a **parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)** technique for large pre-trained language models (PLMs) in which a small number of **continuous embedding vectors** (called *soft prompts* or *trainable prompt tokens*) are learned via backpropagation, while the **entire body of the original model parameters remains frozen**.

In its formal mathematical form, given a frozen transformer model parameterized by $\Phi$ and a sequence of trainable prompt embeddings $P_\theta = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_k\}$ where each $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the conditional probability of generating output $y$ given input $x$ is:

$$P(y \mid x; P_\theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{|y|} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x; P_\theta, \Phi)$$

The model is trained by minimizing the cross-entropy loss **only with respect to $P_\theta$**:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}(P_\theta, \Phi_{\text{frozen}})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** P-Tuning is classified under *Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)* and is explicitly contrasted with **full fine-tuning** (updates all parameters) and **adapter-based methods** (inserts trainable modules). It is also the conceptual precursor to **P-Tuning v2** and is closely related to the **Prefix-Tuning** paradigm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a highly experienced, world-class chef (the frozen pre-trained LLM with **billions** of parameters, e.g., GPT-3 175B). The chef already knows how to cook *any* cuisine, but you want them to specifically prepare **Kerala Sadya** exactly to your grandmother's taste.

You have **two options**:
1. **Full Fine-Tuning (Old Way):** Send the chef to a 2-year culinary re-training program. Expensive, time-consuming, and you risk them "forgetting" their foundational skills (**catastrophic forgetting**).
2. **P-Tuning (Smart Way):** Give the chef a small, leather-bound notebook (the *soft prompt*) with **only 10–100 magic cue words** written in a special ink that only the chef understands. The chef's cooking style (parameters) stays untouched, but the notebook softly steers every recipe they prepare toward *Sadya*-style.

The "magic ink" is **gradient descent on continuous embeddings** — the words aren't real English words; they are vectors in the model's hidden space, discovered by training.

## 1.3 Why P-Tuning Matters — Core Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Engineering Problems P-Tuning Solves:**
> 1. **Storage Cost** — A 175B model fine-tuned for 10,000 tasks would require 10,000 full copies (~175 TB each). With P-Tuning, you only store a **~20 KB prompt file per task**.
> 2. **Training Cost** — Backpropagation touches only $\sim 0.001\%$ of parameters, drastically reducing **GPU memory** and **compute FLOPs**.
> 3. **Deployment Simplicity** — One base model + many task-specific prompts (multi-tenant LLM serving).

## 1.4 Standard Quantitative Metrics in P-Tuning

| Metric | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Prompt Length ($k$)** | **10–150 tokens** | Number of virtual prompt tokens |
| **Trainable Params** | **0.001% – 0.1%** of total | Ratio of $|P_\theta|$ to $|\Phi|$ |
| **Hidden Dim ($d$)** | **768, 1024, 4096, 12288** | Matches the base model's $d$ |
| **Perplexity Gap** | **< 1.0** | Difference vs. full fine-tuning on SuperGLUE |
| **Storage per Task** | **~20 KB – 200 KB** | For $k=100$, $d=1024$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The P-Tuning pipeline operates through **six sequential stages**:

1. **Input Tokenization:** The raw text input $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is tokenized into discrete token IDs and embedded via the model's frozen embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d}$, yielding $X_{\text{emb}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$.

2. **Prompt Initialization:** A trainable matrix $P_\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$ is initialized — either randomly, or from embeddings of real words (e.g., "The question is:") as a warm-start heuristic.

3. **Concatenation (Prepending):** The soft prompt is **concatenated** with the input embeddings along the sequence dimension:
   $$H_0 = [P_\theta \, ; \, X_{\text{emb}}] \in \mathbb{R}^{(k+n) \times d}$$

4. **Frozen Transformer Forward Pass:** The full transformer stack ($L$ layers of self-attention + FFN) processes $H_0$ using the **frozen** parameters $\Phi$ (no gradient updates to $\Phi$):
   $$H_L = \text{Transformer}_L \circ \text{Transformer}_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ \text{Transformer}_1 (H_0)$$

5. **Output Head Projection:** The final hidden states corresponding to the input tokens are projected to vocabulary logits via the **frozen** output matrix $W_o$:
   $$\text{logits} = H_L[\text{input positions}] \cdot W_o$$

6. **Loss Computation & Backprop:** Cross-entropy loss is computed against ground-truth labels $y$, and gradients $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_\theta}$ are back-propagated **only to $P_\theta$** (all other gradients are discarded or frozen).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why prepend (not insert) soft prompts?**
> Prepending preserves causal masking compatibility for autoregressive (decoder-only) models like GPT, because the prompt tokens can attend bidirectionally to themselves but only causally to subsequent input tokens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use continuous (not discrete) tokens?**
> Continuous embeddings live in a **higher-dimensional, differentiable search space** $\mathbb{R}^d$, allowing gradient-based optimization to find configurations that no human-readable word can encode. Discrete prompts are constrained to $\sim 50{,}000$ vocabulary slots.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$ | Trainable soft prompt matrix | $k$ prompt tokens, $d$ hidden dim |
| 2 | $\|P_\theta\| = k \cdot d$ | Number of trainable parameters | $100 \times 4096 = 409{,}600$ |
| 3 | $\|\Phi\| \approx L \cdot 12 d^2$ | Frozen model parameters (approx.) | $12 \cdot 96 \cdot 4096^2 \approx 1.9 \times 10^{10}$ |
| 4 | $\rho = \frac{k \cdot d}{L \cdot 12 d^2} = \frac{k}{12 L d}$ | Trainable parameter ratio | $\sim 10^{-5}$ for typical GPT-3 |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{\vert y \vert} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x; P_\theta, \Phi)$ | Cross-entropy training loss | Minimized wrt $P_\theta$ only |
| 6 | $\theta_{t+1} \leftarrow \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{P_\theta} \mathcal{L}$ | Gradient descent update on prompt | $\eta \approx 1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $5 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 7 | $h_i^{(l)} = \text{Attention}(Q_i^{(l)}, K^{(l)}, V^{(l)})$ | Frozen transformer self-attention | Standard multi-head attention |
| 8 | $H_0 = [P_\theta \, \vert \, X_{\text{emb}}]$ | Prompt-input concatenation | Sequence dim concatenation |
| 9 | **Reparameterization (P-Tuning v1)** | $P_\theta = \text{MLP}(P_{\text{raw}})$ | A small LSTM/MLP encodes raw prompts for stability |
| 10 | **P-Tuning v2 Multi-Layer** | Add $P_\theta^{(l)}$ at every layer $l$ | Each layer gets its own prompt prefix |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) instead of `|` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Example: write $\vert y \vert$ not $|y|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

P-Tuning is the de-facto PEFT technique for:
- **Domain Adaptation** — adapting a general LLM to medical, legal, or code domains.
- **Task-Specific Deployment** — multi-tenant SaaS LLM platforms (one base model, thousands of customer prompts).
- **Edge / Mobile LLMs** — when the base model is quantized and frozen in memory, only the prompt is swapped.
- **Continual Learning** — adding new tasks without overwriting old ones (mitigates catastrophic forgetting).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parameter Reduction Ratio $\rho$

**Goal:** Quantify how many fewer parameters P-Tuning trains vs. full fine-tuning, for a generic transformer model with $L$ layers, hidden dimension $d$, and $H$ attention heads.

### Step 1: Parameters in Full Fine-Tuning

For a single transformer block (decoder layer), the standard parameter count is:
- **Q, K, V projection matrices:** $3 d^2$
- **Output projection matrix:** $d^2$
- **FFN up-projection ($W_1$):** $d \times 4d = 4 d^2$
- **FFN down-projection ($W_2$):** $4d \times d = 4 d^2$
- **LayerNorm parameters:** $\approx 2d$ (negligible)

Per-layer parameters $\approx 3d^2 + d^2 + 4d^2 + 4d^2 = 12 d^2$

For the full $L$-layer model (excluding embeddings & LM head):
$$\|\Phi\| \approx 12 L d^2$$

### Step 2: Parameters in P-Tuning

Only the soft prompt matrix $P_\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$ is trainable:
$$\|P_\theta\| = k \cdot d$$

### Step 3: Derive the Ratio $\rho$

$$\rho = \frac{\|P_\theta\|}{\|\Phi\|} = \frac{k \cdot d}{12 L d^2} = \frac{k}{12 L d}$$

**Numerical Example (GPT-3 Small-style, $L=12$, $d=768$, $k=100$):**
$$\rho = \frac{100}{12 \times 12 \times 768} = \frac{100}{110{,}592} \approx 9.04 \times 10^{-4}$$

This means P-Tuning trains only **0.09%** of the parameters of full fine-tuning. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Flow in P-Tuning

**Goal:** Show formally that gradients flow only to $P_\theta$, not to $\Phi$.

Let the total loss be $\mathcal{L}$ and the trainable parameters be partitioned as $\{P_\theta, \Phi\}$ where $\Phi$ is frozen.

By the chain rule applied to the full transformer forward pass $H_L = f_{\Phi}(H_0)$ where $H_0 = [P_\theta, X_{\text{emb}}]$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_\theta} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H_L} \cdot \frac{\partial H_L}{\partial H_0} \cdot \frac{\partial H_0}{\partial P_\theta}$$

**Computing each term:**

$$\frac{\partial H_0}{\partial P_\theta} = I_{k \times k} \otimes I_{d \times d} \quad \text{(identity slices, since } H_0 = [P_\theta; X_{\text{emb}}] \text{ is a direct concatenation)}$$

$$\frac{\partial H_L}{\partial H_0} = \prod_{l=1}^{L} \frac{\partial H_l}{\partial H_{l-1}} = \prod_{l=1}^{L} J_l \quad \text{(Jacobians of frozen transformer layers)}$$

Since $\Phi$ is frozen, all $J_l$ are treated as **constants** during the backward pass (no gradient computation on $\Phi$).

**Final update rule applied *only* to $P_\theta$:**

$$P_\theta^{(t+1)} = P_\theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_\theta}\bigg|_{P_\theta^{(t)}}$$

The frozen $\Phi$ contributes to the *value* of the gradient (it shapes the loss landscape) but receives **no parameter update**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: P-Tuning vs. Related PEFT Methods

| Method | Trainable Params | Where Inserted | Memory | Quality vs. Full FT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | $12 L d^2$ | All weights | **Very High** | **100% (baseline)** |
| **P-Tuning (v1)** | $k \cdot d$ | Input embedding only | **Very Low** | **85 – 95%** |
| **P-Tuning v2** | $L \cdot k \cdot d$ | Every layer prefix | **Low** | **95 – 99%** |
| **Prefix-Tuning** | $L \cdot (k_K + k_V) \cdot d$ | K, V at every layer | **Low** | **92 – 97%** |
| **Adapter (Houlsby)** | $L \cdot 2 d \cdot r$ | Inside FFN | **Medium** | **95 – 99%** |
| **LoRA** | $L \cdot 2 d \cdot r$ | Q, V low-rank | **Low** | **97 – 100%** |

Where $r$ is the LoRA rank, and $k_K$, $k_V$ are the key/value prefix lengths.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A KTU exam question gives $L=24$, $d=1024$, $k=50$. Compute (a) the trainable parameter count for P-Tuning, and (b) the ratio vs. full fine-tuning.

**Solution (a):**
$$\|P_\theta\| = k \cdot d = 50 \times 1024 = 51{,}200 \text{ parameters}$$

**Solution (b):**
$$\|\Phi\| = 12 L d^2 = 12 \times 24 \times (1024)^2 = 12 \times 24 \times 1{,}048{,}576$$

$$\|\Phi\| = 301{,}989{,}888 \approx 3.02 \times 10^8 \text{ parameters}$$

$$\rho = \frac{51{,}200}{3.02 \times 10^8} \approx 1.70 \times 10^{-4} = 0.017\%$$

**Interpretation:** P-Tuning trains only **51,200 parameters** — a tiny footprint that can be stored in a single 200 KB file. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End P-Tuning Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Raw Input Text X] --> tokenA[Tokenizer]
    tokenA --> embedA[Input Embedding Matrix E FROZEN]
    embedA --> concatA[Concatenation Operator]

    promptA[Trainable Soft Prompt P Theta shape k by d] --> initA[Initialization Random or Word Anchor]
    initA --> reparamA[Reparameterization MLP or LSTM in P Tuning v1]
    reparamA --> concatA

    concatA --> seqA[Sequence H0 of length k plus n]
    seqA --> layerA[Transformer Layer 1 FROZEN]
    layerA --> layerB[Transformer Layer 2 FROZEN]
    layerB --> layerC[Transformer Layer DOTS]
    layerC --> layerD[Transformer Layer L FROZEN]
    layerD --> hiddenA[Final Hidden States HL]

    hiddenA --> headA[LM Head W O FROZEN]
    headA --> logitsA[Vocabulary Logits]
    logitsA --> lossA[Cross Entropy Loss vs Ground Truth Y]

    lossA --> gradA[Backpropagation ONLY to P Theta]
    gradA --> optA[Optimizer Adam or AdamW updates P Theta]
    optA --> promptA

    style reparamA fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

**Color Legend:**
- **Yellow blocks** = trainable (soft prompt + reparameterization).
- **Cyan blocks** = frozen (model body, embeddings, LM head).
- **Red block** = gradient path — flows *only* to the soft prompt.

## 4.2 Comparison: P-Tuning v1 vs. P-Tuning v2 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V1[ARCHITECTURE P TUNING v1]
        v1a[Input Layer] --> v1b[Trainable Prompt P Theta at Input Only]
        v1b --> v1c[Transformer Layers 1 to L FROZEN]
        v1c --> v1d[Output]
    end

        v2d --> v2e[Transformer Layer 2 FROZEN]
```

## 4.3 Training vs. Inference Data Flow

```mermaid

        t2 --> t3[Compute Loss]
        t3 --> t4[Update ONLY Soft Prompt]
    end

    subgraph INF[INFERENCE PHASE]
        i1[Base Model FROZEN] --> i2[Soft Prompt FIXED Pre-Trained]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define P-Tuning in the context of Generative AI. State two advantages of P-Tuning over full fine-tuning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

P-Tuning is a **parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)** technique in which a small set of **continuous, learnable embedding vectors** (called soft prompts) is optimized via backpropagation, while the **entire pre-trained language model is kept frozen**. (1.5 marks)

**Two advantages over full fine-tuning:** (2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks)
1. **Storage Efficiency:** Stores only ~20–200 KB per task (the soft prompt) instead of full model copies (~GBs).
2. **Training Speed:** Updates only ~0.001%–0.1% of parameters, drastically reducing GPU memory and FLOPs.
3. *(Any other valid point: multi-task deployment, no catastrophic forgetting, etc.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between a "soft prompt" and a "hard prompt"? Why are soft prompts preferred in P-Tuning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hard prompt** is a sequence of **discrete, human-readable text tokens** drawn from the model's fixed vocabulary (e.g., *"Translate the following English sentence to French:"*). A **soft prompt** is a sequence of **continuous, trainable embedding vectors** in $\mathbb{R}^d$ that do **not correspond to any actual word** in the vocabulary. (1.5 marks)

**Why soft prompts are preferred:** (1.5 marks)
1. **Differentiable Search Space:** Continuous embeddings live in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and can be optimized by gradient descent; discrete tokens cannot.
2. **Larger Expressive Capacity:** Soft prompts can encode configurations no human-readable word can represent.
3. **Task-Specific Steering:** Each task gets its own optimized soft prompt, enabling efficient multi-task deployment.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and training procedure** of P-Tuning with a neat block diagram. Clearly differentiate between trainable and frozen components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pre-trained transformer has $L=24$ layers, hidden dimension $d=1024$, and uses a vocabulary of size $V=50{,}257$ (GPT-2 style). You decide to apply P-Tuning with $k=100$ soft prompt tokens. Compute:
- (i) The number of **trainable parameters** introduced by P-Tuning.
- (ii) The total number of **frozen parameters** in the transformer body (excluding embeddings and LM head).
- (iii) The **ratio** of trainable to frozen parameters $\rho$, expressed as a percentage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **P-Tuning, P-Tuning v2, and LoRA** in terms of (i) where trainable parameters are inserted, (ii) parameter count, and (iii) typical performance gap vs. full fine-tuning. Present your answer in a comparison table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **three real-world engineering applications** of P-Tuning in deploying Generative AI systems. For each application, explain (i) the use case, (ii) the benefit gained from P-Tuning, and (iii) one limitation or challenge.

---

## Model Solutions for Part B — Question A

### Solution A(a) — Architecture and Training [7 Marks]

The P-Tuning architecture consists of **two parallel information streams** that are merged before entering the frozen transformer:

1. **Frozen Input Embedding Stream:** The raw input $x$ is tokenized and converted to embeddings $X_{\text{emb}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ using the model's frozen embedding matrix $E$. (1 mark)

2. **Trainable Soft Prompt Stream:** A learnable matrix $P_\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$ is initialized (randomly or from word-anchor embeddings) and optionally passed through a small reparameterization MLP for training stability. (1 mark)

3. **Concatenation:** $H_0 = [P_\theta \, ; \, X_{\text{emb}}] \in \mathbb{R}^{(k+n) \times d}$. (1 mark)

4. **Frozen Forward Pass:** All $L$ transformer layers process $H_0$ with their parameters $\Phi$ frozen (no gradient updates). (1 mark)

5. **Loss Computation:** The LM head produces vocabulary logits, and cross-entropy loss is computed vs. the ground-truth $y$. (1 mark)

6. **Selective Backprop:** Gradients $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_\theta}$ are backpropagated **only** to the soft prompt parameters; all other gradients on $\Phi$ are discarded. (1 mark)

7. **Block Diagram:** (1 mark) — See SECTION 4.1 above.

**Trainable components:** Soft prompt $P_\theta$, reparameterization network.
**Frozen components:** Embedding matrix, all $L$ transformer layers, LM head $W_o$.

---

### Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $L=24$, $d=1024$, $k=100$.

**(i) Trainable Parameters [2 marks]:**

$$\|P_\theta\| = k \cdot d = 100 \times 1024 = 102{,}400 \text{ parameters}$$

**(ii) Frozen Parameters in Transformer Body [3 marks]:**

Using the standard transformer block parameter count $\approx 12 d^2$ per layer:

$$\|\Phi\| = 12 \cdot L \cdot d^2 = 12 \times 24 \times (1024)^2$$

$$(1024)^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$$

$$\|\Phi\| = 12 \times 24 \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 301{,}989{,}888 \approx 3.02 \times 10^8$$

**(iii) Ratio $\rho$ [2 marks]:**

$$\rho = \frac{102{,}400}{301{,}989{,}888} \approx 3.39 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\rho \approx 0.0339\%$$

**Interpretation:** P-Tuning trains only **~0.034%** of the parameters in the transformer body — a reduction by a factor of nearly **3,000×**.

---

## Model Solutions for Part B — Question B

### Solution B(a) — Comparison Table [7 Marks]

| Aspect | P-Tuning v1 | P-Tuning v2 | LoRA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Where inserted** | Input embedding only (1 mark) | Prompt prefix at **every transformer layer** (1 mark) | Low-rank decomposition $\Delta W = AB$ on Q, V (1 mark) |
| **(ii) Param count** | $k \cdot d$ (smallest) (1 mark) | $L \cdot k \cdot d$ (medium) (1 mark) | $L \cdot 2 d \cdot r$ (configurable via $r$) (1 mark) |
| **(iii) Perf. gap vs. full FT** | 5–15% (largest gap) (1 mark) | 1–5% (small gap) (0.5 mark) | <1–3% (closest to full FT) (0.5 mark) |

### Solution B(b) — Real-World Applications [7 Marks]

**Application 1: Multi-Tenant SaaS Chatbot Platform** (2.5 marks)
- *Use case:* A company serves 10,000 enterprise customers from one base LLM. Each customer's domain (legal, medical, finance) needs a specialized assistant.
- *Benefit:* P-Tuning stores a **20–200 KB prompt per customer** instead of 7 GB of full model weights, enabling **10,000× storage reduction**.
- *Limitation:* Inference latency increases slightly due to longer input sequences ($k + n$ vs. $n$).

**Application 2: On-Device / Edge LLM Personalization** (2.5 marks)
- *Use case:* A quantized 7B model runs on a smartphone; user wants to personalize it for medical Q&A.
- *Benefit:* Only the prompt is trainable; the frozen quantized model stays small. Prompts can be **downloaded over-the-air** for new tasks.
- *Limitation:* Performance is bounded by the base model's capabilities; soft prompts cannot inject *new knowledge* the base model lacks.

**Application 3: Continual Learning Without Catastrophic Forgetting** (2 marks)
- *Use case:* A research lab sequentially trains an LLM on 100 NLP benchmarks.
- *Benefit:* Each new task adds a new soft prompt; old prompts remain untouched, preserving prior performance.
- *Limitation:* Prompt capacity ($k \cdot d$) may be insufficient for highly dissimilar tasks, causing **negative interference** between prompts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing P-Tuning v1 and v2:** v1 inserts prompts *only at the input layer*; v2 inserts at *every layer*. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Counting parameters wrongly:** Remember that the *embedding matrix* and *LM head* are **frozen** in P-Tuning — do NOT include them in trainable count. Frozen body count is $12 L d^2$, **not** $12 L d^2 + |V|d$.
> 3. **Misidentifying gradient flow:** A common error is writing that *all* model parameters are updated. The hallmark of P-Tuning is that **only $P_\theta$ receives gradient updates**.
> 4. **Writing $|\cdot|$ in markdown tables:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX inside tables to avoid syntax breakage.
> 5. **Forgetting the reparameterization trick:** P-Tuning v1 originally used an LSTM/MLP encoder over the prompt for stability. Mentioning this earns a bonus mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** P-Tuning is a PEFT method that trains **only continuous soft-prompt embeddings** $P_\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times d}$, keeping the entire base model $\Phi$ frozen.
- **Core Formula:** $H_0 = [P_\theta \, ; \, X_{\text{emb}}]$; loss is backpropagated only to $P_\theta$.
- **Trainable Parameter Count:** $\|P_\theta\| = k \cdot d$.
- **Reduction Ratio:** $\rho = \dfrac{k}{12 L d}$ — typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **P-Tuning v1:** Prompt at the *input embedding* layer only; often uses a reparameterization MLP for training stability.
- **P-Tuning v2:** Prompts prepended at **every transformer layer** (closer to Prefix-Tuning); bridges the gap to full fine-tuning.
- **Soft vs. Hard Prompts:** Soft = continuous, differentiable, trainable; Hard = discrete, human-readable, non-trainable.
- **Advantages:** Storage efficiency (~20 KB/task), training speed, multi-task deployment, no catastrophic forgetting.
- **Limitations:** Soft prompts are not human-interpretable; cannot inject new knowledge into the base model; very small models (<1B params) often underperform adapters/LoRA.
- **Comparison Anchor:** P-Tuning ≠ Prefix-Tuning (prefix tunes K,V at every layer; P-Tuning v1 tunes only input). P-Tuning ≠ LoRA (LoRA injects low-rank weight updates; P-Tuning tunes input embeddings).
- **Exam Trick:** If a question asks for the *ratio* of trainable to total parameters, compute $\|P_\theta\| / \|\Phi\| = k / (12 L d)$.
- **Practical Heuristic:** For NLU tasks with small models, prefer **adapters or LoRA**; for NLG and very large models (>10B), P-Tuning is competitive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Rank Adaptation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT602 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low-Rank Adaptation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)** is a *Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)* methodology that freezes the pre-trained weight matrices of a large foundation model and injects **trainable low-rank decomposition matrices** in parallel, thereby drastically reducing the number of learnable parameters required to adapt a model to a downstream task while preserving the original model's knowledge.

Formally, for a pre-trained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, LoRA constrains its update $\Delta W$ to a **low-rank decomposition**:

$$\Delta W = B \cdot A, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}, \; A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}, \; r \ll \min(d, k)$$

where $r$ is termed the **intrinsic rank** of the adaptation.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**  
> LoRA belongs to the family of *PEFT techniques* and is foundational to efficient deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs). The KTU examiner expects students to (a) define the low-rank constraint, (b) write the modified forward equation, and (c) compute parameter/memory savings.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Adjustment" View

Imagine a world-class master chef (the pre-trained LLM) who has spent decades perfecting a base recipe. Now, you want this chef to specialize in **Kerala cuisine** without losing his Italian or French expertise.

- **Full Fine-Tuning** = Sending the chef back to culinary school for 4 years to relearn everything. Expensive, slow, and risks *catastrophic forgetting*.
- **LoRA** = Giving the chef a *small notebook of "Kerala spice adjustments"* (e.g., *add 1 tsp coconut oil; reduce oregano by 30%*). The base recipe is unchanged, but every dish is now subtly adapted.

Mathematically, this "notebook" is the pair $(B, A)$ with **rank $r$** — a tiny, low-dimensional *delta* applied on top of the original weights $W_0$.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why is this valid?**  
> Empirical and theoretical evidence (Aghajanyan et al., 2020 — "Intrinsic Dimensionality of LLMs") shows that the *effective* dimension of weight updates during fine-tuning is surprisingly small — often as low as **r ≈ 4 to 64**, even when the model has billions of parameters. This is the "low intrinsic rank hypothesis" that justifies LoRA.

### 1.3 Key Standard Parameters (Typical Defaults)

- **Rank $r$**: $4, 8, 16, 32, 64$ (higher = more expressive, more parameters)
- **Scaling factor $\alpha$**: often set to **$2r$** or a fixed value like **16** or **32**
- **Target modules**: attention projection matrices $W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o$ and/or feed-forward $W_{up}, W_{down}$
- **Dropout on LoRA layers**: typically **0.05 to 0.1**
- **Initialization**: $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (Gaussian), $B = 0$ (zero) — ensures $\Delta W = 0$ at start
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Walk-Through — The LoRA Forward Pass

The modified forward computation for a LoRA-augmented linear layer is:

$$h = W_0 \, x + \Delta W \, x = W_0 \, x + \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B \cdot A \cdot x$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Input** $x \in \mathbb{R}^k$ enters the layer.
2. **Frozen path**: Compute $W_0 x$ using the *original* (non-trainable) weight matrix.
3. **Adaptation path**: Project $x$ down to a low-dimensional space via $A$ (size $r \times k$).
4. **Lift back up**: Project the $r$-dim vector back to $d$-dim via $B$ (size $d \times r$).
5. **Scale**: Multiply by $\frac{\alpha}{r}$ to control the magnitude of the update.
6. **Sum**: Add the frozen and adapted outputs element-wise.
7. **Output** $h \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is passed to the next layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> At inference time, the LoRA matrices can be **merged** into $W_0$:  
> $W_{\text{merged}} = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B \cdot A$  
> This adds **zero inference latency** — a critical engineering advantage.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

| Design Choice | Justification |
|---|---|
| Freeze $W_0$ | Preserves pre-trained knowledge; reduces trainable memory by orders of magnitude. |
| Low rank $r$ | Exploits the low intrinsic dimensionality of task-specific updates. |
| Scaling factor $\frac{\alpha}{r}$ | Keeps update magnitude stable across different $r$ values (Hu et al., 2021). |
| $B=0$ initialization | Ensures training starts from the *base model* — no random perturbation. |
| Parallel (additive) injection | Avoids architectural changes; modules are plug-and-play. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimensionality |
|---|---|---|
| $W_0$ | Frozen pre-trained weight | $d \times k$ |
| $\Delta W$ | Total trainable update | $d \times k$ |
| $B$ | LoRA "up-projection" matrix | $d \times r$ |
| $A$ | LoRA "down-projection" matrix | $r \times k$ |
| $r$ | LoRA rank (bottleneck) | scalar, $r \ll \min(d,k)$ |
| $\alpha$ | Scaling constant | scalar |
| $P_{\text{full}}$ | Params in full $\Delta W$ | $d \cdot k$ |
| $P_{\text{LoRA}}$ | Params in $(B,A)$ | $r \cdot (d+k)$ |
| $h$ | Layer output | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |

**Core Equations (Kerala Board Exam — must memorize):**

$$h \;=\; W_0 x \;+\; \frac{\alpha}{r} \, B A x$$

$$P_{\text{LoRA}} \;=\; r(d + k)$$

$$\text{Reduction Ratio} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{full}}} \;=\; \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} \;=\; \frac{r}{d} + \frac{r}{k}$$

$$W_{\text{merged}}^{\text{inference}} \;=\; W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A$$

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Multi-task serving**: A single base model + many tiny LoRA adapters (one per task) — used in production at scale (e.g., chatbot platforms serving thousands of customized agents).
- **Edge deployment**: Reduces storage — swapping a 7 GB full fine-tune for a **~20 MB** LoRA adapter.
- **Domain adaptation**: Adapting general LLMs to medical, legal, or code domains cheaply.
- **Foundation of advanced PEFT**: QLoRA (quantized base + LoRA), AdaLoRA (adaptive rank allocation), LoRA-FA (frozen-A variant) all build on this principle.
- **Constitutional AI alignment**: RLHF pipelines use LoRA to avoid catastrophic forgetting of base capabilities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parameter Count Reduction

**Problem:** For a weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{4096 \times 4096}$ and LoRA rank $r = 8$, compute the parameter count for full fine-tuning vs. LoRA, and the reduction factor.

**Step 1 — Full fine-tuning parameter count:**  
The full update $\Delta W$ has dimensionality $4096 \times 4096$.

$$P_{\text{full}} = d \cdot k = 4096 \times 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 2 — LoRA parameter count:**  
With rank $r = 8$, matrices $A$ and $B$ have shapes $8 \times 4096$ and $4096 \times 8$ respectively.

$$P_{\text{LoRA}} = r(d + k) = 8 \times (4096 + 4096) = 8 \times 8192 = 65{,}536 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 3 — Reduction factor:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{full}}} = \frac{65{,}536}{16{,}777{,}216} = \frac{1}{256}$$

So LoRA trains **256× fewer parameters**.

**Step 4 — General formula derivation (for the answer sheet):**

$$\frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{full}}} = \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} = \frac{r}{k} + \frac{r}{d}$$

For $d = k$, this simplifies to $\frac{2r}{d}$, which for $d=4096, r=8$ gives $\frac{16}{4096} = \frac{1}{256}$ — consistent with Step 3.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Low-Rank Hypothesis (Why It Works)

**Statement:** The change in weights $\Delta W$ during fine-tuning lives in a low-dimensional subspace of dimension $r \ll \min(d, k)$.

**Argument (Aghajanyan et al., adapted for KTU-style derivation):**

Consider the pre-trained model $f_\theta$ with parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^D$ where $D$ is the total parameter count (e.g., $D \approx 7 \times 10^9$ for a 7B model). Fine-tuning seeks:

$$\min_{\Delta\theta} \; \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_{\theta_0 + \Delta\theta}, \mathcal{D}_{\text{task}}\bigr)$$

Empirical random projection analysis shows that the *minimal $r$* needed to reach a target loss $\epsilon$ satisfies:

$$r \approx \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{\log D}{\epsilon^2} \right) \ll D$$

In practice, $r \in [4, 64]$ suffices even for $D \approx 10^{10}$. This is the **intrinsic dimensionality** result. LoRA exploits it by parameterizing $\Delta W$ as $BA$ with the smallest viable $r$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Cost Analysis (Training)

**Full fine-tuning memory per parameter (mixed-precision, Adam optimizer):**

- Weights: $2$ bytes (FP16)
- Gradients: $2$ bytes (FP16)
- Optimizer states (momentum + variance): $8$ bytes (FP32)
- **Total per param:** $12$ bytes

For $\Delta W$ of size $d \times k$:

$$M_{\text{full}} = 12 \cdot d \cdot k$$

**LoRA memory:** Only $A$ and $B$ carry optimizer states; $W_0$ is frozen (weights only, no grads, no optimizer states for those bytes).

$$M_{\text{LoRA, trainable}} = 12 \cdot r(d + k)$$

Additionally, frozen $W_0$ storage is $2 \cdot d \cdot k$ bytes (FP16 weights, no optimizer state).

**Net savings factor:**

$$\text{Memory ratio} = \frac{12 \cdot r(d+k) + 2 \cdot d \cdot k}{14 \cdot d \cdot k} \approx \frac{1}{7} \text{ to } \frac{1}{50}$$

For GPT-3 175B with $r = 4$, this reduction is the difference between **1.2 TB** and **35 GB** of GPU memory — a practical impossibility becoming routine.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gradient Flow Through LoRA

Given loss $\mathcal{L}$, gradients w.r.t. $A$ and $B$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \, B^\top \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h} \, x^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial B} = \frac{\alpha}{r} \, \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h} \, x^\top A^\top$$
